title: ALU implementation: rotate/shift (CB prefix)
description:
Implement CB-prefix rotate and shift operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- RLC r: rotate left circular, C=old bit 7, Z=result==0
- RRC r: rotate right circular, C=old bit 0, Z=result==0
- RL r: rotate left through carry, C=old bit 7, bit 0=old C, Z=result==0
- RR r: rotate right through carry, C=old bit 0, bit 7=old C, Z=result==0
- SLA r: shift left arithmetic, C=old bit 7, bit 0=0, Z=result==0
- SRA r: shift right arithmetic, C=old bit 0, bit 7 preserved, Z=result==0
- SRL r: shift right logical, C=old bit 0, bit 7=0, Z=result==0
- SWAP r: swap nibbles, Z=result==0, all other flags 0

Also: unprefixed rotate/shift (RLCA, RRCA, RLA, RRA):
- Same as RLC A, RRC A, RL A, RR A BUT Z flag is always 0 (not set on zero result!)
- This difference between prefixed and unprefixed rotates is a common bug.

title: Pin external PPU test suites and set up expected references
description:
Pin exact revisions of external PPU test suites and set up bench/expected/.

SUITES TO PIN:
- mooneye-test-suite: PPU acceptance tests (vblank_stat_intr-GS, stat_lyc_onoff, etc.)
- mealybug-tearoom-tests: mode-3 register timing tests
- dmg-acid2: whole-PPU composition reference image
- cgb-acid2: (version pin only, actual use deferred to CGB wave)

Record in bench lockfile: exact source_repo, revision, license, suite_class.
Preserve suite class distinctions (acceptance/emulator-only/manual-only/misc)
from mooneye.

Set up bench/expected/:
- suite_owned/: dmg-acid2 reference image, mealybug expected results
- human_reviewed/: manually verified reference artifacts

dmg-acid2 provides explicit grayscale normalization values ($00, $55, $AA, $FF)
for DMG comparison. Encode this in the manifest as explicit image_normalization.

mealybug ships expected results per SoC revision (DMG, CGB-C, CGB-D) and an
automation story. Pin the DMG expected results.

IMPORTANT: PyBoy buffer representations are explicitly subject to change.
Artifacts should never implicitly depend on low-level backend formats. Use
normalized logical shade data as the stable currency.

title: Author Wave A PPU timing ROMs
description:
Write SM83 assembly for Wave A timing/control ROMs.

These ROMs verify register/timing state BEFORE any rendering is trusted.

1. PPU_OFF_ON_BASIC:
   Test LCD enable/disable (LCDC.7). Verify: mode reports 0 when off, VRAM/OAM
   accessible when off, first frame blank after re-enable, LY resets on disable.

2. LY_LYC_BASIC:
   Test LY increment through 0-153 cycle. Verify LYC comparison flag, LYC
   interrupt generation when LYC select enabled in STAT.

3. STAT_MODE_SEQ:
   Test mode sequence: Mode 2 (80 dots) -> Mode 3 (variable) -> Mode 0 (remainder)
   for visible lines, Mode 1 for lines 144-153. Read STAT at various points to
   verify mode bits.

4. VBLANK_IRQ_BASIC:
   Test VBlank interrupt: fires on line 144 entry, IF bit 0 set, ISR executes.

5. VRAM_OAM_GATING:
   Test CPU access restrictions: VRAM blocked in mode 3, OAM blocked in modes 2+3,
   both accessible in modes 0+1 and when LCD off.

ASSEMBLY CONVENTIONS: Follow existing ROM ABI (spec/rom_abi.md):
- __pass and __fail labels
- WRAM signature block at 0xC000
- Optional __checkpoint labels

title: SM83 assembler toolchain and ROM build pipeline
description:
Set up the SM83 assembler toolchain for building custom test ROMs.

TOOLS NEEDED:
- rgbds (rgbasm, rgblink, rgbfix): the standard Game Boy assembler/linker/fixer
- Pin exact version in toolchain/versions.md
- Install via devenv, cx, or nix (per CLAUDE.md conventions)

ROM BUILD PIPELINE:
- Create bench/roms/ directory for ROM source (.asm) and built artifacts (.gb, .sym, .map)
- Create bench/roms/Makefile (or shell script) that:
  1. Assembles each .asm → .o with rgbasm
  2. Links .o → .gb + .sym + .map with rgblink
  3. Fixes header with rgbfix
  4. Validates .sym file has required labels (__pass, __fail)
- Each ROM source file follows the checkpoint ABI (§17.0.1)

ROM SOURCE TEMPLATE:
- Create bench/roms/template.inc with:
  - Standard header (Nintendo logo, title, checksum)
  - SkipBoot entry point at 0x0100
  - __pass and __fail label stubs
  - WRAM signature block macros
  - Helper macros for test assertions (compare register, compare memory, report pass/fail)
  - Detailed per-test logging: each test case writes test ID + expected + actual to WRAM before checking

LOGGING CONTRACT:
- Every test case in every ROM writes to a WRAM log region:
  - 2-byte test ID (unique within ROM)
  - 1-byte status (0x00=not run, 0x01=pass, 0xFF=fail)
  - N bytes of diagnostic data (expected value, actual value, relevant flags)
- The harness reads this log region after ROM execution to produce detailed per-test-case reports
- On failure, the log tells you WHICH test case failed and WHY, not just 'ROM failed'

VERIFICATION:
- Build a trivial 'hello' ROM that writes a known value to WRAM and hits __pass
- Verify the .sym file is generated and contains __pass/__fail
- Verify rgbfix produces a valid Game Boy ROM header

WHY THIS IS CRITICAL: Every 'Green X ROM' bead requires building a custom assembly ROM. Without this toolchain, none of those beads can start.

title: Epic: PPU Formal & Equivalence Verification
description:
Add generated-Verilog formal safety/equivalence checks and backend-differential
testing for the PPU. This is verification lanes 2, 3, and the backend-diff lane
from the revised test taxonomy (history/ppu.md section 16.3).

Because Spade emits Verilog, SymbiYosys-based bounded/unbounded safety checking
and EQY equivalence checking are a natural fit.

THREE VERIFICATION STRATEGIES:

1. BOUNDED FORMAL SAFETY CHECKS on narrow control blocks:
   - Mode state machine phase invariants
   - STAT-line rising-edge properties
   - Window state machine reachability
   - Fetcher epoch monotonicity
   - OAM scan 10-object limit
   These use SymbiYosys with BMC (bounded model checking) on the generated Verilog.

2. EQUIVALENCE CHECKS for refactors:
   - Any timing-visible refactor must satisfy either an equivalence check on the
     affected cut OR a bounded formal proof plus unchanged differential replay.
   - Uses yosys + EQY or miter-based approach.
   - Targets: fetcher restructures, mixer rewrites, optional micro-pipelines.
   Extends the existing bd-mos equivalence framework from CPU to PPU.

3. BACKEND-DIFFERENTIAL TESTING:
   - Run identical test suites against at least two memory backends:
     (a) trivial simulation-array backend
     (b) inferred-synchronous-RAM backend
   - Any divergence proves the memory protocol was underspecified.
   - This is a different failure mode than semantic bugs -- it catches protocol
     assumptions that only happen to work with one backend.

Directory structure: formal/ppu/{safety/, equivalence/, harness/}

RATIONALE: The PPU will undergo refactors (fetcher restructuring, optional
micro-pipelining) after initial bring-up. Without formal/equivalence protection,
each refactor re-risks the entire semantic surface. The investment in formal
properties NOW reduces the cost of all future changes.

title: Green MBC3_SWITCH custom ROM
description:
Write and green MBC3_SWITCH.

Scope:
- ROM bank switching only
- Verify 7-bit bank selection and bank 0→1 remap

Why split: basic MBC3 ROM banking can go green before RAM/RTC behavior.

title: E2E milestone gate test: Milestone D -- interrupt/timer ready
description:
Create tools/gate_milestone_d.sh -- acceptance gate for Milestone D (§22: Interrupt/Timer Ready).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (§22 Milestone D):
1. EI_DELAY and TIMER_IRQ_HALT ROMs green
2. Live instruction-commit differential green on ALL Wave B ROMs
3. Strict M-cycle differential green on targeted subsets (selected timer/interrupt sequences)
4. Seeded interrupt injection scenarios green (bd-27r)
5. Every interrupt source observed in at least one green test: timer, serial (stub), joypad, VBlank (stub)
6. HALT wake behavior confirmed: HALT with pending IE&IF wakes correctly
7. EI+HALT and interrupt edge case interaction tests pass (bd-1g0)
8. JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST passes with deterministic joypad injection

SCRIPT VALIDATES:
- All Wave B ROM tests pass
- JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST passes
- Interrupt injection scenario tests pass
- EI+HALT edge case tests pass
- Timer unit tests pass (bd-v2p)
- Interrupt controller unit tests pass (bd-11x)
- Coverage report confirms all interrupt sources exercised

LOGGING: Per-test pass/fail with interrupt source coverage matrix.

ACCEPTANCE: Script exits 0 when all criteria pass. Milestone D confirms the CPU handles all time-dependent and asynchronous behavior correctly.

title: Interrupt controller unit tests (IE/IF, priority, acknowledge)
description:
Create test/unit/test_interrupts.py — isolated tests for IE/IF storage, pending-vector computation, priority selection, and acknowledge behavior.

These tests should drive the peripheral-side acknowledge input directly and intentionally do NOT wait for the full CPU ServiceInterrupt sequencer. The point is to validate interrupt-controller semantics independently before higher-level interrupt sequencing is debugged.

TEST CASES:
1. IE register read/write and upper-bit behavior
2. IF register read/write, including externally asserted interrupt sources
3. irq_pending = IE & IF across the full 5-bit space
4. Priority resolution when multiple sources are pending
5. Acknowledge clears only the selected IF bit
6. Edge cases around simultaneous set/clear activity

LOGGING: print IE/IF/pending state transitions so failures localize to the controller itself, not the CPU phase machine.

title: Green MBC1_SWITCH custom ROM
description:
Write and green MBC1_SWITCH.

Scope:
- ROM bank switching only
- Verify bank 0→1 remap and 0x20/0x40/0x60 special-case wrapping

Why split: ROM banking can go green before RAM banking.

title: ALU unit tests green (curated + generated vectors)
description:
Green test/unit/test_alu_generated_vectors.py and test/unit/test_alu.py.

Three verification lanes (§11.4):
1. Curated examples: hand-picked edge cases testing specific flag behaviors
   - ADD 0xFF + 0x01 = 0x00 (Z=1, C=1, H=1)
   - ADC 0x0F + 0x00 + carry=1 (H=1 from carry into high nibble)
   - SUB 0x00 - 0x01 (borrow, Z=0, N=1, H=1, C=1)
   - INC 0xFF = 0x00 (Z=1, C unchanged!)
   - DAA after 0x09 + 0x01 = 0x10 (BCD correction)
   
2. Generated vectors: systematic sweep from gbxcule-derived reference
   - Every ADD A,x for x in representative set
   - Every ADC with carry=0 and carry=1
   - ADD SP,e8 for representative SP values and signed offsets
   - LD HL,SP+e8 with same cases

3. Nightly exhaustive (pytest marker for CI):
   - All ADD A,x for all x (256*256 = 65536 cases)
   - All ADC A,x with carry 0/1 (256*256*2 = 131072 cases)
   - All SUB x, all SBC with carry 0/1
   - All DAA inputs over (A:256, N:2, H:2, C:2) = 2048 cases

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone B): All ALU unit tests green.

title: Misc single-cycle CPU instructions: NOP, STOP stub, DI, EI basic handling
description:
Implement phase machine handling for miscellaneous single-cycle instructions that don't fit the load/ALU/flow/CB families.

INSTRUCTIONS:
- NOP (0x00): single fetch cycle, no state change at all. The simplest possible instruction.
- DI (0xF3): set ime_state = Disabled. Takes effect immediately.
- EI (0xFB): set ime_state = PendingEnable. Full PendingEnable→Enabled transition comes in Phase 4 (T53/bd-3o3). For Phase 3b, PendingEnable is set but never transitions to Enabled (no interrupt check in fetch yet). This is correct because without an interrupt system, the IME value has no observable effect.
- STOP (0x10): stub implementation — behaves like a 2-byte NOP for now. Real STOP puts the CPU in low-power mode and has complex behavior involving the joypad register. Full implementation deferred.
- DAA, CPL, SCF, CCF (0x27, 0x2F, 0x37, 0x3F): these are already handled by the ALU phase machine (T42/bd-3ab), but verify they appear in the decoder and route correctly.

WHY THIS IS CRITICAL:
Every custom test ROM will contain NOP instructions (for alignment, timing, or between test cases). Most ROMs start with DI to disable interrupts during setup. Without handling these opcodes, the CPU will enter an undefined state on the very first NOP in any test ROM, making ALL Wave A ROM beads impossible to green.

The Phase 3b phase machine beads (T41-T45) deliberately organize by instruction FAMILY:
- T41: loads
- T42: 8-bit ALU
- T43: 16-bit arithmetic
- T44: stack/control flow
- T45: CB-prefix
None of these cover NOP, DI, EI, or STOP. This bead fills that gap.

VERIFICATION:
- Add test cases to test/unit/test_misc_instructions.py:
  - NOP: verify no registers change, PC increments by 1, 1 M-cycle
  - DI: verify ime_state = Disabled, 1 M-cycle
  - EI: verify ime_state = PendingEnable, 1 M-cycle
  - STOP: verify PC increments by 2 (2-byte instruction), 1 M-cycle
- Each test uses detailed logging: log instruction, expected state, actual state, diff

title: ALU implementation: INC/DEC 8-bit
description:
Implement 8-bit INC/DEC in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- INC r: r + 1, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=half-carry, C=PRESERVED (not affected!)
- DEC r: r - 1, flags Z=result==0, N=1, H=half-borrow, C=PRESERVED

TRICKY: INC/DEC PRESERVE the carry flag (§11.3). This is a common bug source. The f_prev input carries the previous flags, and only Z/N/H are updated.

title: E2E milestone gate: PPU BG/Window ready (gate_ppu_bgwin.sh)
description:
Create tools/gate_ppu_bgwin.sh -- acceptance gate for PPU Milestone C (BG/Window).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (history/ppu.md section 22, Milestone C):
1. All Wave B owned ROMs green (BG_STATIC through WINDOW_WINEN_TOGGLE_REARM)
2. Tile decode unit tests green (test_tile.py)
3. Window trigger unit tests green (test_window.py)
4. Scanline semantic checks green (LineSummary comparison on BG/window scenes)
5. PyBoy tilemap/screen semantic comparisons green on targeted scenes
6. Wave B.5 mealybug canary subset not regressing

Follows the same gate script pattern as gate_ppu_timing.sh.

title: Epic: CPU Core Bring-Up — Wave A (Phase 3b)
description:
Implement the CPU phase machine and bring all Wave A custom ROMs to green at instruction-boundary differential against the PyBoy oracle.

Wave A ROMs (§17.1):
- LOADS_BASIC: 8-bit loads, 16-bit loads, memory reads/writes via register pairs
- ALU_FLAGS: all 8-bit ALU ops with flag verification
- ALU16_SP: 16-bit arithmetic, ADD SP,e8, LD HL,SP+e8
- FLOW_STACK: PUSH, POP, CALL, RET, JP, JR (conditional and unconditional)
- CB_BITOPS: all CB-prefix rotate/shift/bit/res/set operations
- MEM_RWB: memory read/write-back soak test
- ALU_LOOP: ALU stress/iteration test

Implementation scope:
- Fetch/execute phase machine using the Phase enum from §8.1
- Typed continuation-driven control flow (§8.2)
- cpu_core entity wrapping step_mcycle pure function (§7.3)
- Minimal bus fabric: ROM + WRAM + HRAM + dummy IO reads (Wave A from §12.4)
- Write-enable discipline (§9.4): registers update only on explicit writeback
- Formal checks: F low nibble invariant, m_ce=0 hold behavior
- Metamorphic tests (§16.5): untouched registers unchanged, branch-not-taken uses short cycle

This phase does NOT include timers, interrupts, DMA, MBC, or PPU.

RATIONALE: Wave A deliberately covers most of the CPU instruction set without requiring any peripherals. This is the wave-based staging strategy (§17, Risk 5 mitigation) that prevents peripheral work from obscuring CPU bugs. The cpu_core entity is NOT a pipeline (§4.2)—it's a state machine with explicit registers. The phase machine uses typed continuations to make illegal control states unrepresentable.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone C): All Wave A ROMs green at instruction-boundary differential. No unexplained PC/register divergence. F low nibble invariant green.

title: Periph cleanup: io_write dedup, dead code, redundancies
description:
Several quality and reuse issues across the peripheral modules (timer, interrupts, serial, joypad, OAM DMA). These peripherals are relatively stable, but cleaning them up removes dead logic from synthesis and establishes consistent patterns.

## Background

The peripheral modules implement Game Boy I/O registers: timer (DIV/TIMA/TMA/TAC), interrupt controller (IF/IE), serial port (SB/SC), joypad (JOYP), and OAM DMA. They were implemented one-by-one during bring-up, each developing its own copy of common patterns (io_write address comparison, bool-to-bit encoding, irq_ack construction). Now that all peripherals exist, these patterns can be unified.

This bead depends on shared utilities (bd-21p5) for the extracted io_write function.

## Scope

### 1. io_write duplicated 3x + variant
Identical in timer.spade:~33, interrupts.spade:~10, serial.spade:~9:
  fn io_write(write_en, write_addr, target) -> bool { write_en && write_addr == target }
Joypad.spade:~30 has the same logic with a hardcoded address.

Fix: (Handled by bd-21p5 shared utilities.) After bd-21p5 lands, update these three files to import from the shared location. Update joypad to call the shared version with 0xff00u16.

### 2. Dead prev_pressed computation (joypad.spade:~89, ~98)
prev_pressed is computed by calling selected_pressed_nibble (which runs encode_action_pressed + encode_dpad_pressed — non-trivial combinational logic) and then immediately discarded with let _ = prev_pressed. The actual edge detection uses prev_pressed_reg (a registered value), not prev_pressed.

This means the synthesizer generates the full selected_pressed_nibble mux tree and its output is unconnected. The optimizer may or may not prune it.

Fix: Remove lines ~89 and ~98. The registered prev_pressed_reg is what matters for edge detection.

### 3. Redundant guard in oam_dma (oam_dma.spade:~33)
finishing_transfer = advance_transfer && last_oam_index(index_reg). Therefore advance_transfer && finishing_transfer === finishing_transfer. The redundant advance_transfer && appears on line ~33 in next_active_reg.

Fix: Replace advance_transfer && finishing_transfer with just finishing_transfer. The intent is preserved and the logic is clearer.

### 4. Duplicate zext in oam_dma (oam_dma.spade:~64-65)
source_addr_lo and dest_addr_lo are both zext(index_reg) — identical expressions bound to two signals.

Fix: let index_wide: uint<16> = zext(index_reg); and use in both source_addr and dest_addr computations.

### 5. ack_bits manual decode (interrupts.spade:~32-44)
6-arm match manually decoding 1 << bit (0 → 0x01, 1 → 0x02, ..., 4 → 0x10, _ → 0). If Spade supports variable-amount shift:

Fix: trunc(1u8 << bit). If not, keep but add a comment explaining the manual decode.

### 6. Serial redundant state (serial.spade)
sc_reg stores bit 7 (transfer-in-progress flag) AND transfer_active_reg independently tracks the same state. When transfer completes, bit 7 is cleared in sc_reg (~line 59) and transfer_active_reg is set to false. visible_sc re-sets bit 7 based on transfer_active_reg. This dual-tracking is a correctness risk if the two ever diverge.

Fix: Option A (preferred): Never store bit 7 in sc_reg. Always derive it from transfer_active_reg in visible_sc. Clear the write path so incoming CPU writes to SC only affect bits 0-6.
Option B: Drop transfer_active_reg and read bit 7 from sc_reg directly.

Consideration: Check whether any code reads sc_reg bit 7 directly (vs through visible_sc). If so, option A requires updating those sites.

### 7. Nested if-else flattening
timer.spade:~82-102 (TIMA update logic, 4 levels deep) and serial.spade:~65-74 (transfer state machine, 3 levels deep) use deeply nested if/else chains.

Fix: Flatten to else-if chains. Timer TIMA becomes:
  if write_tima { ... }
  else if overflow_delay_reg == 1u3 { ... }
  else if overflow_delay_reg > 1u3 { ... }
  else if timer_tick && tima_reg == 0xFFu8 { ... }
  else if timer_tick { trunc(tima_reg + 1u8) }
  else { tima_reg }

### 8. irq_ack construction in test tops
irq_ack_none() defined locally in joypad_interrupts_test_top and interrupts_test_top. The CPU module already has no_irq_ack() — verify this exists and is importable. If so, test tops should import it instead of defining local copies.

## Considerations
- Item 2 (dead prev_pressed) is the most impactful for synthesis: it removes an entire selected_pressed_nibble combinational tree from the joypad.
- Item 6 (serial redundant state) is a correctness improvement, not just cleanup. Redundant state is a source of subtle bugs.
- Item 7 (flattening) is purely cosmetic — no logic change, just readability.
- All peripheral changes must preserve exact register read/write behavior. The Game Boy's peripherals have precise timing semantics.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. io_write imported from shared location (after bd-21p5)
2. Dead prev_pressed computation removed
3. Redundant advance_transfer guard simplified
4. source_addr_lo/dest_addr_lo unified
5. ack_bits uses shift or documented manual decode
6. Serial bit-7 single source of truth
7. if-else chains flattened
8. irq_ack_none consolidated
9. No behavioral change in any peripheral register behavior

## Test Plan
- swim test for timer_test_top, interrupts_test_top, serial_test_top, joypad_test_top, joypad_interrupts_test_top, oam_dma_test_top
- soc_lockstep_top and soc_rom_top tests pass
- Synth gate passes (expect small improvement from dead logic removal)

title: Behavior configuration types (BehaviorConfig, SocRevision, BehaviorFeature)
description:
Define orthogonal behavior feature flags and SoC revision metadata.

TYPES (src/ppu/sem/profiles.spade):

enum ModelProfile { DMG, CGB }

enum SocRevision {
  DMG0, DMGA, DMGB, DMGC, MGB, SGB, SGB2,
  CGB0, CGBA, CGBB, CGBC, CGBD, CGBE,
}

enum BehaviorFeature {
  DmgStatWriteQuirk,          -- DMG-only spurious STAT interrupt on write
  PreCgbdScyBitplaneDesync,   -- pre-CGB-D scroll sampling difference
  Wx0Stutter,                 -- WX=0 causes a 1-dot stutter
  Wx166NextLine,              -- WX=166 affects next line
  WindowRetriggerGlitch,      -- window retrigger produces a glitch
  ObjFetchCancelTiming,       -- exact object fetch cancel timing
  DmgOamDmaBasic,             -- basic DMA behavior
  DmgOamDmaStrict,            -- strict DMA interaction model
  ExactBlockedReadMaterialization, -- blocked reads return exact $FF
}

struct BehaviorConfig {
  model: ModelProfile,
  soc_revision: Option<SocRevision>,
  features: Set<BehaviorFeature>,
}

Named presets (DmgCommon, DmgStrict) may still exist but they EXPAND to
BehaviorConfig rather than being the core API boundary.

WHY ORTHOGONAL: Pan Docs identifies at least two truly orthogonal behavior axes:
the DMG-only spurious STAT-write quirk, and the pre-CGB-D vs CGB-D-and-later SCY
bitplane behavior. Mealybug tests produce different expected results on DMG, CGB-C,
and CGB-D. This proves behavior is NOT a single linear strictness axis. Named
presets that expand to structured configs give you sharp test manifests, good
coverage reporting, and clean future CGB extension.

NOTE: Most SocRevision variants are aspirational -- this project targets DMG first.
Having the enum costs nothing and prevents future awkwardness when CGB work begins.

title: PPU metamorphic and invariant tests (test/ppu/unit/test_ppu_invariants.py)
description:
Implement metamorphic and invariant tests for the PPU (history/ppu.md section 16.5).

These verify STRUCTURAL PROPERTIES that must hold across all rendering scenarios.
They catch bugs that specific ROM tests miss: timing violations, spurious state
changes, access-policy failures, impossible states.

INVARIANT TESTS:

1. DOT_CE=0 HOLD:
   Hold dot_ce=0 for 100 clock cycles. Verify ALL PPU architectural state unchanged.
   Verify no memory requests issued. Verify no ScanoutEvents emitted.

2. NO PIXELS OUTSIDE VISIBLE REGION:
   Run for 1 full frame. Verify no PixelEmit with x>=160 or y>=144.
   Verify exactly 160 pixels per visible scanline (lines 0-143).

3. EXACTLY 456 DOTS PER SCANLINE:
   Count dots between consecutive line_start assertions. Verify exactly 456.

4. MODE SEQUENCE FOR VISIBLE LINES:
   For lines 0-143, verify mode sequence is always 2 -> 3 -> 0.
   Never 2 -> 0, never 3 -> 2, never 0 -> 3, etc.

5. MODE 1 ONLY ON LINES 144-153:
   Verify Mode 1 is entered exactly at line 144 and exited at line 153->0.

6. LYC==LY IS CONTINUOUSLY EVALUATED:
   Change LYC mid-scanline. Verify STAT bit 2 updates immediately (not deferred).

7. STAT LINE RISING-EDGE ONLY:
   Enable all STAT sources. Verify IRQ count per frame matches expected rising edges,
   not level-based firing.

8. WINDOW STATE ONLY THROUGH DEFINED TRIGGERS:
   Verify WindowState never transitions to ActiveOnLine without passing through
   ArmedThisFrame. Verify ArmedThisFrame only set at mode-2 boundaries.

9. 10-OBJECT LIMIT:
   Place 40 sprites all visible. Verify exactly 10 selected per scanline.

10. X-HIDDEN OBJECTS COUNT:
    Place 10 X=0 sprites + 1 visible sprite. Verify visible sprite excluded (10 limit reached).

11. BLOCKED VRAM/OAM ACCESSES:
    Issue CPU reads to VRAM during mode 3. Verify PpuMemResult::UndefinedRead returned.
    Issue CPU reads to OAM during mode 2. Verify UndefinedRead returned.

12. LCD-OFF SUPPRESSES ALL TRAFFIC:
    Disable LCD. Run 1000 dots. Verify zero PpuMemReqs, zero ScanoutEvent::Pixel,
    zero register writes (except the LCDC write itself).

13. MODE-3 LENGTH IS NEVER HARDCODED:
    Run with different SCX values and object counts. Verify mode-3 length varies.

LOGGING: Each invariant test logs what it checked, the full frame context,
and on failure: the exact dot where the invariant was violated, the PPU state
at that dot, and the last 10 state transitions.

title: Green ALU_LOOP stress ROM
description:
Write the ALU_LOOP stress test ROM and green it.

Purpose: extended ALU computation loops to stress the CPU over many instruction cycles.

Test approach:
- Fibonacci-style computation loop with register accumulation
- CRC-like computation using XOR/shift chains
- Counter loops with conditional branches
- Verify final register state against known values

This exercises the CPU over thousands of instructions, catching subtle state corruption that single-instruction tests miss.

Oracle mode: checkpoint (compare at loop endpoints, not every instruction)

title: Window trigger and counter unit tests (test/ppu/unit/test_window.py)
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for window activation logic and internal line counter.

TEST CASES:

1. WINDOW ACTIVATION BASIC:
   Set WY=0, WX=7, LCDC.5=1. Verify window activates on line 0 at X=0.
   Verify WindowState transitions: Inactive -> ArmedThisFrame -> ActiveOnLine.

2. WINDOW WY CHECK AT MODE-2 START:
   Set WY=50. Verify window does NOT arm on lines 0-49.
   Verify window arms (wy_triggered_this_frame) starting at line 50.
   Log PpuSamplingState at each mode-2 start.

3. WINDOW WX TRIGGER:
   Set WX=87 (rendered X + 7 = 80). Verify window starts at X=80 on the line.
   Verify BG FIFO is cleared and fetcher restarts from window tilemap.

4. WINDOW INTERNAL LINE COUNTER:
   Enable window on line 10. Verify window_line starts at 0.
   On line 11 (window still active), verify window_line = 1.
   Verify window_line increments ONLY on lines where window actually renders.

5. WINDOW MID-FRAME DISABLE:
   Enable window, then disable LCDC.5 mid-frame. Verify window stops.
   Re-enable LCDC.5. Verify window_line continues from where it left off
   (it does NOT reset mid-frame).

6. WINDOW LINE COUNTER VBLANK RESET:
   Verify window_line resets to 0 during VBlank.

7. WINDOW WX=0 STUTTER (BehaviorFeature::Wx0Stutter):
   Set WX=0. Verify the 1-dot stutter behavior.

8. WINDOW WX=166 NEXT LINE (BehaviorFeature::Wx166NextLine):
   Set WX=166. Verify it affects the next scanline.

9. WINDOW RETRIGGER:
   Disable and re-enable window on same line. Verify retrigger behavior.

10. WINDOW WY CHECK IS FRAME-LATCHED:
    Set WY=0 on frame start. Change WY=200 mid-frame.
    Verify window still activates because wy_triggered_this_frame was already set.

LOGGING: Log WindowState, PpuSamplingState, window_line at each mode-2 start
and at each X where window could trigger.

title: Epic: Test Harness Infrastructure (Phase 1)
description:
Build the entire verification harness before writing real HDL. This is the plan's most counterintuitive but most important sequencing decision.

Covers:
- PyBoy oracle interface (§14.1): reset, step_commit, read_mem, write_event, snapshot, restore
- Hook-driven adapter for strict lockstep (§14.3): symbol lookup, instruction-boundary hooks, M-cycle stepping
- Replay capsule format (§16.7): ROM id, model/reset profile, seed, event log, symbols hash, first-divergence metadata
- Deterministic perturbation engine (§16.5.1): seeded event scripts keyed by commit index, not wall-clock time
- Simulation-only stimulus ports (§12.6: SimStimulus record)
- Divergence artifact framework: first bad CommitTrace, commit window, minimized replay capsule, localized waveform
- Scaffolded test files: decode completeness, ALU generated vectors, reset profiles, event determinism
- Placeholder lockstep test as xfail(strict=True)

RATIONALE: Thesis #2 says harness first. The plan explicitly warns (§16.1) against 'everything fails' and instead prescribes sanity-green meta tests proving generators/fixtures work, plus red semantic tests proving HDL isn't correct yet. Building the harness first means that when HDL lands in Phase 2, tests immediately fail on VALUE mismatches, not infrastructure errors. This is the difference between a harness that catches bugs and one that hides them.

The oracle is PyBoy-only (thesis #6). No second CPU emulator. No file-based golden traces (thesis #7). The replay capsule format provides reproducibility without trace files—it records the minimal information needed to re-derive any execution.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone A): Generators run reproducibly. Smoke scripts work from clean checkout. Harness tests green. HDL tests fail semantically, not infrastructurally.

title: Green MBC1_SWITCH and MBC1_RAM custom ROMs
description:
Write and green MBC1_SWITCH and MBC1_RAM test ROMs.

MBC1_SWITCH:
- Switch between ROM banks and read expected data
- Verify bank 0→1 mapping
- Verify bank wrapping for banks 0x00, 0x20, 0x40, 0x60
- Verify upper bits work in ROM banking mode

MBC1_RAM:
- Enable external RAM
- Write and read back data across RAM banks
- Verify RAM bank switching in RAM mode
- Verify RAM is inaccessible when disabled (reads return 0xFF)

Oracle mode: instr_commit
close_reason: Superseded by separate MBC1_SWITCH and MBC1_RAM beads

title: CommitTrace and DebugTrace type definitions
description:
Create src/cpu/debug.spade with trace types from §7.2.

CommitTrace — the versioned comparison boundary (simulation-only):
  enum CommitKind { MCycle, InstrCommit, InterruptAck, Checkpoint }
  struct CommitTrace {
    schema_version: uint<16>,
    kind: CommitKind,
    seq: uint<64>,
    pc_before: uint<16>,
    opcode: Option<uint<8>>,
    arch_after: CpuArchState,
    phase_after: Phase,
    bus_req: BusReq,
    bus_resp: BusResp,
    mem_touch: Option<MemTouch>,
    io_touch: Option<IoTouch>,
  }
  struct MemTouch { kind: MemTouchKind, addr: uint<16>, data: uint<8> }
  struct IoTouch { addr: uint<16>, data: uint<8> }

DebugTrace — narrow internal debug record:
  (Define a minimal internal debug record for hardware-optional debug)

Compare scopes (§7.3):
- Every commit: PC/SP/registers/IME/halt/phase/bus req/bus resp
- On memory touch: address/data/region
- At checkpoints: selected memory windows or signatures
- PPU tests: semantic objects first, full frame hash last

DESIGN CONSTRAINTS (§4.4):
- CommitTrace is simulation-only, exposed only through simulation/test tops
- DebugTrace is narrow, internal only
- Hardware tops are FREE of trace fanout
- Schema version allows CommitTrace format evolution without breaking replay capsules

title: Bus ownership arbitration (BusOwner)
description:
Implement bus ownership arbitration in src/bus/membus.spade.

BusOwner enum (§12.2.2):
  enum BusOwner { Cpu, OamDma, Ppu, Idle }

Arbitration rules:
- Default owner: Cpu
- During OAM DMA: OamDma (CPU restricted to HRAM)
- During PPU pixel transfer: Ppu owns VRAM (CPU VRAM reads return 0xFF)
- During PPU OAM search: Ppu owns OAM (CPU OAM reads return 0xFF)

The CPU always emits plain BusReq. Contention is resolved in the fabric:
- If CPU requests a region owned by another master, the fabric returns 0xFF (DmgConservative) and the write is ignored
- BusObs sideband records the blocked access for diagnostics

This is separate from the CPU because bus ownership is a system-level concern (§12.2.2).

title: CPU minor quality: commit_seq, bus_req destructure, dead catch-all
description:
Five minor quality issues in CPU core.spade, alu.spade, and semantics.spade. Individually small, collectively they improve code clarity and catch future bugs earlier.

## Background

These findings are each too small for their own bead but share a theme: places where the code is correct but unnecessarily verbose, redundant, or masks potential future errors. They primarily affect core.spade (the CPU entity) and alu.spade (the ALU), with a couple of cleanups in semantics.spade.

## Scope

### 1. commit_seq computed twice (core.spade:~253-265)
The expression if m_ce { trunc(commit_seq + 1u64) } else { commit_seq } appears in both the register feedback and the CpuCoreOut struct. For synthesis, this may generate two independent adder instances.

Fix: let next_commit_seq = if m_ce { trunc(commit_seq + 1u64) } else { commit_seq }; then use in both the reg definition and the output. This is both clearer and avoids potential duplicate logic.

### 2. bus_req_kind/addr/data triple match (core.spade:~57-79, ~272-288)
Three separate functions each match on BusReq to extract one field. They are called in groups of three on the same value, meaning BusReq is matched 3 times per call site (6 times total for visible + preview).

Fix: Replace with fn bus_req_fields(req: BusReq) -> (uint<2>, uint<16>, uint<8>) that destructures once. Or inline the destructuring at the two call sites. In synthesis, the optimizer likely CSEs this, but the source-level intent is clearer.

### 3. Dead catch-all in step_mcycle (semantics.spade:~2294)
The _ => handle_passthrough(state, input) arm is unreachable since all Phase variants (Fetch, FetchPrefix, ReadImm8, ReadImm16Lo, ReadImm16Hi, ReadMem, WriteMem, Execute, ServiceInterrupt, Halted) are explicitly matched. This masks exhaustiveness checking: if a new Phase variant is added, the compiler won't flag the missing handler.

Fix: Remove the _ arm. Similarly, make the _ => idle_alu_result() in the alu function (alu.spade:~453) explicit as AluReq::Idle => idle_alu_result(). Both catch-alls currently mask the fact that all variants are already handled.

### 4. load_hl_adjust overly verbose (semantics.spade:~1787-1832)
45 lines of deep matching to determine if a load instruction touches HL+/HL-. The function only needs to know if either dst8 or src8 is AddrHli or AddrHld.

Fix: Extract fn operand_is_hli_hld(op: Option<Operand8>) -> bool (3 match arms). Then the main function becomes: if operand_is_hli_hld(dst8) || operand_is_hli_hld(src8) { compute adjustment } else { None }. Reduces 45 lines to ~10.

### 5. execute_stack_delta Pop uses inline delta (semantics.spade:~950-964)
The Pop arm constructs a full CpuDelta with all-None/idle fields and phase: Fetch. This is exactly what fetch_only_delta() produces but spelled out inline.

Fix: Replace with StackOpKind::Pop => fetch_only_delta(). Single line replaces ~14 lines.

## Considerations
- For item 3, verify that Spade requires exhaustive matches or uses catch-alls for this purpose. If Spade already enforces exhaustiveness without _, removing it is purely defensive. If Spade does not enforce exhaustiveness, the _ arm serves as a runtime fallback — in that case, keep it but add a comment.
- For item 1, the pattern of duplicating register-feedback and output expressions is common in the codebase (appears in PPU test tops too). Establishing the let-and-reuse pattern here sets precedent for the PPU cleanup beads.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. commit_seq bound once and reused (no duplicate expression)
2. bus_req_fields (or inline destructuring) replaces triple-match pattern
3. No wildcard catch-all in step_mcycle; alu function uses explicit AluReq::Idle
4. load_hl_adjust uses operand_is_hli_hld helper, <=15 lines total
5. Pop arm uses fetch_only_delta()
6. No behavioral change

## Test Plan
- All CPU swim tests pass
- Blargg cpu_instrs ROM test passes
- Formal invariants pass
- Synthesis area report shows no regression (item 1 may improve it slightly)

title: Architectural memory protocol types (MemTxnTag, PpuMemReq/Resp, VideoBlockReason)
Serial port stub (SB/SC registers)
description:
Implement minimal serial port registers in src/periph/serial.spade.

The serial port is needed for Wave C (§12.4) and is also commonly used by test ROMs for debug output (writing to SB with SC=0x81 sends a byte to the "serial console").

REGISTERS:
- SB (0xFF01): Serial transfer data. Read/write. Holds the byte being transferred.
- SC (0xFF02): Serial transfer control.
  - Bit 7: Transfer start flag (1=start, cleared when transfer complete)
  - Bit 1: Clock speed (CGB only, ignore for DMG)
  - Bit 0: Clock select (0=external clock, 1=internal clock)

MINIMAL IMPLEMENTATION (Wave C scope):
- SB: simple read/write register
- SC: when written with bit 7=1 and bit 0=1 (internal clock):
  - After 8 serial clock cycles, transfer completes
  - SB receives 0xFF (no connected device)
  - SC bit 7 cleared
  - IF bit 3 (Serial) set
- External clock mode: no action (no external device connected)

FOR TEST ROM DEBUG OUTPUT:
- Many test ROMs use serial output to log results
- The harness should capture bytes written to SB when SC=0x81
- bench/pyboy/serial_capture.py: collects serial output into a string for test assertions

TESTING:
- test/unit/test_serial.py:
  1. SB read/write
  2. Internal clock transfer completes after correct cycle count
  3. Transfer sets IF serial bit
  4. Transfer result is 0xFF (no peer)
  5. External clock mode does nothing

WHY SERIAL MATTERS: Without serial, test ROMs that use serial debug output will trigger unexpected interrupts or hang waiting for serial transfer completion. This stub prevents those failure modes.

title: Oracle comparison framework: CommitTrace vs PyBoy differential
description:
Implement the Python comparison logic that bridges DUT CommitTrace output with PyBoy oracle state, producing detailed differential reports on mismatch.

This is the CORE VERIFICATION LOOP that all ROM tests and lockstep tests use.

FILES:
- bench/pyboy/comparator.py: CommitTrace comparison engine
  - compare_commit(dut_trace: CommitTrace, oracle_state: OracleCommit, scope: CompareScope) -> CompareResult
  - CompareResult: match/mismatch, field-by-field diffs, first divergent field
  - CompareScope (from spec/compare_scopes.py): which fields to compare at each granularity

- bench/pyboy/lockstep_driver.py: Lockstep test orchestrator
  - load_rom(rom_path, sym_path, manifest_entry)
  - run_lockstep(oracle, dut_driver, event_script, oracle_mode) -> TestResult
  - The main loop:
    1. Step DUT one M-cycle (or instruction boundary, per oracle_mode)
    2. Step oracle to matching boundary
    3. Extract DUT CommitTrace from simulation ports
    4. Extract oracle state via Oracle.step_commit()
    5. Compare using comparator with appropriate scope
    6. On mismatch: emit divergence artifacts (bd-2wl) and stop
    7. On match: log progress and continue
  - Support for checkpoint symbols: if __checkpoint_N reached, do extended comparison

- bench/pyboy/trace_formatter.py: Human-readable trace output
  - Format CommitTrace as: "M#1234 PC=0x0150 OP=0x3E(LD A,n) A=0x42 F=0x80 BC=0x0000 ..."
  - On mismatch, show side-by-side:
    "DUT:    A=0x42 F=0x80 [Z=1 N=0 H=0 C=0]"
    "Oracle: A=0x42 F=0xB0 [Z=1 N=0 H=1 C=1]"
    "DIFF:            ^^^^       ^^^   ^^^"
  - Color-coded output for terminal

RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER BEADS:
- Uses: bd-f10 (oracle interface), spec/compare_scopes.py (bd-3hr)
- Used by: bd-3gv (lockstep test), bd-27r (interrupt injection), ALL "Green X ROM" beads
- Divergence artifacts (bd-2wl) are invoked by this framework on mismatch

WHY THIS IS ITS OWN BEAD: The oracle interface (bd-f10) defines HOW to talk to PyBoy. This bead defines HOW TO COMPARE DUT output against oracle output. The comparison logic (scope selection, field-by-field diff, trace formatting) is substantial and reusable.

title: Replay capsule format definition
description:
Define the replay capsule format (§16.7) in bench/pyboy/replay.py.

A replay capsule is the minimal information needed to reproduce any test execution:
- ROM id (from manifest)
- ModelProfile
- ResetProfile
- MemoryBehaviorProfile
- Random seed (for generated action sequences)
- Injected event log (list of (commit_index, SimEvent) pairs)
- Symbol/hook manifest identity (hash of .sym file + hook config)
- First-divergence metadata (commit index, field, expected vs actual)

The capsule provides reproducibility WITHOUT file-based golden traces (thesis #7). Any engineer can re-derive the full execution from the capsule by:
1. Loading the ROM
2. Configuring the profiles
3. Replaying the event log with the same seed
4. Running to the divergence point

Capsules should be serializable to JSON for human readability and YAML for manifest integration.

title: Local Python semantic reference model (bench/ref/ppu_ref.py)
description:
Create the local executable semantic reference for PPU dot-step verification.

This is Layer 1 of the oracle architecture (history/ppu.md section 14.2).
It is NOT a second emulator. It is the executable form of the semantic contract,
implementing the same step_dot-style semantics as the Spade HDL but in Python
for short targeted scripts.

INITIAL SCOPE (grows with each wave):

Wave A coverage:
- Mode sequencing (OamScan -> Transfer -> HBlank -> VBlank)
- LY increment, LYC comparison
- STAT line evaluation and rising-edge IRQ generation
- LCD on/off transitions, WarmupBlankFrame

Wave B coverage (added later):
- BG tile fetch and scroll
- Window trigger and counter

Wave C coverage (added later):
- Object selection, fetch, priority
- Full mixer

INTERFACE:
- step_dot(state, input) -> output (mirrors Spade step_dot)
- Uses the same type names as the Spade types for easy cross-reference
- Carries EvidenceTag on each nontrivial rule
- Uses ExpectedSemantics<T> vocabulary (Exact/OneOf/MaskedBits/DontCare/Hypothesis)

The reference model should be usable in three modes:
1. Direct differential testing against DUT dot-by-dot
2. Line-summary generation for scanline-semantic comparison
3. Standalone execution for debugging semantic questions

Every rule should carry design-note provenance (EvidenceTag) so the confidence
level is visible in test output and can gate compare strictness.

title: Waveform capture and debug visualization configuration
description:
Configure VCD/FST waveform capture for all Cocotb tests and create helper scripts for debugging failed tests.

PURPOSE: When a test fails, the developer needs to inspect signal-level behavior. Waveform files (VCD or FST) are the primary debugging tool for HDL. This bead ensures every test CAN produce waveforms and provides tools to make them useful.

CONFIGURATION:
- test/harness/waveform_config.py: Waveform capture control
  - By default: waveform capture OFF (tests run faster)
  - --waves flag enables capture for specific tests
  - On test FAILURE: automatically save waveform for the failed test
  - Configurable signal groups: cpu_core internals, bus activity, timer, interrupts
  - Waveform output: bench/artifacts/waves/<test_name>.vcd

HELPER SCRIPTS:
- tools/view_waves.sh <test_name>: Opens waveform in GTKWave with pre-configured signal layout
- tools/gtkwave_layouts/: GTKWave save files (.gtkw) with organized signal groups:
  - cpu_overview.gtkw: PC, SP, registers, flags, phase, m_ce
  - bus_activity.gtkw: bus_req, bus_resp, address, data, bus_owner
  - interrupt_flow.gtkw: IE, IF, IME, irq_pending, irq_ack, halt_state
  - timer_detail.gtkw: sys_counter, DIV, TIMA, TMA, TAC, timer_irq
  - power_signals.gtkw: write-enables, alu_req (idle vs active), bus_req (idle vs active)

DIVERGENCE WINDOW CAPTURE:
- When oracle comparison detects a mismatch (via bd-2wl divergence artifacts):
  - Save a LOCALIZED waveform: 50 M-cycles before and 10 M-cycles after the divergence point
  - Include all CPU-internal signals in the localized capture
  - Print the waveform file path in the test failure output

TESTING:
- Verify VCD file is produced when --waves is enabled
- Verify VCD file is NOT produced when --waves is disabled (performance)
- Verify localized divergence waveform captures the correct time window

title: Green ALU16_SP custom ROM
description:
Write the ALU16_SP custom test ROM and green it.

Test coverage:
- ADD HL,BC/DE/HL/SP with half-carry and carry edge cases
- INC/DEC rr (no flag changes)
- ADD SP,e8 with positive and negative offsets, verifying low-byte flag behavior
- LD HL,SP+e8 with same cases

Critical edge cases:
- ADD SP,1 with SP=0x00FF (half-carry from bit 3, carry from bit 7 of LOW byte)
- ADD SP,-1 with SP=0x0000
- ADD HL,rr where bit 11 carry occurs
- INC/DEC rr across 0x0000/0xFFFF boundary (no flags affected)

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: Green EI_DELAY custom ROM
description:
Write and green the EI_DELAY test ROM.

Test coverage:
- EI followed by NOP: interrupts enabled after NOP, not during
- EI followed by HALT: interrupts enabled, then halt waits
- EI followed by DI: DI takes effect, canceling the pending enable
- Multiple EI instructions: still only delayed by one instruction
- EI followed by an instruction that should complete before interrupt service

Verification approach:
- Set up timer to fire interrupt at known time
- Execute EI at precise timing relative to pending interrupt
- Verify interrupt is NOT serviced during the instruction immediately after EI
- Verify interrupt IS serviced starting from the second instruction after EI

This tests the ImeState::PendingEnable → Enabled transition timing exactly.

Oracle mode: mcycle_commit (timing must be exact)

title: BG-only mixer with late palette application (src/ppu/rtl/mixer.spade)
description:
Implement the initial BG-only mixer. Object mixing added in Wave C.

The mixer is a PURE FUNCTION that takes one BG pixel and produces a ScanoutEvent.

BG-ONLY VERSION:
- Pop one pixel from BG FIFO
- Apply BGP palette: use bgp_pop from PpuRenderInputs (sampled at pop boundary)
- Produce PixelEmit { x, y, shade: palette_lookup(color_idx, bgp), source: Bg }

LATE PALETTE APPLICATION:
Pan Docs separates FIFO operation from palette application. The FIFO stores raw
2-bit color indices. Palette is applied at pop/mix time using the current palette
register value. This means mid-scanline palette changes affect pixels that were
already in the FIFO. BGP/OBP0/OBP1 are sampled from PpuRenderInputs at pop time.

WAVE C EXTENSION (separate bead):
The full mixer handles one BG pixel AND one OBJ pixel, applying:
- Object transparency (color 0)
- BG enable / window behavior (LCDC.0)
- BG-over-OBJ semantics (sprite attribute bit 7)
- Object palette selection (OBP0/OBP1 based on sprite attribute bit 4)

BG ENABLE (LCDC.0): When cleared on DMG, background and window are blank (all white).
Objects still render normally. This is a mixer-level concern, not a fetcher concern.

title: Bus: remove dead/identity/redundant functions
description:
Remove or document the dead and identity functions in membus.spade.

## Scope
1. canonical_req (membus.spade:~37-43): Identity function. Check git blame for intent. If no synthesis rationale found, remove. If intentional, add comment.
2. default_bus_obs (types.spade:~49-51): Zero call sites. Remove.
3. blocked_read_data / not_usable_read_data (membus.spade:~132-144): Both return 0xff unconditionally. Collapse to one function OR add TODO comments for future profile divergence.
4. cpu_write_allowed (membus.spade:~146-155): Ignores memory_behavior_profile. Simplify or document.
5. Four trivial region_is_* functions (~68-94): Inline at single call sites if readability permits.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Dead functions removed
- Identity functions removed or documented with synthesis rationale
- Identical functions collapsed or documented with divergence intent
- No behavioral change

title: Joypad register stub (P1 / 0xFF00)
description:
Implement minimal joypad register (P1 / 0xFF00) in src/periph/joypad.spade.

The joypad register is the only unmapped IO register that real ROMs routinely read during initialization. Without it, ROMs that poll P1 may misinterpret the bus-default 0xFF as a button state and branch into interactive menus or stall. cpu_instrs.gb reads P1 during startup.

REGISTER (0xFF00 — P1/JOYP):
- Bits 5-4: select which button group to read (active-low, written by CPU):
  - Bit 5 = 0: select action buttons (A, B, Select, Start)
  - Bit 4 = 0: select direction pad (Right, Left, Up, Down)
  - Both 0: both groups active (ORed together via active-low)
  - Both 1: neither group active (returns 0x0F in low nibble = no buttons)
- Bits 3-0: button state for selected group (active-low, 0 = pressed, read-only):
  - When bit 4 selected: bit3=Down, bit2=Up, bit1=Left, bit0=Right
  - When bit 5 selected: bit3=Start, bit2=Select, bit1=B, bit0=A
- Bits 7-6: unused, read as 1 on DMG

READ BEHAVIOR:
  let raw = match (select_action, select_dpad) {
    (false, false) => 0x0F,  // neither selected -> no buttons
    (true,  false) => action_nibble,
    (false, true)  => dpad_nibble,
    (true,  true)  => action_nibble & dpad_nibble, // OR via active-low
  };
  return 0xC0 | (select_bits << 4) | raw;

Where action_nibble and dpad_nibble come from the JoypButtons input (already defined in src/sim/types.spade), inverted to active-low (0 = pressed).

WRITE BEHAVIOR:
- Only bits 5-4 are writable (group select). Writes to bits 3-0 are ignored.

INTERRUPT:
- Joypad IRQ (IF bit 4) fires on a high-to-low transition on any of bits 3-0 (button press while corresponding group is selected). Used by games to wake from HALT/STOP.
- The interrupt controller already accepts joypad_irq (src/periph/interrupts.spade:60) and the CPU vectors to 0x0060 (src/cpu/semantics.spade:136).

INTERFACE (matching serial.spade pattern):
  pub entity joypad(
      clk: clock,
      rst: bool,
      write_en: bool,
      write_addr: uint<16>,
      write_data: uint<8>,
      buttons: JoypButtons,
  ) -> JoypadOut

  pub struct JoypadOut {
      p1: uint<8>,
      joypad_irq: bool,
  }

INTEGRATION:
- Add joypad module to src/periph/main.spade
- Wire into bus fabric IO decode (0xFF00 reads return joypad.p1, writes route to joypad entity)
- Wire joypad_irq output into interrupt controller (already has the input port)
- Wire SimStimulus.joyp_buttons into joypad entity buttons input

TESTING (test/unit/test_joypad.py):
1. Default read with no buttons pressed -> 0xCF (select=0b11, buttons=0x0F)
2. Select action group (write 0x20), read with A pressed -> bit 0 low
3. Select dpad group (write 0x10), read with Up pressed -> bit 2 low
4. Both groups selected (write 0x30), read with A+Up -> both bits low
5. Neither group selected (write 0x00), read -> 0x0F regardless of buttons
6. Write to low nibble ignored -- read back reflects buttons, not written data
7. Joypad IRQ fires on high-to-low transition of P1 bit (button press while group selected)
8. No IRQ on button release (low-to-high transition)
9. No IRQ when button pressed but group not selected

ACCEPTANCE: All 9 test cases green. cpu_instrs.gb no longer stalls on P1 read during init. Joypad IRQ wired through interrupt controller and observable in simulation.

title: EI+HALT and interrupt edge case interaction tests
description:
Create test/lockstep/test_ei_halt_corners.py — targeted tests for the most subtle interrupt/HALT interactions.

These are the cases that break almost every Game Boy emulator at least once. The plan (§16.5) explicitly calls out "halt-after-ei corner cases" and "deterministic interrupt injection around EI, HALT, RETI, and pending-IF cases."

TEST CASES:

1. EI → HALT (the canonical pattern):
   - EI sets IME=PendingEnable
   - HALT is the next instruction
   - Question: does HALT execute with IME=PendingEnable or IME=Enabled?
   - Answer: HALT executes with PendingEnable. The PendingEnable→Enabled transition happens AFTER HALT starts. So HALT enters halted state, THEN IME becomes Enabled, THEN interrupt wakes and services.
   - Verify: interrupt IS serviced (not just a wake without service)

2. EI → NOP → HALT:
   - EI sets PendingEnable
   - NOP executes: PendingEnable → Enabled
   - HALT with IME=Enabled: behaves normally
   - Verify timing matches case 1

3. DI → HALT with pending interrupt:
   - DI sets IME=Disabled
   - IF bit already set, IE matches
   - HALT wakes immediately (because IE & IF != 0) but does NOT service interrupt
   - CPU resumes at PC after HALT, still with IME=Disabled
   - Verify: no interrupt service occurs

4. EI → interrupt fires before HALT:
   - EI sets PendingEnable
   - An interrupt becomes pending during the next instruction fetch
   - The EI delay means PendingEnable→Enabled happens after the next instruction STARTS
   - If the next instruction is interruptible (single-M-cycle), does the interrupt fire?
   - Answer: No — IME transitions at the END of the next instruction, then the interrupt check happens on the FOLLOWING fetch.

5. RETI → immediate interrupt:
   - RETI sets IME=Enabled immediately (unlike EI)
   - If an interrupt is already pending, it should fire on the NEXT instruction fetch
   - Verify: interrupt fires immediately after RETI returns

6. Nested interrupts:
   - Interrupt handler uses EI+HALT to wait for next interrupt
   - Verify: nested interrupt service works correctly

7. IF bit set during interrupt service:
   - While servicing interrupt A, interrupt B becomes pending
   - Verify: interrupt B is serviced after RETI from handler A

LOGGING: Each test logs cycle-by-cycle IME state and interrupt activity:
  [M#100] EI executed → IME=PendingEnable
  [M#101] HALT entered → HaltState=Halted, IME still PendingEnable
  [M#102] IME transition → IME=Enabled (HALT still active)
  [M#105] IF[2] set (timer) → IE&IF=0b00100 → wake!
  [M#106] ServiceInterrupt begins → IME=Disabled, push PC...

All stimuli injected via deterministic perturbation engine (bd-35l) keyed by commit index.

title: Verilator backend configuration for long regressions
description:
Configure Verilator as an alternative Cocotb simulator backend for long-running regression suites (§16.4).

RATIONALE: Icarus Verilog is the default simulator for development because it compiles fast and has broad SystemVerilog support. However, for nightly exhaustive ALU sweeps, full Wave A-D ROM regressions, and M-cycle lockstep over thousands of instructions, Icarus simulation speed becomes the bottleneck. Verilator compiles Verilog to optimized C++ and provides 10-100x simulation speedup for long runs.

The plan (§16.4) explicitly states: 'If more speed is required for long regressions, use Verilator as the Cocotb simulator backend. Do not make a separate C++/Verilator authored-test lane part of the core bring-up plan.' This means the SAME Python/Cocotb tests run on both backends--no separate C++ test code.

IMPLEMENTATION:
- Pin Verilator version in toolchain/versions.md alongside Icarus
- Configure cocotb Makefile/runner to accept SIM=icarus (default) or SIM=verilator
- Verify all Cocotb test fixtures work with both backends
- Add tools/run_tests.py --sim=verilator option
- Document any Verilator-specific limitations (signal visibility, VPI differences)

PERFORMANCE TARGETS:
- Nightly exhaustive ALU sweeps (bd-3ud): < 10 minutes with Verilator vs potentially hours with Icarus
- Full Wave A ROM regression: < 5 minutes with Verilator

WHY NOT VERILATOR BY DEFAULT: Verilator's compilation step is slow for small tests and its VPI support has edge cases. Icarus gives faster edit-compile-test cycles for development. Verilator is for regressions, not iteration.

ACCEPTANCE: All existing Cocotb tests pass with SIM=verilator. Nightly suite completes within target time.

title: OAM DMA controller
description:
Implement OAM DMA controller in src/periph/ or src/bus/.

DMA behavior:
- Writing to 0xFF46 starts DMA: copies 160 bytes from source (written_value * 0x100) to OAM (0xFE00-0xFE9F)
- Transfer takes 160 M-cycles
- During DMA, CPU can only access HRAM (0xFF80-0xFFFE) and IO registers
- All other memory reads return 0xFF (or bus conflict value per MemoryBehaviorProfile)

Implementation:
- DMA state machine entity with source address counter
- BusOwner::OamDma takes bus ownership during transfer
- Bus fabric routes DMA reads to source, DMA writes to OAM
- CPU reads during DMA are restricted (handled in bus fabric, NOT in CPU)

The CPU doesn't need to know about DMA restrictions — the bus fabric handles it (§12.2.2).

title: Cocotb test base infrastructure and shared utilities
description:
Implement test/harness/ shared Cocotb infrastructure used by ALL dynamic verification tests.

CORE COMPONENTS:

1. test/harness/dut_driver.py: DUT interaction layer
   - CpuTestDriver: wraps cpu_test_top Cocotb DUT
     - reset(reset_profile): apply SkipBoot or RawPowerOn reset sequence
     - step_mcycle(): advance DUT by one M-cycle, return CommitTrace
     - step_instruction(): advance DUT until next instruction boundary
     - read_signal(name): read named signal from DUT
     - inject_stimulus(stim: SimStimulus): apply simulation sideband
   - SoCLockstepDriver: wraps soc_lockstep_top for full-SoC tests

2. test/harness/fixtures.py: Shared pytest/Cocotb fixtures
   - cpu_dut: provides initialized CpuTestDriver
   - oracle: provides initialized PyBoy Oracle
   - lockstep_pair: provides paired (dut, oracle) for differential testing
   - rom_loader(manifest_entry): loads ROM into DUT memory and oracle
   - event_script(seed, manifest_entry): generates deterministic event sequence

3. test/harness/assertions.py: Comparison utilities
   - assert_registers_match(dut_state, oracle_state, scope)
   - assert_commit_trace_match(dut_trace, oracle_commit, scope)
   - format_register_diff(expected, actual): human-readable diff

4. test/harness/logging_std.py: Test logging standard (per bd-dny)
   - TestLogger class with hierarchical output
   - Automatic failure context collection

DESIGN PRINCIPLES:
- All DUT interaction goes through the driver layer (never raw signal access in tests)
- Fixtures handle setup/teardown lifecycle
- Assertions produce actionable diagnostics on failure
- The same infrastructure supports unit tests, ROM tests, and lockstep tests

ACCEPTANCE: At least one test (bd-1jm Spade-Cocotb smoke) uses the infrastructure. All fixtures documented. Driver API stable enough for Phase 3+ tests.

title: MBC3 mapper (ROM/RAM bank switching + RTC)
description:
Implement MBC3 memory bank controller.

MBC3 registers:
- 0x0000-0x1FFF: RAM/RTC Enable
- 0x2000-0x3FFF: ROM Bank Number (7 bits, 0 maps to 1)
- 0x4000-0x5FFF: RAM Bank / RTC Register Select (0x00-0x03: RAM, 0x08-0x0C: RTC)
- 0x6000-0x7FFF: Latch Clock Data (write 0x00 then 0x01 to latch)

RTC registers (optional, can stub initially):
- 0x08: Seconds, 0x09: Minutes, 0x0A: Hours, 0x0B: Day low, 0x0C: Day high + flags

Address mapping:
- 0x0000-0x3FFF: ROM bank 0 (always)
- 0x4000-0x7FFF: ROM bank N (7-bit, 1-127)
- 0xA000-0xBFFF: RAM bank or RTC register

For initial bring-up, RTC can return fixed values. Focus on ROM/RAM banking.

title: CPU match pyramid dedup: Inc/Dec word ALU, direct_load
description:
Deeply nested match pyramids in CPU semantics duplicate logic that can be factored into predicates and shared helpers, removing ~100 lines and making control flow legible.

## Background

The Game Boy CPU has several instruction classes that share structural patterns: Inc/Dec on 16-bit register pairs, LD SP,HL, conditional jumps/calls. In each case, the semantics code handles related variants with near-identical match pyramids that differ in one expression. This happened naturally during instruction-by-instruction implementation but now the patterns are clear and ripe for factoring.

## Scope

### 1. Inc/Dec word ALU handlers (semantics.spade:~559-603)
The Inc arm and Dec arm in execute_word_alu_delta are structurally identical: both match on SP-vs-other, construct a CpuDelta with the new 16-bit value, and handle the SP special case (sp_write vs reg pair write). They differ only in + 1u16 vs - 1u16.

Fix: Extract fn word_inc_dec_delta(state: CpuState, pair: RegPair, next_value: uint<16>) -> CpuDelta. Call with get_r16(state.arch.regs, pair) + 1 or - 1 respectively. The SP-vs-other match moves into the helper.

### 2. direct_load match pyramids (semantics.spade:~1037-1139)
direct_load_sp_write (~50 lines) and direct_load_phase (~50 lines) are near-identical match pyramids checking for LD SP, HL. They destructure the same fields (addressing=Register16, dst16=SP, src16=HL) and only differ in return type (Option<uint<16>> vs Option<Phase>).

Fix: Extract fn is_ld_sp_hl(op: DecodedOp) -> bool that performs the nested match once. Both functions become:
  if is_ld_sp_hl(op) { Some(value) } else { None }
This eliminates ~80 lines of duplicated 5-level nesting.

### 3. Condition-gated execute phase (semantics.spade:~1653-1707)
The JumpAbs and CallTarget arms in handle_read_imm16_hi are nearly identical: both decode the instruction's condition, check condition_matches against flags, and either proceed to Execute or fall back to Fetch. The only difference is a comment about intent.

Fix: Extract fn condition_gated_phase(state: CpuState, op: DecodedOp) -> Phase that returns Execute if condition matches, Fetch otherwise. Both arms call this and construct their delta around the result.

## Considerations
- is_ld_sp_hl is a pattern that may be useful in other contexts (e.g., formal verification assertions). Making it a standalone predicate has value beyond dedup.
- For word_inc_dec_delta, the SP special case is architecturally significant (SP writes go through a different path than register pair writes). The helper should make this distinction clear in its implementation, not hide it.
- condition_gated_phase should NOT be confused with condition_matches (which is purely a flag check). The new helper combines flag checking with phase selection.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. word_inc_dec_delta (or similar) handles both Inc and Dec
2. is_ld_sp_hl predicate exists; direct_load_sp_write and direct_load_phase use it
3. Condition-gated phase helper used in JumpAbs and CallTarget arms
4. Match nesting in affected functions reduced by at least 2 levels
5. No behavioral change

## Test Plan
- semantics_wordalu_test_top passes (Inc/Dec on all register pairs including SP)
- semantics_load_test_top passes (LD SP, HL and all direct loads)
- semantics_flow_test_top passes (conditional jumps, calls, returns)
- Blargg cpu_instrs ROM test passes
- Formal invariants pass

title: STAT/LYC/IRQ unit tests (test/ppu/unit/test_stat_irq.py)
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for STAT line evaluation, LYC comparison, and IRQ generation.

TEST CASES:

1. LYC COMPARISON FLAG:
   Set LYC to various values. Advance PPU. Verify STAT bit 2 (LYC==LY flag)
   is set on the correct line and clear on others.

2. STAT MODE 0 INTERRUPT:
   Enable mode 0 select (STAT bit 3). Verify STAT interrupt fires on each
   HBlank entry. Verify it fires ONCE per HBlank (rising-edge, not level).

3. STAT MODE 1 INTERRUPT:
   Enable mode 1 select (STAT bit 4). Verify STAT interrupt fires on VBlank entry.

4. STAT MODE 2 INTERRUPT:
   Enable mode 2 select (STAT bit 5). Verify STAT interrupt fires on each
   OAM scan entry.

5. STAT LYC INTERRUPT:
   Enable LYC select (STAT bit 6). Set LYC=50. Verify STAT interrupt fires
   when LY reaches 50.

6. STAT BLOCKING:
   Enable mode 2 + mode 0 select. Verify that when mode 2 fires immediately
   after mode 0 on the SAME line, the STAT line stays high and only ONE
   interrupt fires (the mode 2 entry). This tests the rising-edge behavior.

7. STAT MULTI-SOURCE:
   Enable mode 2 + LYC select with LYC=0. Verify correct interrupt behavior
   when both conditions are true on line 0.

8. VBLANK INTERRUPT:
   Verify IF bit 0 (VBlank) is set on line 144 entry.
   Verify STAT mode 1 interrupt is separate from VBlank interrupt.

9. STAT READBACK:
   Read STAT register. Verify bits 0-1 match current mode.
   Verify bit 2 matches LYC==LY. Verify bits 3-6 match written select bits.
   Verify bit 7 always reads 1 (unused, set).

10. STAT READBACK WHEN LCD OFF:
    Disable LCD. Read STAT. Verify mode bits report 0.

LOGGING: Each test case logs the STAT line state, IRQ output, and PpuIrqReq
at each relevant dot. Failed comparisons include full STAT register dump.

title: Phase machine for stack and control flow (PUSH, POP, JP, JR, CALL, RET, RST)
description:
Implement phase machine for stack operations and control flow.

Stack operations:
- PUSH rr: 4 M-cycles (fetch + internal + write high + write low). SP decremented first.
- POP rr: 3 M-cycles (fetch + read low + read high). SP incremented after each read.
  POP AF: low nibble of F is masked to zero (§9.3).

Unconditional jumps:
- JP nn: 4 M-cycles (fetch + read low + read high + internal)
- JP (HL): 1 M-cycle (fetch only, PC ← HL)
- JR e8: 3 M-cycles (fetch + read offset + internal branch)

Conditional jumps (cc = NZ, Z, NC, C):
- JP cc,nn: 3 M-cycles if not taken, 4 if taken
- JR cc,e8: 2 M-cycles if not taken, 3 if taken

Subroutine calls:
- CALL nn: 6 M-cycles (fetch + read low + read high + internal + push high PC + push low PC)
- CALL cc,nn: 3 if not taken, 6 if taken
- RST vec: 4 M-cycles (fetch + internal + push high + push low). Target = vec*8.

Returns:
- RET: 4 M-cycles (fetch + pop low + pop high + internal)
- RET cc: 2 if not taken, 5 if taken (extra internal cycle for condition check)
- RETI: like RET but also sets IME=Enabled

METAMORPHIC TEST (§16.5): Branch not taken MUST use the short cycle tuple.

title: Spade-to-Cocotb integration smoke test
description:
Create test/harness/test_spade_cocotb_integration.py — validates the entire toolchain pipeline end-to-end.

This is a 'sanity-green meta test' (§16.1) that proves the toolchain works, not that any CPU logic is correct.

PIPELINE UNDER TEST:
  Spade source (.spade) → swim compile → Verilog (.v) → simulator (iverilog/verilator) → Cocotb testbench

TEST STEPS:
1. Use the existing main.spade (or a trivial test module) as the Spade source
2. Compile it with swim to produce Verilog
3. Load the Verilog into the simulator
4. Drive the module from a Cocotb testbench:
   - Assert reset
   - Release reset
   - Clock for N cycles
   - Read output signals and verify they match expected behavior
5. Verify waveform dump (VCD) is produced and non-empty

DETAILED LOGGING:
- Log each pipeline step with timestamps and exit codes
- On failure, print the exact command that failed, its stderr, and the state of intermediate files
- On success, print summary: 'Spade compile OK (0.3s) → Verilog OK (42 lines) → Simulator OK → Cocotb 1/1 pass'

WHY THIS IS ITS OWN BEAD:
T03 (Cocotb pin) mentions 'verify Cocotb can drive a trivial Spade-generated Verilog testbench' in passing. But this validation is critical infrastructure — if this pipeline doesn't work, NO Cocotb test can run. It deserves focused attention, explicit pass criteria, and a reusable test that can catch regressions if swim/cocotb/simulator versions change.

title: Sprite rendering
description:
Implement sprite (OBJ) rendering.

OAM scan (Mode 2):
- Scan all 40 OAM entries
- Select up to 10 sprites visible on current scanline
- Sprite visible if: Y-16 <= LY < Y-16+height (height = 8 or 16 per LCDC bit 2)

Sprite attributes (4 bytes per entry):
- Byte 0: Y position (on-screen Y = stored_Y - 16)
- Byte 1: X position (on-screen X = stored_X - 8)
- Byte 2: Tile index (for 8x16 mode, bit 0 ignored)
- Byte 3: Flags
  - Bit 7: BG priority (0=sprite above BG, 1=sprite behind non-zero BG)
  - Bit 6: Y flip
  - Bit 5: X flip
  - Bit 4: Palette (0=OBP0, 1=OBP1)

Rendering priority:
- Sprites with lower X position have higher priority
- If same X, lower OAM index has higher priority
- Color 0 in sprite is always transparent

8x16 mode: tile index bit 0 forced to 0, top tile = index, bottom tile = index+1.
close_reason: Superseded by granular beads under Epic bd-31mf (Object Wave C)

title: Constrained synth_ice40 + nextpnr baseline
description:
Run initial synthesis and place-and-route to establish baselines (§18.7 tier 3).

Steps:
1. Run synth_ice40 with explicit clock constraints
2. Run nextpnr-ice40 with the icebreaker.pcf pin file
3. Record baseline metrics:
   - LUT count
   - DFF count
   - SPRAM usage (4 blocks available on UP5K)
   - EBR usage (30 blocks available on UP5K)
   - Estimated f_max under clock constraint
   - Clock-enable fanout
4. Verify no debug-path contamination: hardware build should NOT include CommitTrace/DebugTrace fanout

The hardware synthesis top (src/board/icebreaker_top.spade) must be clean of simulation-only signals (§18.6).

Record results in a trackable format (JSON or markdown table) for regression comparison.

title: Video/sim cleanup: frame_sink dedup, trace builder, dead signals
description:
Cleanup across video and sim modules: duplicated mismatch logic, trace builder boilerplate, dead signals, tautological conditions, and commit_seq double-computation.

## Background

The video module (frame_sink, access, backend_adapter) and sim module (test tops, ppu_support, trace/state observers) are the output and verification layers of iceboy. frame_sink processes PPU scanout into line/frame hashes for differential testing. The sim test tops expose internal state for cocotb verification. Both have accumulated duplicated patterns.

This bead depends on shared utilities (bd-21p5) for shared encoder functions used in sim test tops.

## Scope

### 1. pixel_mismatch duplicated (frame_sink.spade)
advance_line_sink (~lines 266-269) and advance_diff_sink (~lines 361-363) compute identical mismatch logic:
  !reference.valid || reference.shade != emit.shade || source_bits(reference.source) != source_bits(emit.source)

Fix: Extract fn pixel_mismatch(reference: PixelReference, emit: PixelEmit) -> bool. Use in both sites.

### 2. PpuDebugTrace builder dedup (ppu_support.spade:~153-207)
ppu_debug_trace and idle_trace_for_state share 16 of 19 fields (at, ly_after, dot_in_line_after, run_after, mode_after, phase_after, stat_line_after, first_frame_blank_after, stat_readback_after, window_state, window_line, oam_scan, line_objs, fetcher, bg_fifo, obj_fifo). They differ in mem_reqs, mmio_resp, irq_req, scanout, semantic, line_summary.

Fix: Extract fn base_debug_trace(state: PpuStepState, ...) -> PpuDebugTrace that fills the common 16 fields, then have each function override the differing 3-6 fields. (If Spade lacks struct update syntax, the helper returns the struct and the caller replaces fields via a new constructor — still better than full duplication.)

### 3. Dead signal cpu_ppu_if_bits (soc_rom_top.spade:~270)
ppu_irq_if_bits (lines ~170-174) produces a uint<5>. It's bound to cpu_ppu_if_bits on line ~270 but never read. Dead combinational logic.

Fix: Remove ppu_irq_if_bits function and the dead binding. If the function exists for future use, move it to a comment or deferred code block.

### 4. _ppu_vram_busy / _ppu_oam_busy unused (soc_rom_top.spade:~264-265)
Computed but prefixed with _ (intentionally unused). Pure combinational, so no register cost, but still synthesizes to logic.

Fix: Remove both. They can be trivially recomputed when needed. Add a comment noting they were removed for synthesis area reasons.

### 5. Tautological condition (access.spade:~57)
region_is_vram(r) || region_is_oam(r) where MemRegion has only Vram and Oam variants. This OR is always true.

Fix: Remove the condition. The Mode3 blocking check should unconditionally return CpuBlockedByMode3 in that context.

### 6. Dead bindings in video/sim
- frame_sink.spade:~244: let _ = reason; in Blank arm → use wildcard in pattern
- frame_sink_test_top.spade:~80,104,109,123: let _ = initial_*_state() calls → compute and discard. Remove if not needed for compiler.
- frame_sink_test_top.spade:~14: unused import blank_reason_bits → remove
- icebreaker_top.spade:~59: let _ = bus_obs; → document why bus observation is discarded
- semantic_observe_top.spade:~51: let _ = video_now; → remove unused port or document interface stability
- soc_rom_top.spade:~229: let _keep_interface_stable = profiles; → already well-named

### 7. commit_seq computed twice in 3 test tops
trace_observe_top.spade:~33/36, ppu_state_top.spade:~43/46, semantic_observe_top.spade:~53/56. Same expression for register feedback and output. ppu_test_top already does it right with let visible_seq.

Fix: Bind once with let, reuse. Follow the ppu_test_top pattern.

### 8. advance_line_sink struct-spread (frame_sink.spade:~246-289)
Blank and Pixel arms rebuild entire LineSinkState (13 fields) to change one. Same pattern as other modules.

Fix: If Spade supports struct update, use it. Otherwise, create per-field helpers or accept as language limitation with a comment.

## Considerations
- Item 5 (tautological condition) is a correctness invariant: if MemRegion ever gains a third variant, the condition would become meaningful. Removing it means the compiler will flag the incomplete match — which is actually BETTER for catching future changes. Add a brief comment noting why it was removed.
- Item 3 and 4 (dead signals) are low-risk, high-clarity changes. They also reduce synthesis area.
- Item 2 (trace builder) is high-value for maintainability: PpuDebugTrace gains new fields as the PPU evolves, and having 2 constructors to update instead of the base+overrides is error-prone.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. pixel_mismatch helper extracted and used in both call sites
2. PpuDebugTrace common builder extracted
3. Dead signals removed (cpu_ppu_if_bits, _ppu_vram_busy, _ppu_oam_busy)
4. Tautological OR removed with explanatory comment
5. Dead bindings and imports cleaned up
6. commit_seq bound-once in 3 test tops
7. No behavioral change

## Test Plan
- swim test for frame_sink_test_top, access_test_top, backend_adapter_test_top
- soc_rom_top passes
- trace_observe_top, ppu_state_top, semantic_observe_top tests pass
- Synth gate passes (expect small improvement from dead signal removal)

title: Opcode family coverage tracking infrastructure
description:
Build infrastructure to automatically track which opcode families, phase/continuation constructors, bus regions, interrupt causes, ROM suites, and reset/model profiles are covered by the test suite (§16.3).

RATIONALE: The plan requires tracking coverage across six dimensions. Without automated tracking, coverage gaps are invisible until they cause bugs in production. The canonical opcode metadata source (bd-36u) provides the ground truth for what SHOULD be covered; this task builds the system that measures what IS covered.

COVERAGE DIMENSIONS:
1. Opcode families: group by mnemonic (LD, ADD, SUB, etc.) -- which families have >=1 test?
2. Phase/continuation constructors: which Phase enum variants and ReadCont/WriteCont variants are exercised?
3. Bus regions: which BusRegion values are exercised (Rom, Wram, Hram, Io, Vram, Oam, etc.)?
4. Interrupt causes: which interrupt sources (timer, serial, joypad, VBlank, STAT) are exercised?
5. ROM suites: which custom ROMs from the manifest have passing tests?
6. Reset/model profiles: which (ModelProfile, ResetProfile, MemoryBehaviorProfile) combos are tested?

IMPLEMENTATION:
- test/harness/coverage_tracker.py:
  - Collects coverage events during test execution (e.g., "opcode 0x80 executed", "BusRegion::Wram accessed")
  - Persists to bench/artifacts/coverage/coverage.json after each test run
  - Generates coverage report showing gaps per dimension

- Integration with test runner (bd-2wk):
  - --coverage flag enables coverage collection
  - Summary shows coverage percentages per dimension
  - CI can fail if coverage drops below threshold

- Integration with opcode metadata (bd-36u):
  - cross-references exercised opcodes against full opcode table
  - reports untested opcodes and untested flag policies

COVERAGE REPORT FORMAT:
  === Opcode Family Coverage ===
  LD: 23/28 variants tested (82%)
  ADD: 8/8 variants tested (100%)
  SUB: 4/8 variants tested (50%)  <-- GAP
  ...
  === Phase Constructor Coverage ===
  Fetch: covered
  ReadImm8: covered
  ReadImm16Lo: covered
  WriteMem: NOT COVERED  <-- GAP
  ...

ACCEPTANCE: Coverage report generated after full test suite run. All dimensions tracked. Coverage gaps clearly identified.

title: test_reset_profile.py scaffold
description:
Create test/harness/test_reset_profile.py (Phase 1 step 9).

This test verifies that the DUT's initial state after reset matches the expected post-boot state for the configured (ModelProfile, ResetProfile) pair.

For DMG + SkipBoot, verify:
- A=0x01, F=0xB0, B=0x00, C=0x13, D=0x00, E=0xD8, H=0x01, L=0x4D
- SP=0xFFFE, PC=0x0100
- IE=0x00, IF=0xE1 (typical)
- IME=disabled
- Timer registers at expected values
- Key IO registers at expected values

The test should:
1. Reset both DUT and oracle with the same profile
2. Compare initial register state
3. Optionally compare key memory locations

This is a 'sanity-green meta test' (§16.1) — it proves the reset infrastructure works, not that the CPU executes correctly.

title: Epic: Spade Code Simplification
description: Systematic simplification of the iceboy Spade HDL codebase to reduce boilerplate, flatten deeply nested matches, deduplicate shared logic, and improve maintainability. Estimated ~500+ lines removed across cpu/semantics.spade, cpu/control.spade, cpu/core.spade, and bus/membus.spade without changing behavior.

title: cpu_test_top simulation top
description:
Create src/sim/cpu_test_top.spade — isolated CPU testing simulation top.

This top provides:
- Instantiates cpu_core with controllable inputs
- Mock/minimal bus: responds to reads from a small memory, accepts writes
- Controllable m_ce input for test-driven stepping
- Controllable irq_pending for interrupt injection
- Exposes CommitTrace outputs as flat Verilog-friendly ports for Cocotb
- SimStimulus input ports for deterministic test stimuli

Port naming: use stable, Verilog-friendly names (§4.4). Cocotb tests will reference these by name.

This is SEPARATE from the hardware synthesis top (§4.4, §18.6). Debug/trace fanout exists here, never in icebreaker_top.

title: test_alu_generated_vectors.py scaffold
description:
Create test/unit/test_alu_generated_vectors.py (§16.2, Phase 1 step 7).

This test generates ALU input/output vectors from a Python reference model (spec/flag_policies.py) and verifies the Spade ALU against them.

Three verification lanes (§11.4):
1. Curated examples: hand-picked edge cases for readability (inline in test)
2. Generated vectors: systematic sweep from gbxcule-derived flag formulas
3. Nightly exhaustive: selected small-domain full sweeps (behind pytest marker)

Initial scaffold should:
1. Define the Python reference ALU (spec/flag_policies.py)
2. Generate vectors for at least: ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, CP, INC, DEC
3. Parameterize tests with generated vectors
4. FAIL until ALU is implemented (red semantic tests)

Tricky flag cases to cover from §11.3:
- ADC/SBC: half-carry computed on nibble+carry, not nibble alone
- INC/DEC: preserves carry flag
- ADD SP,e8: zero/half-carry from low byte, not high byte
- LD HL,SP+e8: same flag behavior as ADD SP,e8
