- DAA: depends on N, H, C flags AND accumulator value
- SCF/CCF: clear N and H, SCF sets carry, CCF inverts carry

title: PPU quality: struct spread, dead code, no-op mux, enum equality
description:
Several quality issues across the PPU RTL and semantic modules: excessive struct-rebuild boilerplate, dead code, a no-op mux, and manual enum equality checks. Fixing these improves maintainability as the PPU modules see frequent changes.

## Background

The PPU modules are the most actively developed part of iceboy. The fetcher, FIFO, mixer, OAM scanner, and timing logic are all evolving as rendering accuracy improves. Every new field added to FetcherState or PpuRegs requires updating 5-10 constructor sites. Dead code creates false impressions about what is exercised. No-op muxes waste synthesis area.

This bead depends on PPU timing dedup (bd-3fcu) completing first, since both touch timing.spade and related files.

## Scope

### 1. FetcherState copy-spread boilerplate (fetcher.spade, ~100 lines)
Functions issue_read, state_with_epoch, clear_pending, state_after_resp, advance_no_resp all rebuild FetcherState by spreading 12 fields and changing 1-3. keep_pending_read (lines ~136-138) just returns state.pending_read — pure unnecessary indirection.

Fix: Create builder helpers: with_step(state, step), with_pending_read(state, valid, read), with_epoch(state, epoch). Each rebuilds the struct but encapsulates the field list. Remove keep_pending_read entirely. This does not fully eliminate the boilerplate (Spade lacks ..rest syntax) but centralizes it.

### 2. apply_mmio_write single-field spreads (regs.spade:~94-102)
8 PpuRegs constructors for Scy/Scx/Lyc/Wy/Wx/Bgp/Obp0/Obp1, each copying all 10 fields and changing one. Parallels existing replace_lcdc and replace_stat_select helpers.

Fix: Create replace_scy(regs, value), replace_scx(regs, value), etc. Then apply_mmio_write becomes:
  MmioTarget::Scy => replace_scy(regs, value),
  MmioTarget::Scx => replace_scx(regs, value),
  ...

### 3. sample_render_inputs no-op match (sample.spade:~33-39)
5-way match where every arm returns visible.regs. Synthesizes to a no-op mux on phase.

Fix: Replace with let fetch_regs = visible.regs;. If phase must be consumed to suppress warnings, use let _ = phase;.

### 4. Dead code in obj_fetch.spade
- incoming_row_pixel (~57-66): Never called. All call sites use incoming_row_pixel_clipped.
- overlay_count (~81-86): Only called from obj_fifo_overlay_row.
- obj_fifo_overlay_row (~160-162): Only used in obj_fetch_test_top as a discarded let _ = ....

Fix: Remove all three functions. The clipped variants fully subsume them.

### 5. same_region / same_client manual equality (fetcher.spade:~51-68)
Both manually enumerate every (Variant, Variant) => true pair. The codebase uses != on enums elsewhere (line ~214).

Fix: If Spade enum equality works for MemRegion and MemClient, replace with left == right (~15 lines removed). Test first by trying == in a small context.

### 6. tile_lo_addr wrapper (fetcher.spade:~101-103)
One-line wrapper that extracts render.lcdc_fetch and forwards to bgwin_tile_addr. Adds no abstraction. (tile_hi_addr at least adds + 1.)

Fix: Inline bgwin_tile_addr(render.lcdc_fetch, tile_id, row) at the 2 call sites. Remove tile_lo_addr.

### 7. zero_mmio_write defined twice (sem/events.spade:~28 and ~61)
Module-level version delegates to mmio::zero_mmio_write(). Remove the wrapper; callers import from mmio:: directly.

### 8. Redundant let _ = ... bindings
Multiple files: timing.spade:153 (let _ = input — unused parameter!), 249, 270, 290; irq.spade:28; step.spade:61. Use _ in destructuring patterns. If input is truly unused in advance_timing, remove the parameter.

## Considerations
- Item 1 (FetcherState builders) is the highest-value change. The fetcher is modified frequently and each modification currently requires touching 5 functions. After this change, only the builder helpers need updating.
- Item 5 (enum equality) needs a Spade language check. If == is not derived for these enum types, keep the manual functions but add a comment explaining the limitation.
- Item 4 (dead code) — verify that incoming_row_pixel is truly dead by grepping all .spade files, not just obj_fetch.spade.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. FetcherState builders reduce spread boilerplate; keep_pending_read removed
2. apply_mmio_write uses per-field replace helpers
3. No-op match in sample_render_inputs removed
4. Dead functions removed (incoming_row_pixel, overlay_count, obj_fifo_overlay_row)
5. same_region/same_client simplified (== or documented limitation)
6. tile_lo_addr inlined; zero_mmio_write wrapper removed
7. Dead bindings cleaned up; unused parameters removed
8. No behavioral change

## Test Plan
- swim test passes for fetcher_test_top, core_test_top, mixer_test_top, oam_scan_test_top, irq_test_top, timing_test_top, obj_fetch_test_top (if exists)
- PPU backend diff scaffold passes
- Synth gate passes (no-op mux removal should save a few LUTs)

title: cpu_core entity stub
description:
Create the cpu_core entity STUB in src/cpu/core.spade (Phase 2, step 17).

This is a compile-only stub that defines the entity signature and returns dummy values. It does NOT contain working logic--that comes in Phase 3b when the step_mcycle framework (bd-2wu) and phase machines are implemented.

ENTITY SIGNATURE (from plan section 6.2):
  entity cpu_core(
      clk: clock,
      rst: bool,
      m_ce: bool,
      bus_resp: BusResp,
      irq_pending: IrqPending,
  ) -> (BusReq, CommitTrace)

STUB BEHAVIOR:
- On reset: initialize all registers to SkipBoot values:
  A=0x01, F=0xB0, B=0x00, C=0x13, D=0x00, E=0xD8, H=0x01, L=0x4D, SP=0xFFFE, PC=0x0100
  IME=Disabled, HALT=Running, Phase=Fetch
- On m_ce=true: return BusReq::Idle and a dummy CommitTrace (wrong values, tests should fail semantically)
- On m_ce=false: hold all state

ARCHITECTURAL CONSTRAINTS (enforced from day one):
- Uses reg(clk) for ALL state with explicit hold behavior
- State changes ONLY when m_ce is true
- Returns sparse CpuDelta, not full-state rewrite (critical for power)
- No pipeline abstraction--this is an entity with explicit registers

WHY A STUB: Phase 2 locks type-level API contracts. The stub lets Phase 1 harness tests execute against real compiled Verilog and fail on VALUE mismatches instead of build errors.

TRANSITION TO REAL: The step_mcycle framework task (bd-2wu) fills in the real logic. Each phase machine task adds match arms. The stub evolves into the real cpu_core incrementally.

ACCEPTANCE: Entity compiles. Tests can instantiate it. Reset produces correct initial state. m_ce=false holds state. m_ce=true produces (wrong but non-crashing) output.

title: Create repository directory structure per §5 layout
description:
Create the full directory tree specified in §5 of the architecture plan. This is the physical organization that enforces the denotational/operational split.

Directories to create:
- spec/           — machine-readable opcode metadata, flag policies, compare scopes (Python)
- formal/         — SymbiYosys .sby files for formal verification
- tools/          — local smoke/regress/power/formal shell scripts
- toolchain/      — version pins and lockfiles for all external tools
- bench/manifests/ — ROM manifest YAML files
- bench/pyboy/    — oracle adapter: oracle.py, hooks.py, symbols.py, hook_driver.py, replay.py, checkpoints.py
- bench/actions/  — test action generators
- src/cpu/        — CPU semantic and operational code
- src/bus/        — bus types, memory fabric, address decode
- src/mem/logical/ — logical memory map
- src/mem/phys/   — physical memory primitives (EBR, SPRAM, inferred)
- src/periph/     — timer, serial, interrupts
- src/sim/        — simulation-only tops (cpu_test_top, soc_lockstep_top, inject_top, trace_top)
- src/board/      — hardware synthesis tops (clockgen, icebreaker_top)
- test/harness/   — harness infrastructure tests
- test/unit/      — isolated Spade block unit tests
- test/lockstep/  — live oracle lockstep tests
- test/rom/       — ROM-based integration tests
- test/ppu/       — PPU-specific tests
- test/power/     — power/activity tests

Move existing main.spade content: icebreaker_top goes to src/board/icebreaker_top.spade, update swim.toml source paths.

IMPORTANT: The sim/ vs board/ split is load-bearing for power (§18.6). Debug/trace fanout must NEVER be in hardware tops by default.

title: BG fetcher state machine unit tests (test/ppu/unit/test_bg_fetcher.py)
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for the BG tile fetcher state machine (bd-abdy).

The fetcher is a five-step state machine with an epoch mechanism for stale
response discard. This is one of the most complex PPU sub-blocks and warrants
dedicated unit tests beyond what ROM greening provides.

TEST CASES:

1. FIVE-STEP FETCH SEQUENCE:
   Start fetcher on a visible scanline. Verify it progresses through:
   get_tile -> get_low -> get_high -> sleep -> push.
   Log step transitions with dot counts.

2. MEMORY REQUEST GENERATION:
   Verify correct PpuMemReq emission at each fetch step:
   - get_tile: MemClient::BgFetcher, tilemap address based on tile position
   - get_low: tile data base + row*2
   - get_high: tile data base + row*2 + 1
   Verify MemTxnTag fields (region=Vram, client=BgFetcher, id matching).

3. EPOCH INCREMENT ON WINDOW RESTART:
   Start BG fetch. Trigger window activation mid-fetch. Verify:
   - Epoch counter increments
   - Fetcher resets to window tilemap
   - BG FIFO is cleared (coordinate with FIFO tests)
   Log old epoch, new epoch, restart trigger.

4. EPOCH INCREMENT ON FETCH CANCEL:
   Start BG fetch. Trigger object fetch cancel. Verify epoch increments.
   Same for LCD disable and new-line transfer restart.

5. STALE RESPONSE DISCARD:
   Issue a fetch request at epoch N. Increment epoch (simulate window restart).
   Deliver response with epoch N. Verify response is DISCARDED, not pushed to FIFO.
   Then deliver response with epoch N+1. Verify it IS processed.

6. PUSH ONLY WHEN FIFO HAS ROOM:
   Fill FIFO to capacity. Start a fetch cycle. Verify the fetcher stalls at
   the push step until FIFO has room.

7. SCX/SCY FETCH ADDRESSING:
   Set various SCX and SCY values. Verify tile fetch addresses account for
   (SCX[7:3] + tile_counter) mod 32 for X and (SCY + LY) mod 256 / 8 for Y.
   Verify these use PpuRenderInputs (sampled), NOT direct register reads.

8. SIGNED VS UNSIGNED TILE DATA:
   Set LCDC.4=1 (unsigned/$8000 method). Verify tile data base is $8000.
   Set LCDC.4=0 (signed/$8800 method). Verify tile data base is $9000 and
   signed tile_id interpretation.

9. WINDOW TILEMAP FETCH:
   Activate window. Verify fetcher switches to window tilemap base
   (LCDC.6 selects $9800 or $9C00). Verify window tile position uses
   win_x and win_line, not SCX/SCY.

10. LCD DISABLE DURING FETCH:
    Disable LCD mid-fetch. Verify fetcher enters idle state.
    Verify no further memory requests emitted.

LOGGING FORMAT:
  [SUITE] test_bg_fetcher.py
    [CASE] test_five_step_sequence
      [STEP] Reset PPU, advance to mode 3 (pixel transfer)
      [STEP] Monitor fetcher step transitions
      [CHECK] Step 0: expected=GetTile actual=GetTile OK
      [CHECK] PpuMemReq issued: region=Vram client=BgFetcher addr=0x9800 OK
      [STEP] Provide memory response, advance 2 dots
      [CHECK] Step 1: expected=GetLow actual=GetLow OK
      ...
    [CASE] test_stale_response_discard
      [STEP] Issue fetch at epoch 3
      [STEP] Trigger window restart, epoch becomes 4
      [STEP] Deliver response with epoch 3
      [CHECK] Response discarded: expected=true actual=true OK
      [STEP] Deliver response with epoch 4
      [CHECK] Response accepted: expected=true actual=true OK
      [PASS] test_stale_response_discard (0.03s)

FAILURE DIAGNOSTICS:
On failure, log: current dot, fetcher step, epoch, pending read tag,
memory response tag, FIFO depth, PpuRenderInputs snapshot.

title: Green TIMER_DIV_BASIC custom ROM
description:
Write and green the TIMER_DIV_BASIC test ROM.

Test coverage:
- DIV increments at correct rate (every 256 T-cycles = 64 M-cycles for DMG)
- Writing to DIV resets the internal 16-bit counter to 0
- TIMA increments at rate selected by TAC clock select
- TAC enable/disable controls TIMA counting
- TIMA overflow triggers reload from TMA
- TIMA overflow sets IF bit 2
- Timer reload timing (1 M-cycle delay after overflow)

Edge cases:
- DIV write causing TIMA increment (falling-edge detection)
- TAC clock select change causing spurious increment
- Writing TIMA during the reload delay cycle cancels reload

Oracle mode: instr_commit or mcycle_commit for timing-critical tests

title: Author Wave B BG/window ROMs and green them

OWNED ROMs:

4. WINDOW_BASIC: Window at simple position, verify overlay.
5. WINDOW_LINE_COUNTER: Verify window internal line counter behavior.
6. WINDOW_WX_WY_EDGE: Edge cases for WX and WY values.
7. WINDOW_WX0_STUTTER: WX=0 1-dot stutter behavior.
8. WINDOW_WX166_NEXTLINE: WX=166 next-line effect.

checks green, PyBoy tilemap/screen comparisons green on targeted scenes.

title: Tile decode pure helpers (src/ppu/rtl/tile.spade)
description:
Implement isolated pure functions for tile data decode and addressing.

FUNCTIONS:
- decode_tile_row(lo: uint<8>, hi: uint<8>) -> [uint<2>; 8]
  Each tile row is 2 bytes. Bit 7 = leftmost pixel. Interleave lo/hi bits.

- bgwin_tile_addr(lcdc: Lcdc, tile_id: uint<8>, row: uint<3>) -> uint<16>
  BG/window tiles use either $8000 unsigned or $8800 signed method (LCDC.4).
  Unsigned: base $8000, tile_id * 16 + row * 2
  Signed: base $9000, (tile_id as int<8>) * 16 + row * 2

- obj_tile_addr(obj_size: bool, tile_id: uint<8>, row: uint<4>, flags: ObjFlags) -> uint<16>
  Objects always use $8000 unsigned. In 8x16 mode, low bit of tile_id ignored;
  hardware treats sprite as two vertically stacked tiles.

- apply_x_flip(row: [uint<2>; 8]) -> [uint<2>; 8]
  Reverse pixel order within the row.

- apply_y_flip(row_idx: uint<4>, obj_size: bool) -> uint<4>
  Mirror row index within 8 or 16 pixel height.

These are pure combinational functions with no state. They should be the
FIRST thing tested in PPU unit tests because every rendering path depends on them.

title: Extract is_ld_sp_hl predicate to deduplicate triple-matched instruction
Epic: PPU Background & Window — Wave B
description:
Implement background tile fetch, scrolling, window activation, and the BG pixel
pipeline. This is Wave B of the PPU milestone decomposition (history/ppu.md
sections 11, 17.2).

The pixel pipeline architecture uses explicit FIFOs and a multi-step fetcher:

1. BG FETCHER runs a five-step sequence: get tile, get low byte, get high byte,
   sleep, push. The fetcher carries an epoch counter (uint<4>) that increments on
   window restart, fetch cancel, LCD disable, and transfer restart. Any memory
   response belonging to an older epoch is discarded -- this prevents stale VRAM
   data from corrupting a fetcher that has already moved to a different logical
   generation.

2. BG FIFO holds up to 16 pixels. Entries are typed (2-bit color index + metadata).

3. SAMPLED RENDER STATE is where most raster-effect bugs originate:
   - SCX[2:0] sampled at scanline start for initial discard count
   - SCX[7:3] and SCY re-read on each tile fetch
   - WY checked at mode-2 start only (wy_triggered_this_frame)
   - WX triggers when rendered X + 7 matches (live comparison)
   - Window enable checked at mode-2 start
   - BGP sampled at pop/mix boundary

4. WINDOW TRIGGER uses explicit WindowState:
   - Inactive
   - ArmedThisFrame (WY condition met at mode-2 start)
   - ActiveOnLine { win_x, win_line }
   Window internal line counter resets at VBlank and increments only on lines
   where the window actually starts rendering. This means mid-frame window
   hide/show affects later window line selection.

5. TILE DECODE helpers are pure functions:
   - decode_tile_row(lo, hi) -> [uint<2>; 8]
   - bgwin_tile_addr(lcdc, tile_id, row) -- handles signed/unsigned methods
   - apply_x_flip(row), apply_y_flip(row_idx, obj_size)

6. MIXER (BG-only initial version) handles late DMG palette application.
   Object mixing deferred to Wave C.

Wave B ROMs: BG_STATIC, BG_SCROLL_WRAP, BG_SIGNED_ADDR, WINDOW_BASIC,
WINDOW_LINE_COUNTER, WINDOW_WX_WY_EDGE, WINDOW_WX0_STUTTER,
WINDOW_WX166_NEXTLINE, WINDOW_WX_RETRIGGER_GLITCH, WINDOW_WINEN_TOGGLE_REARM.

Wave B.5 CANARY: Before declaring BG/window mature, add a tiny mealybug canary
subset (SCX low-3, SCX high-5, window timing) as an early architectural alarm bell.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone C): BG/window owned ROMs green, scanline semantic checks
green, PyBoy tilemap/screen comparisons green on targeted scenes.

title: Green Wave A owned ROMs and core external suite tests
description: Bring Wave A timing/control ROM coverage to green for the owned ROMs plus the four external mooneye timing/control cases that are currently practical on the SoC Verilator path. The remaining external case lcdon_write_timing-GS has been split into a dedicated follow-up bug because it is still timing out after the write-result table is correct.
acceptance_criteria: All 5 owned ROMs pass. External suite cases vblank_stat_intr-GS, stat_lyc_onoff, stat_irq_blocking, and lcdon_timing-GS pass on the SoC Verilator path. lcdon_write_timing-GS is tracked separately. No unexplained LY/mode/STAT divergence remains in the four passing external cases.

title: Epic: PPU Raster Timing — Wave D
description:
Achieve dot-level timing accuracy for mode-3 length, raster-effect sensitivity,
and mid-frame register changes. Wave D of the PPU decomposition (history/ppu.md
section 17.4).

This wave uses the suites that actually punish bad dot-level assumptions:
- mealybug-tearoom-tests (mode-3 register timing, fetch behavior)
- mooneye PPU raster-timing acceptance tests

MODE-3 LENGTH EMERGENCE: The transfer state machine GENERATES mode-3 length from
fetcher/object/window activity. It is never a table lookup. Default is 172 dots,
lengthened by SCX discard (0-7 dots), window restart penalty (6 dots), and
per-object fetch penalties (variable, depends on fetch/tile cadence interaction).

TRANSFER EVENTS are recorded as TransferEvent variants (BgWarmup, ScxDiscard,
WindowRestart, ObjFetchStart, ObjFetchCancel, PixelPop). These are hashed into
a TransferDigest for stable regression comparison. The individual events serve as
a rich debugging artifact but are NOT the primary regression contract -- because
Pan Docs pixel FIFO chapter explicitly notes some SCX/object timing is still
unconfirmed.

STRICTNESS TIERS: Each semantic rule carries a StrictnessTier:
- Stable: well-confirmed, regression failures are bugs
- ResearchBacked: strong evidence but not exhaustively confirmed
- Hypothesis: still under research, xfail-eligible

External suites:
- hblank_ly_scx_timing-GS, intr_1_2_timing-GS, intr_2_0_timing,
  intr_2_mode0_timing, intr_2_mode3_timing, intr_2_oam_ok_timing,
  intr_2_mode0_timing_sprites (mooneye)
- targeted mealybug subsets, then full suite as failures localize

Generated sweep families:
- SCX_WX_COMBINATION_SWEEP, WIN_EN_WX_REARM_SWEEP,
  SCY_BITPLANE_DESYNC_PRE_CGBD, OBJ_FETCH_CANCEL_SWEEP

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone E): Targeted mooneye raster-timing tests green, selected
mealybug timing tests green, first-divergence artifacts clean when failures remain.

title: PPU mode state machine unit tests
description:
Create test/unit/test_ppu_modes.py — isolated tests for PPU mode transitions and timing.

PPU MODES:
- Mode 0 (HBlank): ~204 dots, CPU can access VRAM/OAM
- Mode 1 (VBlank): 10 scanlines (4560 dots), CPU can access VRAM/OAM
- Mode 2 (OAM Search): 80 dots, CPU cannot access OAM
- Mode 3 (Pixel Transfer): 168-291 dots (variable), CPU cannot access VRAM or OAM

MODE TRANSITIONS PER SCANLINE (lines 0-143):
  Mode 2 (80 dots) → Mode 3 (variable) → Mode 0 (remainder of 456 dots)
VBLANK (lines 144-153):
  Mode 1 for all 10 scanlines

TEST CASES:
1. Basic mode sequence: verify Mode 2→3→0 for one scanline, then VBlank after line 143
2. LY counter: verify LY increments each scanline, resets to 0 after line 153
3. LYC coincidence: verify STAT LYC=LY flag sets when LY matches LYC register
4. STAT interrupt sources:
   - Mode 0 (HBlank) interrupt
   - Mode 1 (VBlank) interrupt  
   - Mode 2 (OAM) interrupt
   - LYC=LY coincidence interrupt
5. STAT register bits: read-only mode bits, writable interrupt enable bits
6. Mode timing precision: verify exact dot counts for each mode transition
7. LCDC bit 7 (LCD enable): when cleared, PPU is off, LY=0, mode=0
8. LCDC enable/disable timing: verify clean transition

LOGGING: Each test logs mode transitions with dot/scanline precision:
  [LINE 0] Mode 2 @ dot 0 → Mode 3 @ dot 80 → Mode 0 @ dot 252 
  [LINE 1] Mode 2 @ dot 456 → Mode 3 @ dot 536 → Mode 0 @ dot 708
  ...
  [LINE 144] Mode 1 @ dot 65664
close_reason: Superseded: mode tests integrated into bd-o9eu and bd-1s1z

title: Bus: consolidate event bridge
description:
Merge duplicated logic in ppu_event_bridge.spade.

## Scope

2. Bind current_frame_index once with let instead of computing twice.
3. Clean up dead binding: let _ = target → use wildcard in destructure pattern.
4. Factor out common header in encode_event (test top).
5. Consider whether write_fields can reuse active_req gating pattern.

## Acceptance Criteria

title: Epic: PPU Integration — Wave D (Phase 4.6)
description:
Comprehensive PPU bring-up following the revised architecture in history/ppu.md.

The PPU is modeled as a typed dot-step transition system (step_dot pure function)
wrapped by a thin clocked Spade entity (ppu_core), verified at dot, scanline, and
frame semantic boundaries in that order.

KEY ARCHITECTURAL DECISIONS (from history/ppu.md revisions):
1. One canonical run-state: PpuMode derived from LcdRunState+PpuPhase, not stored
2. Explicit sampled render state: PpuSamplingState + PpuRenderInputs
3. Timed event stream: TimedPpuEvent with seq ordering, not single MMIO write
4. Tagged memory protocol: MemTxnTag + structured access outcomes
5. Three-stage object pipeline: ticket -> metadata -> pixel
6. Transfer digest over Mode3PenaltyBreakdown
7. ScanoutEvent with blank reasons
8. Orthogonal BehaviorConfig with feature flags

SUB-EPICS:
- bd-i4o: Architecture & Type Foundation
- bd-20o: Harness & Oracle Infrastructure
- bd-l98: Video Memory & Access Infrastructure
- bd-xsl: Timing & Control (Wave A)
- bd-1rh0: Background & Window (Wave B)
- bd-31mf: Object & Composition (Wave C)
- bd-1s47: Raster Timing (Wave D)
- bd-fr4m: DMA Realism (Wave E)
- bd-10d2: Formal & Equivalence Verification
- bd-8bit: Power Baseline

MILESTONES:
A (Harness): PyBoy surfaces accessible, replay deterministic, HDL tests fail semantically
B (Timing): Wave A ROMs green, mooneye timing tests green
C (BG/Window): Wave B ROMs green, scanline semantics green, PyBoy tilemap green
D (Composition): Wave C ROMs green, dmg-acid2 green
E (Raster): mooneye raster tests green, selected mealybug green
F (Power): LCD-off quiescence green, synthesis baseline recorded

title: Interrupt injection test scenarios with detailed logging
description:
Create comprehensive, deterministic interrupt injection test scenarios (§16.5) with detailed per-cycle logging for debugging interrupt timing issues.

This task COMPLEMENTS bd-27r (Green deterministic interrupt injection scenarios) by providing the detailed TEST SPECIFICATIONS and logging format. bd-27r validates that injection scenarios pass; this task defines WHICH scenarios and HOW they log.

SCENARIOS (test/lockstep/test_interrupt_injection.py):

1. BASIC INTERRUPT DISPATCH:
   - Inject timer interrupt (IF bit 2) while CPU is running
   - Verify: CPU pushes PC, jumps to vector 0x0050, IF bit cleared
   - Log: each M-cycle of the interrupt service sequence

2. EI DELAY PRECISION:
   - Execute: EI / NOP / <next instruction>
   - Inject interrupt during EI, verify NOT serviced until after NOP
   - Log: IME state at each instruction boundary

3. HALT WAKE VARIANTS:
   a. HALT with IME=1: wake + service interrupt
   b. HALT with IME=0, IE&IF!=0: wake WITHOUT servicing (HALT bug path)
   c. HALT with IME=0, IE&IF==0: stay halted indefinitely
   - Log: halt entry/exit cycle, bus activity during halt

4. INTERRUPT PRIORITY:
   - Set multiple IF bits simultaneously (e.g., VBlank + Timer)
   - Verify: highest priority (lowest bit) serviced first
   - Log: which interrupt acknowledged, in which order

5. DI/EI INTERACTION:
   - DI during interrupt service, verify subsequent interrupts blocked
   - RETI, verify interrupts re-enabled
   - EI immediately followed by DI, verify interrupts stay disabled

6. INTERRUPT DURING MULTI-CYCLE INSTRUCTION:
   - Inject interrupt while a 3-M-cycle instruction is executing
   - Verify: interrupt is serviced AFTER instruction completes, not mid-instruction
   - Log: instruction completion M-cycle vs interrupt service M-cycle

7. TIMER OVERFLOW TIMING:
   - Configure timer for known overflow point
   - Verify: IF bit set on exact expected M-cycle
   - Log: TIMA value at each M-cycle leading up to overflow

8. NESTED INTERRUPT EDGE CASE:
   - Service interrupt A, enable interrupts mid-ISR (EI), trigger interrupt B
   - Verify: B serviced, B returns to A's ISR, A returns to main code
   - Log: full call/return stack trace

LOGGING FORMAT (per scenario):
  [SCENARIO] halt_wake_ime_enabled
    [SETUP] IE=0x04 (timer), IME=Enabled
    [INJECT] IF bit 2 at commit #47
    [CYCLE 47] Phase=Halted, bus=Idle, IME=Enabled
    [CYCLE 48] Phase=Halted, bus=Idle, wake detected (IE & IF = 0x04)
    [CYCLE 49] Phase=ServiceInterrupt{irq=Timer, subphase=PushPCHi}
    [CYCLE 50] Phase=ServiceInterrupt{irq=Timer, subphase=PushPCLo}
    [CYCLE 51] Phase=ServiceInterrupt{irq=Timer, subphase=JumpToVector}
    [CYCLE 52] Phase=Fetch, PC=0x0050, IF bit 2 cleared
    [PASS] halt_wake_ime_enabled

ACCEPTANCE: All 8 scenario categories pass. Logging output matches expected format. Each scenario exercises a distinct interrupt timing edge case.

title: Bus: refactor region decode and write helpers
description:
Eliminate redundant decode_region calls in the bus datapath.

## Scope
1. Remove req_region function. Callers compute: let addr = req_addr(req); let region = decode_region(addr);
2. Refactor wram_write, hram_write, oam_write to accept pre-decoded BusRegion parameter instead of calling decode_region internally. This eliminates 3 redundant 10-way comparison chains from the synthesis.

## Why this matters for synthesis
Each decode_region is a 10-way address comparison (checking ROM, VRAM, CartRam, Wram, Echo, Oam, Io, Hram, IeReg, NotUsable address ranges). Removing 3 instances directly saves LUTs on the critical bus datapath.

## Acceptance Criteria
- req_region removed
- Write helpers accept pre-decoded region
- membus entity computes region once and passes to write helpers
- membus_test_top and observe_req_test_top pass
- Synthesis LUT count does not increase (expect decrease)

title: Evidence tagging and compare-policy vocabulary
description:
Define the evidence/confidence types used in the reference model and test harness.

TYPES:

enum EvidenceSourceKind { PanDocs, MooneyeTest, MealybugTest, HardwareCapture, Hypothesis }
enum EvidenceConfidence { Exact, High, Medium, Low, Unknown }
enum EvidenceRuleStrength { Architectural, Empirical, Inferred }
enum CompareSurface { DotCommit, ScanlineSummary, FrameHash, ReferenceImage }

struct EvidenceTag {
  source_kind: EvidenceSourceKind,
  confidence: EvidenceConfidence,
  rule_strength: EvidenceRuleStrength,
  affected_surface: CompareSurface,
  note: string,
}

enum ExpectedSemantics<T> {
  Exact(T),              -- this value is the only correct answer
  OneOf([T; N]),          -- one of these outcomes is correct
  MaskedBits { value: T, mask: T },  -- only these bits matter
  DontCare,              -- any value is acceptable
  Hypothesis(T),         -- we think this is right but cannot confirm
}

WHY: Pan Docs contains areas explicitly marked as incomplete or needing more
research, especially in pixel FIFO timing details. The harness must express
this is exact vs this is one of these outcomes vs this is still a hypothesis
at RULE granularity. This prevents conflating known-exact blocks with
still-researched corners.

Without this, the only tool for uncertainty is whole-test xfail, which is
too coarse. With it, you can keep the strict dot lane mostly exact while
carving out tiny, well-labeled uncertainty islands.

These types exist in the Python reference model and test harness, not in
synthesizable Spade. They guide compare-policy decisions in the oracle layer.

title: Regression test suite tier definitions and nightly configuration
description:
Define the three regression test tiers and configure the nightly exhaustive suite.

TIER DEFINITIONS:

1. SMOKE (< 30 seconds):
   - Spade compilation check (swim build)
   - Cocotb infrastructure smoke (bd-1jm)
   - Oracle init/reset check
   - E2E smoke test (bd-2up)
   Run: on every commit, before any other work

2. FULL (< 10 minutes):
   - All unit tests (ALU, decoder, register, timer, bus, interrupts)
   - All formal checks (F low nibble, reset state, m_ce hold)
   - All Wave A-D ROM differential tests
   - Metamorphic and invariant tests
   - Lockstep tests (instruction-boundary granularity)
   Run: after completing each bead, before closing

3. NIGHTLY (< 2 hours):
   - Everything in FULL
   - ALU exhaustive sweeps (bd-3ud): all ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/DAA inputs
   - M-cycle-granularity lockstep on targeted subsets
   - Extended ROM execution (10000+ commits per ROM)
   - Power/activity capture (SAIF, write-enable duty cycle)
   - Synthesis + P&R resource/timing check
   Run: nightly via cron or manual trigger

CONFIGURATION FILES:
- test/tiers.yaml: maps test files to tiers, with expected-fail entries
- tools/run_tests.py --tier smoke|full|nightly: run specific tier
- bench/artifacts/: all test outputs stored here with timestamps

XFAIL MANAGEMENT (§16.1):
- xfail entries MUST have a reason and a linked bead ID
- xfail(strict=True): test must FAIL; if it passes, that is also a failure (catches premature greening)
- No blanket xfails — each failing test is individually tracked
- When a bead is closed, its xfails must be removed or the test promoted to expected-pass

COVERAGE REPORT:
- After each FULL run, print coverage summary:
  Opcode families: 85% (34/40 families with ≥1 passing test)
  Phase constructors: 70% (14/20 Phase variants exercised)
  Bus regions: 60% (6/10 regions accessed)
  Interrupt sources: 40% (2/5 sources tested)
  ROM suites: 7/15 green

NIGHTLY ALERT:
- If any previously-green test goes RED in nightly, emit a high-priority alert
- Include the exact commit that broke it (binary search via git bisect if needed)

title: HALT implementation
description:
Implement HALT behavior in the CPU phase machine.

States (§8.3):
  enum HaltState {
    Running,          // Normal execution
    Halted,           // CPU halted, waiting for interrupt
    HaltBugPending,   // HALT bug: next byte read twice (defer implementation)
  }

HALT behavior:
- When HALT executed: transition to HaltState::Halted
- In Halted state: CPU emits BusReq::Idle, no register writes, no ALU activity (§18.5)
- Wake condition: (IE & IF) != 0 (any enabled interrupt is pending)
- If IME=Enabled AND wake: service interrupt (transition to ServiceInterrupt phase)
- If IME=Disabled AND wake: resume execution at PC (no interrupt service)

HALT bug (defer to later):
- If IME=Disabled AND (IE & IF)==0 when HALT executes: HALT bug triggered
- Next instruction's first byte is read twice (PC doesn't increment on first read)
- HaltBugPending type exists now but implementation can be stubbed

POWER (§18.5): HALT must mean REAL stillness:
- No register writes
- No bus requests
- No ALU activity
- Stable phase/debug outputs in simulation

This is both a correctness requirement and a power architecture requirement.

title: Epic: HDL Type System & Skeleton (Phase 2)
description:
Create the compile-only Spade HDL skeleton: all types, stubs, and simulation tops that define the semantic boundaries of the CPU.

This phase produces NO working logic—only type definitions and stub entities that compile cleanly. The purpose is to:
1. Lock the type-level API contracts before implementation
2. Let Phase 1 tests run against stubs and fail on value mismatches, not build errors
3. Establish the denotational/operational split (§4.1) in code structure

Key type boundaries from §7.2:
- CpuArchState (regs, ime_state, halt_state)
- CpuMicroState (phase, opcode_latch, imm_lo/hi, decoded, temp)
- Phase enum with typed continuations (§8.1-8.2)
- CommitTrace with schema versioning (§7.2)
- BusReq/BusResp (§12.2) — CPU-facing, minimal
- BusObs/BusOwner (§12.2.1-12.2.2) — fabric-side, simulation-only
- AluReq with Idle case for operand isolation (§11.1-11.2)
- CpuDelta with sparse register writes (§7.2) — critical for power (§18.4)

Simulation tops:
- cpu_test_top: isolated CPU testing with controllable bus/interrupts
- soc_lockstep_top: full SoC for lockstep oracle comparison

RATIONALE: The Conal-style split (§4.1) is the architectural heart. Pure functions specify combinational semantics; entities own state. The type system encodes this split. Typed continuations (§8.2) make illegal phase transitions unrepresentable. The CpuDelta sparse-write pattern (§18.4) is designed-in from day one because power is an architectural property (thesis #3), not a cleanup pass.

ACCEPTANCE: All .spade files compile. Tests execute and fail on value mismatches, not build errors.

title: Add bounded formal LCD-off quiescence property
Formal verification: m_ce=0 architectural state hold
description:
Create formal/cpu_hold.sby — proves that when m_ce=0, ALL architectural state is frozen.

PROPERTY TO VERIFY:
For ANY state the CPU can be in, if m_ce is held low for N cycles:
- All registers (A, F, B, C, D, E, H, L, SP, PC) are unchanged
- IME state is unchanged
- HALT state is unchanged
- Phase is unchanged
- No bus requests are emitted (BusReq remains whatever it was)
- No register write-enables are asserted

This is the FOUNDATIONAL power property (§6.2, §18.1): architectural state does not toggle at board-clock rate. The entire power architecture rests on this invariant.

WHY FORMAL: This property must hold for EVERY possible CPU state, not just states we think to test. A simulation test checks a finite number of states; formal proves the property universally. If this property is violated, the power architecture is broken.

TOOL: yosys + sby (SymbiYosys). Use k-induction to prove the invariant holds at any depth.

RELATIONSHIP TO bd-838 (metamorphic tests): bd-838 tests m_ce=0 hold via simulation across sampled states. This bead proves it formally for ALL states. Both should exist.

title: Consolidate periph and video test helpers
description:
Extract remaining duplicated test helpers from periph and video test tops.

## Scope
1. decode_buttons: 3 copies (joypad_test_top, joypad_interrupts_test_top, membus_test_top). Move to joypad.spade as pub fn or a periph test util module.

2. Video test helpers duplicated between access_test_top and backend_adapter_test_top:
   - decode_region / decode_client / decode_kind (3 functions)
   - region_bit
   Move to a shared video test module or make pub in access.spade / video module.

3. source_bits (frame_sink.spade) / encode_source (frame_sink_test_top.spade) are the same function. Have the test top import source_bits instead of defining encode_source.

## Acceptance Criteria
- decode_buttons in one location, imported by 3 test tops
- Video test helpers in one location, imported by 2 test tops
- encode_source removed; test top uses source_bits
- All affected swim tests pass

title: Green mealybug targeted subsets and mooneye raster timing tests
description:
Bring targeted mealybug and mooneye raster timing tests to green.

MOONEYE RASTER TESTS:
- hblank_ly_scx_timing-GS: HBlank, LY, and SCX timing interaction
- intr_1_2_timing-GS: Mode 1 to mode 2 interrupt timing
- intr_2_0_timing: Mode 2 to mode 0 interrupt timing
- intr_2_mode0_timing: Mode 2 + mode 0 STAT interrupt timing
- intr_2_mode3_timing: Mode 2 + mode 3 STAT interrupt timing
- intr_2_oam_ok_timing: Mode 2 OAM access timing
- intr_2_mode0_timing_sprites: Mode 0 timing with sprites present

MEALYBUG TARGETED SUBSETS:
Start with tests that cover already-implemented surfaces:
- SCX register change timing during mode 3
- SCY register change timing during mode 3
- Window activation timing
- BGP palette change timing
Use DMG expected results.

Then expand to full mealybug suite as failures localize cleanly.

GENERATED SWEEP FAMILIES:
- SCX_WX_COMBINATION_SWEEP: Parameterized SCX and WX value combinations
- WIN_EN_WX_REARM_SWEEP: Window enable/disable with various WX rearm scenarios
- SCY_BITPLANE_DESYNC_PRE_CGBD: SCY changes revealing bitplane desync (pre-CGB-D)
- OBJ_FETCH_CANCEL_SWEEP: Object fetch cancel at various timing points

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone E): Targeted mooneye raster-timing tests green, selected
mealybug timing tests green, first-divergence artifacts clean.

title: Epic: PPU Harness & Oracle Infrastructure
description:
Build the PPU verification infrastructure BEFORE writing PPU HDL. This is the
harness-before-HDL principle that proved so valuable in CPU bring-up.

The PPU oracle architecture is deliberately layered (history/ppu.md section 14):

Layer 1: LOCAL EXECUTABLE SEMANTIC REFERENCE (bench/ref/ppu_ref.py).
  A small Python model implementing step_dot-style semantics for mode sequencing,
  LY/LYC/STAT, window trigger/counter, object selection, mixer rules, mode-3 length.
  This is NOT a second emulator -- it is the executable form of the semantic contract.
  It enables short-run dot-differential testing without depending on PyBoy.

Layer 2: LIVE PYBOY SEMANTIC/FRAME ORACLE.
  PyBoy provides frame buffers, tilemap observation, sprite wrappers, tile imagery,
  per-scanline scroll/window position via tilemap_position_list, and save/load state.
  These are exactly the right semantic surfaces once basic dot/line rules are trusted.
  PyBoy is NOT a public dot-step oracle; treat it as a frame/semantic oracle only.

Layer 3: TARGETED EXTERNAL ROM SUITES.
  mealybug for mode-3 register timing, mooneye for targeted timing/interrupt tests,
  dmg-acid2 as a late coarse composition checksum (its README says a simple line
  renderer suffices and it is NOT a timing torture test).

Additional infrastructure:
- ROM manifest schema updates with behavior_config, schema_version, video_anchor
  coordinate space, rom_sha256, etc.
- Schema versioning (bench/schemas/) for replay capsules, oracle captures, line summaries
- Build flavor configuration (synth, sim_semantic, sim_trace, sim_power)
- Raster stimulus ports with state import/export and symbolic video anchors
- Evidence tagging with rule_strength and ExpectedSemantics vocabulary
- Replay capsule and line narrative tools

RATIONALE: The PPU is the most failure-prone subsystem. Investing in layered oracles,
deterministic replay, and rich divergence artifacts upfront pays back across every
subsequent wave. The CPU experience showed that infrastructure debt during bring-up
is far more expensive than infrastructure investment before bring-up.

title: OAM scan pure helpers producing SelectedObjTickets (src/ppu/rtl/oam_scan.spade)
description:
Implement mode-2 OAM scan that selects up to 10 objects per scanline.

OAM SCAN (80 dots, 2 dots per entry, 40 entries):
For each OAM entry, check if object Y overlaps current scanline:
  Y-16 <= LY < Y-16+height (height 8 or 16 per LCDC.2)

Selection uses ONLY Y position in OAM-entry order. No X comparison.
X-hidden objects (X=0 or X>=168) still count toward the 10-object limit.

OUTPUT: Up to 10 SelectedObjTicket:
struct SelectedObjTicket {
  oam_index: uint<6>,        -- which OAM entry (0-39)
  x: uint<8>,                -- X position from OAM
  y: uint<8>,                -- Y position from OAM
  selection_rank: uint<4>,   -- 0-9, order of selection
}

The ticket array uses Option<uint<6>> slots (per LineSummary.selected_objs)
so absence is explicit rather than relying on obj_count + zero-fill.

MEMORY INTERFACE: Issues PpuMemReq with MemClient::OamScanner to read OAM
entries. 2 reads per entry (Y+X bytes, then tile+flags bytes) but only Y+X
is needed for selection; tile/flags fetched later in object fetch path.

IMPORTANT: Mode 2 produces SelectedObjTickets ONLY. Tile number, flags, row
bytes, and any cancellation/corruption behavior belong to the object-fetch
path (separate bead). This three-stage split keeps DMA/OAM corruption tests
local to the fetch stage.

title: Video: frame_sink dedup, access tautology, dead bindings
description:
Clean up the video module: extract shared mismatch helper, remove tautological condition, clean dead bindings.

## Scope
1. Extract fn pixel_mismatch(reference, emit) -> bool from the identical logic in advance_line_sink and advance_diff_sink (frame_sink.spade).

2. Remove tautological region_is_vram(r) || region_is_oam(r) in access.spade:~57. MemRegion only has Vram and Oam. Add comment explaining why the condition was removed (exhaustive enum).

3. Clean dead bindings:
   - frame_sink.spade:~244: let _ = reason → wildcard in pattern
   - frame_sink_test_top.spade:~80,104,109,123: let _ = initial_*_state() → remove if not needed
   - frame_sink_test_top.spade:~14: unused import blank_reason_bits → remove
   - icebreaker_top.spade:~59: let _ = bus_obs → add comment explaining why discarded

## Acceptance Criteria
- pixel_mismatch helper exists and used at both sites
- Tautological OR removed
- Dead bindings cleaned
- frame_sink_test_top, access_test_top pass

title: Extract shared test/sim encoder utilities
description:
~200 lines of duplicated encoder/helper functions are copy-pasted across sim test tops, PPU test tops, video test tops, and periph test tops. This is the foundational task for the simplification effort: it creates shared modules that other tasks will depend on.

## Background

As the iceboy codebase grew, each new test top was scaffolded by copying helpers from an existing one. Over 10+ test tops, this produced a web of byte-identical encoder functions (cpu_bus_req, encode_bus_region, encode_phase, scanout extractors, decode_buttons, etc.) scattered across files. Adding a new encoder variant (e.g., for a new BusRegion) requires updating 4-8 files instead of 1.

The Spade module system supports pub fn and cross-module imports, so there is no language barrier to consolidation — this is purely accumulated copy-paste debt.

## Scope

### Group A: Shared utility primitives
- Create a shared utility location (e.g., src/sim/encode.spade or src/lib/util.spade)
- pub fn bit(b: bool) -> uint<1> — replaces the if b { 1u1 } else { 0u1 } pattern (18+ sites)
- pub fn io_write(write_en, write_addr, target) -> bool — currently duplicated in timer.spade, interrupts.spade, serial.spade (3 copies + 1 variant in joypad.spade)

### Group B: Sim encoder module
- cpu_bus_req: 4 copies (icebreaker_top, cpu_test_top, soc_lockstep_top, soc_rom_top)
- encode_bus_region: 2 copies (cpu_test_top, soc_lockstep_top) — 10-arm match
- encode_bus_owner: 2 copies (cpu_test_top, soc_lockstep_top)
- encode_ime: 2 copies (cpu_test_top, soc_rom_top)
- encode_halt: 2 copies (cpu_test_top, soc_rom_top)
- encode_phase: 2 copies (cpu_test_top, soc_rom_top) — 11-arm match
- Scanout extractors (encode_scanout_kind, encode_pixel_source, encode_blank_reason, scanout_x/y/shade/source/blank_reason): ~60 lines duplicated between soc_lockstep_top and soc_rom_top
- ppu_vram_active / ppu_oam_active: 2 copies
- count_inc: 3 copies (cpu_test_top, soc_lockstep_top, ppu_power_top)
- mask_has: 2 copies
- phase_is_halted / halt_is_halted: 2 copies

### Group C: PPU test helpers (consolidate into ppu_support.spade)
- encode_phase/encode_mode/encode_run/decode_run: 2-3 copies across core_test_top, timing_test_top, irq_test_top. ppu_support.spade already has encode_ppu_phase and encode_ppu_mode but test tops define their own.
- build_lcdc: 5 copies with slight variations (fifo_test_top, mixer_test_top, oam_scan_test_top, tile_test_top, fetcher_test_top). Unify into a single parameterized function.
- pack_row: 2 copies (fetcher_test_top, tile_test_top)
- encode_mode in ppu_power_top is identical to already-imported encode_ppu_mode — dead duplicate.

### Group D: Periph + video test helpers
- decode_buttons: 3 copies (joypad_test_top, joypad_interrupts_test_top, membus_test_top)
- decode_region/decode_client/decode_kind: 2 copies (access_test_top, backend_adapter_test_top)
- region_bit: 2 copies (access_test_top, backend_adapter_test_top)
- source_bits (frame_sink.spade) / encode_source (frame_sink_test_top.spade): same function, different names

## Considerations
- build_lcdc variants differ slightly in which fields are parameterized. The unified version should take all Lcdc fields as parameters with sensible defaults, or accept a full Lcdc struct directly.
- io_write is used in production code (peripherals), not just test tops. Its shared location should be importable from both prod and test modules.
- Some test tops may have subtly different versions of "the same" function (e.g., different arm ordering). Verify byte-level equivalence before consolidating.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Each duplicated function exists in exactly one canonical location
2. All former call sites import from the shared module
3. No functional behavior change — all test tops produce identical simulation outputs
4. build_lcdc variants unified into a single parameterized function
5. Dead encode_mode in ppu_power_top removed
6. bit() utility available project-wide

## Test Plan
- swim test passes for ALL affected test tops: cpu_test_top, soc_lockstep_top, soc_rom_top, core_test_top, timing_test_top, irq_test_top, fifo_test_top, mixer_test_top, oam_scan_test_top, tile_test_top, fetcher_test_top, membus_test_top, joypad_test_top, joypad_interrupts_test_top, access_test_top, backend_adapter_test_top, frame_sink_test_top
- Synth gate passes (shared modules should not appear in synth, but verify imports do not pull in test-only code)
- grep confirms no remaining local copies of extracted functions

title: Physical memory mapping: SPRAM/EBR for large memories
description:
Verify and implement proper physical memory mapping (§18.7.1).

UP5K resources:
- 4 x SPRAM blocks (256Kbit each = 32KB each, single-port)
- 30 x EBR blocks (4Kbit each = 512B each, true dual-port)

Memory mapping plan:
- WRAM (8KB): 1 SPRAM block (uses 8KB of 32KB capacity) or 16 EBR blocks
- VRAM (8KB): 1 SPRAM block
- OAM (160 bytes): inferred registers or 1 EBR block
- HRAM (127 bytes): inferred registers
- Cartridge RAM: SPRAM or external

Implementation:
- src/mem/phys/spram.spade: SPRAM primitive wrapper
- src/mem/phys/ebr.spade: EBR primitive wrapper
- src/mem/logical/map.spade: logical-to-physical mapping

CRITICAL: Do not let large memories remain accidental register arrays in hardware builds. The clean wrapper boundary (§18.7.1) allows physical mapping changes without affecting CPU/bus semantics.

title: Full BG+OBJ mixer and draw priority resolution
description:
Complete the mixer to handle BG+OBJ pixel composition with DMG priority rules.

The mixer is a PURE FUNCTION over one BG pixel and one OBJ pixel:

INPUTS:
- bg_pixel: { color: uint<2>, from_window: bool }
- obj_pixel: Option<ObjPixel> (None if no object at this X)
- lcdc: Lcdc (for bg_enable check)
- render_inputs: PpuRenderInputs (for palette values at pop time)

PRIORITY RESOLUTION (DMG):
1. If obj_pixel is None or obj color is 0 (transparent): use BG pixel
2. If LCDC.0 (bg_enable) is 0: BG is all white, objects render normally
3. If obj_pixel.bg_over_obj is true AND bg color is non-zero: use BG pixel
4. Otherwise: use OBJ pixel

DRAW RANK (DMG):
When multiple objects overlap at the same X:
  - Smaller X position wins
  - Ties broken by earlier OAM entry (lower oam_index)
This is DIFFERENT from selection priority (which is purely OAM order by Y).

LATE PALETTE APPLICATION:
- BG: apply BGP from render_inputs.bgp_pop
- OBJ: apply OBP0 or OBP1 from render_inputs based on ObjFlags.palette_sel
Palette registers are re-read at pop time, enabling mid-scanline palette effects.

OUTPUT: ScanoutEvent::Pixel(PixelEmit { x, y, shade, source })
where source is Bg, Window, or Obj.
close_reason: Implemented BG+OBJ mixer rules with window provenance and focused unit coverage

title: HALT quiescence verification test
description:
Create test/power/test_halt_quiescence.py using the shared harness on cpu_test_top.

This is a Phase 5 power-validation bead even though it consumes HALT behavior implemented in Wave B. Its job is to prove that HALT is not just functionally correct, but actually quiescent.

Verify HALT means real stillness (§18.5):
1. Enter HALT state
2. Run for many M-cycles with no wake event
3. Verify on every cycle:
   - No register writes
   - BusReq stays Idle
   - AluReq stays Idle
   - Phase output is stable
   - Debug/trace outputs are stable
4. Trigger a wake event and verify the wake cycle and subsequent interrupt behavior are correct

This test is part of the first power baseline, not just the interrupt milestone.

title: E2E milestone gate: PPU Raster Timing ready (gate_ppu_raster.sh)
description:
Create tools/gate_ppu_raster.sh -- acceptance gate for PPU Milestone E.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (history/ppu.md section 22, Milestone E):
1. All targeted mooneye raster-timing tests green
2. Selected mealybug timing tests green (DMG expected results)
3. Generated sweep families green (SCX_WX, WIN_EN_WX, OBJ_FETCH_CANCEL)
4. Transfer event recording consistent (TransferDigest hash stable)
5. First-divergence artifacts clean and local when failures remain
6. Hypothesis-tier rules under xfail; Stable-tier rules exact

This milestone confirms dot-level accuracy for mode-3 timing.

title: Video access policy module (src/video/access.spade)
description:
Implement the access policy module that decides whether each memory attempt
is granted, denied, or undefined.

This module is the SEPARATION OF CONCERNS between policy and storage:
- access.spade decides WHO can access WHAT WHEN
- backend_adapter.spade maps granted requests to physical storage

ACCESS RULES (from Pan Docs):
- Mode 2 (OAM Scan): OAM blocked for CPU, VRAM accessible
- Mode 3 (Pixel Transfer): both VRAM and OAM blocked for CPU
- Modes 0, 1 (HBlank, VBlank): both accessible
- OAM DMA active: CPU restricted to HRAM only
- LCD off: both fully accessible

OUTPUT: PpuMemResult { Ok(data) | Denied | UndefinedRead(reason) }

The VideoBlockReason enum carries WHY an access was blocked:
- CpuBlockedByMode2, CpuBlockedByMode3, CpuBlockedByOamDma, PpuBlockedByOwnership

This matters for debugging AND for compare-policy control. Suites that require
exact $FF materialization for blocked reads can opt in via manifest; the default
semantic contract is UndefinedRead.

INTEGRATION: The existing bus fabric (src/bus/membus.spade) already has
ppu_vram_active and ppu_oam_active inputs. This module provides the PPU-side
logic that drives those signals and evaluates CPU access outcomes.

TESTING: Backend-differential lane should run identical tests against both
sim-array and inferred-sync-RAM backends to catch protocol underspecification.
acceptance_criteria:
1. access_check pure function takes (mode, region, client, dma_active, lcd_enabled) and returns PpuMemResult
2. Mode 2: OAM blocked for CPU, VRAM accessible
3. Mode 3: both VRAM and OAM blocked for CPU, accessible for PPU clients
4. Modes 0/1: both accessible
5. LCD off: both fully accessible regardless of mode
6. OAM DMA: CPU restricted (OAM blocked, HRAM still accessible)
7. PpuMemResult carries VideoBlockReason (not just Ok/Denied)
8. UndefinedRead is default for blocked reads, exact 0xFF materialization is opt-in
9. Integration: drives ppu_vram_active and ppu_oam_active signals for existing bus fabric

title: Echo RAM, unusable region, and memory behavior profile enforcement
description:
Implement the remaining memory map edge cases and enforce the MemoryBehaviorProfile (§13.1.1).

ECHO RAM (0xE000-0xFDFF):
- Mirrors WRAM (0xC000-0xDDFF). Reads/writes to Echo RAM map to WRAM.
- Echo RAM is 8KB but only mirrors the first 7.5KB (0xE000-0xFDFF = 0x1E00 bytes)
- Implementation: in the bus address decoder, map 0xE000-0xFDFF to 0xC000-0xDDFF

UNUSABLE REGION (0xFEA0-0xFEFF):
- Behavior depends on hardware revision and DMA state
- DmgConservative profile: reads return 0xFF, writes are ignored (safest default)
- DmgRevisionSpecific profile: varies — some revisions return ANDed OAM data
- For CPU bring-up, pin DmgConservative (§13.1.1)

BLOCKED VRAM/OAM ACCESS:
- During PPU pixel transfer (mode 3): VRAM reads return 0xFF, writes are ignored
- During OAM search (mode 2) and pixel transfer (mode 3): OAM reads return 0xFF, writes are ignored
- During OAM DMA: CPU cannot access most of memory (only HRAM accessible)
- These restrictions are enforced in the memory fabric, NOT in the CPU (§12.2.2)

MEMORY BEHAVIOR PROFILE ENFORCEMENT:
- MemoryBehaviorProfile enum (from bd-331) selects which set of restrictions apply
- The bus fabric must consult the active profile when resolving access to edge-case regions
- Record the active profile in replay capsules for reproducibility

TESTING (test/unit/test_memory_map.py):
1. Echo RAM: write to 0xC000, read from 0xE000, verify match (and vice versa)
2. Echo RAM boundary: verify 0xFE00+ is NOT echo RAM (it is OAM)
3. Unusable region: read from 0xFEA0, verify 0xFF under DmgConservative
4. Blocked VRAM: set PPU to mode 3, attempt VRAM read, verify 0xFF
5. Blocked OAM: set PPU to mode 2, attempt OAM read, verify 0xFF
6. DMA restriction: during OAM DMA, verify only HRAM is accessible
7. Log each test with address, expected value, actual value, active profile

NOTE: VRAM/OAM blocking tests (#4-6) require PPU mode state or DMA state, so those specific sub-tests are gated on Phase 4.5/4.6 completion. Echo RAM and unusable region tests can run as soon as the bus fabric exists.

title: Pin PyBoy dependency with headless configuration
description:
Add PyBoy as a pinned Python dependency for the oracle/verification layer.

Requirements:
- Pin exact PyBoy version in toolchain/python.lock or requirements.txt
- Configure for headless mode: null window plugin, sound emulation off, unlimited speed (§14.2)
- Verify PyBoy can load a test ROM, step instructions, read registers, read/write memory
- Verify save/load checkpoint (snapshot/restore) works
- Document the specific PyBoy APIs we depend on in toolchain/versions.md

PyBoy is the SOLE behavioral oracle (thesis #6). We do NOT add a second CPU emulator. The hook-driven adapter (separate task) provides the deep control path for lockstep testing.

Run PyBoy in headless performance mode for all non-visual suites: null window, no input, sound emulation off, unlimited speed (§14.2).

title: Simulation-only frame and line sinks (src/video/frame_sink.spade)
description:
Implement simulation-only sinks that collect the ScanoutEvent stream into line
buffers, frame buffers, hashes, and diffs.

The PPU core emits a non-backpressured ScanoutEvent stream. Sinks consume it
without stalling the PPU. This is an architectural invariant: the sink must
NEVER be allowed to stall the PPU.

SINKS:
1. Line buffer: collects 160 pixels per visible scanline, produces LineSummary
2. Frame buffer: collects 144 lines, produces frame hash
3. Hash sink: rolling hash over pixel stream for fast regression comparison
4. Diff sink: computes delta from reference for failure localization

All sinks are simulation-only and excluded from the synth build flavor.
The frame_sink entity appears in sim tops but NOT in board tops.

LINE HASH COMPUTATION: Must be deterministic and fast. Hash over the concatenation
of (shade, source) for all 160 visible pixels on the line.

FRAME HASH COMPUTATION: Hash over all 144 line hashes. Used as a coarse
regression check after line-level semantic surfaces are already green.

ScanoutEvent::Blank and ScanoutEvent::FrameStart/LineStart allow the sink to
distinguish real pixel data from architectural blanking without inspecting
PPU internal state.

title: Phase machine for CB-prefix operations
description:
Implement phase machine for CB-prefixed instructions.

CB prefix handling:
- Fetch 0xCB → Phase::FetchPrefix{prefix: Cb} → fetch next byte → decode_cb → execute

For register operands (B,C,D,E,H,L,A):
- 2 M-cycles: fetch CB + fetch opcode → execute ALU

For (HL) operand:
- BIT b,(HL): 3 M-cycles (fetch CB + fetch opcode + read (HL))
- RES/SET b,(HL): 4 M-cycles (fetch CB + fetch opcode + read (HL) + write (HL))
- Rotate/shift (HL): 4 M-cycles (fetch CB + fetch opcode + read (HL) + write (HL))

The continuation for CB (HL) operations is a ReadMem with the appropriate continuation for the CB operation type.

title: test_event_script_determinism.py scaffold
description:
Create test/harness/test_event_script_determinism.py (Phase 1 step 10).

This test verifies that the deterministic perturbation engine produces identical results across runs:
1. Generate an event script with seed S
2. Run the oracle with that script for N commits
3. Record the final state
4. Repeat with the same seed S
5. Assert identical final state

Also verify:
- Different seeds produce different event scripts
- Event scripts are keyed by commit index, not wall-clock time
- The same event stream applied to both DUT and oracle produces deterministic comparison results

This is another 'sanity-green meta test' (§16.1) — it validates the test infrastructure itself.

title: PPU registers (LCDC, SCX, SCY, WX, WY, BGP, OBP)
description:
Implement PPU control and palette registers.

LCDC (0xFF40) — LCD Control:
- Bit 7: LCD enable
- Bit 6: Window tilemap select (0=9800, 1=9C00)
- Bit 5: Window enable
- Bit 4: BG & Window tile data select (0=8800, 1=8000)
- Bit 3: BG tilemap select (0=9800, 1=9C00)
- Bit 2: OBJ size (0=8x8, 1=8x16)
- Bit 1: OBJ enable
- Bit 0: BG & Window enable/priority

Scroll registers:
- SCX (0xFF43): viewport X scroll
- SCY (0xFF42): viewport Y scroll
- WX (0xFF4B): window X position + 7
- WY (0xFF4A): window Y position

Palette registers:
- BGP (0xFF47): BG palette data (4 x 2-bit color indices)
- OBP0 (0xFF48): OBJ palette 0 (color 0 is transparent)
- OBP1 (0xFF49): OBJ palette 1 (color 0 is transparent)
close_reason: Superseded by bd-3j0w (types) + bd-909g (regs) under Epics bd-i4o, bd-xsl

title: Timed event bridge implementation (bus fabric to PPU)
description:
Implement the bridge that converts bus writes into TimedPpuEvent stream for the PPU.

LOCATION: Either in the bus fabric (src/bus/) or as a dedicated bridge entity.

RESPONSIBILITIES:
1. Detect CPU writes to PPU register range (0xFF40-0xFF4B)
2. Convert each write into TimedPpuEvent { seq, at: VideoCoord, kind: MmioWrite }
3. Detect DMA start (write to 0xFF46) and emit DmaStart event
4. Detect LCDC.7 transitions and emit ForceLcdPower event
5. Assign monotonically increasing seq numbers for same-dot ordering
6. Deliver events to ppu_core via the bus_events array port

VideoCoord GENERATION: The event bridge reads the current video coordinates from
the TIMEBASE entity (bd-7we8), NOT from the PPU timing FSM. The timebase provides
frame/line/dot counters that are the canonical video coordinate source. The PPU
timing FSM (bd-o9eu) is INTERNAL to the PPU and consumes the events that the
bridge produces -- the bridge must not depend on PPU-internal state.

LCDC.7 TRANSITION DETECTION: The bridge maintains a shadow register for LCDC.7
state. Since the bridge sees all bus writes to 0xFF40, it can detect transitions
by comparing old vs new LCDC values. This avoids a circular dependency (bridge
feeds PPU, PPU tells bridge its state).

SAME-DOT ORDERING: If multiple bus writes occur within the same dot (possible
if CPU writes are faster than dot rate), seq preserves their order.

INTEGRATION WITH EXISTING BUS FABRIC:
The bus fabric already decodes address regions. The event bridge taps into
writes targeting the IO region (0xFF00-0xFF7F) and filters for PPU addresses.
It should not change the existing bus fabric semantics for non-PPU addresses.

REPLAY CONTRACT: The TimedPpuEvent stream is the canonical replay format.
The same stream feeds DUT, Python reference model, and replay capsule artifacts.
This means the bridge output must be deterministic given the same bus activity.

TESTING: Unit test in test/unit/test_event_bridge.py covering:
- Correct VideoCoord generation from timebase counters
- MMIO write → TimedPpuEvent conversion for all PPU registers
- DMA start event on FF46 write
- ForceLcdPower event on LCDC.7 transition (shadow register)
- Monotonic seq numbering, including same-dot scenarios
- Non-PPU addresses are NOT converted to events

Use the established logging pattern (bd-dny):
  [SUITE] test_event_bridge.py
    [CASE] test_mmio_to_event
      [STEP] Write 0x91 to 0xFF40 (LCDC)
      [CHECK] event.kind: expected=MmioWrite actual=MmioWrite OK
      [CHECK] event.at.line: expected=0 actual=0 OK
      [CHECK] event.at.dot: expected=42 actual=42 OK

title: Green deterministic interrupt injection scenarios
description:
Create and green test/lockstep/test_interrupt_injection.py.

Scenarios (§16.5):
1. Deterministic interrupt injection around EI: inject IF bits at precise commit indices relative to EI execution
2. Deterministic interrupt injection around HALT: inject at various points before/during/after HALT
3. Interrupt injection around RETI: verify no double-service
4. Multiple simultaneous interrupts: verify priority ordering
5. Interrupt during multi-M-cycle instruction: verify instruction completes first
6. halt-after-ei corner cases (§16.5)
7. Pending IF injection via SimStimulus if_set_bits/if_clear_bits

All stimuli use the deterministic perturbation engine: seeded event scripts keyed by commit index. Same stream applied to both DUT and oracle (§16.5.1).

title: Author and green Wave C object ROMs plus dmg-acid2
description:
Write and green Wave C object test ROMs and the dmg-acid2 composition checksum.

OWNED ROMs:
1. OBJ_BASIC: Simple sprite at known position, verify rendering
2. OBJ_PRIORITY: Multiple overlapping sprites, verify DMG priority (smaller X wins)
3. OBJ_10_PER_LINE: 11+ sprites on one line, verify only 10 selected
4. OBJ_8x16: 8x16 sprite mode (LCDC.2), verify tile pairing and addressing
5. OBJ_FLIP: X-flip and Y-flip flags
6. OBJ_BG_MASK: BG-over-OBJ flag (sprite attribute bit 7) interaction
7. OBJ_X_HIDDEN_STILL_COUNTS: X=0 or X>=168 objects count toward 10 limit
8. OBJ_FETCH_CANCEL_LCDC1: Object fetch cancel when LCDC.1 toggled mid-line
9. OBJ_DMA_METADATA_CORRUPT: OAM DMA during rendering corrupts object metadata

EXTERNAL SUITE:
- dmg-acid2: First coarse whole-PPU composition checksum. Its README says a
  simple line renderer is sufficient and it is NOT a timing torture test.
  Use normalized grayscale comparison ($00, $55, $AA, $FF per dmg-acid2 spec).

ORACLE MODES:
- Owned ROMs: checkpoint (WRAM signature) + line semantic
- dmg-acid2: reference_image with explicit image_normalization

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone D): All owned OBJ ROMs green, object selection and draw
priority invariants green, dmg-acid2 green.

title: Finish lcdon_write_timing-GS external Wave A test
description: The external mooneye Wave A suite is green for vblank_stat_intr-GS, stat_lyc_onoff, stat_irq_blocking, and lcdon_timing-GS on the SoC Verilator path. lcdon_write_timing-GS is still opt-in only and times out end-to-end even after OAM/VRAM write results match the expected table. Remaining work: identify the post-write / per-case LCD disable-wait slowdown or semantic mismatch and make the test terminate with a pass signature.
acceptance_criteria: test_lcdon_write_timing_gs_mooneye_passes passes on the real SoC Verilator path without opt-in gating. The Wave A external suite can run all five cases by default. No unexplained timeout remains after the write-result buffer reaches the expected values.

title: Register write-enable discipline verification tests
description:
Implement explicit tests verifying the register write-enable discipline from §9.4: registers update ONLY on proper write-enable conditions, never spuriously.

RATIONALE (§9.4):
'The register file should not update because the phase changed. It should update only because: an instruction writeback occurred, PC/SP progressed, an interrupt state transition occurred, or reset happened. Every other cycle should hold its value.'

This discipline is both a CORRECTNESS property (spurious writes corrupt state) and a POWER property (unnecessary toggles waste energy). Testing it explicitly catches a class of bugs where the cpu_core entity accidentally updates registers it shouldn't.

TESTS TO IMPLEMENT (test/unit/test_write_enable.py):

1. NOP hold test:
   - Execute NOP instruction
   - Verify: ONLY PC changes (PC += 1). A, F, B, C, D, E, H, L, SP all hold exactly.
   - Verify: no register write-enable is asserted for non-PC registers

2. Targeted write test (per instruction family):
   - For each instruction, verify ONLY the documented target register changes
   - Example: LD B,0x42 should change B and PC only. A, F, C, D, E, H, L, SP unchanged.
   - Example: ADD A,B should change A, F, and PC only. B, C, D, E, H, L, SP unchanged.
   - Use opcode metadata (bd-36u) to generate test parameters

3. m_ce=0 hold test (simulation complement to formal bd-1yk):
   - Set m_ce=0 for N clock cycles
   - Verify: ALL registers hold their values exactly (including PC)
   - Verify: no write-enable signals are asserted
   - This is the behavioral complement to the formal property

4. Multi-cycle instruction hold test:
   - For multi-M-cycle instructions, verify that intermediate M-cycles only change the transient microstate, not architectural registers
   - Example: LD A,(HL) takes 2 M-cycles. During M-cycle 1 (fetch), only PC changes. During M-cycle 2 (read), A changes.

5. Duty cycle measurement:
   - Over a representative instruction sequence, measure what fraction of clock cycles each register's write-enable is asserted
   - Report as a table for power analysis baseline
   - Feed results to bd-2og (register write-enable duty cycle measurement)

LOGGING FORMAT (per test case):
  [CASE] test_nop_hold
    [STEP] State before: A=0x42 F=0xB0 B=0x00 ... PC=0x0100 SP=0xFFFE
    [STEP] Execute NOP (1 M-cycle)
    [CHECK] A held: 0x42 == 0x42 OK
    [CHECK] F held: 0xB0 == 0xB0 OK
    [CHECK] B held: 0x00 == 0x00 OK
    ...
    [CHECK] PC advanced: 0x0101 == 0x0101 OK
    [CHECK] SP held: 0xFFFE == 0xFFFE OK
    [PASS] test_nop_hold

ACCEPTANCE: All tests pass. Every single-byte instruction has at least one targeted write test. Duty cycle report generated.

title: ALU types: AluReq, AluResult
description:
Create src/cpu/alu.spade with ALU type definitions from §11.1 (stub implementation).

AluResult:
  struct AluResult { val8: uint<8>, val16: uint<16>, flags: Flags }

AluReq (§11.1) — exhaustive enum of all ALU operations:
  enum AluReq {
    Idle,                              // §11.2: operand isolation — inputs stay quiet
    Add8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>, carry_in: bool},
    Sub8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>, carry_in: bool},
    And8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Or8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Xor8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Cp8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Inc8{x: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Dec8{x: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Add16{a: uint<16>, b: uint<16>, z_preserve: bool},
    AddSpE8{sp: uint<16>, off: uint<8>},  // signed offset, tricky flags
    Daa{a: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Cpl{a: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Scf{f_prev: Flags},
    Ccf{f_prev: Flags},
    RotShift{...},                     // RLCA, RRCA, RLA, RRA, RLC, RRC, RL, RR, SLA, SRA, SRL, SWAP
    BitResSet{...},                    // BIT, RES, SET
  }

Stub implementation:
  fn alu(req: AluReq) -> AluResult  // Returns zeros for now

The Idle case is NOT aesthetics (§11.2). It ensures that when the CPU is not performing ALU work, the ALU input buses stay quiet and large adders do not see unrelated toggling. This is a power architecture decision.

title: Green ALU_FLAGS custom ROM
description:
Write the ALU_FLAGS custom test ROM and green it.

Test coverage:
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP with various operand values
- Focus on flag edge cases: zero results, half-carries, carries, borrows
- INC/DEC with carry preservation verification
- DAA after ADD and SUB sequences
- CPL, SCF, CCF flag behavior

Each test:
1. Set up registers to known state
2. Execute ALU instruction
3. Check result register AND flag register
4. Write pass/fail to WRAM signature block
5. Jump to __fail if any check fails

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: Sim: trace builder dedup, dead signals, commit_seq
description:
Clean up sim modules: deduplicate trace builder, remove dead signals, fix commit_seq double-compute.

## Scope
1. Extract common PpuDebugTrace builder from ppu_debug_trace / idle_trace_for_state in ppu_support.spade (~153-207). They share 16 of 19 fields. Helper builds common fields; each function overlays the 3-6 differing fields.

   - _ppu_vram_busy / _ppu_oam_busy (~lines 264-265): computed but unused. Remove.

3. Fix commit_seq double-compute in 3 test tops (trace_observe_top:~33/36, ppu_state_top:~43/46, semantic_observe_top:~53/56). Bind once with let, following the ppu_test_top pattern.

4. Clean up remaining dead bindings:

title: Create local smoke scripts for CI-free verification
description:
Create stable local entry-point scripts in tools/ that wrap the canonical runner and tool-specific checks. These scripts are a UX layer, not a second place to encode tier selection or oracle logic.

Scripts to create:
- tools/smoke.sh  — thin wrapper around tools/run_tests.py --tier smoke plus clean-checkout tool/version checks
- tools/regress.sh — thin wrapper around tools/run_tests.py --tier full|nightly
- tools/power.sh  — wrapper for the power/activity-capture path
- tools/formal.sh — wrapper for formal verification checks
- tools/oracle.sh — direct PyBoy oracle smoke path: init, reset, symbol lookup, step, snapshot/restore

Each script should:
- Check for required tool versions
- Exit with clear error messages if tools are missing
- Support a --dry-run flag for CI integration testing
- Delegate real tier selection to tools/run_tests.py and test/tiers.yaml instead of duplicating that logic

WHY THIS SHAPE: these scripts should remain thin, stable entry points for developers working from a clean checkout. That means they intentionally wait for the canonical runner, tier definitions, and oracle interface instead of inventing a parallel control plane.

title: Create shared sim encoder module
description:
Extract the ~15 duplicated encoder/helper functions from sim test tops into a shared module.

## Scope
Create src/sim/encode.spade (or similar) containing:
- cpu_bus_req(kind, addr, data) -> BusReq (currently in 4 files)
- encode_bus_region(region) -> uint<4> (2 files, 10-arm match)
- encode_bus_owner(owner) -> uint<3> (2 files)
- encode_ime(ime) -> uint<2> (2 files)

Then update cpu_test_top, soc_lockstep_top, soc_rom_top, ppu_power_top to import from shared module and delete local copies.

title: Green BG_SCROLL_SIGNED custom ROM
description:
Write and green BG_SCROLL_SIGNED.

Scope:
- Exercise SCX/SCY scrolling with signed-offset edge cases
- Verify wrap-around behavior and semantic pixel output
- Keep this bead separate from window and sprite concerns

Why split: scroll bugs should not block static-background completion or vice versa.
