title: Bus fabric unit tests with detailed address decode verification
description:
Create test/unit/test_bus_fabric.py -- comprehensive unit tests for the minimal bus fabric (bd-2sw) with detailed address decode verification.

RATIONALE: The bus fabric is the CPU's window to memory. Address decode bugs silently corrupt state: a write intended for WRAM lands in IO, a ROM read comes from HRAM. These bugs are invisible to register-level comparison and only show up as mysterious ROM failures. Testing every address boundary prevents this.

TESTS TO IMPLEMENT:

1. ADDRESS DECODE BOUNDARY TESTS:
   For each boundary in the memory map, test addresses on both sides:
   - 0x7FFF (ROM) vs 0x8000 (Vram/unmapped in Wave A)
   - 0x9FFF (Vram) vs 0xA000 (CartRam/unmapped)
   - 0xBFFF (CartRam) vs 0xC000 (WRAM)
   - 0xDFFF (WRAM) vs 0xE000 (Echo)
   - 0xFDFF (Echo) vs 0xFE00 (OAM)
   - 0xFE9F (OAM) vs 0xFEA0 (NotUsable)
   - 0xFEFF (NotUsable) vs 0xFF00 (IO)
   - 0xFF7F (IO) vs 0xFF80 (HRAM)
   - 0xFFFE (HRAM) vs 0xFFFF (IE)

2. ROM READ TESTS:
   - Load known ROM data, read back every byte, verify match
   - Read address 0x0000 (first byte) and 0x7FFF (last ROM byte)
   - Verify ROM is read-only (writes ignored)

3. WRAM READ/WRITE TESTS:
   - Write pattern to 0xC000-0xDFFF, read back, verify
   - Test first byte (0xC000) and last byte (0xDFFF)
   - Test write-then-read in same M-cycle
   - Test address wrapping behavior

4. HRAM READ/WRITE TESTS:
   - Write pattern to 0xFF80-0xFFFE, read back, verify
   - Test boundary bytes

5. IO STUB TESTS:
   - Read from any IO address returns 0xFF
   - Writes to IO addresses are accepted without error

6. BusObs VERIFICATION:
   - For each memory region, verify BusObs.region matches expected BusRegion
   - Verify BusObs.blocked is false for all Wave A accesses
   - Verify BusObs.owner is Cpu for all CPU-initiated accesses

7. IDLE BUS BEHAVIOR:
   - BusReq::Idle produces no memory access
   - BusResp returns 0xFF
   - Memory arrays not read (power: chip-select stays low)

LOGGING FORMAT:
  [SUITE] test_bus_fabric.py
    [CASE] test_address_decode_boundary_rom_vram
      [CHECK] addr=0x7FFF region=Rom OK
      [CHECK] addr=0x8000 region=Vram OK (unmapped in Wave A, returns 0xFF)
    [CASE] test_wram_write_read
      [STEP] Write 0xAA to 0xC000
      [STEP] Read from 0xC000
      [CHECK] data=0xAA OK
    ...

ACCEPTANCE: All boundary addresses tested. All memory regions return correct BusRegion in BusObs. Read/write roundtrip works for WRAM and HRAM. ROM is read-only.

title: Green Blargg cpu_instrs.gb external ROM
description:
Run Blargg cpu_instrs.gb (roms/cpu_instrs.gb) end-to-end in the iceboy simulation harness and verify all 11 sub-tests pass via serial output.

cpu_instrs.gb is the canonical SM83 CPU instruction test ROM. It is an MBC1 cartridge (64KB, 4 banks) that runs 11 independent sub-tests covering all CPU instruction groups. Results are reported via serial output (SB/SC writes). A reference capture already exists at bench/artifacts/cpu_instrs/pyboy_serial.txt:

  cpu_instrs
  01:ok  02:ok  03:ok  04:ok  05:ok  06:ok  07:ok  08:ok  09:ok  10:ok  11:ok
  Passed all tests

WHY THIS MATTERS:
This is the first external (non-custom) ROM validation milestone. Passing cpu_instrs.gb proves that CPU instruction semantics, memory bus, MBC1 bank switching, timer, interrupts, and serial all work together correctly on a real-world test suite not written by us. It is a prerequisite for confidence in running commercial ROMs.

PREREQUISITES (hardware RTL):
- MBC1 bank switching (bd-396, CLOSED) — cpu_instrs.gb uses banks 0-3
- Joypad register stub (bd-1ert) — ROM reads P1 during init
- CPU instructions (complete), timer (complete), serial (complete), interrupts (complete)

IMPLEMENTATION:

1. ROM inventory entry (bench/manifests/rom_inventory.yaml):
   Add a new entry for cpu_instrs.gb. Unlike custom ROMs, this ROM has no .sym file and no __pass/__fail labels. The pass condition must be serial-output-based, not label-based.

   - id: CPU_INSTRS_BLARGG
     path: roms/cpu_instrs.gb
     requires: [cpu, mbc1, joypad]
     oracle_mode: serial_terminal
     timeout_commits: 20000000  # 11 sub-tests, each runs many instructions
     pass_condition:
       kind: serial_substring
       expected_substring: "Passed all tests"
       fail_substrings: ["Failed"]
     compare_scope:
       domains: [serial_output]

2. Pass condition implementation:
   The existing rom_runner.py has run_dut_to_serial_capture() and run_soc_dut_to_serial_capture() which collect serial bytes. A new classifier function is needed:

   BLARGG_PASS_STRING = "Passed all tests"
   BLARGG_FAIL_MARKER = "Failed"

   def classify_blargg_serial_capture(capture: list[int] | tuple[int, ...]) -> str:
       text = bytes(capture).decode("ascii", errors="replace")
       if BLARGG_PASS_STRING in text:
           return "pass"
       if BLARGG_FAIL_MARKER in text:
           return "fail"
       return "incomplete"

3. Test file (test/rom/test_cpu_instrs_blargg.py):
   Following the pattern of test_mbc1_switch.py but using serial capture instead of signature comparison:

   @cocotb.test()
   async def test_cpu_instrs_blargg_all_pass(dut):
       driver = cpu_dut(dut)
       await driver.reset(ResetProfile.SkipBoot)
       rom_bytes = (ROOT / "roms" / "cpu_instrs.gb").read_bytes()
       state = await run_dut_to_serial_capture(
           driver,
           rom_bytes=rom_bytes,
           max_mcycles=20_000_000,
           min_capture_bytes=len("Passed all tests"),
       )
       text = bytes(state.capture).decode("ascii", errors="replace")
       assert "Passed all tests" in text, f"Blargg cpu_instrs serial output: {text!r}"

4. Optional SoC-level variant (test/rom/test_cpu_instrs_blargg_soc.py):
   Same test but using soc_lockstep_top / run_soc_dut_to_serial_capture for full SoC integration validation with PPU running.

VERIFICATION AGAINST PYBOY:
   Run cpu_instrs.gb in PyBoy and compare serial output against bench/artifacts/cpu_instrs/pyboy_serial.txt (already captured). Both should produce identical serial byte sequences.

TIMEOUT CONSIDERATIONS:
cpu_instrs.gb runs ~10-15 million M-cycles to completion (11 sub-tests). The timeout must be generous. If simulation is too slow, individual sub-test ROMs (cpu_instrs/01-cpu_instrs.gb through 11-cpu_instrs.gb) can be tested separately if available, but the aggregate ROM is the primary target.

TESTING:
1. Serial capture collects bytes and produces ASCII text
2. All 11 sub-tests report "ok" in serial output
3. Final line contains "Passed all tests"
4. No "Failed" substring appears in serial output
5. ROM completes within timeout (does not hang)
6. PyBoy reference output matches (compare against bench/artifacts/cpu_instrs/pyboy_serial.txt)

ACCEPTANCE:
- test/rom/test_cpu_instrs_blargg.py green
- Serial output matches bench/artifacts/cpu_instrs/pyboy_serial.txt byte-for-byte
- No simulation hangs or timeouts
- ROM inventory entry validated by tools/validate_rom_manifests.py

title: Epic: Bus Realism — Wave C (Phase 4.5)
description:
Implement realistic bus features: OAM DMA, memory banking (MBC1/MBC3), and bus ownership arbitration.

Components:
- OAM DMA controller: copies 160 bytes from source to OAM, CPU bus access restricted during transfer
- Bus ownership arbitration (§12.2.2): BusOwner enum {Cpu, OamDma, Ppu, Idle}
- DMA-era CPU accessibility restrictions per MemoryBehaviorProfile
- MBC1 mapper: ROM bank switching (5-bit bank), RAM bank switching (2-bit), mode select
- MBC3 mapper: ROM bank switching (7-bit bank), RAM bank switching + RTC registers

Wave C ROMs (§17.3):
- DMA_OAM_COPY: OAM DMA transfer correctness
- MBC1_SWITCH: ROM bank switching
- MBC1_RAM: external RAM enable/bank select
- MBC3_SWITCH: ROM bank switching with larger bank space
- MBC3_RAM: external RAM and RTC register access

RATIONALE: Bus realism is separated from CPU core (thesis: CPU should not directly know memory topology, §12.1). MBC mappers are the gateway to running real commercial ROMs. OAM DMA tests bus contention which is observable in real games. These are prerequisites for PPU work since PPU needs VRAM/OAM access arbitration.

ACCEPTANCE: All Wave C ROMs green. DMA transfer verified correct. Bank switching works for MBC1 and MBC3.

title: Object fetch path and OBJ FIFO unit tests (test/ppu/unit/test_obj_fetch.py)
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for the mode-3 object fetch path (bd-2ndl).

The object fetch path resolves SelectedObjTickets into pixel data through a
multi-step OAM+VRAM fetch sequence. It is as complex as the BG fetcher (which
has its own test bead bd-1oe7) and warrants dedicated unit tests.

TEST CASES:

1. BASIC OBJECT FETCH SEQUENCE:
   Place one sprite on the current scanline. Verify fetch steps:
   (a) Read tile number + flags from OAM (MemClient::ObjFetcher)
   (b) Compute tile row from Y position and flags
   (c) Read tile data low byte from VRAM
   (d) Read tile data high byte from VRAM
   (e) Decode 8 pixels and push to OBJ FIFO
   Log each PpuMemReq with address, client, and tag.

2. 8x16 TILE PAIRING:
   Set LCDC.2=1. Place sprite with tile_id=0x01. Verify:
   - Low bit of tile_id ignored (uses 0x00 for top, 0x01 for bottom)
   - Correct row addressing within each half-tile
   - Y position determines which half is fetched for this scanline

3. Y-FLIP IN 8x8 MODE:
   Place sprite with Y-flip flag set in 8x8 mode. Verify row_index
   is mirrored: row 0 becomes row 7, row 7 becomes row 0.

4. Y-FLIP IN 8x16 MODE:
   Place sprite with Y-flip flag set in 8x16 mode. Verify:
   - Row 0 becomes row 15
   - Tile halves are swapped (top tile becomes bottom and vice versa)
   - Row addressing within each half is also flipped

5. X-FLIP:
   Place sprite with X-flip flag set. Verify pixel array is reversed
   after tile decode (pixel 0 becomes pixel 7).

6. RESOLVED OBJ META OUTPUT:
   Verify ResolvedObjMeta fields are correctly populated:
   - ticket: matches input SelectedObjTicket
   - tile: from OAM fetch
   - flags: from OAM fetch (palette, flip, priority bits)
   - row_index: computed from (LY - (Y - 16)) with flip applied

7. EPOCH-BASED STALE RESPONSE DISCARD:
   Start object fetch at epoch N. Trigger fetch cancel (e.g., window
   activation). Verify epoch increments and in-flight OAM/VRAM responses
   from epoch N are discarded.

8. OBJECT FETCH PENALTIES:
   Place objects at various X positions. Verify that each object fetch
   adds penalty dots to mode 3. Verify the penalty varies based on
   where in the BG tile fetch cadence the object appears. Verify mode-3
   length EMERGES from the state machine (not a lookup table).

9. MULTIPLE OBJECTS ON SAME LINE:
   Place 3 objects at different X positions. Verify they are fetched in
   X-sorted order (smallest X first). Verify cumulative fetch penalties.

10. OBJ FIFO OVERWRITE SEMANTICS:
    Push two objects that overlap at the same pixel. Verify that OBJ FIFO
    overwrites transparent (color 0) pixels but does NOT overwrite opaque
    pixels from earlier objects (respecting DMG draw priority).

11. OBJECT AT X=0 (OFF-SCREEN LEFT):
    Place sprite at X=0. Verify it is fetched but its pixels are not
    visible (all clipped). Verify it still contributes fetch penalty.

12. FETCH CANCEL ON LCD DISABLE:
    Start object fetch. Disable LCD mid-fetch. Verify all pending fetches
    are abandoned and fetcher enters idle state.

LOGGING FORMAT:
  [SUITE] test_obj_fetch.py
    [CASE] test_basic_fetch_sequence
      [STEP] Place sprite: oam_index=0 x=40 y=32 tile=0x10 flags=0x00
      [STEP] Enter mode 3, advance to object fetch trigger at X=33
      [CHECK] OAM read issued: addr=0xFE00 client=ObjFetcher OK
      [STEP] Provide OAM response: tile=0x10 flags=0x00
      [CHECK] VRAM read issued: addr=0x8100 (tile 0x10, row 0, low) OK
      [STEP] Provide VRAM lo response
      [CHECK] VRAM read issued: addr=0x8101 (tile 0x10, row 0, high) OK
      [STEP] Provide VRAM hi response
      [CHECK] Pixels decoded: [3,2,1,0,0,1,2,3] OK
      [CHECK] OBJ FIFO updated with 8 pixels OK
      [PASS] test_basic_fetch_sequence (0.05s)

FAILURE DIAGNOSTICS:
On failure, log: current dot, fetch step, epoch, pending read tags,
memory request addresses, SelectedObjTicket fields, ResolvedObjMeta fields,
OBJ FIFO contents, mode-3 dot counter, BG fetcher cadence position.
close_reason: Expanded test_obj_fetch helper suite with 8x16 pairing, 8x8 Y-flip, X-flip, overwrite semantics, X=0 clipping, and cancel coverage; integrated timing/penalty cases tracked separately

title: test_decode_completeness.py scaffold
description:
Create test/unit/test_decode_completeness.py (§16.2, Phase 1 step 6).

This test verifies that the decoder maps EVERY opcode to a valid DecodedOp:
- All 256 unprefixed opcodes (0x00-0xFF)
- All 256 CB-prefixed opcodes (0xCB00-0xCBFF)
- No opcode maps to an 'unknown' or 'invalid' variant unless it genuinely is (like 0xD3, 0xDB, 0xDD, 0xE3, 0xE4, 0xEB, 0xEC, 0xED, 0xF4, 0xFC, 0xFD on SM83)

Initially this test should be a scaffold that:
1. Imports the opcode metadata from spec/sm83_opcodes.py
2. For each opcode, calls the decoder and checks the result
3. FAILS until the decoder is implemented (red semantic test, not infrastructure failure)

The test should be driven from the canonical opcode metadata (§8.0), not handwritten opcode lists.

title: Epic: Spade logic simplification
description:
Systematic cleanup of the iceboy Spade HDL codebase to reduce duplication, remove dead code, and improve maintainability before the PPU implementation grows further.

## Background

iceboy is a Game Boy emulator implemented in Spade HDL, targeting the iCE40 UP5K FPGA. The UP5K has very tight resources (~5280 LUTs, 8 EBR blocks, 4 SPRAM blocks), so every redundant mux, dead signal, and duplicated logic path directly impacts synthesis area. The codebase has grown organically through CPU bring-up, peripheral implementation, and early PPU work. A comprehensive review identified ~45 individual findings across all src/ subtrees.

## Why now

1. **PPU is actively growing**: The timing, fetcher, FIFO, and rendering modules are seeing frequent changes. Struct-spread boilerplate means every new field requires touching 10+ constructor sites. Duplicated helpers in test tops means every new test copy-pastes from an old one.

2. **CPU semantics reached ~2300 lines**: Repeated MicroOutput assembly and CpuDelta construction patterns make the file harder to navigate and more error-prone to modify.

3. **Synthesis budget pressure**: Dead signals, no-op functions, and unreachable match arms all synthesize to logic that consumes LUTs. Cleaning them up directly supports the UP5K area budget.

4. **Test infrastructure duplication**: ~200 lines of encoder/helper functions are duplicated across 8+ test/sim tops. Adding a new test top currently requires finding and copying the right set of helpers.

## Architecture

The findings are organized into 12 task beads grouped by subsystem:
- Cross-cutting utilities (shared modules for test/sim helpers)
- CPU (4 beads: boilerplate, types, match dedup, minor quality)
- Bus (1 bead: dead functions, region decode, event bridge)
- PPU (4 beads: timing dedup, FIFO compression, quality cleanup)
- Peripherals (1 bead: io_write dedup, dead code, logic simplification)
- Memory (1 bead: SPRAM entity dedup)
- Video/sim (1 bead: frame_sink, trace builder, dead signals)

## Dependency graph

Phase 1 (independent, can run in parallel):
  bd-21p5 (shared utils) | bd-2juf (CPU micro) | bd-3fcu (PPU timing) | bd-30sx (PPU FIFO) | bd-pofr (SPRAM)

Phase 2 (after Phase 1 completes):
  bd-7vgj (bus) ← bd-21p5
  bd-154v (periph) ← bd-21p5
  bd-1kfm (video/sim) ← bd-21p5
  bd-2gis (CPU types) ← bd-2juf
  bd-1mto (PPU quality) ← bd-3fcu

Phase 3 (after Phase 2):
  bd-1icj (CPU match) ← bd-2gis
  bd-1ah3 (CPU minor) ← bd-2gis

## Key considerations

- Spade lacks struct update syntax (no ..rest spread), so some boilerplate is inherent to the language. The goal is to minimize it with helpers, not eliminate it entirely.
- Some "no-op" functions (canonical_req, blocked_read_data) may exist for synthesis reasons. Investigate before removing; document if kept intentionally.
- Changes to ALU and decode tables are correctness-critical. These require full regression testing including Blargg cpu_instrs ROM.
- FIFO push compression changes expression structure, which could affect synthesis QoR. Compare area reports before/after.
- All changes must pass swim tests, synth gate, and (where applicable) Blargg ROM tests.

## Estimated savings
~800-1000 lines of duplicated/dead code removed. Synthesis area savings TBD but expected to be measurable from dead signal removal.
notes:
## Full bead inventory

### Phase 1 — Independent (can run in parallel)
- bd-21p5: Extract shared test/sim encoder utilities [P2]
  - bd-ng30: Create shared utility primitives (bit, io_write)
  - bd-2a4m: Create shared sim encoder module ← bd-ng30
  - bd-4fyn: Consolidate PPU test helpers ← bd-ng30
  - bd-1z29: Consolidate periph + video test helpers ← bd-ng30

- bd-2juf: CPU MicroOutput and Delta boilerplate helpers [P2]

- bd-3fcu: PPU timing dedup [P2]
  - bd-3a0s: Dedup set_lcd_enable + unify render state init
  - bd-33eo: Extract advance_timing shared phase helper ← bd-3a0s
  - bd-3q26: Collapse identical WarmupBlankFrame/Running arms ← bd-33eo

- bd-30sx: PPU FIFO push_bg_row and oam_scan append compression [P2]

- bd-pofr: SPRAM entity dedup [P2]

### Phase 2 — After Phase 1
- bd-7vgj: Bus cleanup ← bd-21p5 [P3]
  - bd-1eji: Remove dead/identity/redundant functions
  - bd-1ugz: Refactor region decode and write helpers ← bd-1eji
  - bd-1twb: Consolidate event bridge ← bd-1ugz

- bd-154v: Periph cleanup ← bd-21p5 [P3]
  - bd-3mwr: Dead code and redundant guards
  - bd-3pyf: ack_bits, serial state, if-else flattening

- bd-1kfm: Video/sim cleanup ← bd-21p5 [P3]
  - bd-21bm: frame_sink dedup, access tautology, dead bindings
  - bd-2940: trace builder dedup, dead signals, commit_seq

- bd-2gis: CPU type consolidation ← bd-2juf [P2]

- bd-1mto: PPU quality ← bd-3fcu [P3]
  - bd-2vcr: FetcherState builders + dead code removal
  - bd-lbav: apply_mmio_write helpers, no-op mux, minor cleanups ← bd-2vcr

### Phase 3 — After Phase 2
- bd-1icj: CPU match pyramid dedup ← bd-2gis [P2]
- bd-1ah3: CPU minor quality ← bd-2gis [P3]

## Totals
- 1 epic + 12 tasks + 16 subtasks = 29 beads
- Estimated: ~800-1000 lines removed
- Critical path: bd-21p5 → bd-7vgj or bd-2juf → bd-2gis → bd-1icj

title: Test epic
description: test

title: CPU type consolidation: Imm16HiCont, RotShiftKind, add8/sub8
description:
Three areas of the CPU type/ALU system carry redundant definitions. Consolidating them removes ~50 lines and, more importantly, reduces the surface area for decode/ALU bugs.

## Background

The CPU's type system evolved incrementally as instructions were implemented. Some patterns that made sense during initial bring-up (separate accumulator vs CB-prefixed rotation variants, separate add vs adc functions, mirror enums for multi-cycle phases) are now pure redundancy. The decode table, ALU, and semantics all carry the cost of these extra variants.

## Scope

### 1. Eliminate Imm16HiCont enum (types.spade:~82-89, semantics.spade:~1585-1591)
Imm16HiCont is a mirror of Imm16Cont where every variant gains an extra lo: uint<8> field. It exists because Phase::ReadImm16Hi needs to carry the low byte read in the previous M-cycle. The handle_read_imm16_lo handler mechanically translates Imm16Cont → Imm16HiCont in a 6-arm match that just threads lo through.

Fix: Move lo into the Phase variant itself:
  ReadImm16Hi { lo: uint<8>, k: Imm16Cont }
This eliminates the entire Imm16HiCont enum definition and the 6-arm translation match. The hi handler destructures Phase::ReadImm16Hi to get both lo and the original continuation.

Consideration: Verify that adding lo to Phase::ReadImm16Hi does not increase the Phase enum's bit width beyond what the register file can handle. (It should not — lo was already being stored in the Imm16HiCont variant, which is part of Phase.)

### 2. Collapse RotShiftKind accumulator variants (alu.spade:~16-29, decode.spade)
RotShiftKind has 12 variants: Rlca/Rrca/Rla/Rra (accumulator) + Rlc/Rrc/Rl/Rr (CB-prefixed) + Sla/Sra/Swap/Srl. The first four are exact duplicates of the second four — in rot_shift_result (alu.spade:~371-386), Rlca and Rlc both call rlc8_result with the same arguments. The only semantic difference (zero flag forced to 0 for accumulator forms) is already captured by the zero_on_result parameter passed to rot_shift_result, NOT by the enum variant.

Fix: Remove Rlca/Rrca/Rla/Rra variants. Update decode.spade to emit Rlc/Rrc/Rl/Rr for both accumulator and CB-prefixed forms. The zero_on_result flag (derived from whether the instruction is prefixed) handles the behavioral difference.

Consideration: The decode table currently uses the accumulator variant names as a signal that "this is an unprefixed instruction." After consolidation, that signal comes from the BitOp's prefix field (or lack thereof). Verify the decode table still correctly sets zero_on_result.

### 3. Inline add8_result / sub8_result into adc8/sbc8 (alu.spade:~79-108)
add8_result(a, b) === adc8_result(a, b, false). sub8_result(a, b) === sbc8_result(a, b, false). The caller at line ~437 already branches on carry_in to choose between them.

Fix: Remove add8_result and sub8_result (~30 lines). Use adc8_result(a, b, carry_in) and sbc8_result(a, b, carry_in) unconditionally. The carry_in=false case produces identical logic.

Consideration: For synthesis, a dedicated non-carry adder might produce slightly simpler logic (no carry-in mux). This is unlikely to matter for an 8-bit operation on UP5K, but verify by comparing synthesis area reports before and after. If area regresses, keep the separate functions but add a comment explaining the intentional duplication.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Imm16HiCont enum is gone; Phase::ReadImm16Hi carries lo + Imm16Cont directly
2. handle_read_imm16_lo no longer has a 6-arm Imm16Cont → Imm16HiCont translation match
3. RotShiftKind has 8 variants (Rlc, Rrc, Rl, Rr, Sla, Sra, Swap, Srl)
4. Decode table updated; zero_on_result correctly set for accumulator-form instructions
5. add8_result and sub8_result removed (or documented with synthesis justification if kept)
6. No behavioral change in any instruction

## Test Plan
- All CPU swim tests pass, especially semantics_alu_test_top and semantics_cb_test_top
- Blargg cpu_instrs ROM test passes (exercises all ALU + rotation instructions)
- Formal invariants pass
- Compare synthesis area report before/after (particularly ALU + decode area)

title: Video backend adapter (src/video/backend_adapter.spade)
description:
Implement the backend adapter that maps granted memory requests to physical storage.

This replaces the old video_mem_adapter concept with a cleaner separation from
access policy.

BACKENDS:
1. Simulation-array: Python-side arrays for cocotb tests (fastest iteration)
2. Inferred-sync-RAM: Yosys/nextpnr infers synchronous RAM from Spade code
3. Explicit-primitive: Direct iCE40 SPRAM/EBR instantiation (future)

The PPU should NOT know which backend is active. It emits PpuMemReq and consumes
PpuMemResp through the same protocol regardless of storage technology.

VRAM (8KB): On iCE40 UP5K, fits in 1 SPRAM block (16Kx4 = 8KB per block, 4 available).
OAM (160 bytes): Small enough for EBR inference (256x8 = 256 bytes per EBR).

The adapter handles:
- Address translation (PPU logical address to storage physical address)
- Read latency (1 cycle for synchronous RAM inference)
- Response tagging (PpuMemResp carries the same MemTxnTag as the request)
- Idle state (no access: hold chip selects low, no address recomputation)

DIFFERENTIAL TESTING: The mandatory backend-differential lane runs regressions
across both simulation-array and inferred-sync-RAM backends. Any divergence
proves the protocol was underspecified.

title: Green live Wave B.5 mealybug canary subset
CPU MicroOutput and Delta boilerplate helpers
description:
The CPU semantics.spade file (~2300 lines) has massive repetition in two structural patterns: MicroOutput construction and CpuDelta boilerplate. Extracting helpers reduces ~90 lines and, more importantly, makes the semantic intent of each handler clearer.

## Background

Every CPU micro-operation handler (handle_fetch, handle_read_imm8, handle_execute, etc.) follows the same tail pattern: compute a CpuDelta, build a BusReq, wrap them in a MicroOutput with a commit record. The MicroOutput assembly is always the same except for the opcode field. Similarly, the "branch not taken" CpuDelta (idle registers, no PC/SP/IME/halt writes, phase = Fetch) appears verbatim ~6 times across conditional branch/call/return handlers.

This repetition is not just a readability issue — it is a correctness risk. If the MicroOutput construction needs to change (e.g., adding a new sideband field), every handler must be updated individually. A helper centralizes that.

## Scope

### 1. MicroOutput assembly helper (~15 call sites)
Every handler ends with:
  let commit = make_commit(state, input, bus_req, delta, opcode_or_none);
  MicroOutput\$(delta: delta, bus_req: bus_req, irq_ack: no_irq_ack(), commit: Option::Some(commit), sideband: Option::None)

Extract:
  fn standard_micro_output(state: CpuState, input: MicroInput, bus_req: BusReq, delta: CpuDelta, opcode: Option<uint<8>>) -> MicroOutput

The single non-standard case is service_entry_output which uses a non-zero irq_ack. Create a variant:
  fn micro_output_with_irq_ack(state, input, bus_req, delta, opcode, irq_ack) -> MicroOutput

Affected functions: handle_fetch (3 return paths), handle_fetch_prefix, handle_halted (2 paths), handle_read_imm8, handle_read_imm16_lo, handle_read_imm16_hi, handle_read_mem, handle_write_mem, handle_execute, handle_passthrough, handle_service_interrupt.

### 2. Branch-not-taken delta helper (~6 call sites)
The pattern:
  CpuDelta\$(regs_write: idle_reg_write_set(), pc_write: Option::None, sp_write: Option::None, ime_write: Option::None, halt_write: Option::None, micro_write: MicroWriteSet\$(phase: some_phase(Phase::Fetch), decoded: Option::None, temp: Option::None, imm_lo: Option::None))

Extract:
  fn fetch_phase_delta() -> CpuDelta
(Name chosen to distinguish from existing delta_with_micro and phase_only_delta helpers.)

Affected sites: conditional relative jump, conditional absolute jump, conditional call, conditional return, and similar branching handlers.

### 3. handle_fetch duplicated fetch path (semantics.spade:~1279-1303)
Two match arms (ImeState::Enabled when no IRQ pending, and the catch-all _) contain identical 5-line fetch code:
  let opcode = input.bus_resp.data;
  let bus_req = BusReq::Read\$(addr: state.arch.regs.pc);
  let delta = fetch_delta(state, opcode);
  let commit = ...;
  MicroOutput\$(...);

Restructure: check for interrupt service entry first, then fall through to the common fetch path. This eliminates the duplicated block.

## Considerations
- The existing helpers delta_with_micro and phase_only_delta should remain. The new helpers complement them for the most common patterns. Audit whether any existing call sites of delta_with_micro can be replaced with the new helpers.
- Verify that fetch_phase_delta covers all the "not taken" sites exactly. Some may have subtle differences in imm_lo or temp fields — inspect each carefully before consolidating.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. standard_micro_output exists and is used at all ~15 call sites
2. fetch_phase_delta (or similar) exists and is used at all ~6 branch-not-taken sites
3. handle_fetch has exactly one copy of the fetch-path code
4. No behavioral change to any CPU micro-operation

## Test Plan
- All CPU swim tests pass: semantics_alu_test_top, semantics_cb_test_top, semantics_load_test_top, semantics_flow_test_top, semantics_misc_test_top, semantics_wordalu_test_top, semantics_interrupt_test_top, write_enable_test_top
- cpu_test_top passes
- Formal invariants (formal_invariants_top) pass
- Blargg cpu_instrs ROM test passes
- soc_lockstep_top lockstep test passes

title: Interrupt service phase machine (ServiceInterrupt)
description:
Implement the interrupt service sequence as a first-class phase (§8.1).

Phase:
  Phase::ServiceInterrupt { irq: IrqSel, subphase: IrqPhase }

Interrupt service sequence (5 M-cycles total):
1. Internal delay (2 M-cycles of bus idle)
2. Push PC high byte to stack (SP-1)
3. Push PC low byte to stack (SP-2)
4. Set PC to interrupt vector address

Interrupt vectors:
- VBlank:   0x0040
- LCD STAT: 0x0048
- Timer:    0x0050
- Serial:   0x0058
- Joypad:   0x0060

During service:
- IME set to Disabled
- The corresponding IF bit is cleared (via IrqAck to peripheral layer)
- SP decremented by 2

Priority: lower bit number = higher priority. If multiple interrupts pending, service the lowest-numbered one first.

The IrqPhase subphase enum tracks progress through the multi-M-cycle service sequence.

title: Green BG_STATIC, BG_SCROLL_SIGNED, PPU_WINDOW, PPU_SPRITES ROMs
description:
Write and green the Wave D1 visual test ROMs (§17.4).

BG_STATIC:
- Load a known tile pattern and tilemap
- Verify rendered pixels match expected output
- Use semantic tilemap/tile data checks, NOT frame hash alone

BG_SCROLL_SIGNED:
- Test background scrolling with SCX/SCY
- Include signed offset arithmetic edge cases
- Verify wrap-around behavior

PPU_WINDOW:

- Verify window overlays background correctly
- Test window enable/disable via LCDC bit 5

PPU_SPRITES:
- Test sprite rendering with various attributes
- Verify priority ordering (X position, OAM index)
- Test flip flags
- Test 8x16 sprite mode
- Test BG priority flag
- Test sprite transparency (color 0)

Oracle mode: frame_semantic first, then frame_hash as coarsest checksum (§17.4)

title: Epic: Timer & Interrupt System — Wave B (Phase 4)
description:
Implement timer, interrupt controller, and all interrupt-related CPU behaviors. Bring Wave B ROMs to green.

Timer (§12.4 Wave B):
- DIV register (free-running 16-bit counter, upper 8 bits readable, write resets to 0)
- TIMA/TMA/TAC: configurable timer with overflow interrupt
- Timer derives from shared timebase (§6.1.1), NOT private local counter
- Timer falling-edge detection for obscure increment timing

Interrupts:
- IE/IF registers live in peripheral/io side, NOT inside CPU (§12.3)
- CPU receives narrow pending vector, emits acknowledge
- IME state machine: Disabled → PendingEnable → Enabled (§8.3)
- EI delay: exactly one instruction later (§16.5 invariant)
- RETI: return + enable interrupts atomically
- ServiceInterrupt phase with proper architectural sequence (§8.1)
- HALT: Running/Halted/HaltBugPending (§8.3) — HaltBugPending can be deferred but type must accommodate it

Wave B ROMs (§17.2):
- TIMER_DIV_BASIC: DIV counting, reset-on-write, TAC prescaler selection
- EI_DELAY: EI enables interrupts one instruction late
- TIMER_IRQ_HALT: timer overflow fires IRQ, wakes from HALT

Additional verification:
- Deterministic interrupt injection scenarios (§16.5)
- halt-after-ei corner cases
- HALT wake behavior matches pending IE & IF
- Interrupt service consumes correct architectural sequence
- JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST: joypad input divergence with persistent state (requires SimStimulus joyp_buttons)

RATIONALE: Interrupts and timers are the second-highest source of subtle CPU bugs after ALU flags. The plan (§8.3) insists they be first-class phase states, not conditionals buried in fetch. Making IE/IF external to the CPU (§12.3) is a modularity decision that simplifies later PPU/timer integration. The shared timebase (§6.1.1) prevents timer/CPU/DMA blocks from drifting by one cycle.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone D): EI_DELAY and TIMER_IRQ_HALT green. Live instruction-commit differential green on Wave B. Strict M-cycle differential green on targeted subsets. Every interrupt source observed in at least one green test. HALT wake confirmed.

title: PPU simulation tops (ppu_test_top, lockstep, semantic/trace observe)
description:
Create dedicated simulation-only Spade tops for PPU verification.

FILES:
- src/sim/ppu_test_top.spade: minimal PPU test harness for unit tests
- src/sim/semantic_observe_top.spade: sim_semantic flavor, emits PpuSemanticCommit
- src/sim/trace_observe_top.spade: sim_trace flavor, emits full PpuDebugTrace
- src/sim/ppu_state_top.spade: state import/export for replay and minimization

NOTE: System-level lockstep integration (PPU into soc_lockstep_top) is handled
by bd-qygd, NOT by this bead.

IMPORTANT ARCHITECTURAL DECISION — ppu_test_top wraps step_dot DIRECTLY:

ppu_test_top does NOT instantiate ppu_core. Instead, it:
1. Wraps step_dot with its own register bank (same pattern as ppu_core)
2. Adds stub memory backend (simulation arrays, not real VRAM/OAM)
3. Adds extra test-observability ports (dot_in_line, phase, full state export)
4. Adds register write injection port (bypasses event bridge for direct testing)

WHY NOT ppu_core? This avoids a dependency on bd-gaf0, allowing the test
infrastructure to be built in PARALLEL with ppu_core. Both ppu_test_top and
ppu_core call step_dot independently. When gaf0 fills in step_dot with
timing/STAT logic, ppu_test_top automatically sees the updated behavior.

This means:
- bd-2mxk depends on bd-2r31 (step_dot stub) — NOT on bd-gaf0
- bd-3k2o (test infra) depends on bd-2mxk — NOT on bd-gaf0
- Test beads (bd-w6xs etc.) depend on bd-gaf0 to ensure step_dot is filled in

The test beads' dependency on gaf0 guarantees that when tests RUN, step_dot
has been composed with timing/STAT helpers. But the test INFRASTRUCTURE can
be set up earlier.

ppu_test_top: Wraps step_dot with registers and stub memory backend. Exposes:
  - dot_ce input, register write port, memory response port
  - All DotOutput fields as output ports
  - Extra: dot_in_line, line_index, phase enum, full PpuState dump
  Used for unit testing individual PPU behaviors in isolation.

semantic_observe_top: step_dot wrapper with PpuSemanticCommit output ports.
  This is the primary differential regression surface for PPU-specific tests.

trace_observe_top: Adds full PpuDebugTrace (fetcher state, FIFO contents,
  mode history, memory request log) for waveform-level debugging.

ppu_state_top: Adds PpuSimSnapshot import/export sideband for:
  - Mid-frame state injection (deterministic replay from snapshot)
  - State capture at arbitrary points (minimization)
  - Checkpoint-based test acceleration

All tops must have stable, Verilog-friendly port names for Cocotb.

title: Object fetch path and OBJ FIFO (ResolvedObjMeta, fetch epochs)
description:
Implement the mode-3 object fetch path that resolves tickets into pixel data.

For each SelectedObjTicket whose X position intersects the current pixel column:
1. Fetch tile number and flags from OAM (PpuMemReq with MemClient::ObjFetcher)
2. Compute correct tile row (accounting for 8x16 mode, Y-flip)
3. Fetch tile data low and high bytes from VRAM
4. Decode into 8 pixel entries, apply X-flip if flagged
5. Push into OBJ FIFO

OUTPUT: ResolvedObjMeta { ticket, tile, flags, row_index }
  Then: decoded pixels pushed to OBJ FIFO

OBJ FIFO:
- Holds pixel data for the current object fetch
- Mixed with BG FIFO at the mixer
- Objects dont stack in the FIFO -- they overwrite transparent slots

EPOCH: Object fetches use the FetcherState epoch. A fetch cancel (e.g., window
activation during an object fetch) increments the epoch and discards any in-flight
memory responses from the old epoch.

OBJECT FETCH PENALTIES: Each object fetch adds penalty dots to mode 3. The
exact penalty depends on where in the tile fetch cadence the object appears.
These penalties EMERGE from the state machine, not from a lookup table.

DMA INTERACTION: If OAM DMA is active during mode-3 object fetch, the fetched
metadata may be corrupted. This behavior is localized to THIS path, not to the
OAM scan path (stage 1). DMA realism is staged for Wave E.
close_reason: Implemented object-fetch helper path, OBJ FIFO overlay, epoch cancel helper, and hook-covered focused unit suite

title: PPU DMA-rendering interaction logic
description:
Implement the interaction between OAM DMA and PPU rendering.

The OAM DMA controller already exists (src/periph/oam_dma.spade). This task
adds the PPU-side interaction logic.

BEHAVIORS:
1. DMA + Mode 2: OAM DMA owns OAM bus. PPU OAM scan reads corrupted/stale data.
   Objects may disappear or show wrong attributes.

2. DMA + Mode 3: OAM DMA owns OAM bus. PPU object fetch reads corrupted metadata.
   This is why OBJ_DMA_METADATA_CORRUPT exists as a test ROM.

3. CPU HRAM-only restriction: Already partially implemented in bus fabric. Verify
   CPU can only access HRAM (0xFF80-0xFFFE) during DMA. Non-HRAM reads return
   0xFF per DmgConservative behavior profile.

4. DMA transfer timing: 160 M-cycles (640 dots). DMA and rendering can overlap
   for multiple scanlines.

WAVE E ROMs:
- DMA_MODE2_HIDE: Verify object disappearance when DMA active during mode 2
- DMA_MODE3_FETCH_GLITCH: Verify metadata corruption when DMA active during mode 3
- DMA_OBJ_DROP_LINES: Verify objects drop on lines where DMA overlaps OAM scan

STAGING: Exact mode-3 DMA fetch glitch path is optional and should only be
implemented if required by chosen suites or owned ROMs. The basic DMA interaction
(mode-2 object disappearance) is the primary target.

title: PyBoy PPU frame/semantic oracle extensions
description:
Extend bench/pyboy/oracle.py with PPU-specific semantic observation surfaces.

PyBoy is Layer 2 of the oracle architecture: a frame/semantic oracle, NOT a
dot-step oracle. Its public API exposes screen buffers, tilemaps, sprite
wrappers, tile imagery, tilemap_position_list, and save/load state.

NEW CAPABILITIES:
1. Frame buffer capture: screen ndarray (144, 160, 4) for frame comparison
2. BG tilemap observation: tilemap_background for tile-level semantic checks
3. Window tilemap observation: tilemap_window
4. Sprite observation: sprite wrappers for attribute verification
5. Per-scanline scroll/window capture: tilemap_position_list for (SCX,SCY,WX,WY)
6. Checkpoint-based testing: save/load state for fast replay
7. Shade buffer normalization: convert PyBoy RGBA to canonical DMG shades
   ($00, $55, $AA, $FF per dmg-acid2 spec)

ORACLE INTERFACE UPDATE:
The VideoOracle trait should be updated to use BehaviorConfig instead of
behavior_profile:
  reset(model_profile, reset_profile, video_start_profile, behavior_config)

NORMALIZATION: All frame comparisons should use normalized logical shade data
as the stable currency. PyBoy buffer format is explicitly subject to change.
Raw RGBA output exists only for artifact generation and suite-specific image
checks (dmg-acid2).

COMPARE MODES:
- frame_semantic: tilemap/scroll/sprite semantic comparison
- frame_hash: shade buffer hash comparison
- reference_image: dmg-acid2-style normalized image comparison
- scanline_semantic: per-line LineSummary comparison

LIMITATIONS: PyBoy hooks are executable-code hooks, not generic memory probes
or dot oracles. Never use hooks on non-executable data or as the primary
mechanism for raster-timing injection.

title: Register write-enable duty cycle measurement
description:
Implement always-on logical quiescence metrics (§18.7 tier 1).

Metrics to track:
1. Register write-enable duty cycle: for each register (A,F,B,C,D,E,H,L,SP,PC), count cycles where write-enable is asserted vs total cycles
2. Bus-active duty cycle: fraction of cycles where BusReq != Idle
3. ALU-active duty cycle: fraction of cycles where AluReq != Idle
4. Halt quiescence ratio: fraction of halted cycles with truly zero activity

Implementation:
- Add counters to simulation tops (NOT hardware tops!)
- Report duty cycles after each test run
- Flag anomalies: e.g., PC write-enable > 50% is suspicious, register write-enable during HALT is a bug

These metrics are the 'canary in the coal mine' for power regressions. Track them in CI.

title: E2E milestone gate: PPU Timing/Control ready (gate_ppu_timing.sh)
description:
Create tools/gate_ppu_timing.sh -- acceptance gate for PPU Milestone B (Timing/Control).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (history/ppu.md section 22, Milestone B):
1. All 5 Wave A owned ROMs green (PPU_OFF_ON_BASIC, LY_LYC_BASIC, STAT_MODE_SEQ,
   VBLANK_IRQ_BASIC, VRAM_OAM_GATING)
2. All 5 mooneye timing tests green (vblank_stat_intr-GS, stat_lyc_onoff,
   stat_irq_blocking, lcdon_timing-GS, lcdon_write_timing-GS)
3. PPU mode FSM unit tests green (test_ppu_modes.py)
4. STAT/LYC/IRQ unit tests green (test_stat_irq.py)
5. PPU invariant tests green (test_ppu_invariants.py -- timing subset)
6. Video access policy unit tests green (test_access_policy.py)
7. No unexplained LY/mode/STAT divergence in lockstep comparison

SCRIPT STRUCTURE:
  #!/bin/bash
  set -euo pipefail
  echo "=== PPU MILESTONE B GATE: Timing/Control Ready ==="

  echo "--- Criterion 1: Wave A owned ROMs ---"
  python tools/run_tests.py --filter=PPU_OFF_ON_BASIC,LY_LYC_BASIC,...

  echo "--- Criterion 2: mooneye timing tests ---"
  python tools/run_tests.py --filter=vblank_stat_intr,stat_lyc,...

  echo "--- Criterion 3: Mode FSM unit tests ---"
  python -m pytest test/ppu/unit/test_ppu_modes.py -v

  echo "--- Criterion 4: STAT/LYC unit tests ---"
  python -m pytest test/ppu/unit/test_stat_irq.py -v

  echo "--- Criterion 5: PPU invariants ---"
  python -m pytest test/ppu/unit/test_ppu_invariants.py -v -k timing

  echo "--- Criterion 6: Access policy unit tests ---"
  python -m pytest test/ppu/unit/test_access_policy.py -v

  echo "=== PPU MILESTONE B: PASSED ==="

LOGGING: Each criterion produces [GATE PASS] or [GATE FAIL]. On failure,
show the specific failing test with diagnostic output. Include wall-clock
time per criterion for performance tracking.

EXIT CODE: 0 if all criteria pass, 1 if any fail. Script stops at first
failing criterion with diagnostic dump.

title: Bus types: BusReq, BusResp, BusObs, BusOwner, BusRegion
Decode types: DecodedOp, opcode enums
description:
Create src/cpu/decode_types.spade with instruction decode output types.

DecodedOp should capture:
- Operation kind (load, alu, control flow, CB-prefix, misc)
- Source and destination operands (register, immediate, memory via register pair)
- ALU operation kind for ALU instructions
- Condition code for conditional operations (NZ, Z, NC, C)
- Addressing mode

The exact enum shape will be refined during decoder implementation, but the type should:
1. Cover all SM83 instruction families
2. Be driven by the canonical opcode metadata (§8.0)
3. Support the continuation-based phase machine (the decoder output feeds into Phase transitions)

This is a stub — the actual decode logic comes in Phase 3a.

title: PPU equivalence checking for refactors
description:
Extend the equivalence checking framework (bd-mos) for PPU refactors.

WHEN TO USE:
- Fetcher restructuring (e.g., pipeline insertion in fetch path)
- Mixer rewrites (e.g., optimized priority resolution)
- Encoding changes (e.g., binary to one-hot for phase enum)
- Any timing-visible refactor

APPROACH: Any timing-visible refactor must satisfy ONE of:
1. An equivalence check on the affected cut
2. A bounded formal proof PLUS unchanged differential replay on the affected window

TOOL: yosys + EQY equivalence checker or miter-based approach with SymbiYosys.
- Take two Verilog netlists (before/after refactor from Spade compilation)
- Prove they produce identical output sequences for all input sequences
- For pipelined equivalence: prove shifted-equivalence (outputs match with delay)

FILES: formal/ppu/equivalence/*.eqy

This is a Phase 6+ tool. It does not need to exist before the first PPU
implementation, but the framework should be ready before any refactor is
attempted on timing-visible logic.

Extends the existing bd-mos (CPU equivalence framework) to PPU scope.

title: Switch CpuDelta and MicroWriteSet to named struct construction
description:
## Problem

Throughout src/cpu/semantics.spade (and to a lesser extent core.spade), CpuDelta and MicroWriteSet are constructed with POSITIONAL arguments:

    CpuDelta(idle_reg_write_set(), Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, micro)
    MicroWriteSet(some_phase(Phase::Fetch), Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None)
    RegWriteSet(Option::None, Option::None, some_u8(value), Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None)

CpuDelta has 6 fields, MicroWriteSet has 6 fields, and RegWriteSet has 8 fields. With so many Option::None values, it is very easy to:
- Accidentally swap two adjacent Option::None fields (e.g., pc_write vs sp_write)
- Miscout the number of Nones when adding or removing a field
- Lose track of which positional argument maps to which field

The struct definitions in types.spade use named fields:

    pub struct CpuDelta {
        regs_write: RegWriteSet,
        pc_write: Option<uint<16>>,
        sp_write: Option<uint<16>>,
        ime_write: Option<ImeState>,
        halt_write: Option<HaltState>,
        micro_write: MicroWriteSet,
    }

But the construction sites use positional syntax, losing all this safety.

## Solution

Convert ALL positional struct construction of CpuDelta, MicroWriteSet, and RegWriteSet to named \$() syntax:

### Before:
    CpuDelta(
        idle_reg_write_set(),
        Option::None,
        some_u16(target),
        Option::None,
        Option::None,
        MicroWriteSet(some_phase(Phase::ServiceInterrupt$(irq: irq, subphase: IrqPhase::PushPcLo)), Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None),
    )

### After:
    CpuDelta$(
        regs_write: idle_reg_write_set(),
        pc_write: Option::None,
        sp_write: some_u16(target),
        ime_write: Option::None,
        halt_write: Option::None,
        micro_write: MicroWriteSet$(
            phase: some_phase(Phase::ServiceInterrupt$(irq: irq, subphase: IrqPhase::PushPcLo)),
            opcode_latch: Option::None,
            imm_lo: Option::None,
            imm_hi: Option::None,
            decoded: Option::None,
            temp: Option::None,
        ),
    )

### Scope

Search for all positional constructions in these files:
- src/cpu/semantics.spade: ~60+ CpuDelta constructions, ~60+ MicroWriteSet constructions, ~20+ RegWriteSet constructions
- src/cpu/core.spade: CpuArchState, CpuState, Registers positional constructions (lines 38-55)
- src/cpu/semantics.spade: apply_reg_writes (line 238-251), apply_micro_writes (lines 253-262), apply_delta (lines 888-900), make_commit (lines 902-918)

Also convert these in semantics.spade:
- CpuState (line 899)
- CpuArchState (lines 893-897)
- Registers (line 239-251)
- CommitTrace (lines 905-917)

### Note on interaction with bd-37qy (helper extraction)

If bd-37qy (extract helpers) is done first, the number of remaining positional constructions will be much smaller. If this bead is done first, the helpers in bd-37qy should be written with named syntax from the start. Either order works but doing bd-37qy first is recommended.

## Files

- src/cpu/semantics.spade (primary — bulk of changes)
- src/cpu/core.spade (secondary — a handful of constructions)

## Estimated impact

No lines saved (may slightly increase line count due to field names), but significantly improves correctness confidence and readability. Prevents a class of silent bugs.

## Verification

- All tests must pass.
- Synthesis output must be identical (this is purely syntactic).

title: Mode-3 length emergence and transfer event recording
description:
Ensure mode-3 length emerges from fetcher/object/window activity and record
transfer subevents for diagnostic comparison.

MODE-3 LENGTH GENERATION:
Default: 172 dots. Lengthened by:
- SCX discard: 0-7 dots (from scx_low3_line)
- Window penalty: 6 dots when window starts (fetcher restart)
- Per-object penalties: variable, depends on fetch/tile cadence interaction

The transfer state machine GENERATES the length from actual fetcher and object
activity. It must NEVER be a table lookup or hardcoded constant.

TRANSFER EVENT RECORDING:
During mode 3, record each TransferEvent variant:
  BgWarmup, ScxDiscard(n), WindowRestart, ObjFetchStart{oam_index},
  ObjFetchCancel, PixelPop{x}

At end of mode 3, compute TransferDigest:
  { event_count, event_hash, window_restart_count, obj_fetch_count }

The digest is included in LineSummary for stable regression comparison.
Individual events serve as a debug artifact.

STRICTNESS TIERS:
- mode3_len: Stable (exact regression contract)
- transfer digest: Stable (hash-based, catches gross differences)
- individual penalty decomposition: ResearchBacked at best (Pan Docs notes some
  SCX/object timing is still unconfirmed -- keep as debug/diagnostic only)

title: step_dot denotational core (src/ppu/sem/step.spade)
description:
Define the pure step_dot function that is the semantic heart of the PPU.

fn step_dot(state: PpuState, input: DotInput) -> DotOutput

This is the denotation of one video step. It is a PURE FUNCTION -- no registers,
no clock, no side effects. The operational realization (ppu_core entity) stores
PpuState in registers, evaluates step_dot combinationally, and commits the next
state on dot_ce.

INITIAL STUB: The function signature and type skeleton should be defined here.
The actual implementation will be filled in across Waves A-D as each subsystem
comes online. The stub should return a pass-through state with no side effects.

WHY PURE: This is the same denotational/operational split used in the CPU
(step_mcycle). It lets you compare only meaningful committed state instead of
trying to diff every combinational ripple. It also enables the local Python
reference model to implement the same semantics for differential testing.

The operational wrapper (ppu_core entity, created in a separate bead) will be:
  entity ppu_core(clk, rst, dot_ce, video_now, bus_events, ...) -> (...)
  {
    let state = reg(clk, ...);
    let output = step_dot(state, input);
    // commit output.next_state on dot_ce
  }

title: Backend-differential testing (sim-array vs inferred-RAM)
description:
Run identical PPU test suites against two different memory backends to catch
protocol underspecification.

BACKENDS:
1. Simulation-array: Python-side arrays (trivial, well-understood)
2. Inferred-sync-RAM: Yosys infers synchronous RAM from Spade code (realistic)

TEST PROCEDURE:
- Run the same ROM test suite through both backends
- Compare PpuSemanticCommit streams dot-by-dot
- Compare LineSummary records scanline-by-scanline
- Compare frame hashes

Any divergence proves the memory protocol was underspecified. Common causes:
- Read latency assumptions (sync RAM has 1-cycle latency, arrays are immediate)
- Response ordering assumptions (positional vs tag-based matching)
- Stale response handling (epoch check needed for sync RAM but not arrays)
- Access conflict behavior (simultaneous read/write to same address)

TEST DIRECTORY: test/ppu/backend_diff/

This lane catches a fundamentally different failure mode than semantic bugs.
Semantic tests verify that the PPU correctly implements Game Boy behavior.
Backend-differential tests verify that the memory protocol is correctly
specified and consistently implemented.

SCHEDULING: Should be part of the nightly regression, not the fast CI loop,
since it doubles the simulation time for every test.
close_reason: Completed backend-differential tooling, nightly manifest, and smoke lane; live mem_req-driven activation tracked separately

title: Green UP5K synthesis and place-and-route, update hardware baseline
description:
Run the full hardware synthesis and place-and-route flow, verify the design fits
within iCE40 UP5K resource limits, and update the baseline record.

PREREQUISITE: The physical memory wiring (bd-ezvi) must be complete. Without it,
synthesis will still fail with 26x resource overrun.

PROCEDURE:

1. Run tools/run_hardware_baseline.sh (no flags needed, default behavior):
   - swim build (generates spade.sv)
   - yosys synth_ice40 (synthesis -> JSON netlist + resource stats)
   - nextpnr-ice40 (place and route -> .asc file + timing report)
   - Python script generates docs/hardware/icebreaker_up5k_baseline.json

2. Verify resource utilization from baseline JSON:
   - lut4_used < 5,280 (fits_up5k_lut4: true)
   - dff_used < 1,024 (fits_up5k_dff: true)
   - spram_used: 2-3 (ROM + WRAM, optionally VRAM)
   - ebr_used: 1-2 (OAM + HRAM)
   - logic_cells_used < 5,280

3. Verify P&R success:
   - pnr.status: "passed" (not "failed")
   - pnr.error: null
   - Clock constraint met: achieved_mhz >= 12.0

4. Run tools/verify_hw_build.sh --enforce-budget:
   - Must exit 0 (LUT and DFF within budget)
   - Must report no debug symbols in netlist

5. Verify no debug contamination (already checked by scripts):
   - No CommitTrace, DebugTrace, PpuDebugTrace, SimStimulus, BusObs signals

TEST CASES:

1. SYNTHESIS COMPLETES WITHOUT ERROR:
   yosys synth_ice40 exits 0. No error messages in yosys.log.

2. RESOURCE BUDGET MET:
   Parse yosys-stat.txt: SB_LUT4 <= 5280, SB_DFF sum <= 1024.

3. P&R SUCCEEDS:
   nextpnr-ice40 exits 0 (no placement or routing failures). The previous
   error ("no BELs remaining to implement cell type ICESTORM_LC") must NOT
   appear in nextpnr.log.

4. TIMING CLOSURE:
   nextpnr timing report shows achieved_mhz >= 12.0 for the CLK domain.
   If timing is tight, record the slack for future monitoring.

5. SPRAM BLOCKS UTILIZED:
   nextpnr report or yosys stat shows ICESTORM_SPRAM > 0 (previously was 0).

6. EBR BLOCKS UTILIZED:
   nextpnr report shows ICESTORM_RAM > 0 (previously was 0).

7. BASELINE JSON UPDATED:
   docs/hardware/icebreaker_up5k_baseline.json is updated with new metrics.
   Compare against previous baseline to confirm the dramatic improvement:
   - DFF: 68,099 -> < 1,024 (expected ~98.5% reduction)
   - LUT4: 72,190 -> < 5,280 (expected ~92% reduction)
   - SPRAM: 0 -> 2-3
   - EBR: 0 -> 1-2
   - P&R: "failed" -> "passed"

8. ENFORCE-BUDGET FLAG PASSES:
   tools/verify_hw_build.sh --enforce-budget --skip-build exits 0.

9. DEBUG-FREE BUILD VERIFIED:
   No forbidden debug symbols in synthesized netlist JSON.

10. BITSTREAM GENERATION:
    If P&R succeeds, icepack can generate the final bitstream (.bin). Verify
    the .asc file is produced and is non-empty. (Actually uploading to
    hardware is out of scope for this bead.)

ACCEPTANCE:
- docs/hardware/icebreaker_up5k_baseline.json updated with passing P&R
- All resource counts within UP5K budget
- tools/verify_hw_build.sh --enforce-budget passes
- Timing closure at 12 MHz achieved
- Commit includes the updated baseline JSON

title: Minimal bus fabric: ROM + WRAM + HRAM + dummy IO
description:
Implement the minimal bus fabric for Wave A CPU bring-up (plan section 12.4 Wave A).

This fabric provides just enough memory infrastructure for the first ROMs to execute. It does NOT include DMA, MBC, PPU, or contention logic--those come in later waves.

MEMORY MAP IMPLEMENTATION (src/bus/membus.spade):
- 0x0000-0x7FFF: ROM (read-only, loaded from test ROM)
- 0xC000-0xDFFF: WRAM (8KB, read/write)
- 0xFF80-0xFFFE: HRAM (127 bytes, read/write)
- 0xFF00-0xFF7F: IO registers (dummy reads return 0xFF, writes ignored for now)
- All other addresses: return 0xFF on read, ignore writes

INTERFACE:
  entity membus(
      clk: clock,
      rst: bool,
      m_ce: bool,
      req: BusReq,
  ) -> (BusResp, BusObs)

  - BusReq::Idle -> BusResp { data: 0xFF }, BusObs { region: Hram, blocked: false, owner: Idle }
  - BusReq::Read { addr } -> BusResp { data: mem[addr] }, BusObs { region: decode(addr), ... }
  - BusReq::Write { addr, data } -> write mem[addr] if writable, BusObs { ... }

MEMORY IMPLEMENTATION:
- ROM: simulation loads ROM data into an inferred memory array (synthesis will use SPRAM later, bd-23s)
- WRAM: inferred memory array (8KB). For simulation this is fine; hardware mapping to SPRAM comes in bd-23s
- HRAM: register array (127 bytes, small enough for registers)
- IO stubs: return 0xFF for reads, discard writes. Individual IO registers are wired in as peripherals are added (timer in bd-3pw, IE/IF in bd-gsm, etc.)

ADDRESS DECODE:
The address decode logic maps the 16-bit address to BusRegion for the BusObs sideband (bd-3pj). This is tested by bd-2an (bus fabric unit tests) which verifies every address boundary.

POWER CONSIDERATIONS:
- When BusReq::Idle, memory arrays should not be read (chip-select low)
- Write strobes false on Idle and Read
- Address decode logic should be clean enough to not create unnecessary fanout

WHY MINIMAL: The plan (section 12.4 Wave A) explicitly limits the first bus to ROM/WRAM/HRAM/dummy-IO. This prevents peripheral complexity from obscuring CPU bugs (Risk 5 mitigation). Wave B adds timer/interrupt IO, Wave C adds MBC/DMA, Wave D adds VRAM/OAM.

ACCEPTANCE: All address boundaries decode correctly. ROM loads and reads back. WRAM read/write works. HRAM read/write works. IO reads return 0xFF. BusObs emitted correctly.

title: EBR-inferred memory wrappers using dp_bram for OAM and HRAM
description:
Rewrite src/mem/phys/ebr.spade to use dp_bram (Spade standard library dual-port
block RAM) instead of clocked_memory_init, so Yosys infers iCE40 EBR blocks
(SB_RAM40_4K) instead of DFF register cascades.

PROBLEM: The current hram_ebr and oam_ebr entities use clocked_memory_init which
produces register arrays. OAM (256 bytes) and HRAM (127 bytes) are small enough for
EBR blocks (each SB_RAM40_4K = 4Kbit = 512 bytes at 8-bit width), but Yosys cannot
infer them from the current Spade output.

iCE40 EBR (SB_RAM40_4K) SPECIFICATION:
- 4Kbit per block, configurable: 256x16, 512x8, 1024x4, 2048x2
- TRUE DUAL PORT: two independent address buses, two data ports
- Each port has independent read/write capability
- Synchronous reads (1-cycle latency)
- Supports initialization (unlike SPRAM)
- 30 blocks available on UP5K

SPADE dp_bram PRIMITIVE:
The Spade standard library provides std::mem::dp_bram which maps directly to
dual-port block RAM during synthesis:

  dp_bram::<depth, addr_bits, word_type>(
      clk,
      // Port A
      (a_addr, a_write_en, a_write_data),
      // Port B
      (b_addr, b_write_en, b_write_data),
  ) -> (word_type, word_type)  // (a_read_data, b_read_data)

Both ports can read and write independently. Yosys recognizes this pattern and
maps it to SB_RAM40_4K during synth_ice40.

ENTITIES TO REWRITE:

1. hram_ebr(clk, cpu_addr, dma_addr, write_en, write_addr, write_data)
   -> (uint<8>, uint<8>)
   - 127 bytes, fits in 1 EBR block (256x8 configuration, ~50% utilized)
   - Port A: CPU read + CPU write (write_en gates writes)
   - Port B: DMA read (read-only for DMA)
   - Implementation: use dp_bram with port A for CPU (read/write), port B for
     DMA (read-only, write_en tied false)
   - Write address and CPU read address may differ; need to mux the port A
     address between write_addr (when writing) and cpu_addr (when reading)

2. oam_ebr(clk, read_addr, cpu_write_en, cpu_write_addr, cpu_write_data,
           dma_write_en, dma_write_addr, dma_write_data) -> uint<8>
   - 256 bytes (actually 160 used, 0xFE00-0xFE9F), fits in 1 EBR block
   - Port A: CPU writes
   - Port B: DMA writes
   - Read port: currently single read, need to handle carefully
   - OAM has 2 write ports (CPU + DMA) which maps well to dp_bram's 2 ports
   - Read address can be multiplexed onto whichever port is not writing
   - When neither port writes, either can read
   - When CPU writes, DMA port can read (and vice versa)
   - When both write simultaneously, Game Boy spec says DMA wins (priority)

IMPORTANT: dp_bram has 1-cycle READ LATENCY (synchronous reads). The current
clocked_memory_init + read_memory pattern provides COMBINATIONAL reads. This
latency change may require adjustments in membus.spade's response timing. The
M-cycle boundaries should absorb this latency since memory reads are sampled
at M-cycle edges, but this must be verified.

TEST CASES:

1. HRAM READ-WRITE ROUND TRIP:
   Write 0xC3 to HRAM address 0 (Game Boy 0xFF80). Read it back on the next
   M-cycle. Verify 0xC3 is returned. Repeat at boundary address 126 (0xFFFE).

2. HRAM DUAL READ (CPU + DMA):
   Pre-load HRAM with known pattern. In a single M-cycle, read addr 0 via
   CPU port and addr 10 via DMA port. Verify both return correct data.

3. HRAM WRITE DOES NOT CORRUPT OTHER ADDRESSES:
   Write to HRAM address 5. Verify addresses 4 and 6 are unaffected.

4. OAM CPU WRITE AND READ BACK:
   Write 0x77 to OAM address 0 (Game Boy 0xFE00). Read it back. Verify 0x77.

5. OAM DMA WRITE AND READ BACK:
   Simulate DMA writing 0xAB to OAM address 0x10. Read it back. Verify 0xAB.

6. OAM SIMULTANEOUS CPU AND DMA WRITE:
   CPU writes 0x11 to OAM address 0x20, DMA writes 0x22 to OAM address 0x20
   in the same cycle. Verify DMA wins (per Game Boy spec, DMA has priority).

7. OAM FULL 160-BYTE DMA TRANSFER:
   Simulate a complete OAM DMA transfer (160 bytes). Read back all 160 bytes.
   Verify all match the source data.

8. EBR 1-CYCLE READ LATENCY:
   Present a read address. Verify the output is NOT available on the same cycle
   (combinational) but IS available on the next cycle (synchronous). This
   confirms dp_bram latency behavior vs the old combinational read_memory.

9. EBR RESOURCE COUNT:
   After synthesis, verify yosys stat shows SB_RAM40_4K cells > 0.
   HRAM should use 1 EBR block. OAM should use 1 EBR block. Total: 2 blocks.

10. INITIALIZATION:
    Verify EBR contents are zero-initialized (matching current clocked_memory_init
    behavior with [0u8; N]). dp_bram supports initialization via Yosys.

ACCEPTANCE:
- hram_ebr and oam_ebr use dp_bram instead of clocked_memory_init
- Yosys synth_ice40 reports SB_RAM40_4K usage > 0
- All existing test/unit/test_membus.py tests still pass (may need timing adjustments
  for 1-cycle read latency)
- No DFF inflation from OAM/HRAM memory storage

title: CPU type definitions: CpuArchState, CpuMicroState, Phase enum, continuations
End-to-end smoke test: first instruction executes correctly
description:
Create test/harness/test_e2e_smoke.py — the earliest end-to-end green test for the real verification loop.

This bead uses the shared Cocotb harness plus the PyBoy oracle interface directly. It intentionally does NOT wait for the full comparator/divergence framework; the goal is the smallest possible proof that DUT wiring, ROM loading, reset, stepping, and basic oracle parity all work.

TEST SCENARIO:
1. Load a trivial ROM containing NOP (0x00) at address 0x0100
2. Initialize DUT at SkipBoot reset state (PC=0x0100)
3. Initialize the PyBoy oracle with the same ROM and reset profile
4. Step the DUT one instruction through cpu_test_top
5. Step the oracle one instruction
6. Compare PC and the core architectural registers directly in the test

EXPECTED RESULT: PASS. This is the verification-loop hello world.

WHY THIS BEAD EXISTS EARLY: if this test is blocked on the full comparator/artifact stack, the team loses a very fast sanity-green signal. Keep it minimal and keep it first.

Follow this up by switching the nightly lane to real semantic_observe_top or ppu_test_top captures once step_dot emits non-idle mem_reqs, so the lane can catch backend latency/order bugs with live memory traffic.

title: PPU: FetcherState builders and dead code removal
description:
Reduce FetcherState copy-spread boilerplate and remove dead code from obj_fetch.

## Scope
1. Create builder helpers for FetcherState: with_step(state, step), with_pending_read(state, valid, read), with_epoch(state, epoch). Apply to issue_read, state_with_epoch, clear_pending, state_after_resp, advance_no_resp.
2. Remove keep_pending_read — inline as state.pending_read at all call sites.
3. Remove dead functions from obj_fetch.spade:
   - incoming_row_pixel (never called; incoming_row_pixel_clipped used everywhere)

4. Remove obj_fifo_start_offset / obj_fifo_left_clip sprite_start duplication (compute once, pass to both).

title: Unified test runner with hierarchical logging and coverage tracking
description:
Create tools/run_tests.py -- the single-command entry point for running all verification tiers with comprehensive logging and coverage tracking.

TEST TIERS (executed in order, each tier independently runnable):
1. META/INFRASTRUCTURE: Spade-Cocotb integration, oracle init, event determinism, reset profile
2. UNIT TESTS: ALU, decoder, register file, flag helpers, timebase, bus fabric, write-enable discipline
3. FORMAL VERIFICATION: F low nibble, reset state, m_ce hold, bus ownership (later)
4. ROM DIFFERENTIAL: Wave A-D ROMs against PyBoy oracle
5. LOCKSTEP: Live instruction-commit and M-cycle-commit comparison
6. INVARIANT: Metamorphic tests, CPU invariant tests
7. POWER: Halt quiescence, ALU idle isolation, write-enable duty cycle

COMMAND INTERFACE:
  python tools/run_tests.py                        # run all tiers
  python tools/run_tests.py --tier unit            # run one tier
  python tools/run_tests.py --tier unit,rom        # run multiple tiers
  python tools/run_tests.py --quick                # fast subset (meta + unit only)
  python tools/run_tests.py --nightly              # include exhaustive/nightly tests
  python tools/run_tests.py --sim=verilator        # use Verilator backend
  python tools/run_tests.py --log-level=verbose    # verbose logging
  python tools/run_tests.py --coverage             # enable coverage tracking
  python tools/run_tests.py --json                 # machine-parseable output

OUTPUT FORMAT (using test logging standard from bd-dny):
  === ICEBOY VERIFICATION SUITE ===
  [TIER 1/7] Meta/Infrastructure
    [SUITE] test_spade_cocotb_integration.py: 3/3 passed (0.8s)
    [SUITE] test_oracle_init.py: 2/2 passed (1.2s)
  [TIER 2/7] Unit Tests
    [SUITE] test_alu.py: 48/48 passed (2.1s)
    [SUITE] test_decode.py: 512/512 passed (0.9s)
    ...
  === SUMMARY ===
  Tiers: 7/7 passed
  Tests: 847/850 passed, 3 xfail
  Time: 4m 32s
  
  === COVERAGE ===
  Opcode families: 24/24 (100%)
  Phase constructors: 8/10 (80%) -- GAPS: ServiceInterrupt, Halted
  Bus regions: 4/10 (40%) -- GAPS: Vram, Oam, CartRam, Echo, NotUsable, Ie
  Interrupt causes: 0/5 (0%) -- Phase 4 not yet implemented

FEATURES:
- Parallel test execution within tiers (configurable parallelism)
- Automatic failure artifact collection
- JUnit XML output for CI integration
- Coverage delta reporting (what changed since last run)
- Estimated time remaining based on historical run times

ACCEPTANCE: All implemented tests runnable via single command. Coverage report generated. JUnit XML output parseable by standard CI tools.

title: Divergence artifact emission framework
description:
Implement the divergence artifact framework (§16.7) for when live oracle comparison fails.

When a CommitTrace comparison fails, emit:
1. The first bad CommitTrace (DUT vs oracle)
2. A commit window before/after the failure (configurable, default ±5)
3. A minimized replay capsule (see replay capsule task)
4. A localized waveform dump (VCD/FST) centered on the failure
5. Optional in-memory oracle state snapshot

Artifact output:
- Human-readable summary to stderr
- Machine-readable JSON to artifact directory
- Waveform file for waveform viewer (surfer, GTKWave)

This framework is critical for debugging. When a test fails, the engineer should be able to:
1. Read the summary to understand WHAT diverged
2. Load the replay capsule to reproduce it
3. Open the waveform to see the signal-level picture
4. Inspect the oracle snapshot to understand the expected state

title: cpu_core semantic architecture: step_mcycle + apply_delta framework
description:
Implement the two pure functions that form the semantic heart of the CPU (§7.2-7.3), and wire them into the cpu_core entity.

This is THE most architecturally significant task in the entire project. The plan (§24) says: 'Build the CPU as a pure, typed M-cycle transition function wrapped by a thin clocked Spade entity.'

FUNCTIONS TO IMPLEMENT:

1. fn step_mcycle(state: CpuState, input: MicroInput) -> MicroOutput
   - Takes current CpuState (arch + micro) and external inputs (bus_resp, irq_pending)
   - Returns MicroOutput containing: CpuDelta, BusReq, CommitTrace, sideband
   - Implemented as a large match on state.micro.phase:
     Phase::Fetch => handle_fetch(state, input)
     Phase::FetchPrefix { prefix } => handle_fetch_prefix(state, input, prefix)
     Phase::ReadImm8 { k } => handle_read_imm8(state, input, k)
     Phase::ReadImm16Lo { k } => ...
     Phase::ReadImm16Hi { lo, k } => ...
     Phase::ReadMem { addr, k } => handle_read_mem(state, input, addr, k)
     Phase::WriteMem { addr, data, k } => handle_write_mem(state, input, addr, data, k)
     Phase::Execute { op } => handle_execute(state, input, op)
     Phase::ServiceInterrupt { irq, subphase } => handle_interrupt(state, input, irq, subphase)
     Phase::Halted => handle_halted(state, input)
   - Each arm delegates to the phase machine handlers from bd-4tk, bd-3ab, bd-3o1, bd-1je, bd-25t

2. fn apply_delta(state: CpuState, delta: CpuDelta) -> CpuState
   - Pure function that applies sparse register writes from CpuDelta to CpuState
   - CpuDelta contains Option fields: only Some values cause writes
   - This function is used by: (a) the cpu_core entity to commit state on m_ce, and (b) test reference models for offline comparison
   - The sparse delta pattern is critical for power (§18.4): it ensures only targeted registers toggle

3. cpu_core entity integration:
   - Owns registers storing CpuState via reg(clk) with explicit hold behavior
   - On each clock edge where m_ce is true:
     a. Evaluate step_mcycle(current_state, input) combinationally
     b. Apply delta to state via apply_delta
     c. Emit BusReq and CommitTrace outputs
   - On m_ce=false: ALL architectural state holds. No toggles. This is the power contract.

WHY THIS ARCHITECTURE:
- step_mcycle is the DENOTATION of one machine step (§7.3). It answers: given this state and input, what happens?
- apply_delta is the OPERATIONAL commit mechanism. It answers: how does state update?
- Separating them means tests can call step_mcycle without a clock, and formal tools can reason about the pure function independently from the entity wrapper.
- The entity wrapper is deliberately thin: it owns registers and the m_ce gate, nothing else.

FILE LOCATIONS:
- src/cpu/semantics.spade: step_mcycle, apply_delta, and the top-level phase dispatch
- src/cpu/core.spade: cpu_core entity (updates the existing stub from bd-1o2)

RELATIONSHIP TO PHASE MACHINE TASKS:
This task creates the FRAMEWORK (the match dispatch and the delta/entity infrastructure). The individual phase machine tasks (bd-4tk, bd-3ab, bd-3o1, bd-1je, bd-25t, bd-13p) fill in the match arms. This task should be done FIRST so that phase machine tasks have a structure to plug into.

ACCEPTANCE: cpu_core compiles, step_mcycle dispatches to at least one phase handler, apply_delta correctly applies sparse deltas, m_ce=false produces no state change.
