title: iCE40 SPRAM primitive wrappers with time-multiplexed dual-port access
description:
Implement extern Spade wrappers for iCE40 UP5K SPRAM (SB_SPRAM256KA) and update
src/mem/phys/spram.spade to use real hardware primitives instead of clocked_memory_init.

PROBLEM: The current SPRAM wrappers in src/mem/phys/spram.spade use
clocked_memory_init internally, which Yosys lowers to DFF register cascades.
A 32KB ROM alone generates ~65,536 DFFs — 64x the entire UP5K DFF budget.

iCE40 UP5K SB_SPRAM256KA SPECIFICATION:
- 16K x 16-bit = 32KB per block
- 4 blocks total on UP5K
- SINGLE PORT: one 14-bit address bus, one 16-bit data in, one 16-bit data out
- Write enable (WREN), chip select (CHIPSELECT), mask write enable (MASKWREN)
- Synchronous read: output appears on DATAOUT one clock after address is presented
- No initialization: contents are undefined at power-up (ROM data must be loaded at runtime)
- Sleep/standby/poweroff pins for power management

EXTERN ENTITY DECLARATION:
Create src/mem/phys/ice40_spram.spade (or add to existing file):

  #[no_mangle(all)]
  extern entity SB_SPRAM256KA(
      ADDRESS: uint<14>,
      DATAIN: uint<16>,
      MASKWREN: uint<4>,
      WREN: bool,
      CHIPSELECT: bool,
      CLOCK: clock,
      STANDBY: bool,
      SLEEP: bool,
      POWEROFF: bool,
      DATAOUT: inv uint<16>,
  );

PORT NOTES:
- ADDRESS is 14-bit (16K words)
- DATAIN/DATAOUT are 16-bit; Game Boy bus is 8-bit, so pack two bytes per word
  or use MASKWREN to write individual bytes
- MASKWREN: 4-bit mask for 4 nibbles of the 16-bit word. To write only the low
  byte, set MASKWREN = 0b0011. To write only the high byte, set MASKWREN = 0b1100.
- POWEROFF must be HIGH (active low: 1 = normal operation, 0 = power off)

TIME-MULTIPLEXING FOR DUAL READ PORTS:
The Game Boy needs two simultaneous read addresses per M-cycle (CPU + DMA for
ROM/WRAM, or CPU + PPU for VRAM). Since SPRAM is single-port, use the timebase's
t_index (0-3) to multiplex:
- T0: Present CPU read address -> SPRAM latches
- T1: Register CPU read result from DATAOUT; present DMA/PPU read address
- T2: Register DMA/PPU read result from DATAOUT; present write address if needed
- T3: Write completes (if any); outputs hold registered values

The exact T-cycle assignment is flexible, but the principle is: each SPRAM access
takes 1 T-cycle (address in, data out next cycle), and we have 4 T-cycles per
M-cycle to schedule 2 reads + 0-1 write.

WRAPPER ENTITIES TO REWRITE:

1. rom_image_spram(clk, t_index, cpu_addr, dma_addr) -> (uint<8>, uint<8>)
   - Uses 1 SB_SPRAM256KA block
   - Write port disabled (WREN=false permanently for ROM)
   - ROM data loaded at startup via a separate initialization mechanism
   - Time-multiplexes CPU and DMA reads across T-cycles
   - Outputs registered so they're stable for the full M-cycle

2. wram_spram(clk, t_index, cpu_addr, dma_addr, write_en, write_addr, write_data)
   -> (uint<8>, uint<8>)
   - Uses 1 SB_SPRAM256KA block (8KB of 32KB capacity, upper address bits tied low)
   - Time-multiplexes CPU read, DMA read, and optional write across 3 T-cycles
   - Write only happens when write_en is true
   - Outputs registered

3. vram_spram(clk, t_index, cpu_addr, ppu_addr, write_en, write_addr, write_data)
   -> (uint<8>, uint<8>)
   - Same pattern as wram_spram but second port is PPU instead of DMA

NEW INPUT: All SPRAM wrappers now need t_index: uint<2> from the timebase to
schedule the time-multiplexed access. This is a new signal that must be threaded
from hardware_core through membus.

SIMULATION COMPATIBILITY:
The extern SB_SPRAM256KA is an iCE40 synthesis primitive. For simulation, provide
a Verilog behavioral model in sim/models/SB_SPRAM256KA.v that implements the same
interface with a simple reg array. Alternatively, use Spade conditional compilation
or a simulation-specific wrapper that uses clocked_memory_init. The simulation model
must match the hardware behavior: 1-cycle read latency, undefined initial contents.

TEST CASES:

1. SPRAM SINGLE READ LATENCY:
   Present an address at T0. Verify data appears at T1 (1-cycle latency).
   Verify data from T0 is NOT available combinationally at T0.

2. SPRAM WRITE-THEN-READ:
   Write value 0xAB to address 0x100 at T2. On the next M-cycle, read address
   0x100. Verify 0xAB is returned. This confirms write-through behavior.

3. TIME-MULTIPLEXED DUAL READ:
   In a single M-cycle, request CPU read at addr 0x200 and DMA read at addr
   0x300 (both pre-loaded with known values). Verify both outputs are correct
   and stable by the end of the M-cycle.

4. BYTE PACKING IN 16-BIT WORD:
   Write 0x12 to Game Boy address N (maps to SPRAM word W, low byte) and
   0x34 to address N+1 (maps to SPRAM word W, high byte). Read back both.
   Verify MASKWREN correctly isolates byte writes within the 16-bit word.

5. SPRAM BLOCK GEOMETRY:
   Verify address mapping: Game Boy 15-bit ROM address (0x0000-0x7FFF)
   maps to SPRAM 14-bit word address (0x0000-0x3FFF) with byte select.
   Game Boy 13-bit WRAM address maps to SPRAM 12-bit word address.

6. POWER PINS DEFAULT STATE:
   Verify POWEROFF is tied HIGH (active), STANDBY and SLEEP are tied LOW.
   Future power optimization may use these for clock gating.

7. WRITE DURING DMA READ CYCLE:
   CPU writes to WRAM at the same time DMA is reading from WRAM. Verify
   the write and the DMA read are scheduled on different T-cycles and both
   complete correctly. This is the critical contention case.

8. ROM READ-ONLY ENFORCEMENT:
   Verify ROM SPRAM wrapper has WREN permanently false. Attempt to write
   to ROM address. Verify the ROM contents are unchanged.

ACCEPTANCE:
- src/mem/phys/spram.spade (or ice40_spram.spade) contains extern SB_SPRAM256KA
- rom_image_spram, wram_spram, vram_spram entities use SB_SPRAM256KA internally
- All wrappers accept t_index: uint<2> for time-multiplexing
- Yosys synth_ice40 reports SPRAM usage > 0 (SB_SPRAM256KA cells in stat output)
- Simulation model works with cocotb tests
- No clocked_memory_init remains in SPRAM wrapper code paths

title: Author Wave A custom ROM assembly source
description:
Write the SM83 assembly source files for all 7 Wave A custom test ROMs (§17.1). Each ROM follows the checkpoint ABI and targets specific CPU instruction families WITHOUT requiring timers, interrupts, DMA, MBC, or PPU.

ROM SPECIFICATIONS:

1. LOADS_BASIC (bench/roms/loads_basic.asm):
   Tests: LD r,r / LD r,n8 / LD r,(HL) / LD (HL),r / LD (HL),n8 / LD A,(BC) / LD A,(DE) / LD (BC),A / LD (DE),A / LD A,(nn) / LD (nn),A / LD r16,nn / LD SP,HL / LD (nn),SP / PUSH/POP pairs for load verification
   Strategy: Load known values into registers, store to WRAM, read back and verify. Each test case writes pass/fail to WRAM signature block.
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_ld_r_r, __checkpoint_ld_r_n8, __checkpoint_ld_mem, __checkpoint_ld_r16

2. ALU_FLAGS (bench/roms/alu_flags.asm):
   Tests: ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/OR/XOR/CP with edge cases for every flag (Z, N, H, C)
   Strategy: For each ALU op, test: zero result, non-zero result, half-carry, full carry, negative flag. Verify flags by pushing AF to stack and checking F byte.
   Critical edge cases: ADC with carry_in=1, SBC with carry_in=1, half-carry on 0x0F+0x01, carry on 0xFF+0x01
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_add, __checkpoint_adc, __checkpoint_sub, __checkpoint_sbc, __checkpoint_logic

3. ALU16_SP (bench/roms/alu16_sp.asm):
   Tests: ADD HL,rr / ADD SP,e8 / LD HL,SP+e8 / INC rr / DEC rr
   Strategy: These have the trickiest flag behavior in the SM83. Flags for ADD SP,e8 and LD HL,SP+e8 are based on low byte arithmetic (not the 16-bit result), which is counterintuitive.
   Critical cases from gbxcule (§11.3): SP=0xFFF8 + e8=0x08 (H=1,C=1), SP=0x0000 + e8=0xFF (signed -1)
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_add_hl, __checkpoint_add_sp_e8, __checkpoint_ld_hl_sp_e8

4. FLOW_STACK (bench/roms/flow_stack.asm):
   Tests: JP nn / JP cc,nn / JR e8 / JR cc,e8 / CALL nn / CALL cc,nn / RET / RET cc / RST vec / PUSH rr / POP rr
   Strategy: Test each control flow instruction with both taken and not-taken conditions. Verify SP changes correctly for PUSH/POP/CALL/RET. Test RST vectors 0x00-0x38.
   Critical cases: conditional branches with all flag conditions (Z/NZ/C/NC), nested CALL/RET, SP underflow
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_jp, __checkpoint_jr, __checkpoint_call_ret, __checkpoint_rst

5. CB_BITOPS (bench/roms/cb_bitops.asm):
   Tests: RLC/RRC/RL/RR/SLA/SRA/SWAP/SRL for all registers + (HL) / BIT b,r / RES b,r / SET b,r
   Strategy: For each rotate/shift, test with known bit patterns (0x80, 0x01, 0x00, 0xFF) and verify result + flags. BIT tests all 8 bit positions. RES/SET verify single-bit modification.
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_rotate, __checkpoint_shift, __checkpoint_bit, __checkpoint_res_set

6. MEM_RWB (bench/roms/mem_rwb.asm):
   Tests: Soak test writing patterns to WRAM and reading back
   Strategy: Write ascending/descending/checkerboard patterns to WRAM region, read back all and compare. Tests bus fabric address decode, WRAM read/write, and memory stability.
   Covers: bus correctness over sustained operation

7. ALU_LOOP (bench/roms/alu_loop.asm):
   Tests: Stress test looping ALU operations
   Strategy: Iterative loops performing ALU ops with accumulating state. Verify final accumulator/flag state matches expected. Tests CPU under sustained instruction throughput.
   Covers: interaction between control flow and ALU over many iterations

COMMON STRUCTURE (all ROMs):
  SECTION "Header", ROM0[$100]
  jp Start
  ds $150 - @, 0  ; Nintendo logo area (rgbfix fills this)
  
  SECTION "Main", ROM0[$150]
  Start:
    di                    ; Disable interrupts
    ld sp, $FFFE          ; Initialize stack
    ; ... test code ...
    ; Write WRAM signature block
    jp __pass / jp __fail
  
  SECTION "Pass", ROM0[$0300]
  __pass: jr __pass       ; Infinite loop on success
  
  SECTION "Fail", ROM0[$0310]  
  __fail: jr __fail       ; Infinite loop on failure

ACCEPTANCE: All 7 ROMs assemble with rgbds. All pass validate_rom_abi.py. All .sym files contain __pass and __fail labels. All ROMs are registered in bench/manifests/roms.yaml with correct oracle_mode and compare_scope.

title: Simulation-only stimulus ports and soc_lockstep_top scaffold
description:
Define the SimStimulus record (§12.6) and create minimal simulation top scaffolds (Phase 1 step 12).

SimStimulus record (simulation-only sideband input):
  struct SimStimulus {
    joyp_buttons: Option<JoypButtons>,
    if_set_bits: uint<5>,
    if_clear_bits: uint<5>,
    ie_override: Option<uint<5>>,
    dma_start: Option<uint<8>>,
    serial_inject: Option<uint<8>>,
    freeze_arch_time: bool,
    cpu_hold_only: bool,
  }

Simulation tops (src/sim/):
- cpu_test_top.spade: isolated CPU with controllable bus mock and interrupt injection
- soc_lockstep_top.spade: full SoC composition with SimStimulus input ports

Design constraints:
- Simulation tops have stable, Verilog-friendly port names for Cocotb (§4.4)
- Keep separate from hardware synthesis top (src/board/icebreaker_top.spade)
- Use SimStimulus ports for deterministic test stimuli, NOT hierarchical force/release (§12.6)
- freeze_arch_time: leaves CPU and peripheral architectural time unchanged
- cpu_hold_only: leaves timer/peripheral time evolution unchanged

title: Decode completeness tests green
description:
Green test/unit/test_decode_completeness.py.

Verification:
1. Every unprefixed opcode (0x00-0xFF) maps to a valid DecodedOp
2. Every CB-prefixed opcode (0x00-0xFF) maps to a valid DecodedOp
3. Known-invalid opcodes map to an explicit Invalid/Undefined variant
4. Decoded output matches canonical metadata (spec/sm83_opcodes.py) for:
   - Mnemonic family (load, alu, control, etc.)
   - Operand count and types
   - Expected cycle count
   - Expected flag policy

5. Snapshot tests: decode output for each opcode is recorded and compared against a known-good snapshot. Any change to decode output requires explicit snapshot update.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone B): Decode snapshot tests green. Every opcode family represented.

title: PPU FIFO push_bg_row and oam_scan append compression
description:
Two functions use massively unrolled match arms to insert elements at a dynamic index into fixed-size arrays. Both can be compressed by 10x using per-slot conditional expressions, which synthesize to equivalent mux logic.

## Background

HDL arrays cannot be indexed with runtime values for writes (unlike reads). The standard workaround is to match on the index and write to each possible slot. However, unrolling this as N separate match arms that each rebuild the entire array is the brute-force approach. The idiomatic HDL approach is to use a conditional expression per slot: if slot_index == target { new_value } else { old_value }. Both produce identical mux networks in synthesis.

## Scope

### 1. push_bg_row (fifo.spade:~68-260, ~190 lines → ~20 lines)
The BG FIFO push operation has 9 match arms (count 0..8 + default). Each arm manually builds a 16-element pixel array. The semantics: "insert 8 pixels starting at position count, keeping existing pixels before count."

Fix: Replace with a single BgFifo constructor where each of 16 pixel slots uses:
  if slot < count { fifo.pixels[slot] }              // keep existing
  else if slot < count + 8 { pushed[slot - count] }  // insert new row
  else { zero_bg_pixel() }                           // zero padding

The slot index is a compile-time constant (0..15), so slot < count and slot - count are runtime comparisons against count, which is exactly what the current match arms compute — just expressed as muxes controlled by count rather than as a match.

Consideration: Spade may not support array indexing with a computed expression like pushed[slot - count] where slot is a compile-time constant but count is runtime. If so, use the alternative form where pushed is indexed with a compile-time constant by nesting the count comparison:
  if count == 0 { pushed[slot] }
  else if count == 1 { if slot >= 1 { pushed[slot-1] } else { fifo.pixels[slot] } }
  ... etc.
This is still far shorter than the current approach because the pixel construction is shared.

### 2. append_ticket (oam_scan.spade:~41-55, ~15 lines → ~12 lines)
The OAM scan appends a found sprite ticket to a 10-element list. 10 match arms each rebuild the slots array changing one element.

Fix: Per-slot conditional:
  slots: [
    if list.count == 0 { ticket } else { list.slots[0] },
    if list.count == 1 { ticket } else { list.slots[1] },
    ...
    if list.count == 9 { ticket } else { list.slots[9] },
  ]

This is 10 lines vs 10 multi-line arms. The synthesized logic is identical: a 10:1 mux per slot controlled by count.

## Considerations
- Verify with a test vector that exercises all count values (0 through 8 for FIFO, 0 through 9 for OAM). The current tests should already cover this.
- The FIFO push is on the PPU rendering hot path. Any change to its structure should be validated with synthesis area comparison to ensure the tool produces equivalent or better results.
- The zero_bg_pixel() padding for slots >= count + 8 may be unnecessary if count is always <= 8 and the FIFO is always consumed before overflow. Verify the invariant, but preserve the current behavior regardless.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. push_bg_row: <=25 lines (down from ~190)
2. append_ticket: <=15 lines (down from ~15 multi-line arms)
3. Synthesized mux structure is equivalent (verify via synthesis)
4. No behavioral change in FIFO or OAM scan

## Test Plan
- swim test passes for fifo_test_top (exercises push with various count values)
- oam_scan_test_top passes (exercises append with various list sizes)
- core_test_top passes (end-to-end rendering through FIFO)
- Synthesis area comparison: record LUT count before and after, expect +-0
- If waveform traces are available, diff FIFO signals before/after on a representative ROM

title: Green TIMER_IRQ_HALT custom ROM

Write and green the TIMER_IRQ_HALT test ROM.

Test coverage:
- Timer overflow generates interrupt

- Correct interrupt service sequence: push PC, jump to vector 0x0050
- Return from interrupt handler with RETI
- Execution resumes after HALT instruction
- HALT with IME=Disabled: wakes but does NOT service interrupt (resumes at PC+1)

Combined scenario:
1. Configure timer for short period
2. EI + HALT
3. Wait for timer overflow
4. Verify: wake from HALT → service interrupt → RETI → continue after HALT

Oracle mode: mcycle_commit

title: Debug-free hardware build configuration
description:
Implement compile-time or top-level mechanism to exclude debug/simulation signals from hardware builds (§18.6).

PROBLEM: Simulation tops expose CommitTrace, DebugTrace, BusObs, and SimStimulus ports. If these accidentally leak into the hardware synthesis, they inflate LUT/DFF counts and toggle rates, contaminating power measurements.

SOLUTION OPTIONS:
1. Separate top-level modules (PREFERRED): simulation tops in src/sim/, hardware top in src/board/. Synthesis targets only src/board/icebreaker_top.spade.
2. Spade conditional compilation: if Spade supports compile-time flags, use them to strip debug ports.
3. Yosys post-synthesis verification: script that checks the synthesized netlist for debug signal names.

IMPLEMENTATION:
- Verify that icebreaker_top.spade does not instantiate or reference any type from src/sim/
- Create tools/verify_hw_build.sh that:
  1. Synthesizes icebreaker_top with yosys
  2. Greps the synthesized JSON netlist for debug signal patterns (CommitTrace, DebugTrace, BusObs, SimStimulus)
  3. Fails if any debug signals found
  4. Reports resource utilization

TESTING:
- Run verify_hw_build.sh as part of the synthesis smoke check
- Verify LUT count is NOT inflated by debug fanout
- Compare resource utilization between debug-free and debug-included builds

ACCEPTANCE: Hardware build contains ZERO simulation-only signals. Resource counts match expectations for a clean design.

title: Epic: PPU Object & Composition — Wave C
description:
Implement OAM scan, object fetch, object FIFO, and the full BG+OBJ mixer.
This is Wave C of the PPU milestone decomposition (history/ppu.md sections 11.3, 17.3).

The object pipeline is a three-stage design:

STAGE 1 — SELECTION (Mode 2, OAM scan):
  Scan all 40 OAM entries. Select up to 10 whose Y overlaps the current scanline.
  Selection uses only Y position in OAM-entry order. X-hidden objects (X=0 or
  X>=168) still count toward the 10-object limit. Output: SelectedObjTicket array
  containing only { oam_index, x, y, selection_rank }. No tile/flag data yet.

STAGE 2 — METADATA FETCH (Mode 3, object fetch path):
  For each selected ticket, fetch tile number, attribute flags, and compute the
  correct row within the tile (accounting for 8x16 mode, Y-flip). Output:
  ResolvedObjMeta { ticket, tile, flags, row_index }. This stage uses the fetch
  epoch mechanism -- if a fetch cancel or window restart occurs, in-flight
  fetches from an older epoch are discarded.

STAGE 3 — PIXEL PRIORITY (per-pixel, at mixer):
  On DMG, overlapping opaque object pixels resolve by: smaller X wins, ties
  broken by earlier OAM entry. The BG-over-OBJ flag is applied AFTER
  object-object priority has chosen the candidate. Output: ObjPixel { color,
  palette, draw_rank, bg_over_obj }.

MIXER (BG+OBJ completion):
  Pure function over one BG pixel and one OBJ pixel. Owns: object transparency
  (color 0), background enable / window behavior, BG-over-OBJ semantics, and
  late DMG palette application.

This three-stage separation is architecturally important because:
- DMA/OAM corruption tests stay local to stage 2 (fetch path)
- Selection logic (stage 1) is independently testable without dragging in VRAM
- Per-pixel priority (stage 3) is a pure function, easily unit-tested

Wave C ROMs: OBJ_BASIC, OBJ_PRIORITY, OBJ_10_PER_LINE, OBJ_8x16, OBJ_FLIP,
OBJ_BG_MASK, OBJ_X_HIDDEN_STILL_COUNTS, OBJ_FETCH_CANCEL_LCDC1,
OBJ_DMA_METADATA_CORRUPT.

Then: dmg-acid2 as the first coarse whole-PPU composition checksum. Its README
says a simple line renderer is sufficient and it is NOT a timing torture test.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone D): Owned OBJ ROMs green, object-selection and draw-priority
invariants green, dmg-acid2 green.

title: Green FLOW_STACK custom ROM
description:
Write the FLOW_STACK custom test ROM and green it.

Test coverage:
- JP nn (unconditional absolute jump)
- JP (HL)
- JR e8 (unconditional relative jump, positive and negative offsets)
- JP cc,nn and JR cc,e8 for all conditions (NZ, Z, NC, C) — both taken and not-taken paths
- CALL nn and CALL cc,nn
- RET and RET cc
- RST 00/08/10/18/20/28/30/38
- Nested CALL/RET sequences
- Stack pointer behavior across all push/pop sequences

Verify:
- Correct target address for all jumps
- Correct cycle count for taken vs not-taken (metamorphic test §16.5)
- Stack grows downward (PUSH decrements SP, POP increments)
- CALL pushes return address (PC after CALL instruction)
- RST pushes return address and jumps to vector

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: SAIF activity capture on representative ROM windows
description:
Implement nightly activity captures (§18.7 tier 2).

Setup:
- Select representative ROM windows: boot sequence, ALU-heavy loop, HALT-heavy idle, mixed game play
- Run simulation with SAIF (Switching Activity Interchange Format) capture enabled
- Record toggle counts per signal
- Compare against baseline

SAIF captures provide the ground truth for dynamic power estimation. They show exactly which signals toggle how often during real workloads.

Output:
- SAIF file per ROM window
- Summary report: top 20 highest-toggle signals
- Comparison against baseline (regression detection)

These should run in the nightly regression, not in the fast CI loop.

title: Define model, reset, and memory behavior profile enums
description:
Define the three orthogonal profile enums that parameterize ALL simulation and testing (§13).

ModelProfile (§13.1):
- DMG: original Game Boy
- CGB: Game Boy Color (future, but type must exist now)

ResetProfile (§13.1):
- SkipBoot: post-boot register/memory state, no boot ROM execution
- RawPowerOn: true power-on state (requires boot ROM)

MemoryBehaviorProfile (§13.1.1):
- DmgConservative: safe defaults for all ambiguous behaviors
- DmgRevisionSpecific: matches a specific DMG hardware revision

The MemoryBehaviorProfile fixes:
- Echo RAM mirroring policy (0xE000-0xFDFF mirrors 0xC000-0xDDFF)
- 0xFEA0-0xFEFF read behavior (prohibited region)
- Blocked VRAM/OAM read/write policy during PPU access
- DMA-era CPU accessibility restrictions

For CPU bring-up: pin ModelProfile::DMG and ResetProfile::SkipBoot. Record these in replay capsules and ROM manifests.

These profiles should be defined in BOTH:
1. Python (spec/compare_scopes.py or similar) for oracle/harness use
2. Spade (src/cpu/types.spade or similar) for HDL parameterization

RATIONALE: §13 calls this 'one of the highest-risk correctness traps.' If reset state is wrong, every test result is suspect. Making profiles explicit and recording them in replay capsules makes divergences diagnosable.

title: PPU: extract advance_timing shared phase helper
description:
The largest single dedup in this epic: extract the shared phase-advance logic from advance_timing, eliminating ~130 lines of duplication.

## Scope
Extract fn advance_phase(phase, dot_in_line, line, is_warmup_line0, input) -> (PpuPhase, PpuRenderState) that handles OamScan/Transfer/HBlank/VBlank transitions. Both the WarmupBlankFrame and Running arms of advance_timing call it with the appropriate is_warmup_line0 flag.

Also extract hblank_with_remaining(warmup_line0, dot_in_line) -> PpuPhase for the duplicated HBlank dots_left pattern.

## Why this is high-risk
advance_timing is the cycle-accurate PPU timing state machine. It controls mode transitions, scanline counting, VBlank entry, and LCD warmup. The warmup_line0 parameterization must correctly handle LCD-enable mid-frame edge cases. Thorough testing is essential.

## Acceptance Criteria
- advance_timing has no duplicated VBlank handler
- OamScan/Transfer/HBlank logic shared via parameterized helper
- HBlank dots_left helper extracted
- advance_timing entity is significantly shorter (primarily LcdRunState dispatch + LCD enable/disable)
- timing_test_top passes with full coverage

## Test Plan
- Run timing_test_top with extra attention to warmup scenarios
- Run core_test_top (end-to-end rendering)
- Run irq_test_top (STAT interrupt timing)
- Verify synthesis area does not regress

title: Green MEM_RWB soak/stress ROM
description:
Write the MEM_RWB soak test ROM and green it.

Purpose: stress-test memory read/write/readback across the address space accessible in Wave A.

Test approach:

- Write patterns to HRAM, read back, verify
- Test various access patterns: sequential, strided, pseudo-random (LFSR-driven)
- Verify read-after-write consistency
- Test boundary addresses (start/end of each region)
- Test that ROM reads return expected values

This is a soak/stress test — it runs many iterations to build confidence in memory infrastructure.

title: E2E milestone gate test: Milestone A -- harness ready
description:
Create tools/gate_milestone_a.sh -- the acceptance gate script for Milestone A (§22: Harness Ready).

This script validates ALL Milestone A acceptance criteria in one invocation. It must be runnable from a clean checkout and produce a clear PASS/FAIL verdict with detailed logging.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (§22 Milestone A):
1. Generators run reproducibly: run test_alu_generated_vectors.py twice with same seed, diff outputs
2. Local smoke scripts run from clean checkout: tools/smoke.sh oracle, cocotb, synth, formal all exit 0
3. Harness tests green: test_decode_completeness.py, test_reset_profile.py, test_event_script_determinism.py pass
4. HDL tests execute and fail SEMANTICALLY, not infrastructurally: test_cpu_lockstep.py runs, fails with value mismatch (not import error, compile error, or timeout)

SCRIPT STRUCTURE:
  #!/bin/bash
  set -euo pipefail
  PASS=0; FAIL=0
  
  echo "=== MILESTONE A GATE: Harness Ready ==="
  
  echo "--- Criterion 1: Generator reproducibility ---"
  # Run generators twice with seed=42, compare output
  
  echo "--- Criterion 2: Smoke scripts ---"
  # Run each smoke script, collect exit codes
  
  echo "--- Criterion 3: Harness tests ---"
  # Run harness test suite, check all green
  
  echo "--- Criterion 4: Semantic failure ---"
  # Run lockstep test, verify it fails with xfail (not crash)
  
  echo "=== MILESTONE A: $PASS passed, $FAIL failed ==="

LOGGING: Each criterion produces [GATE PASS] or [GATE FAIL] with explanation. On failure, the specific failing component and error message are shown.

WHY GATE TESTS: The plan defines milestones (§22) as sequential quality gates. Without automated gate tests, milestone claims are subjective. Gate tests make milestones objectively verifiable and provide a clear signal for when to proceed to the next phase.

ACCEPTANCE: Script exits 0 when all criteria pass, non-zero on any failure. Each criterion independently testable.

title: Timebase entity: T-cycle and M-cycle enables
description:
Create src/board/clockgen.spade with the canonical architectural timebase entity from §6.1.1.

Three clocks (§6.1):
1. board clock: physical iCEBreaker input clock
2. T-cycle: Game Boy timing quantum
3. M-cycle: 4 T-cycles

Timebase entity outputs:
  entity timebase(clk: clock, rst: bool) -> TimebaseOut {
    t_ce: bool,       // T-cycle clock enable
    m_ce: bool,       // M-cycle clock enable (every 4th T-cycle)
    sys_counter: uint<N>,  // canonical system counter
    t_index: uint<2>,      // 0-3 within current M-cycle
    m_index: uint<N>,      // M-cycle count
  }

DESIGN PRINCIPLES:
- CPU commits on m_ce, but cycle-sensitive devices derive edge timing from this shared source (§6.1.1)
- Timer and DIV derive from this shared counter, NOT private local counters
- This prevents timer, CPU, and DMA blocks from drifting by one cycle
- First milestone: m_ce can be asserted every N simulation clocks or even every clock (§6.5)
- Do NOT hardcode exact real-time frequency in first CPU milestone — logical correctness first
- Do NOT use fabric-gated clocks (§6.4) — single root clock + enables
- Harness may accelerate/decelerate m_ce but may NOT skip/synthesize architectural counter states

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (§22 Milestone C):

   - LOADS_BASIC, ALU_FLAGS, ALU16_SP, FLOW_STACK, CB_BITOPS pass against PyBoy oracle
   - MEM_RWB, ALU_LOOP soak/stress tests pass

title: Deterministic perturbation engine
description:
Implement the seeded deterministic perturbation engine (§16.5.1) in bench/actions/generators.py.

Core principle: ALL external stimuli are emitted from a seeded event script keyed by commit index or checkpoint symbol. The SAME event stream is applied to BOTH the DUT and the oracle. Events are NEVER keyed by wall-clock time.

Event types (matching SimStimulus from §12.6):
- Joypad button state changes (joyp_buttons)
- Interrupt flag set/clear (if_set_bits, if_clear_bits)
- IE override (ie_override)
- DMA start trigger (dma_start)
- Serial data injection (serial_inject)
- Architectural time freeze (freeze_arch_time)
- CPU-only hold (cpu_hold_only)

Generator interface:
- SeededEventScript: deterministic sequence from seed + ROM manifest
- Supports both hand-authored event lists and generated sequences
- Every event is (commit_index: int, event: SimEvent)

Do NOT use ad hoc hierarchical forcing (force/release) for ordinary test stimuli (§12.6). Reserve force/release for exceptional fault injection only.

title: Machine-readable opcode metadata source (sm83_opcodes.py)
description:
Create spec/sm83_opcodes.py — the single canonical opcode metadata source (§8.0).

This file is the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for static instruction facts. It must contain:
- opcode byte (0x00-0xFF for unprefixed, 0x00-0xFF for CB-prefix)
- prefix class (none, CB)
- mnemonic (e.g., 'LD B,C', 'ADD A,(HL)', 'BIT 3,H')
- instruction length in bytes (1, 2, or 3)
- cycle tuple(s): (not-taken, taken) for conditional ops, single value otherwise
- flag policy: which flags are affected and how (Z, N, H, C: set/reset/affected/unchanged)
- operand and addressing forms
- default compare scope for differential testing

Format: Python data structure (list of dicts or dataclass instances) that can be:
1. Imported by test generators (test_decode_completeness.py, test_alu_generated_vectors.py)
2. Used to generate Spade lookup tables if needed
3. Used to drive coverage tracking across opcode families

Sources to cross-reference: Pan Docs, gbxcule templates, Gekkio's opcode tables.

The phase machine remains hand-authored (§8.0). Only static instruction facts come from this file.

title: Extract phase_only_delta / fetch_micro helpers to cut CpuDelta boilerplate
description:
## Problem

In src/cpu/semantics.spade, the pattern:

    CpuDelta(
        idle_reg_write_set(),
        Option::None,  // pc_write
        Option::None,  // sp_write
        Option::None,  // ime_write
        Option::None,  // halt_write
        MicroWriteSet(some_phase(Phase::Fetch), Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None),
    )

appears at least ~30 times (lines 351, 360, 395-403, 477-484, 509-516, 536-548, 560-575, 587-594, 596-603, 612-619, 688-695, 697-703, 709-716, 724-731, 733-740, 749-763, 765-772, 785-798, 801-808, 811-818, 821-828, 835-848, 861-875, 877-884, 1289-1296, 1881-1926, 1953-1960, 1961-1968, 1971-1978, 1981-1988, 1991-1998, 2001-2008, 2011-2018, 2021-2028, 2031-2038, 2041-2048, 2067-2074, 2077-2084, 2087-2094). Each is 6-7 lines of positional Option::None fields.

Similarly, MicroWriteSet(some_phase(Phase::Fetch), Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None, Option::None) appears in the majority of those deltas.

## Solution

Add these helpers to semantics.spade (or a new cpu/helpers.spade):

1. **fetch_micro_write()** -> MicroWriteSet
   Returns MicroWriteSet$(phase: some_phase(Phase::Fetch), opcode_latch: Option::None, imm_lo: Option::None, imm_hi: Option::None, decoded: Option::None, temp: Option::None)

2. **phase_only_delta(phase: Phase)** -> CpuDelta
   Returns CpuDelta with idle_reg_write_set(), all None except micro_write with the given phase and all-None other micro fields.

3. **fetch_only_delta()** -> CpuDelta
   Calls phase_only_delta(Phase::Fetch). For the most common case.

4. **delta_with_micro(regs: RegWriteSet, pc: Option<uint<16>>, sp: Option<uint<16>>, micro: MicroWriteSet)** -> CpuDelta
   For cases that set regs/pc/sp but not ime/halt. This covers ~80% of non-trivial deltas.

5. **micro_with_phase_and_temp(phase: Phase, temp: uint<16>)** -> MicroWriteSet
   For the common pattern of advancing phase + storing temp.

6. **micro_with_phase_imm_hi_temp(phase: Phase, imm_hi: uint<8>, temp: uint<16>)** -> MicroWriteSet
   For word ALU cases that also set imm_hi.

## Scope

- Only src/cpu/semantics.spade is affected.
- Every call site that currently constructs CpuDelta/MicroWriteSet with positional arguments and mostly-None fields should be reviewed and converted.
- Estimated ~200 lines removed.
- No behavioral change — pure refactor. All existing tests must continue to pass.

## Verification

- Run the full test suite (tools/run_tests.py) before and after.
- Diff the Verilog output (swim build) to confirm synthesis equivalence.

title: ALU operand isolation (Idle case)
description:
Verify and enforce ALU operand isolation via AluReq::Idle (§11.2).

When AluReq::Idle:
- All ALU input operands should be zero/stable (not receiving toggling data)
- The ALU result should be a defined default (zeros)
- Large adders should not see unrelated toggling

Implementation:
- Ensure the match arm for Idle returns a known-zero AluResult
- Ensure the CPU control logic emits AluReq::Idle in all phases that don't need ALU computation
- Add a test that verifies Idle produces stable outputs

This is a POWER architecture decision (§11.2, §18.2), not aesthetics. In a real FPGA, toggling adder inputs wastes dynamic power even if the result is unused.

title: Timed event bridge types and DotInput
description:
Define the CPU-to-PPU event bridge types that replace single-write-per-dot MMIO input.

TYPES:

struct VideoCoord { frame: uint<32>, line: uint<8>, dot: uint<9> }

enum PpuEventKind {
  MmioWrite(MmioWrite),
  DmaStart(uint<8>),
  ForceLcdPower(bool),
}

struct TimedPpuEvent {
  seq: uint<64>,        -- monotonic, preserves same-dot ordering
  at: VideoCoord,       -- when in video time this event occurs
  kind: PpuEventKind,
}

struct DotInput {
  bus_events: [TimedPpuEvent; N],  -- N >= 2 for practical same-dot scenarios
  bus_event_count: uint<4>,
  mem_resp: PpuMemResp,
  dma_state: OamDmaState,
}

The CPU/bus fabric does NOT directly poke the PPU. A dedicated event bridge is
responsible for: (1) converting bus writes into timed video events, (2) preserving
same-dot ordering via seq, and (3) feeding the same event stream to DUT, reference
model, and replay artifacts.

WHY: Even if the bus normally serializes writes, the architecture needs a
representation that can express multiple same-dot events, ordering, and non-register
stimuli (DMA start, forced LCD power). This is also the canonical artifact format
for raster scripts, replay capsules, and cross-backend lockstep.

The old DotInput { mmio_write: Option<MmioWrite>, ... } was too weak because:
(a) it cannot express >1 event per dot, (b) it has no ordering guarantee for
same-dot events, (c) it mixes register writes with non-register stimuli.

title: Wave B.5 mealybug canary subset
description:
Before declaring BG/window semantics mature, run a tiny mealybug canary subset
covering only already-implemented surfaces.

CANARY TESTS:
- SCX low-3 handling (initial discard at scanline start)
- SCX high-5 refetch handling (tile fetch address computation)
- Window timing (WX trigger, window penalty)
- Selected palette timing where applicable

PURPOSE: Early architectural alarm bell. Mealybug explicitly focuses on mode-3
PPU-register changes and expected results differ across DMG/CGB revisions. That
is exactly the kind of early signal you want once BG/window fetch and window
activation exist, even before full object/composition signoff.

DO NOT require full mealybug signoff here. The goal is to catch gross architectural
mistakes (wrong sampling point, wrong penalty length) before investing in object
rendering. If a canary fails, the failure should be investigated and either:
- Fixed (if it reveals a real bug in BG/window implementation)
- Documented (if it reveals a known limitation staged for Wave D)

REVISION SENSITIVITY: mealybug expected results differ across DMG, CGB-C, CGB-D.
Use DMG expected results with BehaviorConfig { model: DMG }.
close_reason: Completed narrowed offline mealybug canary infrastructure; live DUT green-up deferred to follow-up bug

title: MBC1 mapper (ROM/RAM bank switching)
description:
Implement MBC1 memory bank controller.

MBC1 registers (memory-mapped, no reads):
- 0x0000-0x1FFF: RAM Enable (0x0A enables, other values disable)
- 0x2000-0x3FFF: ROM Bank Number (5 bits, bank 0 maps to 1)
- 0x4000-0x5FFF: RAM Bank Number / Upper ROM Bank (2 bits)
- 0x6000-0x7FFF: Banking Mode Select (0=ROM mode, 1=RAM mode)

Address mapping:
- 0x0000-0x3FFF: ROM bank 0 (or bank N*0x20 in RAM mode)
- 0x4000-0x7FFF: ROM bank N (5-bit + optional 2-bit upper)
- 0xA000-0xBFFF: External RAM (if enabled, 8KB per bank, up to 4 banks)

Special cases:
- ROM bank 0x00 maps to 0x01 (can never select bank 0 in upper region)
- Similarly 0x20→0x21, 0x40→0x41, 0x60→0x61
- RAM mode changes which ROM bank appears in 0x0000-0x3FFF

title: ROM manifest schema updates for revised PPU architecture
description:
Update bench/manifests/rom_inventory.yaml and rom_schema.yaml to reflect the
revised PPU architecture.

NEW MANIFEST FIELDS:
- rom_sha256: content hash for reproducibility
- schema_version: artifact format version
- build_flavor: which build profile (synth, sim_semantic, sim_trace, sim_power)
- memory_backend: which memory backend (sim_array, inferred_sync_ram)
- behavior_config: { model, soc_revision, features } instead of behavior_profile
- rule_confidence: per-ROM confidence level
- allowed_uncertainty: which surfaces allow Hypothesis-tier results
- raster_event_coord_space: frame_line_dot | video_anchor | symbolic
- expected_soc_revisions: replaces expected_behavior_profile
- required_behavior_features: features that must be enabled for this ROM
- forbidden_behavior_features: features that must be disabled

Set up bench/schemas/:
- replay_capsule.schema.json: deterministic replay format
- oracle_capture.schema.json: captured oracle output format
- line_summary.schema.json: per-scanline comparison format

UPDATE EXISTING ROM ENTRIES:
- PPU_STAT_IRQ, BG_STATIC, BG_SCROLL_SIGNED, PPU_WINDOW, PPU_SPRITES: add
  new fields, update oracle_mode to use revised vocabulary.

ADD NEW ROM ENTRIES for Wave A timing ROMs:
- PPU_OFF_ON_BASIC, LY_LYC_BASIC, STAT_MODE_SEQ, VBLANK_IRQ_BASIC, VRAM_OAM_GATING

These entries should reference BehaviorConfig { model: DMG, features: {} } as
the default for all DMG bring-up ROMs.

title: PPU: deduplicate set_lcd_enable and unify render state init
description:
Remove duplicated set_lcd_enable and unify blanked/initial render state constructors.

## Scope
1. Delete set_lcd_enable from one of timing.spade / step.spade. Canonical location: compose from existing replace_lcdc helper in regs.spade.
2. Unify blanked_render_state (timing.spade:~88-101) and initial_ppu_render_state (sem/types.spade:~315-328). They differ only in first_frame_blank field. Make initial_ppu_render_state accept a parameter.
3. Remove trivial re-export wrappers visible_mode / lcd_enabled from timing.spade (~127-133) if callers can import from sem::types directly.

## Acceptance Criteria
- set_lcd_enable in exactly one location
- blanked_render_state calls initial_ppu_render_state (or vice versa)
- No trivial re-exports unless required by module visibility
- timing_test_top, core_test_top pass

title: Phase machine for ALU instructions
description:
Implement phase machine for 8-bit ALU instruction families in src/cpu/control.spade and src/cpu/semantics.spade.

Instruction families:
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP A,r: single M-cycle, ALU op on register operand
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP A,n: fetch immediate, then ALU op
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP A,(HL): read from (HL), then ALU op
- INC/DEC r: single M-cycle with flag-preserving behavior
- INC/DEC (HL): read-modify-write at (HL) — read, ALU, write back
- DAA/CPL/SCF/CCF: single M-cycle, misc ALU ops on A
- RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA: single M-cycle, accumulator rotates (UNPREFIXED — distinct from CB-prefix rotates!)
  CRITICAL: These set Z=0 ALWAYS, unlike CB-prefix rotates which set Z based on result. This is one of the most common implementation bugs. The ALU must handle both variants correctly.

Phase transitions:
- Register-register: Fetch → Execute (1 M-cycle)
- Immediate: Fetch → ReadImm8{k: AluFromImm} → Execute (2 M-cycles)
- Memory: Fetch → ReadMem{addr: HL, k: AluFromMem{kind}} → Execute (2 M-cycles)
- INC/DEC (HL): Fetch → ReadMem → ALU → WriteMem → Done (3 M-cycles)
- Accumulator rotates: Fetch → Execute (1 M-cycle, uses ALU with Z-force-clear flag)

The ALU is invoked via AluReq — the phase machine selects the correct AluReq variant based on DecodedOp. For RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA, the phase machine must use a variant that forces Z=0 (or the ALU must have a separate accumulator-rotate variant).

TESTING: Each instruction family needs at least one curated test case. The RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA Z-flag behavior MUST have explicit test cases comparing against CB-prefix equivalents to catch the most common GB CPU implementation bug.

title: Object selection and draw priority unit tests (test/ppu/unit/test_obj_priority.py)
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for OAM scan selection and per-pixel draw priority.

SELECTION TESTS (SelectedObjTicket stage):

1. BASIC SELECTION:
   Place one sprite at Y=16+LY (visible on current line). Verify it is selected.
   Verify SelectedObjTicket fields: oam_index, x, y, selection_rank.

2. 10-OBJECT LIMIT:
   Place 11 sprites all visible on same line. Verify exactly 10 selected.
   Verify 11th sprite (by OAM order) is excluded.

3. SELECTION ORDER:
   Place 5 sprites with various Y values. Verify selection order matches OAM
   entry order, NOT Y position or X position.

4. X-HIDDEN OBJECTS STILL COUNT:
   Place sprite at X=0 (off-screen left). Verify it IS selected and counts
   toward the 10-object limit.
   Place sprite at X=168 (off-screen right). Same verification.

5. 8x16 MODE SELECTION:
   Set LCDC.2=1 (8x16 sprites). Verify Y overlap check uses height=16.

6. NO SELECTION WHEN OBJ DISABLED:
   Clear LCDC.1 (OBJ enable). Verify no objects selected.

PRIORITY TESTS (ObjPixel stage):

7. DMG DRAW PRIORITY - SMALLER X WINS:
   Two sprites overlap at same pixel. Sprite A at X=10, Sprite B at X=20.
   Both have non-transparent pixel at overlap. Verify Sprite A pixel wins.

8. DMG DRAW PRIORITY - OAM ORDER BREAKS TIES:
   Two sprites at same X. OAM index 5 and OAM index 10. Verify index 5 wins.

9. TRANSPARENCY:
   Sprite pixel with color 0 is transparent. Verify BG pixel shows through.

10. BG-OVER-OBJ FLAG:
    Set sprite attribute bit 7. Verify sprite shows ONLY where BG color is 0.

11. BG ENABLE INTERACTION:
    Clear LCDC.0. Verify BG is all white, sprites render normally.

LOGGING: Log each SelectedObjTicket with oam_index, x, y, rank. Log each
ObjPixel with color, palette, draw_rank, bg_over_obj. On priority mismatch,
log both candidate pixels and the resolution rule that should have applied.
close_reason: Added object selection and draw-priority unit coverage with hook wiring

title: ALU implementation: DAA, CPL, SCF, CCF
description:
Implement miscellaneous ALU operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- DAA: Decimal Adjust Accumulator (BCD correction)
- CPL: Complement A (bitwise NOT), flags N=1, H=1, Z/C preserved
- SCF: Set Carry Flag, flags N=0, H=0, C=1, Z preserved
- CCF: Complement Carry Flag, flags N=0, H=0, C=!C, Z preserved

TRICKY: DAA (§11.3) is the single most complex ALU operation:
- Behavior depends on N flag (was last op add or subtract?)
- Behavior depends on H flag and C flag from previous operation
- Behavior depends on current value of A
- After ADD/ADC (N=0): if A>0x99 or C, add 0x60 and set C; if (A&0xF)>9 or H, add 0x06
- After SUB/SBC (N=1): if C, subtract 0x60; if H, subtract 0x06
- Z flag set if result==0, N preserved, H=0, C set if correction overflowed

Do NOT re-derive DAA from memory. Translate the exact gbxcule formula.

title: Video access policy unit tests (test/ppu/unit/test_access_policy.py)
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for the video access policy module (bd-252k).

The access policy is a pure function: given (ppu_mode, region, client, dma_active)
return PpuMemResult (Ok/Denied/UndefinedRead with VideoBlockReason). This truth
table is the PPU's most critical bus contract and must be tested exhaustively.

TEST CASES:

1. MODE 2 (OAM SCAN) ACCESS:
   - CPU reads VRAM: Ok (VRAM accessible in mode 2)
   - CPU reads OAM: UndefinedRead(CpuBlockedByMode2)
   - CPU writes VRAM: Ok
   - CPU writes OAM: Denied(CpuBlockedByMode2)
   - PPU reads OAM (OamScanner): Ok (PPU owns OAM in mode 2)

2. MODE 3 (PIXEL TRANSFER) ACCESS:
   - CPU reads VRAM: UndefinedRead(CpuBlockedByMode3)
   - CPU reads OAM: UndefinedRead(CpuBlockedByMode3)
   - CPU writes VRAM: Denied(CpuBlockedByMode3)
   - CPU writes OAM: Denied(CpuBlockedByMode3)
   - PPU reads VRAM (BgFetcher): Ok
   - PPU reads OAM (ObjFetcher): Ok

3. MODE 0 (HBLANK) ACCESS:
   - CPU reads VRAM: Ok
   - CPU reads OAM: Ok
   - CPU writes VRAM: Ok
   - CPU writes OAM: Ok

4. MODE 1 (VBLANK) ACCESS:
   - Same as mode 0: all accessible

5. LCD OFF ACCESS:
   - All CPU and PPU accesses to VRAM and OAM: Ok
   - Verify no blocking regardless of would-be mode

6. OAM DMA ACTIVE:
   - CPU reads VRAM: Ok (DMA does not block VRAM)
   - CPU reads OAM: UndefinedRead(CpuBlockedByOamDma)
   - CPU reads HRAM: Ok (HRAM always accessible during DMA)
   - CPU reads non-HRAM: depends on DmgConservative behavior

7. OAM DMA + MODE 3 (COMBINED):
   - Both DMA and mode 3 block OAM: verify reason priority
   - CPU VRAM blocked by mode 3 even if DMA active

8. BOUNDARY CASES:
   - Address at exact VRAM/OAM boundaries (0x8000, 0x9FFF, 0xFE00, 0xFE9F)
   - Mode transitions: verify access changes on exact dot of transition

LOGGING FORMAT (bd-dny):
  [SUITE] test_access_policy.py
    [CASE] test_mode2_oam_blocked
      [STEP] Set mode=OamScan, region=Oam, client=Cpu
      [CHECK] result: expected=UndefinedRead(CpuBlockedByMode2) actual=... OK
    [CASE] test_mode3_all_blocked
      [STEP] Set mode=Transfer, region=Vram, client=Cpu
      [CHECK] result: expected=UndefinedRead(CpuBlockedByMode3) actual=... OK

FAILURE DIAGNOSTICS:
On mismatch, log: mode, region, client, dma_active, lcd_enabled, expected result,
actual result, VideoBlockReason comparison.

title: CPU core single-instruction differential tests
description:
Create test/unit/test_cpu_single_op.py — targeted single-instruction differential tests that verify individual instructions against the PyBoy oracle WITHOUT requiring full ROM execution.

PURPOSE: ROM tests (LOADS_BASIC etc.) test many instructions together. When a ROM fails, it can be hard to isolate WHICH instruction is broken. These tests isolate individual instructions and provide maximally detailed diagnostics.

TEST APPROACH:
For each instruction:
1. Initialize DUT and oracle to identical known state (SkipBoot reset profile)
2. Place a single instruction (+ operands) at the current PC in ROM
3. Place a HALT or infinite loop after the instruction
4. Step DUT through the instruction (known number of M-cycles from opcode metadata)
5. Step oracle through the same instruction
6. Compare full register state, flags, PC, SP, bus activity

LOGGING (per instruction test):
  [TEST] LD B,0x42 (opcode 0x06, 2 bytes, 2 M-cycles)
    [BEFORE] PC=0x0100 A=0x00 F=0x00 B=0x00 C=0x00 DE=0x0000 HL=0x0000 SP=0xFFFE
    [M-CYCLE 1] phase=Fetch bus=Read(0x0100) resp=0x06
    [M-CYCLE 2] phase=ReadImm8 bus=Read(0x0101) resp=0x42
    [AFTER]  PC=0x0102 A=0x00 F=0x00 B=0x42 C=0x00 DE=0x0000 HL=0x0000 SP=0xFFFE
    [ORACLE] PC=0x0102 A=0x00 F=0x00 B=0x42 C=0x00 DE=0x0000 HL=0x0000 SP=0xFFFE
    [PASS] All fields match

INSTRUCTION COVERAGE (parameterized):
- At least one representative from each opcode family
- Driven by the canonical opcode metadata (spec/sm83_opcodes.py)
- Flag-affecting instructions get additional parameterized inputs to test flag transitions

CORNER CASES TO EXPLICITLY TEST:
- LD (HL),n: verify the memory write occurs at the correct address
- POP AF: verify F low nibble is masked to zero
- RLCA vs CB RLC A: verify Z flag difference (the #1 GB CPU implementation bug)
- ADD SP,e8 with negative offset: verify signed extension and flag behavior
- DAA after various flag states: the trickiest flag computation in the entire instruction set
- Conditional branches: verify cycle count differs between taken/not-taken
- INC/DEC (HL): verify read-modify-write sequence

RELATIONSHIP TO ROM TESTS: These are FINER-GRAINED than ROM tests. If LOADS_BASIC fails, run these tests to pinpoint which LD variant is broken. These tests run faster (no ROM build needed) and give more detail.

title: Verify roms/build_micro_rom.py works
close_reason: Verified roms/build_micro_rom.py: fails in bare repo due to missing gbxcule, but succeeds with PYTHONPATH=/Users/bkase/Documents/gbxcule/src; generated 23 deterministic ROMs with valid checksums.

title: BG FIFO, window trigger/counter, and SCX discard (src/ppu/rtl/fifo.spade)
description:
Implement the BG pixel FIFO, window activation logic, and SCX initial discard.

BG FIFO:
- Up to 16 pixels deep (enough for current tile + next tile prefetch)
- Each entry: uint<2> color index + metadata
- Pop from front, push from fetcher
- Cleared on window activation

WINDOW TRIGGER LOGIC:
Uses explicit WindowState enum:
  Inactive -> ArmedThisFrame -> ActiveOnLine { win_x, win_line }

Activation conditions (all must be true for window to appear):
1. LCDC.5 (window enable) was set at mode-2 start (PpuSamplingState.window_enable_at_mode2_start)
2. WY condition triggered this frame (PpuSamplingState.wy_triggered_this_frame):
   WY <= LY at some mode-2 start during this frame
3. WX condition met: rendered X + 7 >= WX (live comparison)

Window internal line counter:
- Resets at VBlank
- Increments ONLY on scanlines where the window actually starts rendering
- Mid-frame window hide/show affects later window line selection
- This is why WindowState must be first-class state, not inferred

SCX DISCARD:
At the start of each visible scanline, SCX[2:0] (sampled in PpuSamplingState) determines
how many pixels to discard from the first BG tile fetch. This adds 0-7 dots to mode 3.

The discard counter runs during the first tile fetch and delays pixel output by
scx_low3_line dots. The renderer does not emit pixels during discard.

title: PPU replay, comparison, and line narrative tools
description:
Create tooling for PPU divergence analysis, replay, and human-readable debugging.

FILES:
- tools/compare_oracles.py: cross-oracle differential comparison driver
- tools/replay_capsule.py: serialize/deserialize/replay stimulus streams
- tools/render_line_narrative.py: human-readable line-level divergence explanation

compare_oracles.py:
  Drives DUT and one or more oracles (ppu_ref, pyboy, rom_self_check) through
  the same stimulus stream. Reports first divergence point with context.
  Supports all compare scopes: dot_commit, scanline_summary, frame_hash.

replay_capsule.py:
  A replay capsule contains: ROM id, model/reset profile, BehaviorConfig, seed,
  raster event log (as TimedPpuEvent stream), first bad frame/line/dot.
  Capsules are schema-versioned (bench/schemas/replay_capsule.schema.json).
  Can replay against DUT, reference model, or both for minimization.

render_line_narrative.py:
  Given a divergence point, produces a human-readable explanation showing:
  - First differing semantic event
  - Last matching fetch epoch
  - Object selection tickets at divergence
  - Window activation state
  - Access-policy outcomes around the divergence
  - Causal chain (e.g. stale response from pre-window-restart epoch)
  This is often faster to debug than raw waveforms.

DIVERGENCE ARTIFACTS (emitted on comparison failure):
  - First bad dot or scanline
  - Window of commits around the failure
  - Minimized replay capsule
  - Localized waveform
  - Optional line diff and frame diff heatmap
  - Schema version, ROM sha256, manifest hash
  - Enabled behavior features / model / revision
  - Backend and tool versions
  - Human-readable line narrative

title: PPU timing dedup: set_lcd_enable, advance_timing, identical arms
description:
The PPU timing module has ~130 lines of near-duplicate code between WarmupBlankFrame and Running run states, plus duplicated helpers shared with the semantic layer. This is the highest-impact PPU cleanup.

## Background

The Game Boy PPU has a one-frame warmup after LCD enable where the first frame is blanked. The RTL models this as two LcdRunState variants: WarmupBlankFrame and Running. Both share nearly identical phase-advance logic (OamScan → Transfer → HBlank → VBlank cycling), but the warmup path has special dot thresholds for line 0.

During initial implementation, the two paths were written independently. Now that the logic is stable, the duplication is clear: the VBlank handler (lines ~268-298 vs ~378-408) is character-for-character identical. OamScan, Transfer, and HBlank differ only in whether warmup_line0 is true.

Similarly, the semantic layer (sem/step.spade, sem/types.spade) has functions where WarmupBlankFrame and Running arms return the same expression — these are trivially collapsible.

## Scope

### 1. set_lcd_enable duplicated verbatim
timing.spade:~103-125 and step.spade:~26-48 contain character-identical copies. Both rebuild the entire PpuRegs struct to toggle lcdc.lcd_enable.

Fix: Delete one copy. The canonical location should be wherever PpuRegs helpers live (regs.spade already has replace_lcdc which handles the PpuRegs spread). Compose: call replace_lcdc with a modified Lcdc that has lcd_enable toggled.

### 2. advance_timing WarmupBlankFrame/Running dedup (~130 lines)
The WarmupBlankFrame block (~lines 156-300) and Running block (~301-410) share nearly identical phase-advance logic. Differences:
- WarmupBlankFrame has special line-0 warmup paths with different dot thresholds
- Running case is WarmupBlankFrame with warmup_line0 = false always
- VBlank handling is byte-identical

Fix: Extract fn advance_phase(phase: PpuPhase, dot_in_line: uint<9>, line: uint<8>, is_warmup_line0: bool, input: DotInput) -> (PpuPhase, PpuRenderState) that handles all phase transitions. Both match arms call it with the appropriate is_warmup_line0 flag.

### 3. HBlank dots_left helper
The pattern:
  if warmup_line0 { HBlank\$(dots_left: warmup_line0_dots_left(dot+1)) }
  else { HBlank\$(dots_left: dots_left_in_line(dot+1)) }
appears at Transfer-complete and HBlank-continuation.

Fix: Extract fn hblank_with_remaining(warmup_line0: bool, dot_in_line: uint<9>) -> PpuPhase.

### 4. Identical WarmupBlankFrame/Running arms in sem module
- visible_mode (sem/types.spade:~358-362): WarmupBlankFrame and Running both return phase_mode(status.phase). Collapse to _ => phase_mode(status.phase).
- lcd_enabled (sem/types.spade:~366-372): WarmupBlankFrame and Running both return regs.lcdc.lcd_enable. Collapse.
- next_dot_in_line (sem/step.spade:~91-99): Same. Collapse.

### 5. blanked_render_state / initial_ppu_render_state near-duplicate
timing.spade:~88-101 vs sem/types.spade:~315-328. Almost identical 10-field struct — only first_frame_blank differs. Make initial_ppu_render_state take a parameter, or have blanked_render_state call it and override one field.

### 6. Trivial re-export wrappers (timing.spade:~127-133)
visible_mode and lcd_enabled forward to sem::types versions with no modification. Remove if module visibility allows direct import. Document if wrappers are required by Spade's module system.

## Considerations
- The advance_timing refactor is the largest single change in this epic. It touches the PPU's cycle-accurate timing logic, which is notoriously sensitive. Careful testing is essential.
- The warmup_line0 parameterization must correctly handle the edge case where the LCD is enabled mid-frame. The current code has this right; the refactored version must preserve it.
- After extracting advance_phase, the advance_timing entity should be much shorter and primarily handle the LcdRunState dispatch + LCD enable/disable transitions.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. set_lcd_enable exists in exactly one location
2. advance_timing has no duplicated VBlank handler
3. OamScan/Transfer/HBlank logic shared via parameterized helper
4. HBlank dots_left helper extracted
5. All identical WarmupBlankFrame/Running arms in sem module collapsed
6. blanked_render_state / initial_ppu_render_state unified
7. Re-export wrappers removed or documented
8. No behavioral change in timing

## Test Plan
- swim test passes for timing_test_top (primary timing verification)
- core_test_top passes (exercises timing through full rendering)
- irq_test_top passes (STAT interrupt timing depends on phase transitions)
- PPU backend diff scaffold passes
- soc_lockstep_top and soc_rom_top tests pass
- Synth gate passes (PPU timing is on the critical path for area)

title: Add integrated object-fetch timing and penalty tests
description: Follow up bd-2e95 with integrated mode-3 object-fetch timing tests once the object fetch path is driven by the live cadence engine. Cover penalty dots, cumulative timing with multiple objects on one line, and cadence-sensitive cancellation/ordering cases that cannot be observed from the current pure obj_fetch helper top.

title: ALU implementation: 8-bit arithmetic (ADD, ADC, SUB, SBC, CP)
description:
Implement 8-bit arithmetic operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- ADD A,r/n: A + operand, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=half-carry, C=carry
- ADC A,r/n: A + operand + carry_in, same flags but H/C include carry_in
- SUB r/n: A - operand, flags Z=result==0, N=1, H=half-borrow, C=borrow
- SBC A,r/n: A - operand - carry_in, same flags but H/C include carry_in
- CP r/n: same as SUB but result discarded, only flags set

TRICKY FLAG CASES (§11.3, lift from gbxcule):
- ADC half-carry: (A & 0xF) + (operand & 0xF) + carry_in > 0xF
- ADC carry: A + operand + carry_in > 0xFF (use wider arithmetic)
- SBC half-borrow: (A & 0xF) - (operand & 0xF) - carry_in < 0 (signed)
- SBC borrow: A - operand - carry_in < 0

Do NOT re-derive from memory. Translate actual gbxcule formulas.
Verify with generated vectors from spec/flag_policies.py.

title: Placeholder lockstep test as xfail
description:
Create test/lockstep/test_cpu_lockstep.py as xfail(strict=True) (Phase 1 step 11).

This test will eventually run live CPU lockstep comparison between the Spade DUT and the PyBoy oracle. Initially it should:
1. Exist as a proper test file
2. Be marked xfail(strict=True) — it MUST fail, and if it accidentally passes, that's a test error
3. Attempt to: load a simple ROM, reset both DUT and oracle, step one instruction, compare CommitTrace
4. Fail because the HDL stub doesn't produce correct values

The xfail is removed in Phase 4 when the private step adapter is ready (Phase 4 step 32).

IMPORTANT (§16.6): Keep the live oracle interface and replay capsule format stable from day one. If strict M-cycle stepping isn't wired up yet, gate only instruction-boundary suites. Do NOT introduce a file-trace fallback as a temporary workaround.

title: Promote strict lockstep from xfail to targeted green subset
description:
Remove the xfail from test_cpu_lockstep.py for a targeted owned Wave B subset.

Scope:
- Promote strict instruction/M-cycle lockstep from placeholder to expected-pass on deliberately instrumented ROMs such as EI_DELAY and TIMER_IRQ_HALT
- Keep strict lockstep limited to owned ROMs with symbols/hooks
- Leave broader suites at coarser-grained comparison modes until they are intentionally promoted

Why add this bead: Phase 4 step 32 in the plan is a real milestone and should not be hidden inside the placeholder xfail task.

title: Python reference specs: flag_policies.py and compare_scopes.py
description:
Create the spec/ directory Python reference implementations that serve as truth sources for test generation and oracle comparison.

FILES:
- spec/flag_policies.py: Canonical flag computation formulas for every ALU operation, lifted directly from gbxcule templates (§11.3). This is the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for flag behavior. Used by test_alu_generated_vectors.py to generate exhaustive test vectors. Every ALU Spade implementation must match these formulas exactly.
  - ADD/ADC: Z=(result==0), N=0, H=((a&0xF)+(b&0xF)+carry>0xF), C=(result>0xFF)
  - SUB/SBC/CP: Z=(result==0), N=1, H=((a&0xF)-(b&0xF)-carry<0), C=(a-b-carry<0)
  - AND: Z=(result==0), N=0, H=1, C=0
  - OR/XOR: Z=(result==0), N=0, H=0, C=0
  - INC: Z=(result==0), N=0, H=((val&0xF)+1>0xF), C=preserved
  - DEC: Z=(result==0), N=1, H=((val&0xF)-1<0), C=preserved
  - DAA: complex (depends on N, H, C flags — must match gbxcule exactly)
  - 16-bit ADD HL: Z=preserved, N=0, H=(bit 11 carry), C=(bit 15 carry)
  - ADD SP,e8 / LD HL,SP+e8: Z=0, N=0, H=(low byte half-carry), C=(low byte carry)
  - Rotates (CB): Z=(result==0), N=0, H=0, C=rotated-out bit
  - Rotates (unprefixed RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA): Z=0 ALWAYS, N=0, H=0, C=rotated-out bit
  - BIT: Z=(bit==0), N=0, H=1, C=preserved
  - SWAP: Z=(result==0), N=0, H=0, C=0

- spec/compare_scopes.py: Defines what to compare at each oracle comparison granularity (§7.2 CommitKind). Maps OracleMode to the set of fields checked. E.g., Unit mode checks only targeted register; InstrCommit checks full register state + flags; MCycleCommit checks everything including bus activity.

WHY SEPARATE FROM OPCODE METADATA: sm83_opcodes.py (bd-36u) has opcode structure (encoding, length, cycles). flag_policies.py has the actual flag COMPUTATION FORMULAS. compare_scopes.py has the comparison STRATEGY. Three orthogonal concerns.

title: Flatten nested Option match pyramids with tuple matching
description:
## Problem

In src/cpu/control.spade, the function loadish_fetch_phase (lines 38-381, 343 lines) contains deeply nested matches on Option::Some/Option::None that reach 6-8 levels of indentation. The same pattern occurs in src/cpu/semantics.spade in:

- direct_load_reg_writes (lines 920-955)
- direct_load_sp_write (lines 957-1007)
- direct_load_phase (lines 1009-1059)
- handle_execute Load arm (lines 1933-2050)
- load_hl_adjust (lines 1581-1626)
- fetch_imm_hi_seed (lines 648-679)

The nesting is caused by sequential unwrapping of Option fields:

    match dst8 {
        Option::Some(dst8_value) => {
            match src8 {
                Option::Some(src8_value) => {
                    match dst8_value {
                        Operand8::Register$(r8) => {
                            match src8_value {
                                Operand8::Imm8 => /* actual logic */,
                                _ => Phase::Fetch,
                            }
                        },
                        _ => Phase::Fetch,
                    }
                },
                Option::None => Phase::Fetch,
            }
        },
        Option::None => Phase::Fetch,
    }

## Solution

Use Spade's tuple matching to flatten. The above becomes:

    match (dst8, src8) {
        (Option::Some(Operand8::Register$(r8)), Option::Some(Operand8::Imm8)) => /* actual logic */,
        (Option::Some(Operand8::AddrHl), Option::Some(Operand8::Imm8)) => /* ... */,
        _ => Phase::Fetch,
    }

### Files to change

**src/cpu/control.spade:**
- loadish_fetch_phase: Refactor each AddressingMode arm to use (dst8, src8) or (dst8, src8, dst16, src16) tuple matching. Focus especially on:
  - Immediate8 arm (lines 43-79): 36 lines -> ~12 lines
  - Immediate16 arm (lines 80-104): 24 lines -> ~8 lines
  - Register16 arm (lines 105-129): 24 lines -> ~8 lines
  - MemoryViaHl arm (lines 130-159): 29 lines -> ~10 lines
  - MemoryViaRegPair arm (lines 160-185): 25 lines -> ~10 lines
  - MemoryImm16 arm (lines 238-313): 75 lines -> ~20 lines
  - IoImm8 arm (lines 315-341): 26 lines -> ~8 lines
  - IoC arm (lines 342-368): 26 lines -> ~8 lines

- aluish_fetch_phase: Flatten the BitOp arm (lines 410-443)

- cb_prefixed_fetch_phase: Minor flattening of Option::Some(PrefixKind::Cb) (lines 547-562)

**src/cpu/semantics.spade:**
- direct_load_reg_writes (lines 920-955): Flatten (dst8, src8) match
- direct_load_sp_write (lines 957-1007): Flatten (dst8, src8, dst16, src16) match — currently 50 lines of nesting for a single instruction check
- direct_load_phase (lines 1009-1059): Same as above, same structure
- handle_execute Load arm (lines 1933-2050): Flatten the 120-line nested match
- load_hl_adjust (lines 1581-1626): Flatten (dst8, src8) nesting
- fetch_imm_hi_seed (lines 648-679): Flatten phase_write + decoded nesting

## Estimated savings

~150 lines removed across both files. Remaining code is significantly more readable with flat pattern matching.

## Verification

- All tests must pass.
- Diff Verilog output to confirm synthesis equivalence.
- Pay attention to Spade's exhaustiveness checker — you may need explicit wildcard arms for tuple matches.

title: STAT line evaluation, LYC comparison, and IRQ generation
description:
Implement the STAT interrupt generation as a continuously-evaluated OR line with
rising-edge detection.

STAT LINE MODEL:
The hardware continuously evaluates multiple STAT sources, ORs them into a shared
STAT line, and only requests a STAT interrupt on a LOW-TO-HIGH transition. This is
why STAT blocking exists: consecutive enabled sources that dont let the line fall
between them suppress the second interrupt.

STAT SOURCES (ORed together):
- Mode 0 (HBlank) interrupt enable (STAT bit 3)
- Mode 1 (VBlank) interrupt enable (STAT bit 4)
- Mode 2 (OAM scan) interrupt enable (STAT bit 5)
- LY==LYC coincidence interrupt enable (STAT bit 6)

Each source fires when its condition is true AND its select bit is set.

LYC COMPARISON: Continuously evaluated. LY==LYC flag is bit 2 of STAT (derived).
When LYC select is enabled, this feeds the STAT line.

IRQ GENERATION:
  struct StatIrqState { line_high: bool }
  On each dot, compute new_line from all enabled sources.
  If !line_high && new_line: request STAT interrupt.
  Update line_high = new_line.

DMG STAT-WRITE QUIRK: A write to STAT on DMG can spuriously trigger the STAT
interrupt. This is a BehaviorFeature flag (DmgStatWriteQuirk), staged behind
a feature gate. Hook point should exist even if the feature is not enabled
in the default BehaviorConfig.

OUTPUT: PpuIrqReq { vblank_req: bool, stat_req: bool }
VBlank is requested on mode 1 entry. STAT is requested on rising edge.
acceptance_criteria:
1. evaluate_stat pure function takes (regs, mode, ly, old_stat_state) and returns (new_stat_state, irq_req)
2. STAT line is OR of all enabled sources (mode 0/1/2 select + LYC select)
3. IRQ fires on LOW→HIGH transition only (rising-edge, not level)
4. LYC==LY flag (STAT bit 2) is continuously evaluated
5. VBlank IRQ request on mode 1 entry (separate from STAT)
6. DMG STAT-write quirk hook point exists (behind BehaviorFeature flag)
7. LCD disabled → STAT line evaluates to false

title: Observability types (PpuSemanticCommit, TransferDigest, LineSummary, DotOutput)
description:
Define the comparison, observability, and output types for PPU verification.

SEMANTIC COMMIT (sim_semantic build flavor only):

struct PpuSemanticCommit {
  ly_after: uint<8>,
  mode_after: PpuMode,
  stat_line_after: bool,
  irq_edge: PpuIrqEdge,
  scanout: Option<ScanoutEvent>,
}

NOTE: No seq: uint<64> field. The old PpuObservedCommit carried a 64-bit sequence
number which is too much default baggage for synthesis. Sequence numbering belongs
in the simulation wrapper, not the semantic core.

TRANSFER EVENT RECORDING:

enum TransferEvent {
  BgWarmup, ScxDiscard(uint<3>), WindowRestart,
  ObjFetchStart { oam_index: uint<6> }, ObjFetchCancel, PixelPop { x: uint<8> },
}

struct TransferDigest {
  event_count: uint<16>, event_hash: uint<64>,
  window_restart_count: uint<2>, obj_fetch_count: uint<4>,
}

enum StrictnessTier { Stable, ResearchBacked, Hypothesis }

WHY TransferDigest instead of Mode3PenaltyBreakdown: Pan Docs pixel FIFO chapter
notes some SCX/object penalty timing is not confirmed and needs more research.
mode3_len is a good stable surface; sub-penalty decomposition is NOT. Record
transfer subevents and derive summaries; keep fine-grained breakdown as a derived
debug artifact under explicit strictness tiers.

LINE SUMMARY:

struct LineSummary {
  frame: uint<32>, ly: uint<8>, mode3_len: uint<9>,
  window_start_x: Option<uint<8>>, window_line_after: uint<8>,
  obj_count: uint<4>, selected_objs: [Option<uint<6>>; 10],
  transfer: TransferDigest, line_hash: uint<64>,
}

NOTE: selected_objs uses Option to make absence explicit, rather than relying
on obj_count + zero-fill conventions.

DOT OUTPUT:

struct DotOutput {
  next_state: PpuState, mem_reqs: PpuMemReqs, mmio_resp: PpuMmioResp,
  irq_req: PpuIrqReq, scanout: Option<ScanoutEvent>,
  semantic: Option<PpuSemanticCommit>, line_summary: Option<LineSummary>,
}

NOTE: No trace: PpuDebugTrace field in the default DotOutput. Debug trace belongs
in the sim_trace build flavor, not the default core.

title: PPU core state types and derived projections (src/ppu/sem/types.spade)

Create tools/gate_milestone_b.sh -- acceptance gate for Milestone B (§22: ALU + Decode Ready).

3. Single-op tests for LD, INC, DEC, ADD, AND, XOR, OR, CP green: test_cpu_single_op.py passes for these families

SCRIPT VALIDATES:
