title: Bounded formal safety checks on PPU control blocks
description:
Add SymbiYosys-based bounded model checking on narrow PPU control blocks.

TARGET PROPERTIES:

1. Mode state machine:
   - Phase sequence invariant: visible lines always go OamScan->Transfer->HBlank
   - VBlank only on lines 144-153
   - No mode transition occurs except on dot_ce
   - LCD-off produces no mode transitions

2. STAT line:
   - IRQ only fires on rising edge (not level)
   - STAT line is false when LCD disabled
   - No spurious IRQ when no select bits enabled (except STAT-write quirk)

3. Window state:
   - Window cannot be ActiveOnLine without ArmedThisFrame in history
   - Window line counter monotonically increases within a frame
   - Window line counter resets at VBlank

4. Fetcher epoch:
   - Epoch is monotonically non-decreasing
   - Epoch increments on window restart, fetch cancel, LCD disable, new line

5. OAM scan:
   - Never selects more than 10 objects
   - Scan completes in exactly 80 dots

6. LY:
   - LY is never written by CPU (formal proof of read-only)
   - LY increments exactly once per 456-dot scanline
   - LY cycles through 0-153

TOOL: SymbiYosys with BMC engine (boolector or bitwuzla backend).
FILES: formal/ppu/safety/*.sby, formal/ppu/safety/*_formal.sv

RATIONALE: These are narrow, bounded properties that catch state machine bugs
early. They complement simulation-based testing by exhaustively checking all
reachable states within the bound window.

title: Author Wave C custom ROM assembly source
description:
Write SM83 assembly for Wave C ROMs (§17.3) testing bus realism: OAM DMA, MBC1, MBC3.

ROM SPECIFICATIONS:

1. DMA_OAM_COPY (bench/roms/dma_oam_copy.asm):
   Tests: OAM DMA transfer correctness, CPU bus restriction during DMA
   Strategy: Write known sprite data to WRAM (0xC000-0xC09F), trigger DMA (write 0xC0 to 0xFF46), wait 160 M-cycles, verify OAM (0xFE00-0xFE9F) matches source. Test that CPU can only access HRAM during DMA.
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_dma_trigger, __checkpoint_dma_complete, __checkpoint_oam_verify

2. MBC1_SWITCH (bench/roms/mbc1_switch.asm):
   Tests: MBC1 ROM bank switching (5-bit bank register, bank 0 maps to 1)
   Strategy: Write to 0x2000-0x3FFF to select ROM banks 1-31. Read known data from each bank (0x4000-0x7FFF) and verify. Test bank 0 -> 1 remapping.

3. MBC1_RAM (bench/roms/mbc1_ram.asm):
   Tests: MBC1 external RAM enable and bank select
   Strategy: Enable RAM (write 0x0A to 0x0000-0x1FFF), select RAM bank, write/read pattern to 0xA000-0xBFFF, verify. Test RAM disable.

4. MBC3_SWITCH (bench/roms/mbc3_switch.asm):
   Tests: MBC3 ROM bank switching (7-bit bank register)
   Strategy: Similar to MBC1_SWITCH but with larger bank space (0-127).

5. MBC3_RAM (bench/roms/mbc3_ram.asm):
   Tests: MBC3 external RAM and RTC register access
   Strategy: Enable RAM/RTC, select RAM banks 0-3, write/read patterns. Test RTC register access (if implemented).

ACCEPTANCE: All 5 ROMs assemble, pass ABI validation, registered in manifest.

title: PPU unit test infrastructure (test/ppu/ setup, base utilities)
description:
Set up the PPU unit test directory structure and shared test utilities.

DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:
test/ppu/
  __init__.py
  conftest.py          -- shared PPU test fixtures
  unit/
    __init__.py
    test_tile.py       -- tile decode and addressing
    test_ppu_modes.py  -- mode FSM and timing
    test_stat_irq.py   -- STAT line and IRQ generation
    test_window.py     -- window trigger and counter
    test_obj_priority.py -- object selection and draw priority
    test_ppu_invariants.py -- metamorphic/invariant properties
  lockstep/
    __init__.py
  rom/
    __init__.py
  backend_diff/
    __init__.py
  raster/
    __init__.py
  power/
    __init__.py

SHARED FIXTURES (conftest.py):
- ppu_test_dut: instantiates ppu_test_top, returns handle with convenience methods
- ppu_lockstep_dut: instantiates ppu_lockstep_top
- write_ppu_reg(addr, val): write a PPU register via TimedPpuEvent
- read_ppu_reg(addr): read a PPU register and return value
- advance_dots(n): advance n video dots
- advance_to_line(ly): advance until LY reaches target
- advance_to_mode(mode): advance until PPU enters target mode
- wait_vblank(): advance to next VBlank entry
- capture_line(ly): capture all ScanoutEvents for one scanline
- capture_frame(): capture all ScanoutEvents for one frame

LOGGING: All test utilities emit structured logs following bd-dny standard:
  [STEP] advance_dots(80) -- mode 2 duration
  [CHECK] ppu_mode: expected=3 actual=3 OK (entered PixelTransfer)

DIAGNOSTIC LEVELS: Controlled by --log-level flag:
- QUIET: failures and summary only
- NORMAL: steps + checks + summary (default)
- VERBOSE: all signal values at each step
- TRACE: full PpuSemanticCommit dumps

INTEGRATION WITH TEST RUNNER:
Register PPU tests in tools/run_tests.py test discovery.
PPU tests should appear in tier definitions (tools/tests/tiers.yaml).

title: Green CB_BITOPS custom ROM
description:
Write the CB_BITOPS custom test ROM and green it.

Test coverage:
- RLC/RRC/RL/RR for representative registers and values
- SLA/SRA/SRL for edge cases (MSB/LSB behavior)
- SWAP for various values
- BIT b,r for all bit positions (verify Z flag = !tested_bit, H=1, N=0)
- RES b,r and SET b,r (verify no flag changes, correct bit manipulation)
- Operations on (HL): read-modify-write with correct timing

Edge cases:
- Rotate through carry with carry=0 and carry=1
- SRA preserves MSB (arithmetic shift)
- SRL clears MSB (logical shift)
- SWAP of 0x00 sets Z flag

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: Raster stimulus ports, video anchors, and replay capsule types
description:
Define the simulation-only stimulus and symbolic anchor types for deterministic
PPU testing and replay.

RASTER STIMULUS (simulation-only sideband):

struct RasterStimulus {
  reg_write: Option<MmioWrite>,
  dma_start: Option<uint<8>>,
  vram_patch: Option<VideoPatch>,
  oam_patch: Option<VideoPatch>,
  state_import: Option<PpuSimSnapshot>,
  state_export: bool,
  freeze_dot_ce: bool,
  break_on_line: Option<uint<8>>,
  break_on_anchor: Option<VideoAnchor>,
}

SYMBOLIC VIDEO ANCHORS:

enum VideoAnchor {
  FrameLineDot { frame: uint<32>, line: uint<8>, dot: uint<9> },
  LineStart { line: uint<8> },
  ModeEnter { line: uint<8>, mode: PpuMode },
  WindowStart { line: uint<8> },
  ObjFetchStart { line: uint<8>, oam_index: uint<6> },
  PixelX { line: uint<8>, x: uint<8> },
}

WHY ANCHORS: Raw (frame, line, dot) coordinates are often the LEAST stable way
to describe an interesting event, because variable mode-3 penalties shift later
dot numbers even when the underlying semantic event is the same. Anchors like
ModeEnter or WindowStart are stable across implementation changes.

STATE IMPORT/EXPORT enables:
- Regression speedup (start from mid-frame snapshot instead of full reboot)
- Failure minimization (export state at last-known-good, replay from there)
- Cross-oracle state alignment (snapshot DUT state, inject into reference)

The same stimulus format feeds DUT, reference model, and replay artifacts.
Replay capsules record the stimulus stream for reproducible failure analysis.

TOOLS (created in a companion bead):
- tools/replay_capsule.py: serialize/deserialize/replay stimulus streams
- tools/render_line_narrative.py: human-readable divergence explanation
- tools/compare_oracles.py: cross-oracle differential comparison driver

title: Register visibility matrices (write and readback)
description:
Create authoritative visibility matrices for both register writes and reads.

WRITE VISIBILITY MATRIX (section 9.4.1):
For each PPU register, specify when a CPU write becomes visible to the renderer:

- LYC: continuously compared against LY (immediate)
- STAT select bits: feed STAT-line evaluation immediately
- SCX[2:0]: sampled at scanline start for initial discard (PpuSamplingState)
- SCX[7:3], SCY: re-sampled on each tile fetch (PpuRenderInputs)
- WX: checked against rendered X + 7 (live comparison)
- WY: checked at start of mode 2 only (PpuSamplingState)
- BGP, OBP0, OBP1: sampled at palette-application / pop boundary
- LCDC: per-bit visibility points defined explicitly

READBACK VISIBILITY MATRIX (section 9.4.1.1):
For each CPU-readable PPU register/region, specify read semantics:

- LY: continuously derived, read-only. Never stored as a writable byte.
- STAT: mixed stored+derived view. Bits 3-6 are stored StatSelect, bits 0-1
  are derived from visible_mode(), bit 2 is derived from LY==LYC.
  Reports 0 for mode bits when LCD disabled.
- VRAM/OAM CPU reads: blocked/undefined by access policy (mode 2/3).
  Default: UndefinedRead. Exact $FF materialization is opt-in per manifest.
- LCD-off: STAT mode bits report 0, VRAM/OAM fully accessible.

IMPLEMENTATION: These matrices should exist as:
1. Documentation (docs/ppu/readback_matrix.md, docs/ppu/behavior_matrix.md)
2. Code comments in regs.spade and sample.spade
3. Executable assertions in the Python reference model
4. Formal properties where applicable (e.g., LY is never written by CPU)

WHY BOTH: Many emulators get writes right but reads wrong, or vice versa.
Having a separate readback matrix prevents debugger-style read semantics from
accidentally becoming architectural semantics.

title: Custom ROM checkpoint ABI specification
description:
Define the formal checkpoint ABI (§17.0.1) that ALL custom test ROMs must follow. This is the contract between ROM authors and the verification harness.

SPECIFICATION:

1. SYMBOL FILE (.sym):
   Every custom ROM MUST produce an RGBDS-compatible .sym file (via rgblink -n) containing at minimum:
   - __pass: executable label reached on successful test completion
   - __fail: executable label reached on test failure
   Both labels must be at executable code locations (not data) because PyBoy hooks work by replacing the instruction at the target address with a special opcode (§14.4).

2. OPTIONAL CHECKPOINT LABELS:
   - __checkpoint_<name>: intermediate verification points within a test
   - __commit_<name>: fine-grained hook-driven differential comparison points
   - __inject_begin_<name> / __inject_end_<name>: regions where external stimulus injection is valid
   These enable the harness to perform targeted M-cycle-level comparison without instrumenting every instruction.

3. WRAM SIGNATURE BLOCK:
   Every custom ROM should write a versioned signature block to a fixed WRAM region (suggested: 0xC000-0xC01F) containing:
   - Byte 0: ABI version (currently 0x01)
   - Byte 1: test result (0x00=running, 0x01=pass, 0xFF=fail)
   - Bytes 2-3: test case count (little-endian)
   - Bytes 4-5: pass count (little-endian)
   - Bytes 6-7: fail count (little-endian)
   - Bytes 8-15: reserved for debug counters
   - Bytes 16-31: ASCII test name (null-padded)
   This allows the harness to read test status from memory even if hooks are unavailable.

4. ROM STRUCTURE CONVENTIONS:
   - Entry point at 0x0150 (after Nintendo logo, using SkipBoot reset profile)
   - Header must pass rgbfix validation
   - ROM should begin with DI unless testing interrupt behavior
   - Tests should be self-contained: setup -> execute -> verify -> write result -> jump to __pass/__fail
   - No reliance on uninitialized memory values

5. MANIFEST INTEGRATION:
   Each ROM entry in bench/manifests/roms.yaml references oracle_mode, checkpoint_symbols, compare_scope, and memory_behavior_profile.

WHY THIS EXISTS: Without a formal ABI, ROM authors and harness maintainers make incompatible assumptions. The WRAM signature block provides a fallback verification path independent of PyBoy hooks. The __checkpoint_* and __commit_* labels enable surgical M-cycle comparison without full-trace overhead.

DELIVERABLES:
- spec/rom_abi.md: Formal specification document
- bench/tools/validate_rom_abi.py: Validation script for ROM+.sym pairs
- bench/roms/template.asm: Template ROM showing ABI in practice

ACCEPTANCE: validate_rom_abi.py passes on template ROM. ABI doc referenced by ROM build pipeline.

title: Periph: dead code and redundant guards
description:
Remove dead computation and simplify redundant boolean guards in peripheral modules.

## Scope
1. Remove dead prev_pressed computation in joypad.spade (~lines 89, 98). The combinational output of selected_pressed_nibble is computed and immediately discarded. Only the registered prev_pressed_reg matters for edge detection. Removing this eliminates synthesized-but-unconnected mux logic.

2. Simplify redundant guard in oam_dma.spade (~line 33): advance_transfer && finishing_transfer → finishing_transfer (since finishing_transfer already implies advance_transfer by definition).

3. Unify duplicate zext in oam_dma.spade (~lines 64-65): source_addr_lo and dest_addr_lo are both zext(index_reg). Use single binding.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Dead prev_pressed lines removed
- Redundant && guard simplified
- Duplicate zext unified
- joypad_test_top, oam_dma_test_top pass

title: Consolidate PPU run/phase helper encoders into ppu_support
description: Finish the remaining helper lift from core_test_top, timing_test_top, and irq_test_top into src/sim/ppu_support.spade. The constructor slice from bd-4fyn landed safely, but lifting encode/decode run/phase helpers caused X-valued output regressions in test_ppu_core_smoke on core_test_top. Reproduce, then consolidate encode_run/decode_run/encode_phase/encode_mode without changing simulation behavior.
close_reason: Consolidated shared PPU run/phase helpers and stabilized core smoke coverage

title: Background and window rendering
description:
Implement background and window pixel rendering.

Background rendering:
1. For each pixel on the current scanline:
2. Compute tilemap coordinates: ((SCY + LY) / 8, (SCX + pixel_x) / 8)
3. Look up tile index from selected tilemap (LCDC bit 3)
4. Fetch tile data from selected tile data area (LCDC bit 4)
5. Extract pixel from tile row using sub-tile coordinates
6. Apply BGP palette

Window rendering:
1. Window is active when LCDC bit 5 is set AND WY <= LY AND WX-7 <= pixel_x
2. Window uses its own internal line counter (doesn't use SCX/SCY)
3. Window tilemap selected by LCDC bit 6
4. Window overlays background (higher priority)

Tile data format:
- Each tile is 8x8 pixels, 2 bits per pixel
- 16 bytes per tile: 2 bytes per row, interleaved bit planes
- Byte 0 = low bits, Byte 1 = high bits for row 0, etc.

LCDC bit 4 tile data addressing:
- 1: unsigned, 0x8000 base (tiles 0-255)
- 0: signed, 0x9000 base (tiles -128 to 127, where tile 0 = 0x9000)
close_reason: Superseded by granular beads under Epic bd-1rh0 (BG/Window Wave B)

title: PPU rendering unit tests (tile fetch, sprites, palette)
description:
Create test/unit/test_ppu_render.py — isolated tests for PPU rendering logic.

BACKGROUND RENDERING TESTS:
1. Tile fetch: given LCDC settings and SCX/SCY, verify correct tile address computation
2. Tile data lookup: given tile index, verify correct tile data address (signed vs unsigned based on LCDC bit 4)
3. Palette application: given 2-bit pixel and BGP register, verify correct shade output
4. Scrolling: verify SCX/SCY offset wraps correctly (both within and across tile boundaries)
5. Tile map selection: verify LCDC bit 3 selects between 0x9800 and 0x9C00 tilemaps

WINDOW RENDERING TESTS:
6. Window activation: window appears when WX/WY conditions met AND LCDC bit 5 set
7. Window position: verify WX-7 offset and WY positioning
8. Window/BG priority: window overlaps background
9. Window internal line counter: window uses its own line counter, not LY

SPRITE RENDERING TESTS:
10. OAM scan: 10-sprite-per-line limit, verify correct sprite selection
11. Sprite X priority: leftmost sprite has priority (ties: lower OAM index wins)
12. Sprite Y positioning: sprite Y in OAM is Y+16 (offset by 16)
13. Sprite flipping: X-flip and Y-flip attributes
14. Sprite palette: OBP0 vs OBP1 selection
15. 8x16 sprite mode: LCDC bit 2, tile index bit 0 ignored
16. Sprite-to-BG priority: OAM attribute bit 7 (BG priority flag)

SEMANTIC VERIFICATION (§17.4):
The plan insists on SEMANTIC checks, not just frame hashes:
- "tilemap/window/sprite state queries"
- Verify the pixel pipeline produces correct intermediate values
- Frame hash is only the coarsest final checksum

LOGGING: Each test prints the rendering computation:
  [TILE] SCX=3 SCY=0 → tile map addr=0x9800 tile_x=0 tile_y=0 tile_idx=0x01
  [DATA] tile_idx=0x01 → data addr=0x8010 row=0 → lo=0x3C hi=0x7E
  [PIXEL] bit 7: (1,0)=0b01 → BGP shade 1 (light gray)
close_reason: Superseded: unit tests distributed across implementation beads

title: Phase machine for 16-bit arithmetic
description:
Implement phase machine for 16-bit arithmetic instructions.

Instructions:
- ADD HL,rr: 2 M-cycles (fetch + internal ALU with extra cycle for 16-bit add)
- INC rr: 2 M-cycles (fetch + internal)
- DEC rr: 2 M-cycles (fetch + internal)
- ADD SP,e8: 4 M-cycles (fetch + read imm + internal + internal)
- LD HL,SP+e8: 3 M-cycles (fetch + read imm + internal)

16-bit INC/DEC do NOT affect flags (unlike 8-bit INC/DEC).
ADD HL,rr affects N, H, C but preserves Z.
ADD SP,e8 and LD HL,SP+e8 have the tricky low-byte flag behavior (see ALU task).

title: IME state machine implementation
description:
Implement the IME (Interrupt Master Enable) state machine in the CPU.

States (§8.3):
  enum ImeState {
    Disabled,       // Interrupts disabled (after DI or reset)
    PendingEnable,  // EI executed, will enable AFTER next instruction
    Enabled,        // Interrupts enabled (can be serviced)
  }

Transitions:
- DI → ImeState::Disabled (immediate)
- EI → ImeState::PendingEnable (NOT immediate! Delayed by one instruction)
- After executing the instruction following EI: PendingEnable → Enabled
- Interrupt service entry → Disabled (interrupts disabled during service)
- RETI → Enabled (immediate, unlike EI)

The EI delay (§16.5 invariant): EI enables interrupts exactly one instruction later. This is critical for the common pattern:
  EI
  HALT  ; interrupts become enabled, then HALT waits for interrupt

If EI took effect immediately, the interrupt could fire before HALT executes.

The PendingEnable state is checked during the fetch phase: if an interrupt is pending AND IME is Enabled (not PendingEnable), service the interrupt instead of fetching the next instruction.

title: Define ROM manifest schema and initial inventory
description:
Create the ROM manifest schema (§17.0) and initial inventory of custom ROMs needed for Wave A-D.

Schema (bench/manifests/rom_schema.yaml or similar):
- id: unique ROM identifier
- path: relative path to ROM binary
- requires: [cpu, timer, dma, mbc1, mbc3, ppu] — capability requirements
- oracle_mode: unit | instr_commit | mcycle_commit | checkpoint | frame_semantic | frame_hash
- memory_behavior_profile: DmgConservative | DmgRevisionSpecific
- timeout_commits: max commits before test timeout
- checkpoint_symbols: list of __checkpoint_* labels
- pass_condition: how to determine pass (e.g., __pass label hit, WRAM signature)
- compare_scope: what to compare at each commit boundary
- action_script: optional deterministic event script path
- action_gen: optional generator for randomized but seeded action sequences

Custom ROM checkpoint ABI (§17.0.1):
- Every custom ROM must emit .sym file
- Reserved executable labels: __pass, __fail, __checkpoint_*, __commit_*, __inject_begin_*, __inject_end_*
- Versioned WRAM signature block for pass/fail and debug counters

Initial inventory (stub entries, ROMs not yet written):
Wave A: LOADS_BASIC, ALU_FLAGS, ALU16_SP, FLOW_STACK, CB_BITOPS, MEM_RWB, ALU_LOOP
Wave B: TIMER_DIV_BASIC, EI_DELAY, TIMER_IRQ_HALT
Wave C: JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST, DMA_OAM_COPY, MBC1_SWITCH, MBC1_RAM, MBC3_SWITCH, MBC3_RAM
Wave D: PPU_STAT_IRQ, BG_STATIC, BG_SCROLL_SIGNED, PPU_WINDOW, PPU_SPRITES

title: Formal verification: reset state correctness
description:
Create formal/cpu_reset.sby — proves that after reset, the CPU is in the exact expected state for the SkipBoot profile.

PROPERTY TO VERIFY:
After rst is asserted and then released, on the first m_ce cycle:
- PC = 0x0100 (SkipBoot entry point)
- SP = 0xFFFE
- A = 0x01, F = 0xB0 (Z=1, N=0, H=1, C=1 — DMG post-boot values)
- B=0x00, C=0x13, D=0x00, E=0xD8, H=0x01, L=0x4D (DMG post-boot values)
- IME = Disabled
- HaltState = Running
- Phase = Fetch
- BusReq = Read(0x0100) (fetching first instruction)

WHY FORMAL: Reset correctness is a boolean property over a small state space — perfect for BMC/induction. A simulation test checks one reset sequence; formal proves ALL possible reset sequences result in the correct state.

TOOL: yosys + sby (SymbiYosys). Pin version in toolchain/.
File: formal/cpu_reset.sby with Spade-generated Verilog as input.

RELATIONSHIP TO bd-1li (test_reset_profile.py): That bead tests reset via simulation. This bead proves it formally. Both should exist — simulation catches integration issues, formal proves the property exhaustively.

title: Bus observation sideband (BusObs) implementation in bus fabric
description:
Implement the simulation-only bus observation sideband (§12.2.1) in the bus fabric so CommitTrace records include memory region and access diagnostics.

BACKGROUND:
The CPU-facing bus contract (BusReq/BusResp from §12.2) is deliberately minimal--the CPU should not know whether 0xC000 is SPRAM, a Python array, or dual-port memory. But for VERIFICATION, we need to know which memory region was accessed and whether the access was blocked (e.g., during DMA or PPU pixel transfer). This information flows through BusObs, a simulation-only sideband that the bus fabric emits alongside the normal BusResp.

TYPES (already defined in bd-2on, implemented here):

  enum BusRegion { Rom, Vram, CartRam, Wram, Echo, Oam, NotUsable, Io, Hram, Ie }

  struct BusObs {
    region: BusRegion,   -- which address region was decoded
    blocked: bool,       -- was this access blocked by contention?
    owner: BusOwner,     -- who currently owns the bus?
  }

  enum BusOwner { Cpu, OamDma, Ppu, Idle }

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Extend the bus fabric (src/bus/membus.spade) to compute BusObs for every bus transaction
2. Address decode logic maps addr to BusRegion:
   - 0x0000-0x7FFF: Rom (or CartRam for MBC-mapped regions)
   - 0x8000-0x9FFF: Vram
   - 0xA000-0xBFFF: CartRam
   - 0xC000-0xDFFF: Wram
   - 0xE000-0xFDFF: Echo (mirrors Wram)
   - 0xFE00-0xFE9F: Oam
   - 0xFEA0-0xFEFF: NotUsable
   - 0xFF00-0xFF7F: Io
   - 0xFF80-0xFFFE: Hram
   - 0xFFFF: Ie
3. Blocked status is false for Wave A (no contention). Set true when DMA/PPU arbitration is added (Wave C/D).
4. BusOwner is Cpu for Wave A. Extended in Wave C (bd-1a6).

INTEGRATION WITH COMMIT TRACE:
- cpu_test_top and soc_lockstep_top expose BusObs alongside BusResp
- CommitTrace includes Optional<MemTouch> with region information
- The oracle comparator (bd-1au) can optionally compare BusRegion to detect address decode bugs

WHY THIS MATTERS:
- Catches address decode bugs that silently write to the wrong memory region
- Enables memory-region-aware coverage tracking (§16.3)
- Future DMA/PPU contention checking relies on this infrastructure
- Without BusObs, a mis-decoded address might pass all register comparisons but corrupt memory state

ACCEPTANCE: Bus fabric emits correct BusRegion for every address. Simulation tops expose BusObs. CommitTrace MemTouch includes region. At least one test validates address decode boundaries.

title: Green PPU_STAT_IRQ custom ROM
description:
Write and green PPU_STAT_IRQ test ROM.

Test coverage:
- Mode transitions: verify correct mode sequence per scanline
- LY increments correctly through 0-153
- LY=LYC coincidence flag in STAT
- STAT interrupt on mode 0, 1, 2 transitions
- STAT interrupt on LY=LYC
- STAT interrupt source combinations
- VBlank interrupt (IF bit 0) on line 144

Oracle mode: mcycle_commit for timing, instr_commit for flag/register checks
close_reason: Superseded by bd-1s1z (Green Wave A ROMs) which includes PPU_STAT_IRQ

title: Spade project configuration (swim.toml, Spade version pin)
description: Set up Spade HDL build system. Create swim.toml (project manifest, source dirs, test dirs, simulator backend, synthesis target) and pin exact Spade compiler version in toolchain/versions.md. BUILD VERIFICATION: swim build succeeds on trivial source, swim test discovers Cocotb testbenches, swim synth produces Verilog. WHY P0: Without swim.toml, NO Spade code compiles. Every Phase 2+ bead depends on this.

title: Timer/DIV entity (DIV, TIMA, TMA, TAC)
description:
Implement the timer subsystem in src/periph/timer.spade.

Registers:
- DIV (0xFF04): upper 8 bits of 16-bit system counter. Writing ANY value resets to 0.
- TIMA (0xFF05): timer counter. Increments at rate selected by TAC. Overflows → interrupt + reload from TMA.
- TMA (0xFF06): timer modulo. Value loaded into TIMA on overflow.
- TAC (0xFF07): timer control. Bit 2 = enable. Bits 1-0 = clock select:
  - 00: CPU clock / 1024 (4096 Hz)
  - 01: CPU clock / 16 (262144 Hz)
  - 10: CPU clock / 64 (65536 Hz)
  - 11: CPU clock / 256 (16384 Hz)

CRITICAL DESIGN DECISION (§6.1.1):
Timer derives from the SHARED timebase entity's sys_counter, NOT a private local counter. This prevents timer, CPU, and DMA blocks from drifting by one cycle.

Timer increment uses falling-edge detection on the selected bit of the system counter. This is the source of obscure timer bugs:
- Writing 0 to DIV can cause TIMA to increment if the selected counter bit was 1
- Changing TAC clock select can cause spurious TIMA increment
- These are real hardware behaviors that test ROMs verify

Timer overflow sequence:
1. TIMA overflows (0xFF → 0x00)
2. One M-cycle delay
3. TIMA reloaded from TMA
4. IF bit 2 (timer) set
5. During the delay cycle, writing TIMA cancels the reload and interrupt

title: Periph: ack_bits, serial state, if-else flattening
description:
Logic simplification across interrupts, serial, and timer peripherals.

## Scope
1. ack_bits (interrupts.spade:~32-44): Replace 6-arm manual decode with trunc(1u8 << bit) if Spade supports variable shifts. If not, keep but add comment.

2. Serial redundant state (serial.spade): sc_reg bit 7 and transfer_active_reg track the same thing. Resolve by either:
   (a) Never storing bit 7 in sc_reg; derive from transfer_active_reg in visible_sc (preferred)
   (b) Dropping transfer_active_reg; reading bit 7 from sc_reg directly
   Check whether any code reads sc_reg bit 7 directly before choosing.

3. Flatten nested if-else in timer.spade (~82-102, TIMA update logic) and serial.spade (~65-74) to else-if chains for readability.

4. Consolidate irq_ack_none: test tops define local irq_ack_none(). The CPU module has no_irq_ack(). Have test tops import it instead.

## Acceptance Criteria
- ack_bits simplified or documented
- Serial bit-7 has single source of truth
- Nested if-else flattened
- irq_ack_none consolidated
- timer_test_top, interrupts_test_top, serial_test_top pass

title: PPU: collapse identical WarmupBlankFrame/Running arms in sem
description:
Three functions in the semantic layer have WarmupBlankFrame and Running arms that return identical expressions. Collapse to wildcard matches.

## Scope
1. visible_mode (sem/types.spade:~358-362): Both arms return phase_mode(status.phase). Collapse:
   LcdRunState::Disabled => PpuMode::LcdOff, _ => phase_mode(status.phase)
2. lcd_enabled (sem/types.spade:~366-372): Both arms return regs.lcdc.lcd_enable. Collapse.
3. next_dot_in_line (sem/step.spade:~91-99): Both arms have identical if dot_in_line == 455 wrapping logic. Collapse.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Each function has 2 match arms (Disabled + wildcard) instead of 3
- No behavioral change
- Relevant swim tests pass

title: Tile decode and addressing unit tests (test/ppu/unit/test_tile.py)
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for tile decode pure helpers.

TEST CASES:

1. TILE ROW DECODE:
   decode_tile_row(lo=0x3C, hi=0x7E) should produce pixel array [0,1,2,3,3,2,1,0].
   Verify bit 7 is leftmost pixel. Test all-zero, all-one, checkerboard patterns.

2. BG TILE ADDRESSING UNSIGNED ($8000 method):
   LCDC.4=1. tile_id=0x00 -> base $8000. tile_id=0xFF -> $8000 + 0xFF*16.
   Verify row offset: tile_addr + row*2 for lo byte, +1 for hi byte.

3. BG TILE ADDRESSING SIGNED ($8800 method):
   LCDC.4=0. tile_id=0x00 -> $9000. tile_id=0x80 -> $8800 (signed -128).
   tile_id=0x7F -> $9000 + 0x7F*16. Verify wrap-around at boundaries.

4. OBJ TILE ADDRESSING 8x8:
   Always unsigned from $8000. Verify for various tile_id and row values.

5. OBJ TILE ADDRESSING 8x16:
   Low bit of tile_id is IGNORED. tile_id=0x01 uses tile 0x00 (top) and
   0x01 (bottom). Verify correct row addressing for both halves.

6. X FLIP:
   apply_x_flip reverses pixel order. [0,1,2,3,0,0,0,0] -> [0,0,0,0,3,2,1,0].

7. Y FLIP 8x8:
   apply_y_flip(row=0) -> row=7. apply_y_flip(row=7) -> row=0.

8. Y FLIP 8x16:
   apply_y_flip(row=0) -> row=15. apply_y_flip(row=8) -> row=7.

9. PALETTE APPLICATION:
   BGP=0xE4 (3,2,1,0 mapping). Color 0->shade 0, color 1->shade 1, etc.
   BGP=0x00 (all shade 0). BGP=0xFF (all shade 3).

LOGGING: Each test logs input bytes, expected pixel array, actual pixel array.
On failure, log hex dump of lo/hi bytes and bit-by-bit decode.

title: Decoder: unprefixed opcodes
description:
Implement unprefixed opcode decoder in src/cpu/decode.spade.

Decoder function:
  fn decode(opcode: uint<8>) -> DecodedOp

Must cover all 256 unprefixed opcodes (0x00-0xFF):
- 8-bit loads: LD r,r / LD r,n / LD r,(HL) / LD (HL),r / LD (HL),n / LD A,(rr) / LD (rr),A
- 16-bit loads: LD rr,nn / LD SP,HL / LD (nn),SP / PUSH rr / POP rr / LD HL,SP+e8
- 8-bit ALU: ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP with r/(HL)/n
- 16-bit arithmetic: ADD HL,rr / INC rr / DEC rr / ADD SP,e8
- 8-bit INC/DEC: INC r / DEC r / INC (HL) / DEC (HL)
- Rotate/shift (unprefixed): RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA
- Control flow: JP/JR/CALL/RET/RETI/RST (conditional and unconditional)
- Misc: NOP, HALT, STOP, DI, EI, DAA, CPL, SCF, CCF
- Invalid: 0xD3,0xDB,0xDD,0xE3,0xE4,0xEB,0xEC,0xED,0xF4,0xFC,0xFD

Drive the decode table from the canonical opcode metadata (spec/sm83_opcodes.py) to ensure completeness.

title: Decoder: CB-prefixed opcodes
description:
Implement CB-prefix decoder in src/cpu/decode.spade.

Function:
  fn decode_cb(opcode: uint<8>) -> DecodedOp

All 256 CB-prefixed opcodes follow a regular pattern:
- 0x00-0x07: RLC r (rotate left circular)
- 0x08-0x0F: RRC r (rotate right circular)
- 0x10-0x17: RL r (rotate left through carry)
- 0x18-0x1F: RR r (rotate right through carry)
- 0x20-0x27: SLA r (shift left arithmetic)
- 0x28-0x2F: SRA r (shift right arithmetic)
- 0x30-0x37: SWAP r (swap nibbles)
- 0x38-0x3F: SRL r (shift right logical)
- 0x40-0x7F: BIT b,r (test bit)
- 0x80-0xBF: RES b,r (reset bit)
- 0xC0-0xFF: SET b,r (set bit)

Register encoding in bits [2:0]: B,C,D,E,H,L,(HL),A
Bit index in bits [5:3] for BIT/RES/SET

The regular structure means decode_cb can be largely computed from bit slicing rather than a 256-entry lookup table.

title: Green DMA_OAM_COPY custom ROM
description:
Write and green the DMA_OAM_COPY test ROM.

Test coverage:
- Trigger DMA by writing source address to 0xFF46
- Verify all 160 bytes copied correctly from source to OAM
- Verify DMA takes correct number of M-cycles
- Verify CPU is restricted to HRAM during DMA
- Verify CPU can read/write HRAM normally during DMA
- Verify non-HRAM reads during DMA return expected values (0xFF per DmgConservative)

Oracle mode: mcycle_commit (timing-sensitive)

title: ALU exhaustive nightly sweeps
description:
Add nightly exhaustive ALU verification sweeps (§11.4 lane 3).

Behind a pytest marker (e.g., @pytest.mark.nightly or @pytest.mark.exhaustive):
- All ADD A,x: 256 * 256 = 65,536 input pairs
- All ADC A,x with carry 0 and carry 1: 256 * 256 * 2 = 131,072
- All SUB x: 256 * 256 = 65,536
- All SBC A,x with carry 0 and carry 1: 131,072
- All DAA inputs: A(256) * N(2) * H(2) * C(2) = 2,048

Each case compares Spade ALU output against Python reference model.
Total: ~400K+ test vectors per nightly run.

These should NOT be in the fast CI loop — they run in the nightly regression (tools/regress.sh with --nightly flag).

title: ALU implementation: BIT/RES/SET (CB prefix)
description:
Implement CB-prefix bit operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- BIT b,r: test bit b of register r. Z=!bit_b, N=0, H=1, C=preserved
- RES b,r: reset (clear) bit b of register r. No flag changes.
- SET b,r: set bit b of register r. No flag changes.

b ranges from 0-7, covering all 8 bits.
Each operation exists for all 8 registers (B,C,D,E,H,L,(HL),A) = 8*8*3 = 192 CB opcodes total for these three families.

BIT is the only one that affects flags. RES and SET are pure data operations.

title: Switch CPU core and helper constructors to named syntax
Consolidate PPU test helpers into ppu_support
description:
Move duplicated PPU test helpers into the existing ppu_support.spade module.

## Scope
1. encode_phase/encode_mode/encode_run/decode_run: ppu_support already has encode_ppu_phase and encode_ppu_mode. Either rename test-top versions to match or vice versa. Move encode_run/decode_run to ppu_support. Delete local copies from core_test_top, timing_test_top, irq_test_top.

2. build_lcdc: 5 copies with slight variations across fifo_test_top, mixer_test_top, oam_scan_test_top, tile_test_top, fetcher_test_top. Create a single parameterized version in ppu_support that takes all Lcdc fields. Default values for common case (all-enabled).

3. pack_row: 2 copies in fetcher_test_top + tile_test_top. Move to ppu_support.

4. Remove dead encode_mode in ppu_power_top (identical to already-imported encode_ppu_mode).

## Acceptance Criteria
- All PPU test helpers consolidated in ppu_support.spade
- build_lcdc is a single parameterized function
- No local copies remain in test tops
- All PPU swim tests pass

## Considerations
- build_lcdc variants differ in which fields are parameterized. The unified version should accept all fields. Test tops that previously used build_lcdc(obj_enable) will need to pass all fields explicitly, or use default values.
close_reason: Consolidated shared build_lcdc/pack_row helpers and split remaining run/phase helper lift into a follow-up bead

title: Fetch/execute phase machine for load instructions
description:
Implement the Phase state machine in src/cpu/control.spade and src/cpu/semantics.spade for load instruction families.

Load families to implement:
- LD r,r: single M-cycle, no memory access
- LD r,n: fetch immediate byte (ReadImm8 → LoadReg8 continuation)
- LD r,(HL): read from memory at HL (ReadMem → LoadReg8)
- LD (HL),r: write register to memory at HL (WriteMem → StoreDone)
- LD (HL),n: fetch immediate, then write to (HL) — two M-cycles after fetch
- LD A,(rr): read from BC/DE/HL address
- LD (rr),A: write A to BC/DE/HL address
- LD A,(nn): fetch 16-bit immediate, then read from that address
- LD (nn),A: fetch 16-bit immediate, then write A to that address
- LD rr,nn: fetch 16-bit immediate into register pair
- LD SP,HL: copy HL to SP (1 extra M-cycle)
- LD (nn),SP: store SP to memory at nn (16-bit immediate address)
- LD HL,SP+e8: SP + signed immediate, special flags (shares ALU with ADD SP,e8)
- LDH A,(n): read from 0xFF00+n
- LDH (n),A: write A to 0xFF00+n
- LDH A,(C): read from 0xFF00+C
- LDH (C),A: write A to 0xFF00+C
- LD A,(HL+): load A from (HL), then increment HL
- LD A,(HL-): load A from (HL), then decrement HL
- LD (HL+),A: store A to (HL), then increment HL
- LD (HL-),A: store A to (HL), then decrement HL
- PUSH rr: decrement SP, write high byte, decrement SP, write low byte
- POP rr: read low byte, increment SP, read high byte, increment SP

The phase machine uses typed continuations (§8.2). Each memory access transitions through ReadMem/WriteMem with the appropriate continuation carrying the semantic intent.

fn step_mcycle drives the transition: given current Phase + bus response → next Phase + bus request + delta.

title: ALU implementation: 16-bit ADD, ADD SP,e8, LD HL,SP+e8
description:
Implement 16-bit arithmetic in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- ADD HL,rr: HL + rr, flags Z=PRESERVED, N=0, H=half-carry(bit 11), C=carry(bit 15)
- ADD SP,e8: SP + signed_extend(e8), flags Z=0, N=0, H=half-carry(bit 3!), C=carry(bit 7!)
- LD HL,SP+e8: same computation as ADD SP,e8, same flag behavior

TRICKY FLAG CASES (§11.3):
- ADD HL,rr: half-carry is from bit 11 (not bit 3), carry from bit 15 (not bit 7). Z flag is PRESERVED.
- ADD SP,e8: despite being 16-bit, H/C flags are computed from the LOW BYTE of SP + e8. Z is always 0. This is extremely counterintuitive.
- LD HL,SP+e8: SAME flag behavior as ADD SP,e8 (Z=0, N=0, H from low byte, C from low byte)

These are the most commonly mis-implemented ALU operations in Game Boy cores. Lift directly from gbxcule.

title: Synthesis budget enforcement in precommit CI gate
description:
Add synthesis budget enforcement to the precommit check pipeline so that future
changes cannot regress the design past UP5K resource limits.

PROBLEM: The current precommit (tools/run_precommit_checks.sh) runs synthesis
only when ICEBOY_PRECOMMIT_INCLUDE_SYNTH=1 is set, and even then it does NOT
use --enforce-budget. A developer could add a feature that blows the resource
budget and not know until manually running the hardware baseline script.

IMPLEMENTATION:

1. Update tools/run_precommit_checks.sh synthesis section (lines 312-317):
   Change from:
     run_checked tools/verify_hw_build.sh --skip-build
   To:
     run_checked tools/verify_hw_build.sh --skip-build --enforce-budget

   This makes the precommit fail if LUT4 > 5,280 or DFF > 1,024 when
   synthesis checking is enabled.

2. Add a separate ICEBOY_PRECOMMIT_ENFORCE_BUDGET env var (optional):
   If users want synthesis checking without budget enforcement (e.g., during
   development of a feature that temporarily exceeds budget), they can set
   ICEBOY_PRECOMMIT_ENFORCE_BUDGET=0 to skip the enforcement. Default is 1
   when synth is enabled.

3. Update verify_hw_build.sh to also check SPRAM/EBR utilization:
   Currently only checks LUT4 and DFF. Add checks for:
   - SPRAM usage > 0 (memories should be using SPRAM, not registers)
   - If SPRAM falls to 0 while DFF spikes, that's a regression signal

TEST CASES:

1. PRECOMMIT WITH SYNTH CATCHES BUDGET VIOLATION:
   Temporarily add a large register array to a test module. Run precommit
   with ICEBOY_PRECOMMIT_INCLUDE_SYNTH=1. Verify it fails with the budget
   error message.

2. PRECOMMIT WITHOUT SYNTH SKIPS CHECK:
   Run precommit without ICEBOY_PRECOMMIT_INCLUDE_SYNTH. Verify synthesis
   is skipped entirely (existing behavior, no regression).

3. PRECOMMIT WITH SYNTH PASSES ON CLEAN BUILD:
   Run precommit with ICEBOY_PRECOMMIT_INCLUDE_SYNTH=1 on the current
   (budget-compliant) codebase. Verify it passes.

4. VERIFY_HW_BUILD REPORTS SPRAM USAGE:
   Run verify_hw_build.sh --skip-build. Verify output includes SPRAM count
   in addition to existing LUT4 and DFF counts.

5. ENFORCE-BUDGET BYPASS:
   Run with ICEBOY_PRECOMMIT_INCLUDE_SYNTH=1 and
   ICEBOY_PRECOMMIT_ENFORCE_BUDGET=0. Verify synthesis runs but budget
   violations are reported as warnings, not errors.

ACCEPTANCE:
- tools/run_precommit_checks.sh passes --enforce-budget when synth is enabled
- Budget violations cause precommit failure (exit non-zero)
- SPRAM utilization reported in verify_hw_build.sh output
- All existing precommit behavior preserved when synth is disabled

title: Bus cleanup: dead functions, redundant logic, region re-decode
description:
The bus module has accumulated dead functions, identity wrappers, redundant computations, and duplicated decision trees. This bead cleans up membus.spade, ppu_event_bridge.spade, and their test tops.

## Background

The bus module (membus) is the central interconnect for all CPU, DMA, and PPU memory traffic. It was built conservatively — some functions exist as placeholders for future behavioral divergence (different MemoryBehaviorProfile handling), while others are artifacts of incremental development. The ppu_event_bridge translates bus writes into PPU events and has its own set of duplicated patterns.

Cleaning up the bus module is important because: (a) it is instantiated in every SoC-level entity (soc_lockstep_top, soc_rom_top, icebreaker hardware), so dead logic multiplies; (b) the membus is on the critical path for LUT usage; (c) the PPU event bridge will grow as more PPU register interactions are modeled.

## Scope

### Group A: Dead/identity/redundant functions in membus.spade

1. **canonical_req is a no-op** (~37-43): Destructures each BusReq variant and reconstructs the exact same value. Called 3 times. Possible reasons it exists: (a) synthesis hint to break tool-inferred aliases, (b) placeholder for future request canonicalization. INVESTIGATE before removing. If intentional, add a comment explaining why.

2. **default_bus_obs is dead** (types.spade:~49-51): Alias for idle_bus_obs() with zero call sites. Remove.

3. **blocked_read_data and not_usable_read_data are identical** (~132-144): Both return 0xff unconditionally across all MemoryBehaviorProfile variants. They may be intentionally separate for future profile-specific behavior (e.g., DmgRevisionSpecific returning a different value for "not usable" regions). If so, add a TODO comment. If not, collapse to a single function.

4. **cpu_write_allowed ignores memory_behavior_profile** (~146-155): Both arms return !blocked && region_is_cpu_writable(region). Same treatment as item 3.

### Group B: Region decode redundancy

5. **req_region redundantly re-extracts addr** (~45-59): req_addr and req_region both independently match BusReq. In the membus entity, both are called on the same request (lines ~245-248). req_region internally extracts the address and passes it to decode_region. Replace with: let addr = req_addr(req); let region = decode_region(addr);. Remove req_region.

6. **wram_write/hram_write/oam_write re-decode region** (~174-203): Each function calls decode_region(addr) internally. But callers at lines ~284, 294, 303 have already computed region on line ~246. Refactor to accept pre-decoded BusRegion parameter. Eliminates 3 redundant decode_region calls (each is a 10-way address comparison chain), which directly saves LUTs.

7. **Four trivial region_is_* functions** (~68-94): region_is_hram, region_is_io, region_is_vram, region_is_oam are each a match with one true arm. Could be inlined at their single call sites if readability permits. Low priority.

### Group C: Event bridge consolidation

8. **event_count and build_events duplicate decision tree** (ppu_event_bridge.spade:~65-107): Both have the same branching structure and call decode_mmio_target independently. They are always called together. Merge into a single function returning (events, count).

9. **current_frame_index called twice** (~119-134): Same arguments, same result. Bind once with let.

10. **let _ = target dead binding** (~67): Destructured from decode_mmio_target but immediately discarded. Use wildcard in destructure.

11. **write_fields partially duplicates active_req** (~38-47): The rst || !m_ce guard duplicates active_req in membus.spade. Consider reusing active_req.

12. **encode_event duplicated header** (test_top:~44-63): Each match arm begins with the same header expression. Factor out to a let.

## Considerations
- Items 1, 3, 4 may be intentional design-for-future. The approach should be: check git blame for original commit messages, and if no clear intent, add a TODO comment and simplify. Do NOT silently remove if there's any chance it was intentional.
- Item 6 (region decode removal) is a genuine synthesis improvement — 3 fewer 10-way comparators in the bus datapath.
- Item 8 (event merge) changes the function signature, which affects ppu_event_bridge entity and its test top.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. canonical_req: removed with comment in commit OR documented with comment explaining synthesis intent
2. default_bus_obs removed
3. blocked_read_data/not_usable_read_data: collapsed or documented
4. cpu_write_allowed: simplified or documented
5. req_region removed; callers use decode_region(addr) directly
6. Write helpers accept pre-decoded region parameter
7. event_count/build_events merged into single function
8. current_frame_index bound once
9. Dead bindings cleaned up
10. No behavioral change

## Test Plan
- swim test passes for membus_test_top, observe_req_test_top, ppu_bridge_core_test_top, ppu_event_bridge_test_top
- soc_lockstep_top and soc_rom_top tests pass
- Synth gate passes — verify LUT count does not increase (expect slight decrease from region decode removal)

title: Extend timebase entity with video-dot timing (dot_ce, line/frame signals)
description:
Extend src/board/clockgen.spade timebase entity to emit PPU video-dot signals.

CURRENT STATE: The timebase entity outputs t_ce (T-cycle enable) and m_ce
(M-cycle enable) derived from a system counter. The PPU needs additional signals.

NEW OUTPUTS (added to TimebaseOut):
- dot_ce: bool         -- video dot enable (1 dot = 1 T-cycle on DMG)
- line_start: bool     -- asserted on first dot of each scanline
- frame_start: bool    -- asserted on first dot of each frame
- line_index: uint<8>  -- current scanline number (0-153)
- dot_in_line: uint<9> -- current dot within the scanline (0-455)

For the DMG milestone, dot_ce may equal t_ce (both are 4 MHz). But the NAME
and CONTRACT must be distinct: the video pipeline consumes dot_ce, not t_ce.
This matters immediately for clarity and later for CGB double-speed separation
where CPU T-cycles double but video dots remain constant.

IMPLEMENTATION:
- Add line counter (0-153, wraps to 0)
- Add dot-in-line counter (0-455, wraps to 0 and increments line)
- Derive line_start = (dot_in_line == 0)
- Derive frame_start = (dot_in_line == 0 && line_index == 0)
- dot_ce = t_ce for DMG (may differ for CGB)

TESTING: Unit test in test/unit/test_timebase_video.py:
- Verify 456 dots per scanline
- Verify 154 scanlines per frame (0-153)
- Verify 70224 dots per frame
- Verify line_start fires exactly 154 times per frame
- Verify frame_start fires exactly once per frame
- Verify dot_ce relationship to t_ce

Use the established logging pattern (bd-dny):
  [SUITE] test_timebase_video.py
    [CASE] test_dots_per_scanline
      [STEP] Run 456 dots on line 0
      [CHECK] dot_in_line wraps: expected=0 actual=0 OK
      [CHECK] line_index increments: expected=1 actual=1 OK
acceptance_criteria:
1. TimebaseOut struct extended with dot_ce, line_start, frame_start, line_index, dot_in_line
2. dot_ce = t_ce for DMG (contract distinct from t_ce even if value is same)
3. Unit test test/unit/test_timebase_video.py PASSES with all cases:
   a. 456 dots per scanline (dot_in_line wraps 455→0)
   b. 154 scanlines per frame (line_index wraps 153→0)
   c. 70224 dots per frame total
   d. line_start fires exactly 154 times per frame
   e. frame_start fires exactly once per frame
   f. dot_ce relationship to t_ce verified
4. Tests use structured logging format (bd-dny):
   [SUITE] test_timebase_video.py
     [CASE] test_dots_per_scanline
       [STEP] Run 456 dots on line 0
       [CHECK] dot_in_line wraps: expected=0 actual=0 OK
5. Existing CPU and timer tests still pass (timebase extension is additive)

title: Metamorphic and invariant tests for CPU core
description:
Implement metamorphic and invariant tests from plan section 16.5 in test/unit/test_cpu_invariants.py.

These tests verify STRUCTURAL PROPERTIES of the CPU that must hold across ALL instructions, not just specific instruction correctness. They catch bugs that per-instruction tests miss: spurious register writes, timing violations, hold failures.

TESTS TO IMPLEMENT:

1. UNTOUCHED REGISTERS UNCHANGED:
   For each instruction, verify that registers NOT targeted by that instruction remain bit-identical after execution. Uses opcode metadata (bd-36u) to determine which registers each instruction writes.
   Example: LD B,0x42 must leave A, F, C, D, E, H, L, SP unchanged.

2. m_ce=0 HOLD (simulation complement to formal bd-1yk):
   Hold m_ce=0 for 100 clock cycles. Verify ALL architectural state (registers, PC, SP, IME, phase) unchanged.
   Verify no write-enable signals asserted.

3. BRANCH NOT-TAKEN CYCLE COUNT:
   For each conditional branch (JR cc, JP cc, CALL cc, RET cc), verify that when the condition is NOT met, the instruction takes the SHORT cycle count (from opcode metadata).

4. F LOW NIBBLE ALWAYS ZERO (simulation complement to formal bd-lfg):
   After every instruction execution, verify F & 0x0F == 0.
   Parameterized over all instruction families.

5. MEMORY WRITE TIMING:
   For instructions that write to memory, verify the write occurs only in the documented M-cycle (from opcode metadata). No early or late writes.

6. cpu_hold_only:
   Set cpu_hold_only=true in SimStimulus. Verify CPU state frozen but timer/peripheral time continues to advance.

7. freeze_arch_time:
   Set freeze_arch_time=true in SimStimulus. Verify both CPU and peripheral architectural time frozen.

8. HALT STILLNESS (when HALT implemented in Phase 4):
   HALT with no wake source: verify zero register writes, zero bus requests, zero ALU activity, stable phase/debug outputs. Tests plan section 18.5.

9. EI DELAY INVARIANT (when interrupts in Phase 4):
   EI enables interrupts exactly one instruction later. EI followed by DI leaves interrupts disabled.

10. INTERRUPT SERVICE SEQUENCE:
    When interrupt is acknowledged: push PC, jump to vector, clear IF bit. Verify exact M-cycle sequence.

PARAMETERIZATION:
- Tests 1, 3, 4, 5 are parameterized over instruction families using opcode metadata
- Test generates parameters dynamically from sm83_opcodes.py
- Missing opcode metadata coverage is flagged as a test warning

LOGGING FORMAT:
  [INVARIANT] untouched_registers
    [PARAM] instruction=LD_B_n8 (opcode=0x06)
    [CHECK] A unchanged: 0x00 == 0x00 OK
    [CHECK] F unchanged: 0xB0 == 0xB0 OK
    [CHECK] C unchanged: 0x13 == 0x13 OK
    ...
    [PASS] untouched_registers[LD_B_n8]

ACCEPTANCE: All invariants pass for all implemented instruction families. Coverage report shows which invariants are tested for which opcode families.

title: Epic: PPU Power Baseline
description:
Validate that the PPU power architecture delivers measurable benefits and
establish the PPU contribution to the system power baseline. This is Phase 7
of the PPU bring-up (history/ppu.md section 18).

The PPU is potentially the noisiest toggle source in the system. The power-friendly
patterns must be validated, not assumed:

1. LCD-OFF QUIESCENCE: When LCDC.7 is off, verify zero render traffic, zero pixel
   emission, zero fetcher activity, zero FIFO churn, mode reported as disabled,
   VRAM/OAM fully accessible. This is both a correctness and power requirement.

2. MODE-LOCAL ENABLES: OAM scanner quiet in HBlank, fetcher quiet in mode 2,
   mixer quiet when no pixel emitted. Per-structure write enables for LY/mode,
   window state, OAM scan cursor, selected-object list, fetcher latches,
   BG FIFO, OBJ FIFO.

3. QUIET VIDEO-MEMORY PATHS: When no access is needed, emit Idle, hold chip
   selects low, hold write strobes false, avoid recomputing unused addresses.

4. LOGICAL PIXELS: Carry compact 2-bit color index + palette select + priority
   metadata until the last responsible moment. No early inflation to wide color.

5. ACTIVITY CAPTURE: SAIF/VCD-derived switching on representative PPU ROM windows.
   Record PPU-specific duty cycles: fetcher-active, FIFO-active, VRAM/OAM read,
   mode duty cycle, LCD-off quiescence.

6. SYNTHESIS BASELINE: PPU contribution to LUT/DFF/BRAM, constrained f_max
   impact, enable fanout.

ACCEPTANCE (part of Milestone F): LCD-off quiescence green, mode-local activity
measurable, quiet-bus metrics recorded, synthesis baseline updated, no debug-path
contamination in hardware build.

title: PPU register types and pure update functions (src/ppu/rtl/regs.spade)
description:
Implement the PPU register update logic as pure helper functions with typed records.

This module provides the TYPES and PURE UPDATE FUNCTIONS for PPU registers.
It is NOT a clocked entity -- ppu_core (bd-gaf0) owns all clocked state and
calls step_dot as a pure function. The register logic in regs.spade is invoked
WITHIN step_dot to compute the next register values.

This is consistent with the CPU architecture pattern: cpu/regs.spade defines
register types and update helpers, semantics.spade (step_mcycle) calls them,
and core.spade stores the result in registers on clock edge.

REGISTERS (MMIO 0xFF40-0xFF4B):
- LCDC (0xFF40): Lcdc struct with per-bit fields
- STAT (0xFF41): writable select bits only (StatSelect), derived status bits
- SCY (0xFF42), SCX (0xFF43): scroll position
- LY (0xFF44): current scanline, DERIVED from timing state, read-only
- LYC (0xFF45): LY compare value
- DMA (0xFF46): DMA trigger (handled by OAM DMA controller, not PPU)
- BGP (0xFF47): BG palette data
- OBP0 (0xFF48), OBP1 (0xFF49): OBJ palette data
- WY (0xFF4A), WX (0xFF4B): window position

PURE FUNCTIONS TO DEFINE:

fn apply_mmio_write(regs: PpuRegs, event: MmioWrite) -> PpuRegs
  Apply a CPU write to the appropriate register field. Returns updated regs.

fn read_stat(regs: PpuRegs, status: PpuStatusState, ly: uint<8>) -> uint<8>
  Compute the full STAT readback byte: bits 0-1 from visible_mode(status),
  bit 2 from LY==LYC, bits 3-6 from StatSelect, bit 7 always 1.
  Reports mode=0 when LCD disabled.

fn readback_register(addr: uint<8>, regs: PpuRegs, status: PpuStatusState, ly: uint<8>) -> uint<8>
  CPU read from any PPU register. Routes to read_stat for STAT,
  returns LY for 0xFF44, returns raw stored value for others.

KEY ARCHITECTURAL RULES:
- STAT canonical derivation: store only StatSelect. Derive mode bits from
  visible_mode(status), derive LYC==LY from continuous comparison. Bits 0-1
  report 0 when LCD disabled.
- LY is architectural: driven by timing state, not by bus writes. If later
  revision-specific LY-write behavior matters, add as a BehaviorFeature quirk.
- Register writes arrive as TimedPpuEvent, not raw bus pokes.
- Register reads use the readback visibility matrix (section 9.4.1.1).

WRITE-ENABLE DISCIPLINE: In the pure function model, this means that
apply_mmio_write returns the SAME regs when no write happens. The ppu_core
entity only sees a register change when step_dot's output.next_state differs
from the current state. Per-field write enables emerge naturally from the
pure function returning unchanged fields for most dots:
  1. CPU wrote a visible register (via TimedPpuEvent) -> field changes
  2. PPU advanced LY / mode -> ly/phase fields change
  3. STAT line changed -> stat_irq field changes
  4. LCD enable state transitioned -> run state changes
  5. Reset happened -> everything changes
  Every other dot: step_dot returns same register values -> no change.

title: BG fetcher state machine with epochs (src/ppu/rtl/fetcher.spade)
description:
Implement the background tile fetcher as a five-step state machine.

FETCH SEQUENCE (Pan Docs pixel FIFO):
1. Get tile index: read from tilemap based on current tile position
2. Get tile data low byte: read from tile data area
3. Get tile data high byte: read from tile data area + 1
4. Sleep: one dot idle
5. Push: push 8 pixels into BG FIFO (only if FIFO has room)

EPOCH MECHANISM:
struct FetcherState { epoch: uint<4>, step: FetcherStep, ... }

The epoch counter increments on:
- Window restart (window activation clears BG FIFO, resets fetcher)
- Fetch cancel (object fetch interrupts BG fetch)
- LCD disable
- Transfer restart on a new line
- Any transition that semantically invalidates in-flight fetch work

Any PpuMemResp whose PendingRead.epoch does not match the current FetcherState.epoch
is DISCARDED. This prevents stale VRAM data from an older generation from corrupting
fetcher state that has already moved on.

MEMORY INTERFACE: Issues PpuMemReq with MemClient::BgFetcher. Address computed
from PpuRenderInputs.scx_fetch, scy_fetch (re-read on each fetch) and visible
regs for tilemap/tile data selection.

INTERACTION WITH WINDOW: When window activates mid-line, the BG FIFO is cleared
and the fetcher restarts from the window tilemap. This is the primary source of
mode-3 window penalty (6 dots).

SAMPLED STATE: Uses PpuRenderInputs, NOT direct register reads. SCX[7:3] and
SCY are obtained from sample_render_inputs().

title: Green JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST custom ROM

Write and green the JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST test ROM.

Purpose: test joypad input with persistent state divergence between DUT and oracle, verifying that the deterministic stimulus system keeps both in sync.

Test approach:
1. Configure joypad interrupt
2. Use SimStimulus joyp_buttons to inject button presses at specific commit indices
3. Verify joypad register (0xFF00) reads correct values
4. Verify joypad interrupt fires when buttons pressed
5. Verify state remains consistent after button release

This ROM tests the SimStimulus → DUT path AND the equivalent event injection into the PyBoy oracle. Both must see the same button events at the same logical time.

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: Author Wave B custom ROM assembly source
description:
Write SM83 assembly source for all 3 Wave B custom test ROMs (§17.2) targeting timer/interrupt correctness.

ROM SPECIFICATIONS:

1. TIMER_DIV_BASIC (bench/roms/timer_div_basic.asm):
   Tests: DIV free-running counter, DIV reset-on-write, TIMA increment rate, TAC prescaler selection, TMA reload on overflow
   Strategy:
   - Read DIV, wait known cycles, read again, verify increment
   - Write to DIV, verify reset to 0
   - Configure TAC with each prescaler (4096/262144/65536/16384 Hz), wait calibrated cycles, verify TIMA value
   - Set TMA to known value, let TIMA overflow, verify TIMA reloads from TMA
   Critical: Timer derives from shared timebase (§6.1.1), not private counter. Falling-edge detection for obscure increment timing.
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_div_count, __checkpoint_div_reset, __checkpoint_tac_prescaler, __checkpoint_tma_reload

2. EI_DELAY (bench/roms/ei_delay.asm):
   Tests: EI enables interrupts exactly one instruction later (§16.5 invariant)
   Strategy:
   - Set up timer interrupt (IE bit 2, configure TIMA to overflow soon)
   - Execute: EI / NOP / <test instruction>
   - Verify: interrupt fires AFTER the NOP, not during EI or the instruction after EI
   - Test EI followed by: NOP, LD, ADD, JP, DI (verify DI after EI cancels)
   - Test EI followed by EI (verify no double-enable issues)
   - Test RETI (enables interrupts AND returns -- verify timing)
   Critical edge case: EI immediately followed by DI should leave interrupts disabled.
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_ei_nop, __checkpoint_ei_di, __checkpoint_reti

3. TIMER_IRQ_HALT (bench/roms/timer_irq_halt.asm):
   Tests: Timer overflow fires IRQ, wakes CPU from HALT, interrupt service routine executes correctly
   Strategy:
   - Configure timer for near-immediate overflow
   - Enable timer interrupt (IE bit 2)
   - EI / HALT
   - Verify: CPU halts, timer overflows, IF bit set, CPU wakes, vectors to 0x0050, ISR executes, RETI returns to instruction after HALT
   - Test with different wake sources (timer, serial, joypad via SimStimulus)
   - Test HALT without EI (HALT bug: CPU halts but wakes without servicing interrupt, PC fails to increment)
   Critical: HALT must produce real stillness (§18.5): no register writes, no bus requests, no ALU activity.
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_halt_enter, __checkpoint_irq_fire, __checkpoint_isr_execute, __checkpoint_halt_wake

ACCEPTANCE: All 3 ROMs assemble, pass ABI validation, registered in manifest with oracle_mode=instr_commit.

title: RETI implementation
description:
Implement RETI instruction in the CPU phase machine.

RETI = RET + set IME to Enabled (immediately, unlike EI).

Sequence (4 M-cycles, same as RET):
1. Fetch RETI opcode
2. Pop low byte of return address from stack
3. Pop high byte of return address from stack
4. Internal cycle, set PC to popped address, set IME=Enabled

Unlike EI, RETI enables interrupts IMMEDIATELY (no one-instruction delay). This is because RETI is typically the last instruction of an interrupt handler, and the next instruction will be in the interrupted code which should already be interruptible.

RETI is the normal way to exit an interrupt handler. Using RET from an interrupt handler would leave IME=Disabled, causing interrupts to stop working.
close_reason: Already implemented and covered by test_semantics_flow

title: Green PPU_WINDOW custom ROM
description:
Write and green PPU_WINDOW.

Scope:
- Verify WX/WY positioning and LCDC window enable behavior
- Check semantic overlay behavior against the background path
- Keep this bead focused on window semantics, not sprite rendering

Why split: window correctness is an independent milestone after base background rendering.
