title: Sampled render state layer (src/ppu/sem/sample.spade)
description:
Implement the explicit sampling layer that separates register-file values from
what the renderer actually sees at each phase boundary.

This is the HIGHEST-LEVERAGE architectural revision in the PPU plan. The gap
between prose visibility matrix and typed sampling layer is exactly where
raster-effect bugs breed.

TYPES:

struct PpuSamplingState {
  scx_low3_line: uint<3>,          -- sampled at scanline start
  wy_triggered_this_frame: bool,   -- checked at mode-2 start only
  window_enable_at_mode2_start: bool,
}

struct PpuRenderInputs {
  scx_fetch: uint<8>,              -- re-read on each tile fetch
  scy_fetch: uint<8>,              -- re-read on each tile fetch
  scx_low3_line: uint<3>,          -- from PpuSamplingState
  wx_live: uint<8>,                -- live comparison (rendered X+7 == WX)
  wy_triggered_this_frame: bool,   -- from PpuSamplingState
  bgp_pop: uint<8>,                -- sampled at pop/mix boundary
  obp0_pop: uint<8>,               -- sampled at pop/mix boundary
  obp1_pop: uint<8>,               -- sampled at pop/mix boundary
  lyc_eq_live: bool,               -- continuously evaluated
}

fn sample_render_inputs(
  visible: PpuVisibleState,
  sampled: PpuSamplingState,
  phase: PpuPhase,
) -> PpuRenderInputs

The visibility matrix (history/ppu.md section 9.4.1) should compile into either:
- fields in PpuSamplingState (phase-edge latched values),
- PpuRenderInputs helpers (derived from visible+sampled+phase),
- or explicit phase-edge update functions.

PHASE-EDGE UPDATE POINTS:
- At mode-2 start: latch scx_low3_line, check WY, check window enable
- On tile fetch: re-read SCX[7:3], SCY from visible regs
- On pixel pop: re-read BGP/OBP0/OBP1 from visible regs
- Continuously: LYC==LY comparison

WHY: Pan Docs explicitly distinguishes several different register visibility
mechanisms. Without an explicit sampling layer, it is too easy for the HDL to
accidentally read a live register value where a sampled value is architecturally
correct (or vice versa). This caused subtle bugs in many emulators.

title: HALT bug (HaltBugPending) implementation
description:
Implement the HALT bug behavior using the HaltBugPending state.

THE HALT BUG: When HALT is executed with IME=Disabled and no interrupt pending (IE & IF == 0), the CPU resumes execution but the HALT bug is triggered: the byte after HALT is read twice (PC fails to increment on the first read of the next instruction).

This means the first byte of the next instruction is fetched twice:
- First fetch: reads byte at PC, does NOT increment PC
- Second fetch: reads same byte at PC, increments PC normally
- If the next instruction is multi-byte, only the first byte is doubled

Example:
  DI          ; IME=Disabled
  HALT        ; no pending interrupts → HALT bug
  LD A,0x42   ; opcode 0x3E fetched twice
              ; CPU sees: 0x3E 0x3E 0x42 → LD A,0x3E (wrong!)

STATE MACHINE:
- HaltState::HaltBugPending is set when: HALT executes, IME=Disabled, (IE&IF)==0
- On next fetch: read byte but do NOT increment PC, transition to Running
- The instruction decoder sees a corrupted instruction stream

IMPLEMENTATION:
- In the Fetch phase: check if HaltState == HaltBugPending
- If so: emit BusReq::Read(PC) but set pc_write to None (don't advance PC)
- Transition HaltState to Running
- Next M-cycle: normal fetch resumes (PC hasn't advanced, so same byte fetched again)

TESTING:
- Create test/unit/test_halt_bug.py with cases:
  1. HALT bug with single-byte instruction after HALT
  2. HALT bug with multi-byte instruction after HALT
  3. HALT bug does NOT occur when IME=Enabled
  4. HALT bug does NOT occur when (IE&IF)!=0
  5. Verify exact PC behavior through the double-read

WHY P2: The plan (§8.3) says "You can defer HaltBugPending until the interrupt milestone if you want, but design the type so that adding it later is a local extension rather than a rewrite." The type is already designed in. This bead adds the implementation. P2 because it is needed for full correctness but not for initial Wave B green.

title: Test logging and diagnostics standard
description:
Define and implement the project-wide standard for test output logging, diagnostic formatting, and failure reporting. Applies to ALL test tiers (unit, formal, ROM differential, lockstep, power).

LOGGING HIERARCHY:
  [SUITE] test_alu.py
    [CASE] test_add8_basic
      [STEP] Initialize DUT with A=0x0F, B=0x01, carry_in=0
      [STEP] Execute ADD A,B
      [CHECK] Result: expected=0x10 actual=0x10 OK
      [CHECK] Z flag: expected=0 actual=0 OK
      [CHECK] H flag: expected=1 actual=1 OK
      [PASS] test_add8_basic (0.003s)
    [CASE] test_add8_overflow
      [STEP] Initialize DUT with A=0xFF, B=0x01
      [CHECK] Z flag: expected=1 actual=0 FAIL
      [FAIL] test_add8_overflow -- Z flag mismatch
        [CONTEXT] Opcode: ADD A,B (0x80)
        [CONTEXT] Inputs: A=0xFF B=0x01
        [CONTEXT] First divergent field: flags.z
  [SUMMARY] test_alu.py: 47/48 passed, 1 failed (0.15s)

DIAGNOSTIC LEVELS:
- QUIET: Only failures and summary (CI dashboards)
- NORMAL: Steps + checks + summary (default)
- VERBOSE: All signal values at each step (debugging)
- TRACE: Full CommitTrace dumps (lockstep debugging)

IMPLEMENTATION:
- test/harness/logging_std.py: TestLogger class with suite/case/step/check/context/summary methods
- Configurable via --log-level flag
- Automatic failure context: register dump, last N bus transactions, phase state
- JSON output mode (--json) for machine parsing
- Color output for terminal, disabled when piped

FAILURE ARTIFACTS (emitted automatically):
- Summary of first divergent field
- Full expected vs actual state comparison
- Instruction context from opcode metadata
- Waveform file path (if capture enabled)
- Replay capsule (for lockstep tests)

RATIONALE: When a test fails in CI, the developer must immediately understand: what was tested, expected behavior, actual behavior, and first divergent field. Without a standard, each test invents its own format, making output noisy and hard to parse.

ACCEPTANCE: Every test bead uses TestLogger. CI output parseable by test runner for coverage tracking. Failure output actionable without re-running.

title: Wire physical memory wrappers into membus, eliminate DMA mirrors
description:
Replace all inline clocked_memory_init calls in src/bus/membus.spade with
instantiations of the physical memory wrappers from src/mem/phys/. This
eliminates the DMA mirror duplication pattern and connects the bus to actual
SPRAM/EBR-backed storage.

PROBLEM: membus.spade currently has 7 clocked_memory_init instances:
  Line 262: rom_mem         (32KB, 1 write port)  -> 65,536 DFFs
  Line 263: dma_rom_mem     (32KB, 1 write port)  -> 65,536 DFFs  [DMA MIRROR]
  Line 276: wram_mem        (8KB,  1 write port)  -> 16,384 DFFs
  Line 281: dma_wram_mem    (8KB,  1 write port)  -> 16,384 DFFs  [DMA MIRROR]
  Line 287: hram_mem        (127B, 1 write port)  ->    254 DFFs
  Line 292: dma_hram_mem    (127B, 1 write port)  ->    254 DFFs  [DMA MIRROR]
  Line 307: oam_mem         (256B, 2 write ports) ->  1,024 DFFs
  TOTAL:                                           ~165,372 DFFs

After this change: 0 DFFs from memory storage (all in SPRAM/EBR blocks).
The 3 DMA mirror memories (dma_rom_mem, dma_wram_mem, dma_hram_mem) are
COMPLETELY ELIMINATED because the physical wrappers provide dual-read ports.

IMPLEMENTATION PLAN:

1. THREAD t_index INTO MEMBUS:
   The SPRAM wrappers need timebase t_index for time-multiplexing. Add t_index:
   uint<2> as a new parameter to membus entity. Thread it from hardware_core,
   which already has access to timebase output (tb.t_index).

   membus entity signature change:
     pub entity membus(
         clk: clock,
         rst: bool,
         m_ce: bool,
         t_index: uint<2>,   // NEW: T-cycle index for SPRAM time-multiplexing
         req: BusReq,
         ...
     ) -> (BusResp, BusObs)

   hardware_core wiring change:
     let (bus_resp_next, bus_obs) = inst membus(
         clk, rst, tb.m_ce,
         tb.t_index,           // NEW
         cpu_bus_req(...),
         ...
     );

   membus_test_top wiring change:
     Add t_index_i: uint<2> input port, wire through to membus.

2. REPLACE ROM MEMORY:
   Remove: rom_mem, dma_rom_mem (lines 262-263)
   Add: let (rom_data, dma_rom_data) = inst rom_image_spram(clk, t_index,
            rom_addr(region, addr), rom_addr(dma_source_region, dma.source_addr));

3. REPLACE WRAM MEMORY:
   Remove: wram_mem, dma_wram_mem (lines 276-284)
   Add: let (wram_data, dma_wram_data) = inst wram_spram(clk, t_index,
            wram_addr(region, addr), wram_addr(dma_source_region, dma.source_addr),
            wram_we, wram_waddr, wram_wdata);

4. REPLACE HRAM MEMORY:
   Remove: hram_mem, dma_hram_mem (lines 287-296)
   Add: let (hram_data, dma_hram_data) = inst hram_ebr(clk,
            hram_addr(region, addr), hram_addr(dma_source_region, dma.source_addr),
            hram_we, hram_waddr, hram_wdata);

5. REPLACE OAM MEMORY:
   Remove: oam_mem (lines 307-314)
   Add: let oam_data = inst oam_ebr(clk,
            oam_addr(region, addr),
            cpu_oam_we, cpu_oam_waddr, cpu_oam_wdata,
            dma.write_en, trunc(dma.dest_addr - 0xfe00u16), dma_oam_wdata);

6. UPDATE READ PATHS:
   Remove individual read_memory calls (lines 298-300, 316-318, 329) since the
   physical memory wrappers return data directly from their dual-read ports.

   The DMA read data mux (dma_read_data function, line 217-225) now uses the
   second output of each SPRAM wrapper instead of separate mirror memories.

7. REMOVE UNUSED IMPORTS:
   Remove: use std::mem::{clocked_memory_init, read_memory};
   These are no longer needed once all memory is physical.

8. REMOVE DEAD CODE:
   Lines 260-261 have dummy references to physical_memory_kind:
     let _ = physical_memory_kind(region);
     let _ = physical_memory_kind(dma_source_region);
   Remove these since physical memory is now actually used.

READ LATENCY CONSIDERATION:
The physical memory wrappers have 1-cycle read latency (synchronous reads) vs
the current combinational reads. This affects response timing:
- CURRENT: address presented -> data available SAME cycle (combinational)
- NEW: address presented at Tn -> data available at Tn+1 (registered)

The M-cycle boundary already provides a natural registration point. CPU reads
happen during the M-cycle, and bus_read_data_reg in hardware_core latches the
response at the M-cycle edge. As long as the SPRAM/EBR output is stable before
the M-cycle edge, timing is met. The time-multiplexing schedule in the SPRAM
wrappers must ensure this.

For simulation: the test harness samples output after RisingEdge + Timer(1ns).
If read latency changes, some tests may need adjustment to account for the
1-cycle delay. Specifically: write-then-read tests may need an extra cycle
between write and read.

TEST CASES:

1. WRAM ROUND TRIP (EXISTING test_wram_round_trips_and_m_ce_gates_writes):
   Write 0x5A to 0xC123, read back. Must still return 0x5A.
   This verifies the SPRAM wrapper's write-then-read path works through the
   new wiring. May need timing adjustment for synchronous read latency.

2. HRAM ROUND TRIP (EXISTING test_hram_round_trips):
   Write 0xC3 to 0xFF80, read back. Must still return 0xC3.
   Verifies EBR wrapper integration.

3. ECHO RAM MIRRORS WRAM (EXISTING test_echo_ram_mirrors_wram_in_both_directions):
   Write via WRAM addr, read via Echo addr (and vice versa). Both must see
   same data. This is critical: the old DMA mirror pattern used synchronized
   writes to keep copies coherent. Now there's only ONE memory — Echo and WRAM
   addresses must map to the same SPRAM location.

4. OAM DMA FULL TRANSFER (EXISTING test_ff46_write_starts_internal_dma_copy_for_160_mcycles):
   Write to WRAM, trigger DMA, verify OAM gets the data. This is the most
   complex integration test: DMA reads from WRAM SPRAM (second read port)
   and writes to OAM EBR (DMA write port). Both must work through the new
   physical memory wiring.

5. ROM READS ZERO IMAGE (EXISTING test_rom_reads_zero_image_and_ignores_writes):
   ROM should read initial values. NOTE: SPRAM cannot be initialized, so the
   ROM initialization mechanism must be addressed. For simulation, the Verilog
   behavioral model can be pre-loaded. For synthesis, ROM data is loaded at
   boot time. This test may need to be updated to account for SPRAM's undefined
   initial state.

6. IDLE RETURNS FF (EXISTING test_idle_returns_ff_and_idle_bus_obs):
   BusReq::Idle must still return 0xFF data and idle BusObs. No memory access
   should occur during idle.

7. BUS OWNERSHIP BLOCKING (EXISTING tests for DMA/PPU blocking):
   All existing blocking tests must pass unchanged. The physical memory
   wrappers don't affect bus ownership logic.

8. NEW: CONCURRENT CPU AND DMA READS FROM SAME MEMORY:
   During an OAM DMA transfer from WRAM (source 0xC1xx), CPU reads from a
   different WRAM address (e.g., 0xC000). In the old design, these went to
   separate mirror memories. Now they go to the same SPRAM block via time-
   multiplexed ports. Verify both CPU and DMA get correct data.
   NOTE: CPU WRAM reads are blocked during DMA, but this tests the SPRAM
   time-multiplexing correctness at the hardware level.

9. NEW: t_index SIGNAL THREADING:
   Verify membus_test_top accepts the new t_index_i input. Verify it reaches
   the SPRAM wrappers. Can be verified by examining the generated Verilog
   or by simulation with different t_index values.

10. NEW: MEMORY-FREE DFF COUNT:
    After synthesis (tools/verify_hw_build.sh --skip-build), check yosys stat.
    DFF count should drop from ~68,000 to < 1,000. LUT4 count should drop
    from ~72,000 to < 5,280. This is the primary quantitative acceptance
    criterion for the synthesis fit.

11. ALL EXISTING ROM TESTS PASS:
    Run the full precommit test suite (tools/run_precommit_checks.sh). All
    tests must pass, including:
    - test/unit/test_membus.py (12 tests)
    - test/rom/test_loads_basic.py
    - tools/run_cpu_instrs_blargg_verilator.sh
    - test/harness/test_e2e_smoke.py
    Any test failure indicates a behavioral regression from the memory wiring
    change and must be investigated.

FILES CHANGED:
- src/bus/membus.spade (major: replace all memory instantiation)
- src/bus/membus_test_top.spade (add t_index_i port)
- src/board/icebreaker_top.spade (thread t_index to membus)
- test/unit/test_membus.py (add t_index_i driving, adjust for read latency)

ACCEPTANCE:
- membus.spade contains ZERO clocked_memory_init calls
- membus.spade contains ZERO DMA mirror memory instances
- All 12 existing test_membus.py tests pass
- All precommit tests pass
- Yosys stat shows SB_SPRAM256KA > 0, SB_RAM40_4K > 0, SB_DFF < 1024

title: PPU LCD-off quiescence and mode-local power verification
description:
Verify that the PPU power architecture delivers measurable stillness.

LCD-OFF QUIESCENCE:
When LCDC.7 is cleared, verify:
- Zero render traffic (no memory requests)
- Zero pixel emission (no ScanoutEvent::Pixel)
- Zero fetcher activity (fetcher in idle state)
- Zero FIFO churn (FIFOs empty and not toggling)
- Mode reported as disabled (visible_mode returns appropriate value)
- VRAM/OAM fully accessible to CPU

MODE-LOCAL ENABLES:
- OAM scanner: quiet in HBlank and VBlank (no toggling)
- Fetcher: quiet in mode 2 (no memory requests)
- Mixer: quiet when no pixel emitted
- FIFO shifters: quiet when empty

PER-STRUCTURE WRITE ENABLES:
Verify separate write-enable logic for: LY/mode, window state, OAM scan cursor,
selected-object list, fetcher latches, BG FIFO, OBJ FIFO.
Do not update the entire state bundle every dot when only one field changed.

QUIET VIDEO-MEMORY PATHS:
When no access needed: Idle emission, chip selects low, write strobes false,
no unused address recomputation.

TESTING:
- Simulation test that runs PPU through LCD-off period and verifies zero toggling
- Activity capture showing duty cycles per subsystem
- Formal property: LCD-off implies zero register writes (except LcdRunState itself)

This task is part of the broader PPU contribution to Milestone F (power baseline).
close_reason: Completed with simulation-backed PPU quiescence counters and coverage; bounded formal split to bd-1ye9

title: PyBoy oracle interface scaffold
description:
Implement the stable live oracle interface (§14.1) in bench/pyboy/oracle.py.

Interface (Python class or protocol):
  class Oracle:
    def reset(model_profile, reset_profile) -> None
    def step_commit() -> OracleCommit
    def read_mem(addr: int) -> int
    def write_event(ev: SimEvent) -> None
    def snapshot() -> bytes
    def restore(snapshot: bytes) -> None

OracleCommit should map to the CommitTrace schema (§7.2) — at minimum: pc_before, opcode, register state after, phase after, bus request, bus response.

Implementation details:
- Wrap PyBoy instance in headless mode
- step_commit() calls pyboy.tick() and extracts post-state
- read_mem() uses pyboy.memory[] or equivalent API
- snapshot()/restore() use PyBoy save state mechanism
- Must be deterministic: same ROM + same events = same trace

This is the ONLY oracle (thesis #6). No second emulator. No file-based golden traces (thesis #7).

title: Formal verification: bus ownership mutual exclusion invariant
description:
Create formal/bus_owner.sby — proves that at most one bus owner is active at any time.

PROPERTY: For all reachable states, exactly one of the following is true:
- BusOwner == Cpu (CPU controls the bus)
- BusOwner == OamDma (DMA controller controls the bus)
- BusOwner == Ppu (PPU controls the bus)
- BusOwner == Idle (no one controls the bus)

ADDITIONAL PROPERTIES:
- Bus ownership transitions are clean: no glitching between owners within a cycle
- When owner is Idle, no bus transactions occur
- DMA ownership preempts CPU (CPU cannot initiate transactions during DMA)
- PPU ownership blocks CPU access to VRAM/OAM regions

WHY FORMAL: Bus ownership violations cause data corruption and are extremely hard to debug from waveforms. Formal verification proves the invariant holds for ALL possible state sequences, including corner cases at ownership transitions.

TOOL: yosys + sby (SymbiYosys). Use k-induction.

title: Epic: PPU DMA Realism — Wave E
description:
Implement realistic DMA/OAM interaction behavior after BG/OBJ/composition are
already green. Wave E of the PPU decomposition (history/ppu.md section 17.5).

This is deliberately staged LAST among the rendering waves because DMA-PPU
interaction is the most complex bus-ownership scenario and should not entangle
earlier rendering bring-up.

Components:
- OAM DMA + PPU rendering overlap behavior
- CPU HRAM-only restriction during DMG OAM DMA (already partially in bd-1gq)
- Mode-2 object disappearance when DMA is active
- Optional: exact mode-3 DMA fetch glitch path (staged, only if required by suites)

The OAM DMA controller entity already exists (src/periph/oam_dma.spade). This
epic adds the PPU-side interaction logic and verifies the cross-subsystem behavior.

Wave E ROMs: DMA_MODE2_HIDE, DMA_MODE3_FETCH_GLITCH, DMA_OBJ_DROP_LINES.

ACCEPTANCE: Wave E owned ROMs green, staged DMA interaction behavior verified,
CPU HRAM-only restriction enforced during DMA.

title: soc_lockstep_top simulation top
description:
Create src/sim/soc_lockstep_top.spade — full SoC lockstep simulation top.

This top provides:
- Instantiates cpu_core + timebase + bus fabric + memory
- Full system composition for lockstep oracle comparison
- SimStimulus input ports (§12.6)
- CommitTrace outputs as flat ports for Cocotb
- BusObs sideband output for memory access diagnostics

Unlike cpu_test_top, this one composes the full system and is used for ROM-based integration tests and live oracle lockstep.

Initially instantiates only Wave A memory model: ROM + WRAM + HRAM + dummy IO. Expanded in later waves.

title: Pixel pipeline and scanout types (PixelEmit, ScanoutEvent, object stages)
ppu_core entity (src/ppu/rtl/core.spade)
description:
Create the clocked ppu_core entity that wraps the pure step_dot function, AND
compose the timing/STAT/register helpers INTO step_dot's body.

This bead is the INTEGRATION POINT where step_dot goes from stub to working.

COMPOSITION RESPONSIBILITY:
bd-2r31 creates step_dot as a type-correct stub (signature + pass-through).
bd-o9eu, bd-3iak, and bd-909g create pure helper functions in rtl/*.spade.
THIS BEAD (gaf0) wires them together:

  fn step_dot(state: PpuState, input: DotInput) -> DotOutput {
    // 1. Apply register writes (from bd-909g helpers)
    let next_regs = apply_mmio_write(state.visible.regs, input.bus_events);

    // 2. Advance timing/mode (from bd-o9eu helpers)
    let (next_phase, next_ly, ...) = advance_timing(state, input, next_regs);

    // 3. Evaluate STAT line and IRQ (from bd-3iak helpers)
    let (next_stat, irq_req) = evaluate_stat(next_regs, next_phase, next_ly, ...);

    // 4. Update sampled state at phase boundaries (from bd-d2sn)
    let next_sampled = update_sampling(state.sampled, next_phase, next_regs);

    // 5. Compose DotOutput
    DotOutput { next_state: PpuState { ... }, irq_req, scanout: ..., ... }
  }

The stub rendering paths (fetcher, FIFO, mixer, OAM scan) remain pass-through
in Wave A. They are filled in by Wave B (BG/window) and Wave C (objects) beads.

OPERATIONAL WRAPPER (ppu_core entity):
entity ppu_core(
    clk: clock,
    rst: bool,
    dot_ce: bool,
    video_now: VideoCoord,
    bus_events: [TimedPpuEvent; N],
    bus_event_count: uint<4>,
    mem_resp: PpuMemResp,
    dma_state: OamDmaState,
) -> (PpuMmioResp, PpuMemReqs, PpuIrqReq, ScanoutEvent, PpuDebugTrace)

INTERNAL STRUCTURE:
1. Register bank holds PpuState (visible + status + sampled + render)
2. On each dot_ce, form DotInput from ports
3. Call step_dot(current_state, input) -> DotOutput
4. Commit output.next_state to registers
5. Drive output ports from DotOutput fields

RESET STATE: On rst, initialize to a known state consistent with the current
BehaviorConfig (DMG skip-boot equivalent for the default profile).

CLOCK DISCIPLINE:
- Single root clock (clk)
- All state changes gated by dot_ce
- No fabric-gated clocks
- The entity itself does NOT generate dot_ce; it receives it from the timebase

BUILD FLAVORS:
- synth: outputs only PpuMmioResp, PpuMemReqs, PpuIrqReq, pixel stream
- sim_semantic: additionally outputs PpuSemanticCommit
- sim_trace: additionally outputs PpuDebugTrace

Build flavor selection is handled by which simulation top instantiates this
entity, not by conditional logic inside the entity itself.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
1. step_dot is no longer a stub — it produces non-trivial output:
   - Mode transitions (OamScan→Transfer→HBlank→VBlank) actually occur
   - LY increments through 0-153
   - STAT line evaluation produces correct IRQ requests
   - Register writes via TimedPpuEvent are applied
2. ppu_core compiles and advances state on dot_ce
3. Smoke test: run ppu_core for 70224 dots (1 frame), verify mode sequence
4. Existing CPU/bus/timer tests still pass (regression safety)

TESTING: Smoke test in test/unit/test_ppu_core_smoke.py:
  [SUITE] test_ppu_core_smoke.py
    [CASE] test_one_frame_mode_sequence
      [STEP] Reset ppu_core, advance 70224 dots
      [CHECK] Saw mode transitions: 2→3→0 on visible lines OK
      [CHECK] Saw VBlank on lines 144-153 OK
      [CHECK] LY cycled 0-153 OK
    [CASE] test_dot_ce_hold
      [STEP] Hold dot_ce=0 for 100 clocks
      [CHECK] State unchanged OK

title: Encoding optimization evaluation
description:
Evaluate encoding optimizations based on measurement (§18.8).

Candidates:
- Phase enum: binary vs one-hot encoding
- BusReq: binary vs one-hot
- AluReq: binary vs one-hot
- Register select: binary vs one-hot
- State machine encodings

Process:
1. Measure toggle counts from SAIF activity captures
2. Compare area (LUTs) and toggle counts for different encodings
3. Only change encodings where measurement shows significant benefit
4. Re-verify correctness after any encoding change

IMPORTANT (§18.8): Binary enums, one-hot, and Gray encodings all trade area and toggles differently on FPGA fabrics. Let measurement drive encoding decisions. The first pass should prioritize clarity and typed structure.

title: Pipeline insertion evaluation
description:
Evaluate pipeline insertion candidates based on synthesis timing reports (§19).

Candidates (§19.3):
1. decode → operand select → ALU → flag pack (main CPU datapath)
2. pixel fetch → shade/merge → output (PPU)
3. cartridge/PPU address pipelines

Evaluation criteria:
- Does the combinational path exceed the target f_max?
- How much area does pipeline insertion cost (extra registers)?
- Does pipeline insertion change the semantic interface?

Process (§19.4):
1. Run synthesis/P&R with timing analysis
2. Identify critical paths
3. If critical path is in a candidate cluster: wrap in pipeline(N)
4. Re-verify with formal equivalence (formal/cpu_refactor.eqy) or differential tests
5. Update CPU phase machine if execute latency changes (§19.5)

IMPORTANT (§19.0): Do NOT use dynamic pipelines in CPU control core. Confine to leaf datapaths with clear request/response boundaries.

This task should only be started AFTER Milestone E baseline is recorded.

title: E2E milestone gate test: Milestone E -- power baseline ready
description:
Create tools/gate_milestone_e.sh -- acceptance gate for Milestone E (§22: Power Baseline Ready).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (§22 Milestone E):
1. HALT quiescence test green (bd-24t): in HALT with no wake, verify zero register writes, zero bus requests, zero ALU activity, stable outputs
2. ALU idle isolation measurable: when AluReq::Idle, ALU input buses show no toggling
3. Activity baseline recorded for representative ROM windows (bd-32w): SAIF files generated
4. Synthesis baseline recorded under explicit nextpnr iCE40 clock constraints (bd-1ke): LUT/DFF/SPRAM counts, f_max
5. No debug-path contamination in hardware build (bd-31b): hardware top has no CommitTrace/DebugTrace ports

SCRIPT VALIDATES:
- HALT quiescence test passes
- ALU idle isolation test reports zero toggling
- SAIF activity files exist and are non-empty
- nextpnr synthesis report exists with resource utilization
- Hardware top Verilog has no debug signal ports
- Register write-enable duty cycle report exists (bd-2og)

LOGGING: Reports resource utilization summary (LUTs, DFFs, SPRAMs, f_max), activity metrics, and quiescence verification.

ACCEPTANCE: Script exits 0 when all criteria pass. Milestone E confirms that the power-friendly architecture actually delivers measurable benefits.

title: Epic: ALU & Decode Subsystems (Phase 3a)
description:
Implement and fully verify the ALU and instruction decoder as isolated, pure Spade functions before integrating them into the CPU core.

ALU (§11):
- Pure combinational function: fn alu(req: AluReq) -> AluResult
- 8-bit arithmetic: ADD, ADC, SUB, SBC, CP with correct flag computation
- 8-bit logic: AND, OR, XOR with correct flag computation
- INC/DEC 8-bit with flag preservation rules
- 16-bit: ADD HL,rr / ADD SP,e8 / LD HL,SP+e8 (tricky flag cases from gbxcule)
- Miscellaneous: DAA, CPL, SCF, CCF
- CB-prefix: all rotate/shift variants, BIT/RES/SET
- Operand isolation: Idle case ensures ALU inputs stay quiet when unused (§11.2)
- Three verification lanes (§11.4): curated examples, generated vectors from gbxcule, nightly exhaustive sweeps

Decoder (§8.0):
- Machine-readable opcode metadata source (sm83_opcodes.py): opcode, prefix, mnemonic, length, cycles, flag policy, operands, compare scope
- Unprefixed opcode decode: maps opcode byte to DecodedOp
- CB-prefixed decode: maps CB opcode byte to DecodedOp
- Decode completeness test: every opcode maps to something, no holes

RATIONALE: These are the two largest pure-function subsystems. Verifying them in isolation (§16.2) removes semantic uncertainty before lockstep testing. The ALU flag formulas are the #1 source of subtle CPU bugs in Game Boy implementations—lifting directly from gbxcule (§11.3) and generating test vectors from the same source provides defense in depth. Operand isolation (§11.2) is a power architecture decision, not an aesthetic one: it prevents large adders from seeing toggling inputs during non-ALU cycles.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone B): ALU unit tests green. Decode snapshot tests green. Single-op tests for LD/INC/DEC/ADD/AND/XOR/OR/CP green. Every opcode family in at least one test.

title: IE/IF registers in peripheral/IO layer
description:
Implement IE and IF registers in src/periph/interrupts.spade.

Registers:
- IE (0xFFFF): Interrupt Enable. Bits [4:0] enable individual interrupt sources.
- IF (0xFF0F): Interrupt Flag. Bits [4:0] indicate pending interrupts.

Interrupt sources (bit positions):
- Bit 0: VBlank
- Bit 1: LCD STAT
- Bit 2: Timer
- Bit 3: Serial
- Bit 4: Joypad

Design (§12.3):
- IE/IF live in the peripheral/IO side, NOT inside the CPU
- CPU receives a narrow pending vector: irq_pending = IE & IF (5 bits)
- CPU emits an acknowledge signal when servicing an interrupt
- On acknowledge, the corresponding IF bit is cleared

Interface to CPU:
  struct IrqPending { pending: uint<5> }  // = IE & IF, computed in peripheral layer
  struct IrqAck { ack_bit: Option<uint<3>> }  // which interrupt the CPU is acknowledging

Upper bits of IE/IF read as 1 on real hardware (bits [7:5] = 0xE0 for IF).

title: Pin Cocotb and simulator backend versions
description:
Pin Cocotb version and configure the default simulator backend.

Requirements:
- Pin Cocotb version in toolchain/python.lock
- Select and pin default simulator: Icarus Verilog for initial development, with Verilator as optional backend for long regressions (§16.4)
- Verify Cocotb can drive a trivial Spade-generated Verilog testbench
- Pin the swim (Spade build) version if not already locked
- Document simulator selection rationale in toolchain/versions.md

POLICY (§16.4): Authored dynamic testbenches are written in Python/Cocotb. If more speed is needed, switch simulator backend to Verilator. Do NOT create a separate C++/Verilator authored-test lane.

title: Epic: PPU Architecture & Type Foundation
description:
Define the complete PPU type system before any HDL implementation begins. This epic
establishes the architectural vocabulary that the entire PPU bring-up will use.

The revised PPU plan (history/ppu.md) makes several critical architectural decisions
that must be encoded in types FIRST:

1. ONE CANONICAL RUN-STATE, NOT REDUNDANT MODE/LCD FIELDS. PpuVisibleState stores
   only regs+LY. PpuMode and lcd_enabled are DERIVED from LcdRunState+PpuPhase via
   pure projection functions. This eliminates impossible states like mode=PixelTransfer
   while lcd_enabled=false.

2. EXPLICIT SAMPLED RENDER STATE. SCX low-3 bits sampled at scanline start, WY
   checked at mode-2 start, window enable sampled at mode-2 start -- these are NOT
   direct register reads. PpuSamplingState + PpuRenderInputs make these architectural
   samples first-class, preventing the most common class of raster-effect bugs.

3. TIMED EVENT STREAM, NOT SINGLE-WRITE MMIO. CPU-to-PPU writes arrive as
   TimedPpuEvent with seq ordering in video coordinates, not as a direct poke. This
   decouples CPU cadence from video cadence and gives replay/oracle/DUT one canonical
   event representation.

4. TAGGED MEMORY PROTOCOL. PpuMemReq carries MemTxnTag (region, client, id) and
   PpuMemResp carries structured outcomes (Ok/Denied/UndefinedRead with reasons). No
   positional response matching. Fetch epochs prevent stale responses from corrupting
   post-restart fetcher state.

5. THREE-STAGE OBJECT PIPELINE. SelectedObjTicket (mode-2 Y-overlap scan) then
   ResolvedObjMeta (mode-3 tile/flags fetch) then ObjPixel (per-pixel priority
   resolution). Keeps DMA corruption tests local to the fetch path.

6. TRANSFER DIGEST, NOT Mode3PenaltyBreakdown. Mode-3 sub-penalty decomposition
   is still partially unconfirmed per Pan Docs. Record transfer subevents and derive
   summaries; keep exact mode3_len + transfer digest as the stable regression surface.

7. SCANOUT EVENT WITH BLANK REASONS. The PPU output contract explicitly represents
   LCD-off blanks, warmup blanks, and structural events (FrameStart, LineStart), not
   just Option<PixelEmit>.

8. ORTHOGONAL BEHAVIOR FEATURES. BehaviorConfig { model, soc_revision, features }
   replaces the linear PpuBehaviorProfile enum. DMG STAT-write quirk, pre-CGB-D SCY
   behavior, etc. are independent feature flags, not points on a strictness axis.

All types defined here become the shared vocabulary for: the Spade HDL (src/ppu/sem/),
the Python reference model (bench/ref/ppu_ref.py), the test harness, the oracle
comparison layer, and the replay/minimization tools.

RATIONALE: The CPU bring-up proved that getting the type system right before writing
entities saves enormous debugging time. The PPU is more complex (variable mode-3,
mid-frame register effects, multiple ownership regimes) so the type discipline is
even more important here.

title: VRAM and OAM memory regions
description:
Implement VRAM and OAM in the bus fabric.

VRAM (0x8000-0x9FFF, 8KB):
- 0x8000-0x8FFF: tile data block 0
- 0x9000-0x97FF: tile data block 1
- 0x9800-0x9BFF: tilemap 0
- 0x9C00-0x9FFF: tilemap 1
- CPU access blocked during PPU mode 3 (reads return 0xFF, writes ignored)

OAM (0xFE00-0xFE9F, 160 bytes):
- 40 entries x 4 bytes each
- Entry format: Y position, X position, tile index, attributes
- CPU access blocked during PPU mode 2 and 3
- Written by OAM DMA

Access restrictions (per BusOwner arbitration):
- Mode 2: PPU owns OAM, CPU blocked from OAM
- Mode 3: PPU owns both VRAM and OAM, CPU blocked from both
- Mode 0, 1: CPU has full access

Implementation should use the BusOwner mechanism from the bus arbitration task.
close_reason: Superseded by bd-252k (access) + bd-2i0q (adapter) + bd-1alk (mem protocol)

title: Hook-driven adapter for strict lockstep
description:
Implement the hook-driven adapter (§14.3) for instruction-boundary and M-cycle lockstep in bench/pyboy/hooks.py, hook_driver.py, symbols.py.

Components:
- symbols.py: Load .sym files, map symbol names to addresses
- hooks.py: Register hooks at executable labels (__pass, __fail, __checkpoint_*, __commit_*)
- hook_driver.py: Drive lockstep comparison via hook callbacks

Hook discipline (§14.4.1):
- Prefer symbol-driven hooks via .sym files over raw addresses
- Hooks work by replacing instruction at target address with special opcode
- ONLY use on deliberate executable labels, never arbitrary data/code
- Keep hook placement in one manifest owned by the harness
- Promise strict hook-driven commit comparison only for owned/instrumented ROMs
- Third-party ROMs stay at checkpoint/frame-semantic modes unless deliberately instrumented

The deep-control path is hook callbacks + snapshot/restore, NOT offline trace export (§14.3). Do NOT introduce file-based trace export as an intermediate truth source.

title: PPU mode state machine and STAT interrupt
description:
Implement PPU mode state machine in src/periph/ppu.spade.

PPU modes:
- Mode 0: HBlank (204 T-cycles typical, variable)
- Mode 1: VBlank (4560 T-cycles, lines 144-153)
- Mode 2: OAM Search (80 T-cycles)
- Mode 3: Pixel Transfer (172 T-cycles typical, variable)

Scanline timing (456 T-cycles per line):
- Lines 0-143: Mode 2 → Mode 3 → Mode 0
- Lines 144-153: Mode 1 (VBlank)

STAT register (0xFF41):
- Bits [1:0]: current mode (read-only)
- Bit 2: LY=LYC coincidence flag (read-only)
- Bit 3: Mode 0 HBlank interrupt enable
- Bit 4: Mode 1 VBlank interrupt enable
- Bit 5: Mode 2 OAM interrupt enable
- Bit 6: LY=LYC interrupt enable

STAT interrupt (IF bit 1) fires on rising edge of:
  (mode0_enable AND mode==0) OR (mode1_enable AND mode==1) OR
  (mode2_enable AND mode==2) OR (lyc_enable AND LY==LYC)

LY register (0xFF44): current scanline (0-153). Writing resets to 0.
LYC register (0xFF45): LY compare value.
close_reason: Superseded by granular beads under Epic bd-xsl (PPU Timing Wave A)

title: Declare or remove roms/build_micro_rom.py dependency on local gbxcule checkout
close_reason: Removed gbxcule dependency from roms/build_micro_rom.py by inlining ROM/RAM size tables; verified bare python3 run generates 23 valid ROMs without PYTHONPATH.

title: Epic: Video Memory & Access Infrastructure
description:
Implement the video memory subsystem that the PPU core consumes through the
architectural memory protocol. This is the bridge between PPU semantic requests
and physical storage.

KEY ARCHITECTURAL DECISION: Access policy is separated from backend adaptation.

ACCESS POLICY (src/video/access.spade):
  Decides whether a CPU/PPU/DMA memory attempt is granted, denied, or undefined.
  Pan Docs distinguishes: mode-2 blocks OAM but permits VRAM, mode-3 blocks both,
  modes 0/1 restore access, OAM DMA adds a separate ownership regime. The policy
  module owns all these rules.

  Access outcomes are structured (PpuMemResult: Ok/Denied/UndefinedRead with
  VideoBlockReason), not just returned bytes. This matters for debugging AND for
  compare-policy control -- suites that need exact $FF materialization opt into it
  explicitly, while the default contract is UndefinedRead.

BACKEND ADAPTATION (src/video/backend_adapter.spade):
  Maps granted requests onto: simulation arrays, iCE40 synchronous RAM inference,
  or explicit primitives. The PPU does not know what storage technology backs VRAM/OAM.

VRAM (0x8000-0x9FFF, 8KB):
  Tile data blocks 0-1 (0x8000-0x97FF) and tilemaps 0-1 (0x9800-0x9FFF).
  On iCE40 UP5K: 4 SPRAM blocks available, each 16Kx4. VRAM fits in 1 SPRAM block.

OAM (0xFE00-0xFE9F, 160 bytes):
  40 sprite entries x 4 bytes. Small enough for EBR inference.

FRAME SINK (src/video/frame_sink.spade):
  Simulation-only pixel collection from the ScanoutEvent stream. Produces line
  buffers, frame buffers, hashes, or diffs. Not synthesized in the hardware build.

The existing bus fabric (src/bus/membus.spade) already has ppu_vram_active and
ppu_oam_active inputs in region_owner(). This epic implements the PPU-side of that
contract.

RATIONALE: Keeping PPU semantics separate from VRAM/OAM implementation prevents
the PPU from accidentally depending on storage technology choices. It also enables
backend-differential testing (sim-array vs inferred-RAM) to catch protocol
underspecification bugs.

title: PPU: apply_mmio_write helpers, no-op mux, minor cleanups
description:
Create per-field replace helpers for PpuRegs and clean up miscellaneous PPU quality issues.

## Scope
1. Create replace_scy, replace_scx, replace_lyc, replace_wy, replace_wx, replace_bgp, replace_obp0, replace_obp1 helpers (paralleling existing replace_lcdc, replace_stat_select). Update apply_mmio_write to use them.

2. Remove no-op match in sample_render_inputs (sample.spade:~33-39). All 5 arms return visible.regs. Replace with let fetch_regs = visible.regs;.

3. Simplify same_region / same_client (fetcher.spade:~51-68): If Spade enum == works, replace manual enumeration with left == right. If not, document.

4. Inline tile_lo_addr (fetcher.spade:~101-103): one-line wrapper that adds no value. Inline bgwin_tile_addr(render.lcdc_fetch, tile_id, row) at call sites.

5. Remove zero_mmio_write wrapper (sem/events.spade:~61-63): delegates to mmio::zero_mmio_write(). Have callers use mmio:: directly.

6. Clean up let _ = ... bindings: use wildcard in destructuring patterns. Remove unused input parameter from advance_timing if confirmed unused.

## Acceptance Criteria
- apply_mmio_write uses per-field helpers
- No-op mux in sample removed
- same_region/same_client simplified
- Wrapper functions removed
- Dead bindings cleaned up
- All PPU swim tests pass

title: F low nibble formal invariant
description:
Add formal verification check in formal/cpu_invariants.sby: F register low nibble is always zero.

Property:
  always (F[3:0] == 4'b0000)

This is an unconditional invariant of the SM83. The F register only uses bits [7:4] for Z, N, H, C flags. Bits [3:0] are hardwired to zero.

If this property fails, it means:
1. Some ALU operation incorrectly sets low nibble bits, OR
2. Some register write path bypasses the mask_f helper, OR
3. POP AF doesn't mask the low nibble

This should be one of the first formal checks (Phase 1 step 8). Requires SymbiYosys setup.

title: Equivalence checking framework (cpu_refactor.eqy)
description:
Create formal/cpu_refactor.eqy — equivalence checking framework for safe refactoring and pipeline insertion (§19, §16.3 lane 4).

PURPOSE: When you insert a pipeline stage, change an encoding, or refactor internal wiring, you need to PROVE the before/after designs are functionally equivalent. This is local equivalence testing for refactors.

USE CASES:
1. Pipeline insertion (§19.3): wrapping decode→operand→ALU→flag in a pipeline(N) must produce identical committed results, only delayed by N cycles
2. Encoding changes (§18.8): switching an enum from binary to one-hot or Gray encoding must not change behavior
3. Internal wiring refactors: restructuring the cpu_core entity without changing semantics

TOOL: yosys + eqy (EQY equivalence checker) or miter-based approach with sby.
- Take two Verilog netlists (before and after refactor)
- Prove they produce identical output sequences for all input sequences
- For pipelined equivalence: prove shifted-equivalence (outputs match with constant delay)

FILE: formal/cpu_refactor.eqy (template, parameterized by before/after designs)
SCRIPT: tools/check_equivalence.sh <before.v> <after.v>

WHY P3: This is a Phase 5 optimization tool. §18.8 explicitly says "Let measurement drive encoding decisions" and §19.4 says "Design for pipeline insertion without requiring it initially." This framework enables safe optimization WHEN measurement proves it is needed, not before.

title: Green PPU_SPRITES custom ROM
description:
Write and green PPU_SPRITES.

Scope:
- Verify OBJ priority, flip flags, 8x16 mode, BG priority interaction, and transparency
- Treat sprite semantics as their own milestone after background/window work exists

Why split: sprite rendering is the most failure-prone Wave D1 ROM and should not hide earlier progress.

title: Create shared utility primitives: bit(), io_write
description:
Foundation subtask: create the shared utility module and populate it with the two most broadly-used helpers.

## Scope
1. Create a new module (e.g., src/lib/util.spade or add to an existing shared location) with:
   - pub fn bit(b: bool) -> uint<1> { if b { 1u1 } else { 0u1 } }
   - pub fn io_write(write_en: bool, write_addr: uint<16>, target: uint<16>) -> bool { write_en && write_addr == target }
2. Update swim.toml / module declarations if needed to make the module importable
3. Replace all 18+ inline if b { 1u1 } else { 0u1 } sites with bit()
4. Replace io_write copies in timer.spade, interrupts.spade, serial.spade with imports
5. Update joypad.spade variant to call shared io_write with hardcoded address

## Acceptance Criteria
- Shared module exists and is importable from both production and test code
- No remaining inline bool-to-bit conversions
- No remaining local io_write definitions
- All affected swim tests pass

## Why this is first
Other subtasks (and other beads like bd-154v) depend on this module existing. It is the smallest and safest change that unblocks the rest.
