title: Epic: Power Optimization Pass (Phase 5)
description:
Measure, validate, and optimize the power architecture that was designed-in from Phase 2 onwards.

Three-tier measurement (§18.7):
1. Always-on logical quiescence metrics: register write-enable duty cycle, bus-active duty cycle, halt quiescence
2. Nightly activity captures: SAIF switching activity on representative ROM windows
3. Synthesis/P&R dashboard: LUT/DFF/SPRAM usage, constrained f_max, clock-enable fanout

Specific validations:
- HALT quiescence (§18.5): in HALT with no wake event, zero register writes, zero bus requests, zero ALU activity, stable phase/debug outputs
- ALU idle isolation (§18.2): when AluReq::Idle, ALU input buses stay quiet
- No debug-path contamination in hardware build (§18.6)
- Physical memory mapping (§18.7.1): WRAM/VRAM/OAM use UP5K embedded memories (SPRAM/EBR), not register arrays

Optimization candidates (measurement-driven, §18.8-18.9):
- Pipeline insertion for decode→operand→ALU→flag datapath if timing requires (§19.3)
- Encoding optimization (binary vs one-hot vs Gray) only when measurement justifies
- Clock-enable fanout optimization

RATIONALE: Thesis #3 is 'power is an architectural property, not a cleanup pass.' The power-friendly patterns (clock enables, operand isolation, sparse deltas, quiescent buses) were designed-in from Phase 2. This phase VALIDATES those decisions with actual synthesis data and ONLY optimizes where measurement proves it's needed. §18.8 explicitly warns against forcing clever encodings too early.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone E): Halt-quiescence test green. ALU idle isolation measurable. Activity baseline recorded. Synthesis baseline under explicit nextpnr clock constraints. No debug-path contamination in hardware build.

title: PPU mode/timing state machine with LcdRunState (src/ppu/rtl/timing.spade)
description:
Implement the PPU phase machine as PURE HELPER FUNCTIONS that compute the next
timing/mode state from current state. State storage is in ppu_core registers;
these functions are called by step_dot.

This follows the same pattern as bd-909g (register helpers): the rtl/*.spade
files define pure transition functions, step_dot calls them, ppu_core stores
state. The "state machine" is the transition function, not a clocked entity.

PURE FUNCTIONS TO DEFINE:

fn advance_timing(
  state: PpuState, input: DotInput, next_regs: PpuRegs
) -> (PpuPhase, uint<8>, PpuSamplingState, bool, bool)
  Returns: (next_phase, next_ly, updated_sampling, line_start, frame_start)

fn visible_mode(status: PpuStatusState) -> PpuMode
  DERIVED PROJECTION: maps LcdRunState+PpuPhase to visible PpuMode.

fn lcd_enabled(status: PpuStatusState, regs: PpuRegs) -> bool
  DERIVED PROJECTION: maps LcdRunState to bool.

fn handle_lcd_transition(
  state: PpuState, old_lcdc7: bool, new_lcdc7: bool
) -> PpuState
  LCD ON/OFF transitions: Disabled↔WarmupBlankFrame↔Running.

STATE MODEL:
- LcdRunState {Disabled, WarmupBlankFrame, Running} is the stored truth
- PpuPhase enum carries rich continuation state per phase
- PpuMode {HBlank, VBlank, OamScan, PixelTransfer} is a DERIVED PROJECTION

MODE SEQUENCE PER SCANLINE (lines 0-143):
  Mode 2 (OamScan, 80 dots) -> Mode 3 (Transfer, 172-289 dots variable) ->
  Mode 0 (HBlank, remainder of 456 dots)

VBLANK (lines 144-153):
  Mode 1 for all 10 scanlines

TOTAL: 154 lines x 456 dots = 70224 dots per frame

LY ADVANCEMENT: Increments at the end of each 456-dot scanline. Resets to 0
after line 153.

LCD ON/OFF TRANSITIONS:
- LCDC.7 clear -> Disabled: immediate. Screen blanks, VRAM/OAM fully accessible,
  LY resets, mode reports 0 (via derived projection).
- LCDC.7 set -> WarmupBlankFrame: first frame is blank but timing runs normally.
  After one full frame, transitions to Running.

INTRA-DOT ORDERING (canonical, section 9.4.2):
  1. Accept timed events for dot N in seq order
  2. Refresh continuous conditions (LYC==LY, STAT-line)
  3. Evaluate phase-local sampling points
  4. Emit side effects
  5. Commit next state

The phase machine is the control backbone. All other PPU subsystems (fetcher,
FIFO, mixer, OAM scan) are gated by the current phase.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
1. advance_timing produces correct mode sequence for visible lines (2→3→0)
2. advance_timing produces VBlank for lines 144-153
3. LY increments correctly through 0-153
4. LCD disable: LcdRunState→Disabled, LY resets, mode projection returns 0
5. LCD re-enable: WarmupBlankFrame for first frame, then Running
6. visible_mode and lcd_enabled projections are consistent with LcdRunState

title: SPRAM entity dedup: extract shared dual-read-port entity
description:
Three SPRAM wrapper entities share ~60 lines of identical body code. Extract a shared entity to centralize the iCE40 SPRAM instantiation pattern.

## Background

The iCE40 UP5K has 4 SPRAM blocks (256Kbit each, organized as 16K x 16). iceboy uses three of them: one for ROM image, one for WRAM, one for VRAM. Each has a wrapper entity (rom_image_spram, wram_spram, vram_spram) that:
1. Computes the SPRAM drive signals from the bus request
2. Instantiates SB_SPRAM256KA with identical port connections
3. Reads the primitive output via mut_wire
4. Implements read-capture registers (last_read_valid, last_read_aux, last_read_high)
5. Calls update_read_capture twice (for low/high bytes)

Steps 2-5 are byte-identical across all three entities (~25 lines each). The only difference is step 1 (drive computation), which varies because ROM is read-only, WRAM uses a different address range, and VRAM has PPU-controlled access gating.

## Scope

### Extract shared spram_dual_read_port entity
Create an entity that takes a SpramDrive (the output of the per-memory drive computation) and encapsulates steps 2-5:
  pub entity spram_dual_read_port(clk: clock, rst: bool, drive: SpramDrive) -> (uint<8>, uint<8>)

Each wrapper entity (rom_image_spram, wram_spram, vram_spram) becomes:
  1. Compute drive from bus request
  2. let (lo, hi) = inst spram_dual_read_port(clk, rst, drive);

This reduces each wrapper from ~30 lines to ~10 lines, and centralizes the SPRAM instantiation pattern.

### Minor: byte_is_high15 / byte_is_high13 (ice40_spram.spade:~50-56)
Two functions that test the LSB of addresses at different widths. Both do (addr & 0x0001) == 0x0001.

Fix: Single fn byte_is_high(addr_lsb: uint<1>) -> bool. Pass trunc(addr) at call sites.

## Considerations
- SPRAM wrappers are critical for UP5K synthesis fit. Any change to the instantiation pattern must preserve exact SB_SPRAM256KA port connections. The POWEROFF, STANDBY, and SLEEP signals are particularly sensitive.
- The shared entity must not add any combinational logic between the SpramDrive and the SB_SPRAM256KA ports — it should be a pure structural wrapper.
- Verify that the Spade compiler handles entity instantiation inside another entity correctly (it should — this is standard HDL composition).
- The read-capture registers (last_read_valid, etc.) handle the SPRAM's one-cycle read latency. Their logic must be preserved exactly.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. spram_dual_read_port entity exists and encapsulates SPRAM instantiation + read capture
2. rom_image_spram, wram_spram, vram_spram each compute drive and delegate to shared entity
3. No duplicated SB_SPRAM256KA instantiation
4. byte_is_high helpers unified
5. Synthesis report shows identical SPRAM utilization (4 SPRAM blocks)
6. No behavioral change in memory read/write timing

## Test Plan
- swim test passes for spram_test_top (if it exists — verify)
- swim test passes for ebr_test_top
- membus_test_top passes (exercises SPRAM through the bus)
- soc_lockstep_top and soc_rom_top pass (end-to-end memory access)
- Synth gate passes with identical SPRAM block count
- Compare synthesis area: expect +-0 LUTs (structural change only)

title: Merge identical MemoryViaHlIncrement / MemoryViaHlDecrement arms in control.spade
Hardware synthesis top (icebreaker_top.spade + clockgen.spade)
description:
Create the hardware synthesis top-level and clock generation entity for iCEBreaker FPGA.

FILES:
- src/board/clockgen.spade: Clock generation entity
  - Takes board clock input (12 MHz on iCEBreaker)
  - Generates t_ce and m_ce enables at correct frequencies
  - Game Boy master clock: 4.194304 MHz (T-cycle rate)
  - M-cycle rate: 1.048576 MHz (T-cycle / 4)
  - Implementation: counter-based enable generation from board clock
  - For first milestone: simple divider (§6.5 — do not hardcode exact real-time frequency)
  - Board clock / Game Boy T-cycle ratio determines the counter modulus

- src/board/icebreaker_top.spade: Top-level hardware module
  - Instantiates clockgen, cpu_core, bus fabric, and peripherals
  - MUST NOT include any simulation-only signals (§18.6):
    - No CommitTrace output ports
    - No DebugTrace output ports  
    - No SimStimulus input ports
    - No BusObs sideband
  - Pin mapping via .pcf file for iCEBreaker board
  - Status LED output (optional: heartbeat or CPU running indicator)

DESIGN PRINCIPLES:
- Single root clock + enables (§6.4, §18.1): do NOT use fabric-gated clocks
- Hardware top is a CLEAN composition — no debug instrumentation leaking into synthesis
- §18.6: "Debug outputs should be removed from the hardware top by default, or else your power measurements are partly measuring your instrumentation"

VERIFICATION:
- Synthesis check: hardware top synthesizes cleanly with yosys
- No debug signal contamination: verify CommitTrace/DebugTrace are not in the synthesized netlist
- Resource utilization within UP5K limits (5280 LUTs, 1024 DFFs, 4 SPRAMs, 30 EBRs)

WHY THIS IS SEPARATE FROM SIMULATION TOPS: cpu_test_top and soc_lockstep_top include simulation-only ports for oracle comparison. The hardware top must be free of these to get accurate power/area measurements.

title: Build flavor configuration (build/profiles/)
description:
Create build profile configurations that control what observability is included.

FILES:
- build/profiles/synth.yaml: default hardware build, no semantic/debug overhead
- build/profiles/sim_semantic.yaml: adds PpuSemanticCommit outputs
- build/profiles/sim_trace.yaml: adds full PpuDebugTrace
- build/profiles/sim_power.yaml: adds activity counters and duty-cycle instrumentation

Each profile specifies:
- Which simulation tops to build
- Which output ports are enabled
- Which debug instrumentation is included
- Resource budget expectations

The synth profile must produce a clean hardware build with NO semantic/debug
fanout. The sim_semantic profile is the daily driver for regression testing.
sim_trace is for debugging. sim_power is for Phase 7 power analysis.

RATIONALE (history/ppu.md section 4.4): There is an internal tension between
needing rich observability for verification and keeping the synthesizable core
clean. Build flavors resolve this by making observability opt-in per profile,
not hardwired into the default core.

IMPLEMENTATION: This may use Spade conditional compilation if available, or
separate top-level modules that include/exclude observability wrappers. The
existing src/sim/ vs src/board/ split is the right foundation; this formalizes
it with explicit profile definitions.

title: Integrate PPU into soc_lockstep_top simulation top
description:
Extend src/sim/soc_lockstep_top.spade to instantiate the PPU and wire it into
the bus fabric.

CURRENT STATE: soc_lockstep_top instantiates CPU, bus, interrupts, timer, and
OAM DMA. It has placeholder signals for ppu_vram_active and ppu_oam_active in
the bus fabric but no real PPU module.

CHANGES:
1. Instantiate ppu_core with timebase dot_ce signal
2. Wire PPU memory requests/responses through the bus fabric
3. Wire PPU interrupt requests (vblank_req, stat_req) to interrupt controller
4. Wire PPU MMIO register reads/responses to bus fabric IO region
5. Connect timed event bridge between bus writes and PPU event input
6. Drive ppu_vram_active and ppu_oam_active from PPU access policy module
7. Expose PPU ScanoutEvent and PpuSemanticCommit through lockstep output ports
8. Expose PPU-specific debug signals (mode, LY, dot_in_line) for test observability

NEW LOCKSTEP OUTPUTS:
- ppu_scanout: ScanoutEvent (for frame/line sinks in Cocotb)
- ppu_mode: uint<2> (derived mode for quick checks)
- ppu_ly: uint<8> (for LY verification)
- ppu_stat: uint<8> (full STAT readback for verification)
- ppu_dot_in_line: uint<9> (for timing checks)

TESTING: Smoke test that the extended lockstep top compiles and runs 1 frame
without hanging. The PPU should advance through modes and emit pixels (or
blanks if LCD is disabled in reset profile).

This is a critical integration point — all ROM-based PPU tests run through
soc_lockstep_top.

title: Test child
description: test child

title: Epic: Repository Structure & Toolchain Setup (Phase 0)
description:
Lock down all truth sources before writing any real HDL. This epic covers:
- Creating the recommended repository directory layout (§5) with explicit CPU/bus/periph/sim/board splits
- Pinning all external dependencies: PyBoy (behavioral oracle), Cocotb (testbench framework), simulator backend (Icarus/Verilator), yosys+nextpnr-ice40 (synthesis/P&R), and formal verification tools
- Establishing the ROM manifest schema and initial custom ROM inventory
- Defining explicit model/reset/memory behavior profile enums

RATIONALE: Phase 0 exists because the architecture plan's thesis #2 is 'verification before implementation.' If truth sources drift or are ambiguous, every subsequent phase inherits that uncertainty. Pinning versions in lockfiles ensures reproducible CI and developer environments. The repo layout follows §5 to keep CPU semantic code, simulation tops, board integration, and test infrastructure cleanly separated—this separation is load-bearing for the power architecture (§18) because debug/sim code must never leak into hardware builds.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone A prerequisite): Every smoke script runs reproducibly from a clean checkout. All version pins are recorded in toolchain/ manifests.

title: Register file and flag helper unit tests
description:
Create test/unit/test_regs.py — isolated unit tests for register accessors and flag helpers.

These are the most-called pure functions in the entire CPU. Bugs here cascade into every instruction.

TEST CASES FOR REGISTER ACCESSORS:
1. get_r8 / set_r8:
   - For each R8 variant (A, B, C, D, E, H, L):
     - set_r8(zeros, sel, 0x42) → get_r8(result, sel) == 0x42
     - set_r8 doesn't modify OTHER registers (isolation check)
     - set_r8 with 0x00 and 0xFF (boundary values)

2. get_r16 / set_r16:
   - For each RegPair (BC, DE, HL, SP, AF):
     - set_r16(zeros, pair, 0x1234) → get_r16(result, pair) == 0x1234
     - Verify byte ordering: for BC, B=0x12 and C=0x34
     - set_r16 doesn't modify other register pairs (isolation)
     - Boundary values: 0x0000, 0xFFFF
   - AF special case: set_r16 for AF must mask F low nibble to zero

3. Round-trip consistency:
   - set_r8 then get_r16 (verify pair assembly)
   - set_r16 then get_r8 (verify byte extraction)

TEST CASES FOR FLAG HELPERS:
4. flags_from_f:
   - 0xF0 → Flags{z:true, n:true, h:true, c:true}
   - 0x80 → Flags{z:true, n:false, h:false, c:false}
   - 0x00 → Flags{z:false, n:false, h:false, c:false}
   - All 16 combinations of {Z,N,H,C}

5. pack_flags:
   - Flags{z:true, n:true, h:true, c:true} → 0xF0
   - Round-trip: pack_flags(flags_from_f(f)) == mask_f(f) for all f

6. mask_f:
   - 0xFF → 0xF0
   - 0xF0 → 0xF0
   - 0x0F → 0x00
   - 0x00 → 0x00
   - Low nibble is ALWAYS zero (SM83 invariant)

DETAILED LOGGING:
  [regs] test_set_r8_B:
    Input:  Registers{A=0,F=0,B=0,C=0,...}, sel=B, val=0x42
    Output: Registers{A=0,F=0,B=0x42,C=0,...}
    Check:  get_r8(output, B) == 0x42 ✓
    Check:  get_r8(output, A) == 0x00 ✓ (isolation)
    Check:  get_r8(output, C) == 0x00 ✓ (isolation)
    PASS

title: Delete Python symbol-asserting architecture contract tests
description: Remove low-value Python architecture contract tests that only assert generated source/text contains expected symbols or surfaces. Focus on the tools/tests scaffold and contract coverage that uses patterns like assertIn(symbol, text) and '*matches_architecture_contract*' rather than behavior-level verification. Representative files include tools/tests/test_ppu_scaffold.py, tools/tests/test_video_scaffold.py, tools/tests/test_membus_scaffold.py, tools/tests/test_sim_scaffolds.py, tools/tests/test_cpu_types.py, tools/tests/test_cpu_semantics.py, and similar Python symbol-surface checks under tools/tests/.

title: Timebase entity unit tests
description:
Create test/unit/test_timebase.py — unit tests for the timebase entity (§6.1.1).

The timebase is the single source of timing truth for the entire system. Timer, CPU, and DMA derive their timing from it. Bugs here cause system-wide timing drift.

TEST CASES:
1. Reset behavior:
   - After reset: sys_counter=0, t_index=0, m_index=0
   - t_ce and m_ce at defined initial values

2. T-cycle enable pattern:
   - t_ce fires at the expected rate relative to board clock
   - t_ce duty cycle is correct (1 board clock high per N board clocks)
   - Count T-cycles over 100 board clocks, verify count matches expected

3. M-cycle enable pattern:
   - m_ce fires every 4th t_ce
   - m_ce is a subset of t_ce (m_ce implies t_ce on that cycle)
   - Count M-cycles over 100 board clocks, verify count = T-cycles / 4

4. t_index cycling:
   - t_index cycles 0→1→2→3→0→1→... on each t_ce
   - t_index holds between t_ce assertions
   - m_ce fires when t_index wraps from 3→0

5. sys_counter and m_index:
   - sys_counter increments monotonically on each t_ce
   - m_index increments monotonically on each m_ce
   - After N m_ce pulses: m_index == N

6. Long-run consistency:
   - Run for 1000+ board clocks
   - Verify no drift: m_ce count * 4 == t_ce count (within 1 cycle of reset)
   - Verify no glitches: t_ce/m_ce never fire on consecutive board clocks (unless rate is 1:1 for sim)

DETAILED LOGGING:
  [timebase] test_m_cycle_pattern:
    Board clocks elapsed: 100
    T-cycle enables seen: 25 (expected 25) ✓
    M-cycle enables seen: 6  (expected 6)  ✓
    t_index at m_ce edges: [0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0] (all 0) ✓
    sys_counter final: 25 ✓
    PASS

NOTE: For first CPU milestone (§6.5), m_ce can be asserted every board clock (1:1 ratio). The timebase test should parameterize the clock ratio so it works for both simulation-fast mode and hardware-realistic mode.

title: Register file struct and pure accessor helpers
description:
Create src/cpu/regs.spade with the register file and all accessor functions from §9.

Register struct (§9.1):
  struct Registers {
    a: uint<8>, f: uint<8>,
    b: uint<8>, c: uint<8>,
    d: uint<8>, e: uint<8>,
    h: uint<8>, l: uint<8>,
    sp: uint<16>, pc: uint<16>,
  }

Pure accessor/update helpers (§9.2) — write once, use everywhere:
  fn get_r8(regs: Registers, sel: R8) -> uint<8>
  fn set_r8(regs: Registers, sel: R8, val: uint<8>) -> Registers
  fn get_r16(regs: Registers, pair: RegPair) -> uint<16>
  fn set_r16(regs: Registers, pair: RegPair, val: uint<16>) -> Registers
  fn flags_from_f(f: uint<8>) -> Flags
  fn pack_flags(flags: Flags) -> uint<8>

Flag struct:
  struct Flags { z: bool, n: bool, h: bool, c: bool }

F register canonical form (§9.3):
  fn mask_f(f: uint<8>) -> uint<8>  // Always zeros low nibble
  NEVER duplicate this masking ad hoc. Always use the helper.

R8/RegPair enums:
  enum R8 { A, B, C, D, E, H, L }  // Note: (HL) is NOT R8, it's a memory access
  enum RegPair { BC, DE, HL, SP, AF }

DESIGN NOTE: These are pure functions — no state, no side effects. They specify combinational semantics (§4.1 denotational layer). The operational layer (cpu_core entity) uses them but owns the actual registers.

title: Timer/DIV entity unit tests with edge case coverage
description:
Create test/unit/test_timer.py — comprehensive unit tests for the timer subsystem.

The timer is one of the most edge-case-rich components in the Game Boy. These tests verify it in ISOLATION before it participates in ROM-level testing.

TEST CASES:

1. DIV basic counting:
   - DIV increments at correct rate (every 256 T-cycles = 64 M-cycles)
   - DIV reads return upper 8 bits of 16-bit counter
   - Writing ANY value to DIV resets the entire 16-bit counter to 0
   - Log: each test prints counter value at key cycle boundaries

2. DIV reset side effects (the obscure case):
   - If the selected TAC bit was 1 when DIV is reset, TIMA may increment
   - Test: set TAC to select a bit that is currently 1, write to DIV, verify TIMA behavior
   - This is a real hardware behavior that Mooneye tests verify

3. TIMA basic counting:
   - TIMA increments at each TAC-selected rate (div-by-1024/16/64/256)
   - TIMA overflow (0xFF→0x00) triggers reload from TMA and sets IF timer bit
   - Test each TAC clock-select value with precise cycle counting

4. TIMA overflow timing:
   - Overflow has a 1-M-cycle delay before TMA reload and IF set
   - During the delay cycle, writing TIMA cancels the reload AND the interrupt
   - During the delay cycle, writing TMA changes the value that will be loaded
   - Test the exact cycle boundaries

5. TAC enable/disable:
   - Clearing TAC bit 2 stops TIMA counting
   - Setting TAC bit 2 resumes counting
   - Verify no spurious increments on enable/disable edges

6. TAC clock-select change:
   - Changing TAC bits [1:0] while timer is enabled can cause spurious TIMA increment
   - Test: change from fast rate to slow rate (selected bit goes 1→0, falling edge detected)
   - This is the FALLING-EDGE DETECTION behavior

7. Shared timebase verification:
   - Timer derives from the shared timebase sys_counter (§6.1.1)
   - Verify DIV and TIMA agree with the timebase entity's counter
   - Verify no off-by-one drift between timer and CPU M-cycle counting

LOGGING: Each test logs cycle-by-cycle timer state:
  [CYCLE 63] DIV=0x00 TIMA=0x00 TAC=0x05 sys_counter=0x00FF
  [CYCLE 64] DIV=0x01 TIMA=0x00 TAC=0x05 sys_counter=0x0100  ← DIV incremented

ERROR REPORTING: On failure, log the full timer state at the failing cycle plus 5 cycles before/after for context.

title: BG FIFO mechanics and SCX discard unit tests (test/ppu/unit/test_bg_fifo.py)
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for the BG pixel FIFO and SCX initial discard logic (bd-3dts).

The BG FIFO, SCX discard counter, and window-clear behavior are critical to
correct pixel output timing. SCX discard directly affects mode-3 length (0-7
extra dots), and FIFO clear on window activation affects window penalty (6 dots).
These mechanics deserve dedicated unit tests beyond the invariant tests.

TEST CASES:

1. FIFO PUSH AND POP ORDERING:
   Push 8 pixels from fetcher. Pop them one by one. Verify FIFO order
   (first pushed = first popped). Verify pixel data integrity.

2. FIFO DEPTH TRACKING:
   Push pixels and track depth. Verify depth increments on push, decrements
   on pop. Verify maximum depth (16 entries: current + prefetch tile).

3. SCX DISCARD AT SCANLINE START:
   Set SCX=3 (scx_low3_line=3). Start a scanline. Verify first 3 pixels
   from the initial tile fetch are discarded (not emitted as ScanoutEvent).
   Set SCX=0. Verify no pixels discarded. Set SCX=7. Verify 7 discarded.

4. SCX DISCARD USES SAMPLED VALUE:
   Set SCX=5 at frame start. Change SCX=0 mid-scanline. Verify discard
   still uses the sampled value (5), not the live register value.
   Verify scx_low3_line comes from PpuSamplingState.

5. SCX DISCARD ADDS TO MODE-3 LENGTH:
   Run scanline with SCX=0. Record mode-3 dot count.
   Run scanline with SCX=7. Record mode-3 dot count.
   Verify difference is 7 dots. (Exact mode-3 length = 172 + scx_discard +
   window_penalty + object_penalties.)

6. NO PIXEL OUTPUT DURING DISCARD:
   Set SCX=4. Monitor ScanoutEvent output during first tile fetch.
   Verify zero ScanoutEvent::Pixel emitted during the 4-dot discard period.
   Verify x_out counter does not advance during discard.

7. FIFO CLEARED ON WINDOW ACTIVATION:
   Push pixels into FIFO. Trigger window activation. Verify FIFO is empty
   after window activation. Verify fetcher restarts from window tilemap.
   Verify window penalty is approximately 6 dots.

8. FIFO EMPTY STALL:
   Start mode 3 before fetcher has pushed pixels. Verify no pop occurs
   from empty FIFO. Verify pixel output stalls until FIFO has data.

9. DOUBLE PUSH PREVENTION:
   Verify fetcher cannot push while FIFO is full (>= 8 pixels from previous
   tile still present). Verify fetcher stalls at push step.

10. FIFO STATE AFTER LCD DISABLE:
    Disable LCD. Verify FIFO is cleared. Verify no spurious pops occur.
    Verify FIFO state is clean for next LCD enable.

11. SCX DISCARD WITH WINDOW INTERACTION:
    Set SCX=3 and window at WX=15. Verify SCX discard completes before
    window activation is evaluated. Verify window activation clears FIFO
    including any remaining discard state.

LOGGING FORMAT:
  [SUITE] test_bg_fifo.py
    [CASE] test_scx_discard_count
      [STEP] Set SCX=5 (scx_low3_line=5)
      [STEP] Start mode 3 on line 0
      [STEP] Monitor pixel output during first tile
      [CHECK] Pixels discarded: expected=5 actual=5 OK
      [CHECK] First visible pixel x: expected=0 actual=0 OK
      [STEP] Record mode-3 dot count
      [CHECK] Mode-3 extra dots from SCX: expected=5 actual=5 OK
      [PASS] test_scx_discard_count (0.04s)

FAILURE DIAGNOSTICS:
On failure, log: FIFO contents (all entries with color/metadata), FIFO depth,
discard counter value, scx_low3_line sampled value, x_out counter, current dot,
fetcher step, current pixel output state.

title: Author Wave D custom ROM assembly source
description:
Write SM83 assembly for all PPU test ROMs across Waves A through E.

The revised architecture (history/ppu.md) splits PPU ROMs across five internal waves.
ROM authoring is organized per wave, with each wave's ROM bead handling its own
authoring. This bead tracks the overall inventory.

WAVE A (timing/control): PPU_OFF_ON_BASIC, LY_LYC_BASIC, STAT_MODE_SEQ,
  VBLANK_IRQ_BASIC, VRAM_OAM_GATING
  -> Authored in bd-108n

WAVE B (BG/window): BG_STATIC, BG_SCROLL_WRAP, BG_SIGNED_ADDR, WINDOW_BASIC,
  WINDOW_LINE_COUNTER, WINDOW_WX_WY_EDGE, WINDOW_WX0_STUTTER, WINDOW_WX166_NEXTLINE,
  WINDOW_WX_RETRIGGER_GLITCH, WINDOW_WINEN_TOGGLE_REARM
  -> Authored in bd-1pms

WAVE C (objects): OBJ_BASIC, OBJ_PRIORITY, OBJ_10_PER_LINE, OBJ_8x16, OBJ_FLIP,
  OBJ_BG_MASK, OBJ_X_HIDDEN_STILL_COUNTS, OBJ_FETCH_CANCEL_LCDC1,
  OBJ_DMA_METADATA_CORRUPT
  -> Authored in bd-27uw

WAVE D (raster timing): Generated sweep families + mealybug/mooneye
  -> Tracked in bd-1zoy

WAVE E (DMA realism): DMA_MODE2_HIDE, DMA_MODE3_FETCH_GLITCH, DMA_OBJ_DROP_LINES
  -> Tracked in bd-2nvb

All ROMs follow the ABI in spec/rom_abi.md: __pass/__fail labels, WRAM signature
block, optional __checkpoint labels.
close_reason: Superseded by per-wave ROM beads (bd-108n, bd-1pms, bd-27uw). All dependents (bd-mtc, bd-crv, bd-2ag, bd-2xi, bd-3pr) are already closed.

title: ALU implementation: 8-bit logic (AND, OR, XOR)
description:
Implement 8-bit logic operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- AND A,r/n: A & operand, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=1, C=0
- OR A,r/n: A | operand, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=0, C=0
- XOR A,r/n: A ^ operand, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=0, C=0

These are the simplest ALU operations with fixed flag patterns. Good starting point for ALU verification.

title: PPU mode FSM and timing unit tests (test/ppu/unit/test_ppu_modes.py)
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for the PPU mode state machine and timing behavior.

TEST CASES:

1. MODE SEQUENCE PER VISIBLE LINE:
   Advance through one visible scanline. Verify mode sequence:
   Mode 2 (80 dots) -> Mode 3 (>=172 dots) -> Mode 0 (remainder of 456 dots).
   Log each mode transition with dot count.

2. VBLANK ENTRY AND EXIT:
   Verify Mode 1 starts at line 144, continues through line 153,
   then wraps to line 0 with Mode 2.

3. LY COUNTER:
   Verify LY increments exactly once per 456-dot scanline.
   Verify LY cycles through 0-153 (154 values total).
   Verify LY reads correctly from STAT readback.

4. SCANLINE TIMING EXACTNESS:
   Verify exactly 456 dots per scanline, 70224 dots per frame.
   Log dot counts between mode transitions.

5. LCD DISABLE:
   Write LCDC.7=0. Verify: mode reports 0, LY resets, VRAM/OAM accessible.
   Log all state changes on LCD disable.

6. LCD RE-ENABLE:
   Write LCDC.7=1 after disable. Verify: first frame is blank
   (WarmupBlankFrame), then normal operation resumes.

7. LCD DISABLE DURING MODE 3:
   Disable LCD mid-transfer. Verify immediate cessation of render activity.

8. MODE DERIVED FROM LCDRUNSTATE:
   Verify visible_mode() returns correct PpuMode for each LcdRunState.
   Verify lcd_enabled() returns correct bool.
   Verify impossible states (e.g., mode=PixelTransfer while Disabled) cannot occur.

LOGGING FORMAT (per bd-dny):
  [SUITE] test_ppu_modes.py
    [CASE] test_mode_sequence_visible_line
      [STEP] Reset PPU, advance to line 0
      [STEP] Read mode at dot 0: Mode 2 (OAM Scan)
      [STEP] Advance 80 dots
      [CHECK] Mode at dot 80: expected=3 actual=3 OK (PixelTransfer)
      [STEP] Advance 172 dots (minimum mode 3)
      [CHECK] x_out: expected>=0 actual=12 OK
      [STEP] Continue until mode changes
      [CHECK] Mode 0 entry at dot 252: expected=0 actual=0 OK (HBlank)
      [STEP] Advance to dot 456
      [CHECK] line_start: expected=true actual=true OK
      [PASS] test_mode_sequence_visible_line (0.05s)

FAILURE DIAGNOSTICS:
On mode transition failure, log: current dot, expected mode, actual mode,
LcdRunState, PpuPhase, dot_in_line, line_index. Include waveform dump path.

title: Epic: Hardware Synthesis Fit — iCE40 UP5K
description:
Restore synthesizability of the iceboy design on iCE40 UP5K-SG48.

PROBLEM: The design currently exceeds UP5K resources by ~26x (140,235 logic cells
vs 5,280 available). Root cause: all memory in membus.spade uses clocked_memory_init
which Yosys lowers to LUT+DFF register cascades instead of dedicated SPRAM/EBR blocks.
The DMA mirror pattern (separate ROM/WRAM/HRAM copies for CPU and DMA read paths)
doubles the already-massive register footprint.

CURRENT RESOURCE SNAPSHOT (2026-04-04 baseline):
- Logic cells: 140,235 / 5,280 (26.5x over)
- DFF:          68,099 / 1,024 (66.5x over)
- LUT4:         72,190 / 5,280 (13.7x over)
- SPRAM:             0 / 4     (unused)
- EBR:               0 / 30    (unused)
- P&R status: FAILED (no BELs remaining)

TARGET RESOURCE BUDGET (after fix):
- SPRAM: 3 of 4 used (ROM 32KB, WRAM 8KB, VRAM 8KB)
- EBR: 1-2 of 30 used (OAM 256B, HRAM 127B)
- Logic cells: < 5,280 (CPU + bus decode + peripherals only)
- DFF: < 1,024 (CPU state + peripheral registers only)
- P&R status: PASSED

PHYSICAL MEMORY MAPPING (§18.7.1, src/mem/logical/map.spade):
- ROM (32KB)   -> 1 SPRAM block (SB_SPRAM256KA, 16Kx16 = 32KB)
- WRAM (8KB)   -> 1 SPRAM block (uses 8KB of 32KB capacity)
- VRAM (8KB)   -> 1 SPRAM block (uses 8KB of 32KB capacity)
- CartRAM      -> 1 SPRAM block (future, uses remaining block)
- OAM (256B)   -> EBR block(s) (SB_RAM40_4K, 256x16)
- HRAM (127B)  -> EBR block or inferred registers (small enough)

KEY CHALLENGE: SPRAM is SINGLE-PORT (one address bus, shared read/write). The
current design needs two simultaneous read ports (CPU + DMA, or CPU + PPU). This
requires time-multiplexing within the M-cycle's 4 T-cycles, using the timebase's
t_index signal to schedule port access.

ARCHITECTURE REFERENCES: §18.7.1 (physical memory mapping), §18.6 (debug-free
hardware), §12.4 (bus fabric).

RATIONALE: bd-23s created the physical memory wrapper entities in src/mem/phys/
but did NOT wire them into membus.spade or make them use actual SPRAM/EBR
primitives. Those wrappers also use clocked_memory_init internally, which still
infers registers. This epic completes the work: rewrite wrappers to use real
iCE40 primitives, wire them into the bus, eliminate DMA mirrors, and verify
the design fits on UP5K.

ACCEPTANCE:
- tools/run_hardware_baseline.sh completes with P&R status "passed"
- Baseline JSON shows SPRAM > 0 used, EBR > 0 used
- LUT4 < 5,280, DFF < 1,024
- All existing membus simulation tests (test/unit/test_membus.py) still pass
- All existing ROM tests (cpu_instrs Blargg, loads_basic, etc.) still pass
- tools/verify_hw_build.sh --enforce-budget passes

title: Epic: PPU Timing & Control — Wave A
description:
Implement PPU mode timing, register architecture, STAT interrupt generation,
and LCD on/off behavior. This is Wave A of the PPU milestone decomposition
(history/ppu.md section 17.1).

GOAL: Zero ambiguity about register/timing state before real rendering is trusted.

The revised architecture makes several key choices here:

1. MODE STATE MACHINE uses LcdRunState + PpuPhase as the stored truth.
   PpuMode (HBlank/VBlank/OamScan/PixelTransfer) is a derived projection, not
   stored state. This eliminates impossible (mode, lcd_enabled) combinations.

2. STAT LINE is modeled as a continuously-evaluated OR of enabled sources with
   rising-edge interrupt generation. The DMG STAT-write quirk is a hook point
   behind a BehaviorFeature flag, staged for later.

3. LCD ON/OFF uses LcdRunState {Disabled, WarmupBlankFrame, Running}. Disabling
   LCDC.7 immediately blanks the screen and grants full VRAM/OAM access.
   Re-enabling leaves the first frame blank.

4. TIMED EVENT BRIDGE converts bus writes into TimedPpuEvent with seq ordering.
   The PPU core does not receive raw MMIO pokes; it processes an ordered event
   stream at each dot.

5. INTRA-DOT ORDERING is canonical:
   (1) accept timed events for dot N in seq order,
   (2) refresh continuous conditions (LYC==LY, STAT-line),
   (3) evaluate phase-local sampling,
   (4) emit side effects,
   (5) commit next state.

6. REGISTER VISIBILITY includes both write and readback matrices:
   - Write: when does a CPU write to SCX become visible to the renderer?
   - Readback: what does a CPU read of STAT return? (mixed stored+derived view)
   - LY is always derived/read-only, STAT mode bits report 0 when LCD disabled.

Wave A ROMs: PPU_OFF_ON_BASIC, LY_LYC_BASIC, STAT_MODE_SEQ, VBLANK_IRQ_BASIC,
VRAM_OAM_GATING.

Wave A external suite: vblank_stat_intr-GS, stat_lyc_onoff, stat_irq_blocking,
lcdon_timing-GS, lcdon_write_timing-GS (from mooneye PPU set).

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone B): Owned Wave A ROMs green, targeted mooneye timing tests
green, no unexplained LY/mode/STAT divergence.

title: Flag packing/unpacking helpers
description:
Implement flag helpers in src/cpu/regs.spade (or extend existing).

Functions:
  fn flags_from_f(f: uint<8>) -> Flags   // Extract Z,N,H,C from F register bits 7,6,5,4
  fn pack_flags(flags: Flags) -> uint<8>  // Pack Z,N,H,C into bits 7,6,5,4, low nibble always 0

F register canonical form (§9.3):
  fn mask_f(f: uint<8>) -> uint<8>  // f & 0xF0 — always zeros low nibble

Invariant: pack_flags(flags_from_f(f)) == mask_f(f) for all f.
The low nibble of F is ALWAYS zero on SM83. This must be enforced everywhere F is written.

These helpers are used by ALU, decoder, and CPU core. They must be pure functions.

title: Green MBC1_RAM custom ROM
description:
Write and green MBC1_RAM.

Scope:
- External RAM enable, bank switching, and disabled-read behavior
- Keep RAM behavior separate from ROM-bank-switching bring-up

Why split: RAM-mode bugs should not block MBC1 switch-path progress.

title: Deduplicate sign_extend_e8_u16 and hi_byte/lo_byte across modules
description:
## Problem

Several small utility functions are defined identically in multiple files:

### 1. sign_extend_e8_u16(off: uint<8>) -> uint<16>

Defined identically in two places:
- src/cpu/alu.spade:230-236
- src/cpu/semantics.spade:624-630

Both implementations:

    fn sign_extend_e8_u16(off: uint<8>) -> uint<16> {
        if (off & 0x80u8) == 0x80u8 {
            0xff00u16 | zext(off)
        } else {
            zext(off)
        }
    }

### 2. hi_byte(word: uint<16>) -> uint<8> and lo_byte(word: uint<16>) -> uint<8>

Defined identically in two places:
- src/cpu/regs.spade:101-107 (private, used by get_r16/set_r16)
- src/cpu/semantics.spade:180-186 (private, used extensively)

Both implementations:

    fn hi_byte(word: uint<16>) -> uint<8> { trunc(word >> 8) }
    fn lo_byte(word: uint<16>) -> uint<8> { trunc(word) }

## Solution

### For hi_byte/lo_byte:

Make the existing definitions in src/cpu/regs.spade public:

    pub fn hi_byte(word: uint<16>) -> uint<8> { trunc(word >> 8) }
    pub fn lo_byte(word: uint<16>) -> uint<8> { trunc(word) }

Then import them in semantics.spade and remove the local copies:

    use lib::cpu::regs::{hi_byte, lo_byte};

### For sign_extend_e8_u16:

Move to src/cpu/regs.spade (or a new src/cpu/util.spade if preferred) and make public:

    pub fn sign_extend_e8_u16(off: uint<8>) -> uint<16> { ... }

Then import in both alu.spade and semantics.spade, removing local copies.

## Files

- src/cpu/regs.spade: Make hi_byte/lo_byte public, add sign_extend_e8_u16
- src/cpu/semantics.spade: Remove local hi_byte, lo_byte, sign_extend_e8_u16; add imports
- src/cpu/alu.spade: Remove local sign_extend_e8_u16; add import

## Estimated savings

~15 lines removed (3 duplicate function bodies).

## Verification

- All tests must pass.
- Synthesis output must be identical.
- Verify that Spade's module system allows pub fn in regs.spade to be imported by sibling modules (alu.spade, semantics.spade). If not, use src/cpu/main.spade to re-export.
